> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Concept of Stress, Types of Stress, Uniaxial Tension Test, Stress-Strain Diagram, Ductile and Brittle Materials, Conditions Affecting Mechanical Properties, Hardness and Impact Strength

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Stress?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stress (σ)** is defined as the **internal resistance force per unit area** developed inside a material when an external load is applied. It is a measure of the intensity of the internal force distributed over a cross-sectional area.

$$\sigma = \lim_{\Delta A \to 0} \frac{\Delta F}{\Delta A} = \frac{dF}{dA}$$

**Unit of Stress:** Newton per square metre ($\text{N/m}^2$) or **Pascal (Pa)**.  
In engineering practice, **Megapascal (MPa)** and **Gigapascal (GPa)** are commonly used.

- $1 \text{ MPa} = 10^6 \text{ N/m}^2$
- $1 \text{ GPa} = 10^9 \text{ N/m}^2$

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> Imagine you are standing on a **freshly laid beach sand**. If you stand on **one foot**, your foot sinks deep — but if you lie down flat (distributing your weight over a larger area of contact), you barely sink. The intensity of "sinking pressure" depends not just on your weight, but on the **area over which it is distributed**. This "intensity of internal force per unit area" is exactly what engineers call **STRESS**.

So when a 1000 N force is applied on a bar:
- On a cross-section of $10 \text{ mm}^2$ → Stress = $100 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 100 \text{ MPa}$ (very high)
- On a cross-section of $100 \text{ mm}^2$ → Stress = $10 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 10 \text{ MPa}$ (moderate)

**Same load, different stress** — because stress depends on **area**.

---

## 1.2 What is Strain?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Strain (ε)** is defined as the **ratio of change in dimension (deformation) to the original dimension** of the body. It is a **dimensionless quantity** (often expressed in mm/mm or as a percentage).

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L}$$

Where:
- $\Delta L$ = change in length (elongation or contraction)
- $L$ = original length

Strain represents the **relative deformation** of the material — it is the response of the material to applied stress.

### Intuition for Strain

> [!NOTE]
> If a rubber band of original length 100 mm stretches to 110 mm, the strain is $(110-100)/100 = 0.10$ or $10\%$. Strain tells us **"how much did it deform relative to its original size?"** — not the absolute change.

---

## 1.3 Types of Stress

Stress at a point in a body is broadly classified based on the **orientation of the internal force** relative to the cross-section.

### 1.3.1 Normal Stress (σ)

When the **internal force acts perpendicular (normal)** to the cross-sectional area.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Normal Stress (σ)** = $\dfrac{P}{A}$, where $P$ is the axial force and $A$ is the cross-sectional area.

**Two subtypes:**
- **Tensile Stress ($\sigma_t$)**: Internal force **pulls** the material apart (e.g., stretching a rod).
- **Compressive Stress ($\sigma_c$)**: Internal force **pushes** the material together (e.g., column under load).

By convention:
- **Tensile stress → Positive (+)**
- **Compressive stress → Negative (−)**

### 1.3.2 Shear Stress (τ)

When the **internal force acts parallel (tangential)** to the cross-sectional area.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Shear Stress (τ)** = $\dfrac{V}{A}$, where $V$ is the shear force and $A$ is the area resisting shearing.

**Examples:**
- Scissors cutting paper.
- Rivets and bolts in a joint.
- Punching a hole in a metal sheet.

### 1.3.3 Bearing Stress (σ_b)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bearing Stress (σ_b)** is the contact pressure (compressive stress) developed between two bodies pressed against each other, such as a bolt bearing on a plate or a pin inside a hole.

$$\sigma_b = \frac{P}{A_b} = \frac{P}{d \cdot t}$$

Where:
- $A_b$ = projected contact area = diameter $\times$ thickness
- $P$ = compressive load
- $d$ = bolt/pin diameter, $t$ = plate thickness

### 1.3.4 Volumetric / Hydrostatic Stress (σ_v)

When equal normal stresses act on all faces of a small element (e.g., a body submerged in fluid under pressure).

$$\sigma_v = -p \quad \text{(compressive)}$$

---

## 1.4 The Uniaxial Tension Test (Tensile Test)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Uniaxial Tension Test** is the most fundamental mechanical test used to determine the mechanical properties of a material. A standardized specimen (typically cylindrical or flat dog-bone shape) is pulled in tension at a controlled rate until failure.

**Standard Specimen:**
- Circular cross-section (round bar) — most common
- Flat cross-section (for sheet/plate materials)
- Has **gauge length** $L_0$ and **gauge diameter** $d_0$ (or width $b_0$)

**Why called "Uniaxial"?**
Because stress is applied along **only ONE axis** (the longitudinal axis of the specimen).

### Intuition for the Test

> [!NOTE]
> Think of slowly pulling apart a **piece of chewing gum** or **toffee**. Initially, it resists, then stretches, becomes thinner in the middle (necking), and finally snaps. The tensile test is a **scientific, instrumented, and standardized** version of this exact observation — but it records force and elongation continuously to plot the **Stress–Strain Diagram**.

**Test Machine:** Universal Testing Machine (UTM) — capable of applying tensile or compressive load and recording the elongation.

---

## 1.5 The Stress–Strain Diagram

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Stress–Strain Diagram** is a graphical plot of stress (σ) on the Y-axis versus strain (ε) on the X-axis, obtained from a uniaxial tension test. It is the **fingerprint** of a material's mechanical behaviour.

**Key Points Labeled on a Typical Ductile Material Curve:**

| Label | Point | Significance |
|-------|-------|--------------|
| **A** | Proportional Limit | End of Hooke's law region (σ ∝ ε) |
| **B** | Elastic Limit | Maximum stress up to which body returns to original shape |
| **C** | Upper Yield Point | Stress at which yielding begins (sudden drop) |
| **D** | Lower Yield Point | Stress during continuous yielding |
| **E** | Ultimate Stress Point | Maximum stress the material can withstand |
| **F** | Fracture / Breaking Point | Stress at which material fails |

**Key Engineering Properties Read from the Diagram:**
- **Young's Modulus (E)**: Slope of the initial linear region (OA)
- **Yield Strength ($\sigma_y$)**: Stress at yield point
- **Ultimate Tensile Strength ($\sigma_u$)**: Maximum stress on the curve
- **Fracture Strength ($\sigma_f$)**: Stress at fracture
- **Percentage Elongation**: Ductility measure
- **Percentage Reduction in Area**: Another ductility measure

---

## 1.6 Ductile vs. Brittle Materials

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ductile Material:** A material that undergoes **large plastic deformation** before fracture (e.g., mild steel, copper, aluminium, gold).
>
> **Brittle Material:** A material that fractures with **little or no plastic deformation** (e.g., cast iron, glass, concrete, ceramics).

### Comparison Table

| Property | Ductile Material | Brittle Material |
|----------|------------------|------------------|
| Plastic deformation before fracture | **Large** | **Negligible / None** |
| Yielding | **Yes** (clear yield point) | **No** clear yield |
| Fracture type | Cup-and-cone (ductile fracture) | Flat, granular (cleavage) |
| Energy absorbed before failure | **High** | **Low** |
| Warning before failure | **Yes** (visible deformation) | **No** (sudden failure) |
| Examples | Mild steel, copper, aluminium | Cast iron, glass, concrete |
| Stress–strain curve | Long plastic region | Almost linear till fracture |

### Engineering Insight

> [!NOTE]
> Ductile materials are **preferred for structures subjected to dynamic or impact loads** (bridges, vehicles, buildings) because they deform visibly before failing, giving warning. Brittle materials are preferred where rigidity and shape stability are critical (e.g., machine tool beds, ceramic tiles).

---

## 1.7 Conditions Affecting Mechanical Properties

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The mechanical properties of a material (yield strength, ductility, hardness, impact strength) **are NOT constant** — they vary significantly with the following conditions:

### 1.7.1 Temperature
- **As temperature increases:**
  - Yield strength and ultimate strength **decrease**.
  - Ductility **increases**.
  - Material becomes **softer and more ductile**.
- **At very low temperatures:**
  - Material becomes **brittle** (e.g., **Ductile-to-Brittle Transition Temperature (DBTT)** in steels).
  - Charpy impact tests are conducted at various temperatures to find DBTT.

### 1.7.2 Strain Rate (Loading Rate)
- **High strain rate** (impact loading):
  - Increases yield strength (material appears stronger).
  - Reduces ductility.
  - Can cause **brittle failure even in ductile materials**.
- **Low strain rate** (static loading):
  - More time for plastic deformation → higher ductility.

### 1.7.3 Stress State (Triaxiality)
- **Uniaxial stress state** → more ductile behaviour.
- **Triaxial stress state** (e.g., under a notch or thick section) → suppresses plastic flow → favours brittle fracture.

### 1.7.4 Notches and Stress Concentrations
- Sharp notches, holes, and cracks act as **stress raisers**.
- Notch sensitivity reduces the actual strength of the material below its theoretical value.
- **Notch effect** is captured by the **Stress Concentration Factor ($K_t$)**.

### 1.7.5 Environmental Conditions
- **Corrosive environment**: Reduces effective strength (stress corrosion cracking).
- **Radiation**: Affects microstructure → changes properties.
- **Hydrogen embrittlement**: Hydrogen atoms diffuse into metal → makes it brittle.

### 1.7.6 Heat Treatment and Cold Working
- **Annealing** → softer, more ductile.
- **Quenching** → harder, more brittle.
- **Cold working (strain hardening)** → increases strength, decreases ductility.

### 1.7.7 Size and Geometry (Size Effect)
- Larger specimens may exhibit different properties than smaller ones (especially in impact tests — due to stress distribution and metallurgical differences).

---

## 1.8 Hardness

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hardness** is the **resistance of a material to localized plastic deformation**, typically by indentation, scratching, or abrasion. It is a surface property and is closely related to wear resistance and strength.

### Intuition for Hardness

> [!NOTE]
> Think of trying to **scratch one material with another**. Diamond scratches glass — so diamond is harder than glass. **Hardness measures "how difficult is it to make a permanent mark on the surface?"**

### Common Hardness Tests

| Test | Indenter | Load | Application |
|------|----------|------|-------------|
| **Brinell (HB)** | Tungsten carbide / hardened steel ball (10 mm) | 500–3000 kgf | Rough surfaces, castings, forgings |
| **Vickers (HV)** | Diamond pyramid (square base, 136° angle) | 1–100 kgf | All materials, thin sections |
| **Rockwell (HR)** | Diamond cone (HRC) or steel ball (HRB) | Minor + Major load | Quick shop-floor testing |
| **Shore (Scleroscope)** | Steel hammer dropped from fixed height | Dynamic (rebound) | Large parts, rolls |
| **Mohs** | Scratch with 10 reference minerals | — | Minerals, ceramics |
| **Knoop (HK)** | Elongated diamond pyramid | Very low load | Microhardness, brittle materials |

### Engineering Significance of Hardness
- Hardness correlates with **tensile strength** (empirical relation: $\sigma_u \approx C \times \text{HB}$, where $C \approx 3.45$ for steels).
- Hardness tests are **non-destructive** (only a small indentation is left).
- Used for **quality control** in manufacturing.

---

## 1.9 Impact Strength / Impact Toughness

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Impact Strength** is the **ability of a material to absorb energy and plastically deform without fracturing under a suddenly applied (impact) load**. It is measured as the **energy absorbed per unit area** at the notch.

$$I = \frac{E_{absorbed}}{A_{notch}}$$

**Unit:** Joules (J) for total energy, or **J/m²** for energy per unit area.

### Common Impact Tests

| Test | Specimen Support | Notch Position | Common Use |
|------|-----------------|----------------|------------|
| **Izod** | Cantilever (vertical, clamped at bottom) | At the top, on the **striking side** | Plastics, metals (BS standard) |
| **Charpy** | Simply supported (horizontal, both ends) | In the middle, on the **tension side (opposite to strike)** | Metals (ASTM standard) |

### Key Observations
- **Ductile materials** → High impact strength (absorb a lot of energy before fracture).
- **Brittle materials** → Low impact strength (fracture suddenly).
- **Lowers with decreasing temperature** → DBTT (Ductile-Brittle Transition Temperature).
- **Notched specimens always have lower impact strength** than un-notched ones.

### Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> Imagine trying to break a **dry twig** versus a **green twig** by snapping it suddenly. The dry twig snaps instantly (low impact strength — brittle). The green twig bends and absorbs a lot of energy before breaking (high impact strength — ductile). Impact strength measures this "ability to absorb sudden shock energy."

### Engineering Significance
- Critical in selecting materials for **bridges, pressure vessels, ships, military vehicles, and pipelines** that may face sudden shocks, collisions, or earthquakes.
- Used to determine **DBTT** for steels used in cold-environment service (e.g., Arctic pipelines).

---

## 1.10 Summary Mapping at a Glance

| Concept | One-Line Definition | Symbol | SI Unit |
|---------|--------------------|--------|---------|
| Stress | Internal force per unit area | $\sigma, \tau$ | Pa (N/m²) |
| Strain | Deformation per unit length | $\varepsilon$ | Dimensionless |
| Normal Stress | Force perpendicular to area | $\sigma$ | Pa |
| Shear Stress | Force parallel to area | $\tau$ | Pa |
| Bearing Stress | Contact compressive stress | $\sigma_b$ | Pa |
| Young's Modulus | Slope of σ–ε in elastic region | $E$ | Pa |
| Poisson's Ratio | Lateral strain / axial strain | $\nu$ | Dimensionless |
| Hardness | Resistance to localized deformation | HB, HV, HR | Various |
| Impact Strength | Energy absorbed under sudden load | $I$ | J or J/m² |

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation of Stress

### 2.1.1 The Concept of Internal Force

When a body is subjected to external forces, the body maintains equilibrium through the development of **internal resisting forces** at every imaginary cross-section.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> To find the stress at any cross-section, we make an **imaginary cut** perpendicular to the axis, isolate one portion, and replace the removed portion by the **internal force** that was acting across the cut surface.

**Step-by-step reasoning:**
1. Consider a bar in tension with axial load $P$ at its ends.
2. Pass an imaginary section perpendicular to the axis.
3. Isolate one side; it experiences an external load $P$ at its free end.
4. For equilibrium, the **internal force at the cut section** must equal $P$ in magnitude.
5. This internal force, distributed over the cross-sectional area $A$, gives stress:
$$\sigma = \frac{P}{A}$$

---

### 2.1.2 Why Stress and Not Just Force?

**Force** is an **extensive** property (depends on size of the body), while **stress** is an **intensive** property (independent of size, depends only on material state and loading).

**Example:**
- A steel rod of area $100 \text{ mm}^2$ and a steel rod of area $200 \text{ mm}^2$ both carry a load of 10 kN.
- The larger rod has **half the stress** and will stretch **less**.
- Hence, **stress** is the proper quantity for designing and comparing materials.

---

### 2.1.3 Average vs. True Stress

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Average (Engineering) Stress ($\sigma_{eng}$):** Force divided by the **original** cross-sectional area.
>
> **True Stress ($\sigma_{true}$):** Force divided by the **instantaneous (actual)** cross-sectional area (which decreases as the specimen necks).

$$\sigma_{eng} = \frac{P}{A_0}, \quad \sigma_{true} = \frac{P}{A_i}$$

Where $A_0$ = original area, $A_i$ = instantaneous area (after deformation).

In a tensile test, true stress is **higher** than engineering stress beyond the ultimate point because the area is reducing rapidly during necking.

Assuming **constant volume** during plastic deformation:
$$A_i \cdot L_i = A_0 \cdot L_0$$
$$A_i = \frac{A_0 \cdot L_0}{L_i} = \frac{A_0}{1+\varepsilon}$$

$$\sigma_{true} = \sigma_{eng} \cdot (1 + \varepsilon)$$

---

### 2.1.4 Average vs. True Strain

**Engineering Strain:**
$$\varepsilon_{eng} = \frac{L_i - L_0}{L_0} = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0}$$

**True (Natural / Logarithmic) Strain:**
$$\varepsilon_{true} = \int_{L_0}^{L_i} \frac{dL}{L} = \ln\left(\frac{L_i}{L_0}\right) = \ln(1+\varepsilon_{eng})$$

**Why we need true strain:**
Engineering strain assumes uniform strain, which breaks down at large deformations and necking. True strain gives the **accumulated strain** and is more accurate for large plastic deformations.

---

## 2.2 Hooke's Law — Linear Elastic Behaviour

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hooke's Law:** Within the proportional limit, stress is directly proportional to strain.
> $$\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$$
> where $E$ is the **Modulus of Elasticity (Young's Modulus)**.

**Geometric meaning:** $E$ is the slope of the initial linear portion of the stress–strain diagram.

$$E = \frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon} = \frac{P/A}{\Delta L/L_0} = \frac{P \cdot L_0}{A \cdot \Delta L}$$

From this, the **elongation of a uniform bar under axial load**:
$$\Delta L = \frac{P \cdot L_0}{A \cdot E}$$

For a bar of **varying cross-section**, sum over segments:
$$\Delta L = \sum_{i} \frac{P_i \cdot L_i}{A_i \cdot E_i}$$

---

## 2.3 Poisson's Ratio (ν)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> When a bar is stretched axially, it contracts laterally. The ratio of **lateral strain to axial strain** is called **Poisson's Ratio**.
>
> $$\nu = -\frac{\varepsilon_{lateral}}{\varepsilon_{axial}} = -\frac{\varepsilon_y}{\varepsilon_x} = -\frac{\varepsilon_z}{\varepsilon_x}$$

The negative sign ensures $\nu$ is **positive** (since lateral and axial strains have opposite signs).

**Typical values:**
- Steel: $\nu \approx 0.30$
- Copper: $\nu \approx 0.33$
- Aluminium: $\nu \approx 0.35$
- Concrete: $\nu \approx 0.10$ to $0.20$
- Rubber: $\nu \approx 0.50$ (theoretical maximum for isotropic materials)
- Cork: $\nu \approx 0.00$ (ideal for wine bottle stoppers!)

---

## 2.4 Shear Stress, Shear Strain, and Shear Modulus (G)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> When shear force $V$ acts on area $A$:
> $$\tau = \frac{V}{A}$$
>
> **Hooke's Law in Shear:**
> $$\tau = G \cdot \gamma$$
> where $G$ is the **Shear Modulus (Modulus of Rigidity)** and $\gamma$ is the **shear strain** (angle of deformation in radians).

**Relationship between E, G, and ν (for isotropic materials):**
$$G = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)}$$

---

## 2.5 Bulk Modulus (K)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bulk Modulus (K)** measures resistance to **uniform volumetric compression** (e.g., hydrostatic pressure).
>
> $$\sigma_v = -p = -K \cdot \varepsilon_v$$
> where $\varepsilon_v = \dfrac{\Delta V}{V_0}$ is the volumetric strain.

**Relationship:**
$$K = \frac{E}{3(1-2\nu)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Note that as $\nu \to 0.5$, $K \to \infty$ → **incompressible material** (rubber-like).

---

## 2.6 Relationship Between Elastic Constants (E, G, K, ν)

For a **homogeneous, isotropic, linearly elastic** material, only **TWO** of these constants are independent. The relations are:

$$G = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)} \qquad K = \frac{E}{3(1-2\nu)} \qquad \frac{E}{9K} + \frac{1}{3G} = \frac{1}{3K} \cdot \frac{(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)}{(1+\nu)} = \frac{1-2\nu}{6K(1+\nu)} \text{ ...}$$

A more useful relation:
$$E = \frac{9KG}{3K+G}$$

---

## 2.7 Resilience and Toughness (Energy Concepts)

### 2.7.1 Strain Energy per Unit Volume (Modulus of Resilience)

The area under the elastic portion of the σ–ε curve up to the elastic limit equals the **strain energy density** (energy stored per unit volume).

$$u = \int_0^{\varepsilon_y} \sigma \, d\varepsilon$$

For linearly elastic region:
$$u = \frac{1}{2} \sigma_y \varepsilon_y = \frac{1}{2} \sigma_y \left(\frac{\sigma_y}{E}\right) = \frac{\sigma_y^2}{2E}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Modulus of Resilience ($u_r$):** Maximum energy a material can absorb per unit volume **without permanent deformation**.
> $$u_r = \frac{\sigma_y^2}{2E}$$

### 2.7.2 Modulus of Toughness

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Modulus of Toughness ($u_t$):** Total energy per unit volume the material can absorb **before fracture** — equal to the **total area under the entire σ–ε curve** up to fracture.
> $$u_t = \int_0^{\varepsilon_f} \sigma \, d\varepsilon$$

For a simplified bilinear or linear-with-plastic-region model:
$$u_t = \frac{\sigma_y^2}{2E} + \sigma_y (\varepsilon_f - \varepsilon_y)$$

---

## 2.8 Hardness Formulas

### 2.8.1 Brinell Hardness Number (HB / BHN)

$$\text{BHN} = \frac{2P}{\pi D \left[D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2}\right]}$$

Where:
- $P$ = applied load (kgf)
- $D$ = indenter ball diameter (mm)
- $d$ = diameter of indentation (mm)

### 2.8.2 Vickers Hardness Number (HV / VHN)

$$\text{VHN} = \frac{1.854 \, P}{d^2}$$

Where:
- $P$ = applied load (kgf)
- $d$ = diagonal length of indentation (mm)

### 2.8.3 Rockwell Hardness Number (HR)

$$\text{HR} = N - \frac{h}{s}$$

Where:
- $N$ = scale constant (100 for diamond cone scales, 130 for ball scales)
- $h$ = depth of indentation (mm)
- $s$ = scale unit (0.002 mm)

### 2.8.4 Relationship to Tensile Strength

For steels (empirical):
$$\sigma_u (\text{MPa}) \approx 3.45 \times \text{BHN}$$

---

## 2.9 Impact Strength — Theoretical Basis

The energy absorbed in an impact test is:
$$E_{absorbed} = W (\text{height}) - w (\text{height after swing})$$

$$E_{absorbed} = mg(H - h) - \text{friction/energy losses}$$

In terms of pendulum angle:
$$E_{absorbed} = mgR(\cos\beta - \cos\alpha)$$

Where:
- $H, h$ = initial and final pendulum heights
- $\alpha$ = initial swing angle, $\beta$ = final swing angle
- $R$ = pendulum length
- $m$ = pendulum mass

**Impact strength (specific):**
$$\text{Impact Strength} = \frac{E_{absorbed}}{A_{notch}} \quad \text{(J/m}^2\text{)}$$

---

## 2.10 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|----------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Normal Stress | $\sigma = P/A$ | Pa or MPa | Axial loading |
| 2 | Shear Stress | $\tau = V/A$ | Pa or MPa | Parallel loading |
| 3 | Bearing Stress | $\sigma_b = P / (d \cdot t)$ | Pa or MPa | Bolt/plate contact |
| 4 | Engineering Strain | $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L_0$ | Dimensionless | — |
| 5 | True Strain | $\varepsilon_{true} = \ln(1+\varepsilon)$ | Dimensionless | Large deformation |
| 6 | Hooke's Law | $\sigma = E \varepsilon$ | — | Linear elastic |
| 7 | Poisson's Ratio | $\nu = -\varepsilon_{lat}/\varepsilon_{ax}$ | Dimensionless | $0 \leq \nu \leq 0.5$ |
| 8 | Shear Modulus | $G = E / [2(1+\nu)]$ | Pa | Isotropic materials |
| 9 | Bulk Modulus | $K = E / [3(1-2\nu)]$ | Pa | Hydrostatic loading |
| 10 | Elongation | $\Delta L = PL_0/(AE)$ | m | Uniform bar |
| 11 | Modulus of Resilience | $u_r = \sigma_y^2/(2E)$ | J/m³ | Elastic energy |
| 12 | Modulus of Toughness | $u_t = \int \sigma\, d\varepsilon$ | J/m³ | Total area |
| 13 | Brinell Hardness | $\text{HB} = 2P/(\pi D[D-\sqrt{D^2-d^2}])$ | kgf/mm² | — |
| 14 | Vickers Hardness | $\text{HV} = 1.854\, P/d^2$ | kgf/mm² | — |
| 15 | Tensile Strength vs. HB | $\sigma_u \approx 3.45 \times \text{HB}$ | MPa | Steels only |
| 16 | Impact Strength | $I = E_{absorbed}/A_{notch}$ | J/m² | Izod/Charpy |

---

## 2.11 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Property Used | Why It Matters |
|-------------|--------------|----------------|
| Cable/wire design | Tensile strength | Must not snap under load |
| Bridge structural steel | Yield strength, ductility | Must deform visibly before collapse |
| Pressure vessels | Toughness, impact strength | Avoid catastrophic brittle fracture |
| Cutting tools | Hardness | Resist wear at cutting edge |
| Aircraft skin | High strength-to-weight ratio | Aluminium alloys |
| Cold-region pipelines | DBTT from Charpy tests | Avoid brittle fracture at low temperature |
| Earthquake-resistant buildings | Ductility, energy absorption | Dissipate seismic energy |
| Springs | Modulus of resilience, elastic limit | Store and release energy |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Expression for Stress (σ = P/A)

**Statement to Prove:** The internal normal stress on a cross-section of an axially loaded bar equals the axial force divided by the cross-sectional area.

**Given:**
- A prismatic bar of uniform cross-section $A$ subjected to axial tensile load $P$ at both ends.
- The bar is in static equilibrium.

**Step 1: Define the geometry.**
Consider a bar of length $L$ and uniform cross-sectional area $A$, with axial load $P$ applied along its centroidal axis.

**Step 2: Pass an imaginary section.**
Make a section perpendicular to the axis at any point along the length. This exposes an internal surface of area $A$.

**Step 3: Apply equilibrium to one portion.**
Consider the right portion of the bar after sectioning. The external force $P$ acts at its free end. For the portion to be in equilibrium, an **internal force** $F_{int}$ must act on the cut face.

**Step 4: Write the equilibrium equation.**
Summing forces along the axis:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum F_x &= 0 \\
P - F_{int} &= 0 \\
F_{int} &= P
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Distribute the force over the area.**
The internal force $F_{int}$ is not concentrated at one point; it is distributed over the area $A$. The intensity (force per unit area) is the **normal stress**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma &= \frac{F_{int}}{A} \\
\sigma &= \frac{P}{A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** $\sigma = \dfrac{P}{A}$, with units of $\text{N/m}^2$ or Pa. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Expression for Elongation of a Uniform Bar

**Statement to Prove:** For a uniform bar of length $L_0$, cross-section $A$, modulus $E$, under axial load $P$, the elongation is:
$$\Delta L = \frac{P L_0}{A E}$$

**Step 1: Start from Hooke's Law.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma &= E \varepsilon \\
\frac{P}{A} &= E \cdot \frac{\Delta L}{L_0}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Solve for $\Delta L$.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{P}{A} &= \frac{E \cdot \Delta L}{L_0} \\
E \cdot \Delta L &= \frac{P \cdot L_0}{A} \\
\Delta L &= \frac{P \cdot L_0}{A \cdot E}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** Elongation $\Delta L = \dfrac{P L_0}{AE}$. $\blacksquare$

**Extension to a bar of varying cross-section (Stepped Bar):**
The total elongation is the sum of elongations of each segment:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta L_{total} &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} \Delta L_i = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{P_i L_i}{A_i E_i}
\end{aligned}
$$

If the same load $P$ flows through and $E$ is constant:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta L_{total} &= \frac{P}{E} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{L_i}{A_i}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Relationship Between E, G, and ν (for isotropic materials)

**Statement to Prove:** $G = \dfrac{E}{2(1+\nu)}$

**Step 1: Consider a square element subjected to pure shear.**
Let the element have sides of length $a$. The shear stress $\tau$ acts on all four faces, producing shear strain $\gamma$ (change in angle from 90°).

**Step 2: Observe the deformation.**
The element distorts into a rhombus. The diagonal $AC$ elongates (becomes the long diagonal) and the diagonal $BD$ contracts (becomes the short diagonal).

**Step 3: Express strains along the diagonals.**
The diagonal of an undeformed square makes an angle of $45°$ with the sides. After shear strain $\gamma$, the long diagonal has elongation corresponding to tensile strain:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_{diag} = \frac{\tau}{E}(1+\nu) \cdot (\text{factor depending on orientation})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Apply Mohr's circle or stress transformation.**
The principal stresses for pure shear are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_1 &= +\tau \quad \text{(tension along one diagonal)} \\
\sigma_2 &= -\tau \quad \text{(compression along the other diagonal)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Compute strain along the principal direction using generalized Hooke's law.**
For principal direction 1:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_1 &= \frac{1}{E}[\sigma_1 - \nu \sigma_2] = \frac{1}{E}[\tau - \nu(-\tau)] = \frac{\tau(1+\nu)}{E}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Relate shear strain $\gamma$ to principal strain.**
By geometry of deformation, the shear strain is twice the principal strain along the diagonal (for small deformations):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\gamma &= 2 \varepsilon_1 = \frac{2\tau(1+\nu)}{E}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7: Apply Hooke's law in shear.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau &= G \gamma \Rightarrow \gamma = \frac{\tau}{G}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8: Equate the two expressions for $\gamma$.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\tau}{G} &= \frac{2\tau(1+\nu)}{E}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 9: Solve for G.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{G} &= \frac{2(1+\nu)}{E} \\
G &= \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** $G = \dfrac{E}{2(1+\nu)}$ $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Bulk Modulus from Hydrostatic Pressure

**Statement to Prove:** $K = \dfrac{E}{3(1-2\nu)}$

**Step 1: Consider hydrostatic pressure $p$ on a cubic element.**
The stress state is:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_x = \sigma_y = \sigma_z = -p
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute volumetric strain using generalized Hooke's law.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_x &= \frac{1}{E}[\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)] = \frac{1}{E}[-p - \nu(-p - p)] \\
\varepsilon_x &= \frac{1}{E}[-p + 2\nu p] = \frac{-p(1-2\nu)}{E}
\end{aligned}
$$

By symmetry, $\varepsilon_y = \varepsilon_z = \varepsilon_x$.

**Step 3: Volumetric strain.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_v = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z = \frac{3 \cdot (-p)(1-2\nu)}{E} = \frac{-3p(1-2\nu)}{E}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: By definition of bulk modulus.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
p &= -K \varepsilon_v \quad \text{(pressure = negative of stress × volumetric strain)} \\
p &= -K \cdot \frac{-3p(1-2\nu)}{E} \\
p &= \frac{3pK(1-2\nu)}{E}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Solve for K.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
1 &= \frac{3K(1-2\nu)}{E} \\
K &= \frac{E}{3(1-2\nu)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** $K = \dfrac{E}{3(1-2\nu)}$ $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: True Stress from Engineering Stress (Constant Volume Assumption)

**Statement to Prove:** $\sigma_{true} = \sigma_{eng} (1 + \varepsilon_{eng})$

**Step 1: Assume constant volume during plastic deformation.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
A_0 \cdot L_0 &= A_i \cdot L_i
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Express instantaneous area in terms of engineering strain.**
Since $\varepsilon = (L_i - L_0)/L_0$, we have $L_i = L_0(1+\varepsilon)$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_i &= \frac{A_0 L_0}{L_i} = \frac{A_0 L_0}{L_0(1+\varepsilon)} = \frac{A_0}{1+\varepsilon}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: True stress definition.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{true} &= \frac{P}{A_i} = \frac{P}{A_0/(1+\varepsilon)} = \frac{P(1+\varepsilon)}{A_0}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Substitute engineering stress.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{eng} &= \frac{P}{A_0} \\
\Rightarrow \sigma_{true} &= \sigma_{eng}(1+\varepsilon)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** $\sigma_{true} = \sigma_{eng}(1+\varepsilon)$ $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.6 Derivation 6: Modulus of Resilience (Energy Density in Elastic Region)

**Statement to Prove:** $u_r = \dfrac{\sigma_y^2}{2E}$

**Step 1: Strain energy density is area under stress–strain curve.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
u = \int_0^{\varepsilon_y} \sigma \, d\varepsilon
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Substitute Hooke's law $\sigma = E\varepsilon$.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
u = \int_0^{\varepsilon_y} E\varepsilon \, d\varepsilon = E \cdot \frac{\varepsilon_y^2}{2} = \frac{E \varepsilon_y^2}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Substitute $\varepsilon_y = \sigma_y / E$.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
u_r &= \frac{E}{2} \cdot \left(\frac{\sigma_y}{E}\right)^2 = \frac{E \sigma_y^2}{2 E^2} = \frac{\sigma_y^2}{2E}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** $u_r = \dfrac{\sigma_y^2}{2E}$ $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.7 Derivation 7: Brinell Hardness Number (BHN)

**Statement to Prove:** $\text{BHN} = \dfrac{2P}{\pi D \left[D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2}\right]}$

**Step 1: Geometry of indentation.**
A ball of diameter $D$ is pressed into the surface with load $P$, creating a circular indent of diameter $d$. The depth of indent is $h$.

**Step 2: Relate $h$, $D$, and $d$ via the circle.**
The radius of the ball is $D/2$. By geometry:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left(\frac{D}{2}\right)^2 &= \left(\frac{D}{2} - h\right)^2 + \left(\frac{d}{2}\right)^2 \\
\frac{D^2}{4} &= \frac{D^2}{4} - Dh + h^2 + \frac{d^2}{4} \\
0 &= -Dh + h^2 + \frac{d^2}{4} \\
Dh - h^2 &= \frac{d^2}{4} \\
h &= \frac{d^2}{4(D - h)} \quad \text{(since } h << D \text{, approximate } D - h \approx D) \\
h &\approx \frac{d^2}{4D}
\end{aligned}
$$

A more accurate closed form (without approximation):
$$
\begin{aligned}
h &= \frac{D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2}}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Curved surface area of indentation.**
The indentation is a spherical cap. Its surface area:
$$
\begin{aligned}
A_s &= \pi D h = \pi D \cdot \frac{D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2}}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Brinell Hardness is load divided by curved area.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{BHN} = \frac{P}{A_s} &= \frac{P}{\pi D \cdot \frac{D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2}}{2}} = \frac{2P}{\pi D \left[D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2}\right]}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** $\text{BHN} = \dfrac{2P}{\pi D \left[D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2}\right]}$ $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.8 Analytical Matrix — Properties vs. Material Type

| Property | Mild Steel | Aluminium | Cast Iron | Copper | Concrete |
|----------|-----------|-----------|-----------|--------|----------|
| Yield Strength (MPa) | 250 | 95–200 | (None — brittle) | 70–330 | 20–40 |
| Ultimate Strength (MPa) | 400–550 | 150–300 | 150–400 | 220–350 | 2–5 (tension) |
| Young's Modulus (GPa) | 200 | 70 | 100–170 | 110 | 25–35 |
| Poisson's Ratio | 0.30 | 0.35 | 0.25 | 0.33 | 0.15 |
| Density (kg/m³) | 7850 | 2700 | 7200 | 8960 | 2400 |
| % Elongation | 25–40 | 12–25 | 0–1 | 30–50 | ~0 |
| Behaviour | Ductile | Ductile | Brittle | Ductile | Brittle |
| Typical HB | 120–180 | 30–50 | 150–250 | 40–80 | — |

---

## 3.9 Worked Example: Stress and Elongation in a Stepped Bar

**Problem:** A stepped bar made of steel ($E = 200$ GPa) has two segments:
- Segment 1: $L_1 = 200$ mm, $A_1 = 100$ mm²
- Segment 2: $L_2 = 300$ mm, $A_2 = 50$ mm²
An axial load $P = 20$ kN is applied at the end.

**Find:** (i) Stress in each segment, (ii) Total elongation.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Stress in Segment 1.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_1 = \frac{P}{A_1} = \frac{20 \times 10^3 \text{ N}}{100 \text{ mm}^2} = 200 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 200 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Stress in Segment 2.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_2 = \frac{P}{A_2} = \frac{20 \times 10^3 \text{ N}}{50 \text{ mm}^2} = 400 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 400 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Elongation of Segment 1.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta L_1 = \frac{P L_1}{A_1 E} = \frac{(20 \times 10^3)(200)}{(100)(200 \times 10^3)} = \frac{4 \times 10^6}{2 \times 10^7} = 0.20 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Elongation of Segment 2.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta L_2 = \frac{P L_2}{A_2 E} = \frac{(20 \times 10^3)(300)}{(50)(200 \times 10^3)} = \frac{6 \times 10^6}{10^7} = 0.60 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Total Elongation.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta L_{total} = \Delta L_1 + \Delta L_2 = 0.20 + 0.60 = 0.80 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $\sigma_1 = 200$ MPa, $\sigma_2 = 400$ MPa, $\Delta L_{total} = 0.80$ mm. $\blacksquare$

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Stress Classification System

The following diagram maps how the concept of **stress** branches into its subtypes, the underlying loading modes, and the real-world mechanisms producing each.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Applied External Load P] --> B[Internal Resistance Force Fint]
    B --> C[Stress sigma equals Fint by A]
    C --> D{Normal to Area?}
    D -->|Yes| E[Normal Stress sigma]
    D -->|No| F[Parallel to Area?]
    F -->|Yes| G[Shear Stress tau]
    F -->|No| H[Contact Compression]
    E --> E1[Tensile Stress plus sigma]
    E --> E2[Compressive Stress minus sigma]
    E --> E3[Hydrostatic Volumetric]
    G --> G1[Pure Shear tau]
    G --> G2[Transverse Shear in Beams]
    H --> H1[Bearing Stress sigma b]
    H --> H2[Contact Pressure p]
    E1 --> R1[Example: Tie Rod, Cable]
    E2 --> R2[Example: Column, Strut]
    E3 --> R3[Example: Submerged Body]
    G1 --> R4[Example: Rivets, Bolts]
    G2 --> R5[Example: Beam Web Shear]
    H1 --> R6[Example: Bolt on Plate]
    H2 --> R7[Example: Ball Bearing Contact]
```

---

## 4.2 Functional Flow: Uniaxial Tension Test Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Prepare Standard Specimen] --> S2[Measure Initial Length L0 and Area A0]
    S2 --> S3[Mount in UTM Jaws]
    S3 --> S4[Apply Gradual Axial Tensile Load]
    S4 --> S5[Record Load P and Elongation dL Continuously]
    S5 --> S6{Elastic Region?}
    S6 -->|Yes| S7[Linear sigma equals E epsilon Hookes Law]
    S6 -->|No| S8[Yielding Begins at sigma y]
    S8 --> S9[Plastic Deformation Strain Hardening]
    S9 --> S10[Ultimate Stress sigma u Maximum]
    S10 --> S11[Necking Local Area Reduction]

    S14 --> S15[Compute Percent Elongation and Reduction in Area]
    S15 --> S16[Plot Stress Strain Diagram]

```


# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q:** Define the following terms with proper units:
(i) Normal Stress
(ii) Shear Stress
(iii) Bearing Stress

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Normal Stress ($\sigma$):** When the internal force on a cross-section acts **perpendicular** to the area, the resulting intensity of force is called normal stress.

$$
\sigma = \frac{P}{A}
$$

**Unit:** N/m² or Pascal (Pa); commonly MPa (10⁶ Pa) or GPa (10⁹ Pa).

**(ii) Shear Stress ($\tau$):** When the internal force acts **parallel (tangential)** to the cross-sectional area, the resulting intensity of force is called shear stress.

$$
\tau = \frac{V}{A}
$$

**Unit:** N/m² or Pa (MPa in engineering).

**(iii) Bearing Stress ($\sigma_b$):** The **contact compressive stress** developed between two bodies pressed against each other, e.g., a bolt bearing on a plate.

$$
\sigma_b = \frac{P}{d \cdot t}
$$

where $d$ = bolt diameter, $t$ = plate thickness.

**Unit:** N/m² or Pa (MPa in engineering). **(3 Marks)**

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q:** Differentiate between **ductile** and **brittle** materials with examples. State any **four** distinguishing characteristics.

**Model Answer:**

| Sl. No. | Ductile Material | Brittle Material |
|---------|------------------|------------------|
| 1 | Undergoes **large plastic deformation** before fracture | **Negligible** plastic deformation |
| 2 | Exhibits a **clear yield point** on stress–strain curve | **No** clear yield point |
| 3 | Fracture surface shows **cup-and-cone** | Fracture surface is **flat/granular** |
| 4 | **Gives warning** before failure (visible deformation) | Fails **suddenly without warning** |
| 5 | **High** energy absorption before fracture | **Low** energy absorption |
| 6 | **Examples:** Mild steel, copper, aluminium | **Examples:** Cast iron, glass, concrete |

**(Any 4 points × 0.75 = 3 Marks)**

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q:** A mild steel specimen of diameter 20 mm and gauge length 50 mm was tested in a Universal Testing Machine. The following data were obtained from the uniaxial tension test:

| Load (kN) | Elongation (mm) |
|-----------|------------------|
| 20 | 0.025 |
| 40 | 0.050 |
| 60 | 0.075 |
| 80 | 0.100 |
| 100 | 0.130 |
| 120 | 0.180 |
| 140 (Yield) | 0.350 |
| 180 (Ultimate) | 2.500 |
| 130 (Fracture) | 4.000 |

**(a)** Plot the **stress–strain diagram** and label all the salient points. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Determine:
(i) Young's Modulus
(ii) Yield Strength
(iii) Ultimate Tensile Strength
(iv) Percentage Elongation
(v) Modulus of Resilience **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Calculate the original cross-sectional area.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
A_0 &= \frac{\pi}{4} d_0^2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(20)^2 = 314.159 \text{ mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate stress ($\sigma$) for each load.**
$$
\sigma = \frac{P}{A_0}
$$

| $P$ (kN) | $P$ (N) | $\sigma = P/A_0$ (MPa) |
|----------|---------|------------------------|
| 20 | 20,000 | 63.66 |
| 40 | 40,000 | 127.32 |
| 60 | 60,000 | 190.99 |
| 80 | 80,000 | 254.65 |
| 100 | 100,000 | 318.31 |
| 120 | 120,000 | 381.97 |
| 140 (Yield) | 140,000 | 445.63 |
| 180 (Ultimate) | 180,000 | 573.0 |
| 130 (Fracture) | 130,000 | 413.80 |

**Step 3: Calculate strain ($\varepsilon$) for each elongation.**
$$
\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0}, \quad L_0 = 50 \text{ mm}
$$

| $\Delta L$ (mm) | $\varepsilon = \Delta L/50$ |
|------------------|---------------------------|
| 0.025 | 0.0005 |
| 0.050 | 0.0010 |
| 0.075 | 0.0015 |
| 0.100 | 0.0020 |
| 0.130 | 0.0026 |
| 0.180 | 0.0036 |
| 0.350 | 0.0070 |
| 2.500 | 0.0500 |
| 4.000 | 0.0800 |

**(a) Stress–Strain Diagram:**

```
       Stress σ (MPa)
        600 ┤                              ● E (Ultimate, σ_u = 573)
        500 ┤                         ╱───
        400 ┤                    ● F (Fracture, σ_f = 413.8)
        300 ┤               ╱
        200 ┤          ╱
        100 ┤     ╱
          0 ┼──┴──┴──┴──┴──┴──┴──┴──┴──────── Strain ε (×10⁻³)
              0  0.5 1.0 1.5 2.0 2.6 3.6 7.0  50  80
                  ↑ Linear Region (Hooke's Law)
```

**Labeled Points:**
- **O** (Origin)
- **A** (Proportional Limit) — end of linear region (~318 MPa)
- **B** (Elastic Limit)
- **C/D** (Yield Points) — σ_y = 445.63 MPa
- **E** (Ultimate) — σ_u = 573 MPa
- **F** (Fracture) — σ_f = 413.8 MPa

**(b) Calculations:**

**(i) Young's Modulus (slope of linear region):**
Using the first point ($\sigma = 63.66$ MPa, $\varepsilon = 0.0005$):
$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= \frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon} = \frac{63.66 \text{ MPa}}{0.0005} = 127,320 \text{ MPa} \approx 127.3 \text{ GPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

Using second point: $E = 127.32/0.001 = 127.32$ GPa ✓ (consistent)

$$
\boxed{E \approx 127.3 \text{ GPa}}
$$

**(ii) Yield Strength:** From the table, the load at yield is 140 kN.
$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_y &= \frac{140{,}000}{314.159} = 445.63 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\sigma_y \approx 445.6 \text{ MPa}}
$$

**(iii) Ultimate Tensile Strength:** At maximum load = 180 kN.
$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_u &= \frac{180{,}000}{314.159} = 573.0 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\sigma_u \approx 573 \text{ MPa}}
$$

**(iv) Percentage Elongation:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\% \text{ Elongation} &= \frac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{(50 + 4) - 50}{50} \times 100 = \frac{4}{50} \times 100 = 8\%
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\% \text{ Elongation} = 8\%}
$$

**(v) Modulus of Resilience:** (energy stored per unit volume in elastic region)
$$
\begin{aligned}
u_r &= \frac{\sigma_y^2}{2E} = \frac{(445.63 \times 10^6)^2}{2 \times (127.3 \times 10^9)} \\
&= \frac{1.986 \times 10^{17}}{2.546 \times 10^{11}} = 7.80 \times 10^5 \text{ J/m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{u_r \approx 0.78 \text{ MJ/m}^3 = 0.78 \text{ MPa}}
$$

**(Total: 14 Marks — 7 + 7)** $\blacksquare$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q:** With the help of a **neat sketch of the stress–strain diagram for a ductile material**, explain the following:
**(a)** Salient points (Proportional Limit, Elastic Limit, Yield Point, Ultimate Stress, Fracture Point) and the corresponding stress values. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss **ductile vs. brittle materials**, and explain how **temperature, strain rate, and notches** affect mechanical properties. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Salient Points on the Stress–Strain Diagram (7 Marks):**

The stress–strain curve for a ductile material (e.g., mild steel) is shown below:

```
           σ (MPa)
            │
            │              ● F (Fracture) σ_f ≈ 400
            │            ╱╲
            │           ╱  ● E (Ultimate) σ_u ≈ 573
            │          ╱   │
        σ_y │●D  ●C  ╱     │
            │ ╲  ╱         │
            │  ╲╱          │
            │  ●B          │ ← Elastic Limit
            │  │           │
            │  │ Linear    │
            │  │ Region    │
            │  │ (Hooke's  │
            │  │  Law)     │
            │  ●A          │
            │  │ (Proportional Limit)
            └──┴───────────┴──────────────► ε
              O   ε_p   ε_y         ε_f
```

**Salient Points — Description (with typical mild steel values):**

| Point | Name | Stress (MPa) | Significance |
|-------|------|--------------|--------------|
| **O** | Origin | 0 | Zero load, zero strain |
| **A** | Proportional Limit | ~280 | End of region where σ ∝ ε (Hooke's law holds) |
| **B** | Elastic Limit | ~300 | Maximum stress up to which body fully recovers shape |
| **C** | Upper Yield Point | ~350 | Sudden drop from elastic to plastic region |
| **D** | Lower Yield Point | ~280 | Stable yielding stress |
| **E** | Ultimate Stress Point | ~400–573 | Maximum load the material can carry |
| **F** | Fracture Point | ~330 | Stress at which material breaks |

**Engineering Properties from the Curve:**
- **Young's Modulus (E)**: Slope of OA = (σ_A / ε_A)
- **Yield Strength ($\sigma_y$)**: Stress at point D
- **Ultimate Tensile Strength ($\sigma_u$)**: Stress at point E
- **Fracture Strength ($\sigma_f$)**: Stress at point F
- **Resilience**: Area under OA (proportional region)
- **Toughness**: Total area under O → F curve

**(7 Marks — Sketch 3 + Description 4)**

---

**(b) Ductile vs. Brittle, and Factors Affecting Properties (7 Marks):**

**Ductile Materials:**
- Undergo **large plastic deformation** before fracture (e.g., mild steel, copper).
- Have a **distinct yield point**.
- Fracture surface: **cup-and-cone** (ductile fracture).
- **High** energy absorption (high toughness).
- Provide **visible warning** before failure.

**Brittle Materials:**
- Fracture with **little or no plastic deformation** (e.g., cast iron, glass, concrete).
- **No** clear yield point — failure occurs directly from elastic region.
- Fracture surface: **flat, granular** (cleavage).
- **Low** energy absorption.
- Failure is **sudden and catastrophic**.

**Comparison Table:**

| Property | Ductile | Brittle |
|----------|---------|---------|
| % Elongation | > 5% | < 5% |
| Yield Point | Present | Absent |
| Toughness | High | Low |
| Failure Warning | Yes | No |

**Factors Affecting Mechanical Properties:**

**(i) Temperature:**
- Higher temperature → **decrease** in strength, **increase** in ductility.
- Lower temperature → **increase** in strength, **decrease** in ductility, may cause **brittle fracture**.
- **DBTT (Ductile-to-Brittle Transition Temperature):** The temperature below which a normally ductile material (like BCC steel) fractures in a brittle manner. Critical for Arctic pipelines and ship hulls in cold seas.

**(ii) Strain Rate:**
- **High strain rate** (impact loading): strength increases (apparent), but ductility decreases. Even ductile materials can fail in a brittle manner under impact.
- **Low strain rate**: more time for plastic flow → higher ductility, lower apparent strength.
- This is why impact tests are essential — they simulate high strain-rate behaviour.

**(iii) Notches and Stress Concentrations:**
- Sharp corners, holes, and cracks act as **stress raisers**.
- A notched specimen has a much lower impact strength than an un-notched one.
- The **Stress Concentration Factor** $K_t$ relates the peak stress at the notch to the nominal stress:
$$
K_t = \frac{\sigma_{max}}{\sigma_{nominal}}
$$
- Notches convert a uniaxial stress state into a **triaxial stress state**, which restricts plastic flow and favours brittle fracture.
- Notch sensitivity is more pronounced in BCC metals and at low temperatures.

**Summary:** Engineers must consider **temperature, loading rate, and geometry (notches)** when selecting materials and designing components to prevent unexpected brittle failures. **(7 Marks)** $\blacksquare$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit mistakes**: Always quote stress in **MPa** for engineering use (avoid Pa unless explicitly stated). Examiners deduct 0.5–1 mark for wrong units.
> 2. **Sign conventions**: Tensile stress is **positive**, compressive is **negative**. Confusing these loses marks.
> 3. **Confusing true vs engineering stress/strain**: In Module 1, unless asked explicitly, use **engineering values**.
> 4. **Plot must be labeled**: Stress–strain diagram questions require **all salient points labeled** — Proportional Limit, Elastic Limit, Yield, Ultimate, Fracture. Skipping labels costs 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Yield vs Ultimate confusion**: Yield = onset of plastic deformation. Ultimate = maximum load carrying capacity. They are **not the same**.
> 6. **Forgetting to convert units**: When $P$ is in kN and $A$ in mm², the result is in N/mm² = **MPa directly** — but be careful with length conversions.
> 7. **Forgetting DBTT discussion** in toughness/impact questions costs marks — KTU specifically tests this concept.
> 8. **In derivation questions**: write each step with **justification** — partial marks are awarded for logical steps even if final answer is wrong.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 🔑 Must-Know Concepts
- **Stress = Internal Force / Area** ($\sigma = P/A$ for normal, $\tau = V/A$ for shear)
- **Strain is dimensionless** ($\varepsilon = \Delta L/L_0$)
- **Hooke's Law:** $\sigma = E \varepsilon$ (only in elastic region)
- **Poisson's Ratio:** $\nu = -\varepsilon_{lateral}/\varepsilon_{axial}$, typically **0 to 0.5**
- **Shear Modulus:** $G = E/[2(1+\nu)]$
- **Bulk Modulus:** $K = E/[3(1-2\nu)]$

### 🎯 Key Diagram Points
- **Proportional Limit (A)** → end of linear region
- **Elastic Limit (B)** → maximum recoverable stress
- **Yield Point (C/D)** → onset of plastic deformation
- **Ultimate Point (E)** → maximum stress the material can bear
- **Fracture Point (F)** → where the material breaks

### ⚙️ Ductile vs. Brittle — Always Remember
- **Ductile** → large plastic strain, visible warning, high toughness
- **Brittle** → small/no plastic strain, sudden failure, low toughness
- Examples: Steel = ductile, Cast iron = brittle

### 🌡️ Factors Affecting Properties
- **Temperature ↑** → strength ↓, ductility ↑
- **Strain rate ↑** → strength ↑, ductility ↓
- **Notches** → create triaxial stresses → promote brittle failure
- **Cold working** → strength ↑, ductility ↓
- **DBTT** — critical for cold-environment service

### 💎 Hardness Tests — Quick Recall
| Test | Indenter | Best For |
|------|----------|----------|
| Brinell | Steel/Tungsten ball | Rough surfaces |
| Vickers | Diamond pyramid | All materials |
| Rockwell | Cone or ball | Quick shop-floor |
| Shore | Rebound hammer | Large parts |
| Mohs | Mineral scratch | Minerals |

### 💪 Impact Tests — Quick Recall
| Test | Specimen | Notch | Standard |
|------|----------|-------|----------|
| Izod | Cantilever | Top, strike side | BS (UK) |
| Charpy | Simply supported | Middle, opposite to strike | ASTM (international) |

### 📐 Key Formulas to Memorize
1. $\sigma = P/A$
2. $\varepsilon = \Delta L/L_0$
3. $\Delta L = PL_0/(AE)$
4. $G = E/[2(1+\nu)]$
5. $K = E/[3(1-2\nu)]$
6. $\sigma_u \approx 3.45 \times \text{BHN}$ (for steels)
7. $u_r = \sigma_y^2/(2E)$

### 🚨 Common Pitfalls to Avoid
- Don't confuse **yield strength** with **ultimate tensile strength**.
- Don't confuse **modulus of resilience** (elastic energy) with **modulus of toughness** (total energy).
- Always use **MPa** or **N/mm²** in engineering answers unless told otherwise.
- In impact tests, **lower temperature = lower impact strength** (for BCC steels).
- **Higher hardness ≈ higher tensile strength** (for steels, $\sigma_u \approx 3.45 \times HB$).

### 🧠 KTU Examiner's Quick Checklist
✔ Define clearly with units ✔
✔ Show every step in derivations ✔
✔ Label every point in diagrams ✔
✔ Discuss DBTT when talking about temperature ✔
✔ Use correct sign conventions (tension +, compression −) ✔
✔ Distinguish ductile vs brittle with examples ✔

> - **Topic:** Cartesian and Cylindrical Coordinate Systems, Stress at a Point, Complementary Shear Stress, Concept of Strain, Shear Strain, Generalized Hook's Law, Principal Strain in Terms of Stress, Plane Stress, Hydrostatic Stress and Plane Strain, Stress-Strain Relations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 1 — FUNDAMENTALS OF STRESS, STRAIN AND COORDINATE SYSTEMS

## 1.1 Cartesian Coordinate System

The **Cartesian Coordinate System** is the standard three-dimensional orthogonal reference frame used in solid mechanics. A point P in space is uniquely located using three mutually perpendicular axes ($x$, $y$, $z$).

### Formal Definition
> [!NOTE]
> **Cartesian Coordinate System:** A right-handed, three-dimensional, orthogonal coordinate system where the position of any point is defined by the perpendicular distances $x$, $y$, $z$ from three mutually perpendicular planes (the coordinate planes). Unit vectors $\hat{i}, \hat{j}, \hat{k}$ satisfy $\hat{i} \times \hat{j} = \hat{k}$.

The position vector is:
$$\vec{r} = x\hat{i} + y\hat{j} + z\hat{k}$$

### 🍎 Intuitive Analogy
Imagine the corner of your room where the floor meets two walls. From that corner, you can describe the location of a light bulb hanging in the room by saying it is $x$ meters from one wall, $y$ meters from the other wall, and $z$ meters from the floor. That corner is your **origin** — exactly what a Cartesian system uses.

### Why Orthogonal?
Because each axis is at **90°** to the others, calculations of distances, areas, and volumes become simple Pythagorean operations — no curved geometry required.

---

## 1.2 Cylindrical Coordinate System

For bodies with axial symmetry (shafts, pressure vessels, pipes), the **Cylindrical Coordinate System** simplifies analysis.

### Formal Definition
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cylindrical Coordinate System:** A three-dimensional coordinate system where a point is located by $(r, \theta, z)$, where $r$ is the radial distance from the $z$-axis, $\theta$ is the angle from the $x$-axis in the $x$–$y$ plane, and $z$ is the axial coordinate (same as Cartesian).

**Conversion from Cartesian → Cylindrical:**

$$r = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2}, \quad \theta = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right), \quad z = z$$

**Conversion from Cylindrical → Cartesian:**

$$x = r\cos\theta, \quad y = r\sin\theta, \quad z = z$$

### 🍎 Intuitive Analogy
Picture a **round birthday cake** on a table. To locate a cherry on top, instead of saying "3 cm right and 4 cm forward" (Cartesian), you could say "5 cm from the center along a line tilted 53° from the front" (Cylindrical). For circular things, cylindrical is just *easier*.

---

## 1.3 Stress at a Point

Stress is the **internal force per unit area** that a body experiences under external loading. At any point, stress is described by a **second-order tensor**.

### Normal Stress ($\sigma$)
Component of stress **perpendicular** to the surface.

$$\sigma = \lim_{\Delta A \to 0} \frac{\Delta F_n}{\Delta A} \quad \text{(units: N/m}^2\text{ or Pa)}$$

### Shear Stress ($\tau$)
Component of stress **tangential** to the surface.

$$\tau = \lim_{\Delta A \to 0} \frac{\Delta F_t}{\Delta A}$$

### Stress Tensor at a Point

$$\sigma_{ij} = \begin{bmatrix} \sigma_x & \tau_{xy} & \tau_{xz} \\ \tau_{yx} & \sigma_y & \tau_{yz} \\ \tau_{zx} & \tau_{zy} & \sigma_z \end{bmatrix}$$

> [!NOTE]
> At a point, there are **9 stress components** (3 normal + 6 shear), but equilibrium reduces the independent ones to **6** because $\tau_{ij} = \tau_{ji}$ (complementary shear).

### 🍎 Intuitive Analogy
When you **press your palm** against a wall, the force you apply is along the normal — that creates *normal stress*. When you **slide your palm** along the wall, the force is parallel to the surface — that creates *shear stress*. A real material experiences a mixture of both, captured by the stress tensor.

---

## 1.4 Complementary Shear Stresses

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Complementary Shear Stress Theorem:** Shear stresses acting on perpendicular planes of an infinitesimal element and pointing toward (or away from) the line of intersection are **equal in magnitude** and form a couple in opposite sense.

$$\tau_{xy} = \tau_{yx}, \quad \tau_{yz} = \tau_{zy}, \quad \tau_{xz} = \tau_{zx}$$

This is what makes the **stress tensor symmetric**, reducing 9 components to 6.

---

## 1.5 Concept of Strain

Strain is the **geometric measure of deformation** — the change in shape or size of a body relative to its original configuration.

### Normal Strain ($\varepsilon$)
Change in length per unit original length.

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0}$$

### Units
Strain is **dimensionless** (m/m or mm/mm), often reported as microstrain ($\mu\varepsilon$).

### 🍎 Intuitive Analogy
Stretch a rubber band slightly. If it was 10 cm and now is 10.2 cm, the normal strain is 0.2/10 = **0.02** (or 2%). The rubber band is *strained* but has not *failed* — the distinction between strain and stress.

---

## 1.6 Shear Strain ($\gamma$)

> [!NOTE]
> **Shear Strain:** The change in angle (in radians) between two lines that were originally perpendicular.

$$\gamma_{xy} = \frac{\pi}{2} - \theta'$$

where $\theta'$ is the new angle after deformation. For small deformations, $\gamma_{xy} \approx \tan\gamma_{xy}$ (radians).

### Engineering vs. Tensorial Shear Strain
- **Engineering shear strain:** $\gamma_{xy}$ (the full angle change)
- **Tensorial shear strain:** $\varepsilon_{xy} = \gamma_{xy}/2$ (half of the angle change)

---

## 1.7 Generalized Hooke's Law

The 3D extension of Hooke's law for a linear elastic, homogeneous, isotropic material:

$$\begin{bmatrix} \sigma_x \\ \sigma_y \\ \sigma_z \\ \tau_{xy} \\ \tau_{yz} \\ \tau_{zx} \end{bmatrix} = \frac{E}{(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)} \begin{bmatrix} 1-\nu & \nu & \nu & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ \nu & 1-\nu & \nu & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ \nu & \nu & 1-\nu & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & \frac{1-2\nu}{2} & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & \frac{1-2\nu}{2} & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & \frac{1-2\nu}{2} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \varepsilon_x \\ \varepsilon_y \\ \varepsilon_z \\ \gamma_{xy} \\ \gamma_{yz} \\ \gamma_{zx} \end{bmatrix}$$

where $E$ is Young's modulus and $\nu$ is Poisson's ratio.

---

## 1.8 Principal Strain in Terms of Stress

Inverting Hooke's law gives strains in terms of stresses:

$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)\right]$$
$$\varepsilon_y = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_y - \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_z)\right]$$
$$\varepsilon_z = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_z - \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_y)\right]$$

**Principal strains** are the normal strains in the directions where shear strain vanishes. They are found by solving:

$$\left(\varepsilon - \varepsilon_{\text{avg}}\right)^2 = \varepsilon_{\text{avg}}^2 - \varepsilon_x\varepsilon_y - \varepsilon_y\varepsilon_z - \varepsilon_z\varepsilon_x + \text{(shear terms)}$$

For 2D (plane stress/plane strain):
$$\varepsilon_{1,2} = \frac{\varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\varepsilon_x - \varepsilon_y}{2}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{\gamma_{xy}}{2}\right)^2}$$

---

## 1.9 Plane Stress

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plane Stress:** A state of stress where $\sigma_z = \tau_{xz} = \tau_{yz} = 0$. Typical in thin plates loaded in their plane.

Reduced stress tensor:
$$\sigma_{ij} = \begin{bmatrix} \sigma_x & \tau_{xy} & 0 \\ \tau_{yx} & \sigma_y & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

In plane stress, the out-of-plane strain is **not zero**:
$$\varepsilon_z = -\frac{\nu}{E}(\sigma_x + \sigma_y)$$

### 🍎 Intuitive Analogy
A **thin sheet of paper** lying on a table. Push on it from the edges. The top and bottom faces have no load, so $\sigma_z = 0$. The sheet stress is purely two-dimensional.

---

## 1.10 Hydrostatic Stress

> [!NOTE]
> **Hydrostatic (Volumetric) Stress:** A state of equal normal stress on all faces, with no shear components. $\sigma_x = \sigma_y = \sigma_z = -p$, where $p$ is pressure.

$$\sigma_{ij} = \begin{bmatrix} -p & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & -p & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & -p \end{bmatrix}$$

**Volumetric strain (dilatation):**
$$e = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z = \frac{1-2\nu}{E} \cdot 3p = \frac{3(1-2\nu)}{E}p$$

The hydrostatic stress only causes **volume change**, not shape change.

### 🍎 Intuitive Analogy
A **submarine** deep underwater. Water pushes equally on every face — pure hydrostatic compression. The hull compresses in volume but does not distort in shape.

---

## 1.11 Plane Strain

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plane Strain:** A state where $\varepsilon_z = \gamma_{xz} = \gamma_{yz} = 0$. Typical in long structures (dams, tunnels, thick cylinders) where the geometry and loading are uniform along one axis.

In plane strain, $\sigma_z$ is **not zero** but is constrained by the boundary condition:
$$\sigma_z = \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_y)$$

### Difference from Plane Stress

| Feature | Plane Stress | Plane Strain |
|---|---|---|
| $\sigma_z$ | 0 | $\nu(\sigma_x+\sigma_y) \neq 0$ |
| $\varepsilon_z$ | $-\nu(\sigma_x+\sigma_y)/E$ | 0 |
| Typical case | Thin plates, diaphragms | Long dams, tunnels |

---

## 1.12 Stress-Strain Relations Summary

For isotropic, linear-elastic materials, the complete set of stress-strain relations in 3D:

$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}[\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)]$$
$$\varepsilon_y = \frac{1}{E}[\sigma_y - \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_z)]$$
$$\varepsilon_z = \frac{1}{E}[\sigma_z - \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_y)]$$
$$\gamma_{xy} = \frac{\tau_{xy}}{G}, \quad \gamma_{yz} = \frac{\tau_{yz}}{G}, \quad \gamma_{zx} = \frac{\tau_{zx}}{G}$$

where the **Shear Modulus** $G$ is related to $E$ and $\nu$ by:
$$G = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)}$$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Stress Tensor Decomposition

Any stress state can be decomposed into two physically meaningful parts:

$$\sigma_{ij} = \underbrace{\frac{\sigma_{kk}}{3}\delta_{ij}}_{\text{Spherical (Hydrostatic)}} + \underbrace{s_{ij}}_{\text{Deviatoric}}$$

where:
- **Hydrostatic part** $(\sigma_{kk}/3)\delta_{ij}$: causes **volume change** only
- **Deviatoric part** $s_{ij}$: causes **shape distortion** only

### Hydrostatic Stress
$$\sigma_m = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y + \sigma_z}{3}$$
$$\sigma_{ij}^{\text{hydro}} = \sigma_m \delta_{ij} = \begin{bmatrix} \sigma_m & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & \sigma_m & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & \sigma_m \end{bmatrix}$$

### Deviatoric Stress
$$s_{ij} = \sigma_{ij} - \sigma_m \delta_{ij}$$
$$s_x = \sigma_x - \sigma_m, \quad s_y = \sigma_y - \sigma_m, \quad s_z = \sigma_z - \sigma_m$$

Note: $s_x + s_y + s_z = 0$ (the deviatoric tensor is **traceless**).

### 🍎 Why This Matters in Engineering
- **Material yielding** (ductile metals) is governed by the deviatoric part (von Mises, Tresca criteria).
- **Brittle fracture** (concrete, rock) is governed by the maximum principal stress.
- **Volumetric failure** (porous materials) is governed by the hydrostatic part.

---

## 2.2 Coordinate Transformations (Stress)

The transformation of stress components from one set of axes to another is a 2nd-order tensor transformation:

$$\sigma_{x'} = \sigma_x \cos^2\theta + \sigma_y \sin^2\theta + 2\tau_{xy}\sin\theta\cos\theta$$
$$\sigma_{y'} = \sigma_x \sin^2\theta + \sigma_y \cos^2\theta - 2\tau_{xy}\sin\theta\cos\theta$$
$$\tau_{x'y'} = (\sigma_y - \sigma_x)\sin\theta\cos\theta + \tau_{xy}(\cos^2\theta - \sin^2\theta)$$

Using double-angle identities:
$$\sigma_{x'} = \frac{\sigma_x+\sigma_y}{2} + \frac{\sigma_x-\sigma_y}{2}\cos 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\sin 2\theta$$
$$\sigma_{y'} = \frac{\sigma_x+\sigma_y}{2} - \frac{\sigma_x-\sigma_y}{2}\cos 2\theta - \tau_{xy}\sin 2\theta$$
$$\tau_{x'y'} = -\frac{\sigma_x-\sigma_y}{2}\sin 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\cos 2\theta$$

---

## 2.3 Principal Stresses and Maximum Shear Stress

**Principal stresses** are the normal stresses on planes where shear stress vanishes. They are eigenvalues of the stress tensor.

For 2D stress:
$$\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$$

**Principal angle:**
$$\tan 2\theta_p = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}$$

**Maximum in-plane shear stress:**
$$\tau_{\max} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2} = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_2}{2}$$

**Absolute maximum shear stress (3D):**
$$\tau_{\text{abs max}} = \frac{\sigma_{\max} - \sigma_{\min}}{2}$$

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Quantity | Formula | Units |
|----------|---------|-------|
| Normal stress | $\sigma_n = F_n/A$ | Pa (N/m²) |
| Shear stress | $\tau = F_t/A$ | Pa |
| Normal strain | $\varepsilon = \Delta L/L_0$ | dimensionless |
| Shear strain (engineering) | $\gamma = (\pi/2) - \theta'$ | rad |
| Hooke's law (uniaxial) | $\sigma = E\varepsilon$ | Pa |
| Poisson's effect | $\varepsilon_{\text{lat}} = -\nu\varepsilon_{\text{ax}}$ | — |
| Shear modulus relation | $G = E/[2(1+\nu)]$ | Pa |
| Bulk modulus relation | $K = E/[3(1-2\nu)]$ | Pa |
| Hydrostatic stress | $\sigma_m = (\sigma_x+\sigma_y+\sigma_z)/3$ | Pa |
| Volumetric strain | $e = 3\sigma_m(1-2\nu)/E$ | — |
| Principal stress (2D) | $\sigma_{1,2} = \sigma_{\text{avg}} \pm R$ | Pa |
| Mohr's circle radius | $R = \sqrt{[(\sigma_x-\sigma_y)/2]^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$ | Pa |
| Plane stress: $\varepsilon_z$ | $\varepsilon_z = -\nu(\sigma_x+\sigma_y)/E$ | — |
| Plane strain: $\sigma_z$ | $\sigma_z = \nu(\sigma_x+\sigma_y)$ | Pa |
| Generalized Hooke (3D) | $\varepsilon_i = [\sigma_i - \nu\sum \sigma]/E$ | — |
| Cartesian → Cylindrical | $r=\sqrt{x^2+y^2}$, $\theta=\tan^{-1}(y/x)$ | — |
| Cylindrical → Cartesian | $x=r\cos\theta$, $y=r\sin\theta$ | — |
| Complementary shear | $\tau_{ij}=\tau_{ji}$ | Pa |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always identify the **type of problem state** (plane stress vs plane strain) before writing Hooke's law — the form differs!

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

1. **Pressure Vessels** → Use **plane stress** (thin walls) or 3D thick-wall theory (Lamé's equations).
2. **Dam Foundations** → Use **plane strain** since the dam is very long compared to its cross-section.
3. **Turbine Shafts** → Use **cylindrical coordinates** due to rotational symmetry.
4. **Sheet Metal Forming** → Plane stress in rolling/stamping.
5. **Soil Mechanics** → Hydrostatic + deviatoric split for compaction and shear failure.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📐 STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Complementary Shear Stress Theorem

**Statement:** Shear stresses on perpendicular faces of a stress element, pointing toward or away from the common edge, are equal in magnitude.

### Proof (Using Moment Equilibrium)

Consider an infinitesimal 2D stress element of size $dx \times dy$ and thickness $t$ (unit).

**Forces on each face:**
- Face with normal $x$ (right): $\tau_{xy} \cdot dy \cdot 1$ acting in $+y$ direction
- Face with normal $x$ (left): $\tau_{xy} \cdot dy \cdot 1$ acting in $-y$ direction
- Face with normal $y$ (top): $\tau_{yx} \cdot dx \cdot 1$ acting in $+x$ direction
- Face with normal $y$ (bottom): $\tau_{yx} \cdot dx \cdot 1$ acting in $-x$ direction

**Taking moment about the bottom-left corner (z-axis):**

$$\sum M_z = 0$$

The two vertical face shears ($\tau_{xy}$) form a **couple** of magnitude:
$$M_1 = \tau_{xy} \cdot dy \cdot 1 \cdot dx$$

The two horizontal face shears ($\tau_{yx}$) form a **couple** of magnitude:
$$M_2 = \tau_{yx} \cdot dx \cdot 1 \cdot dy$$

**For rotational equilibrium** (since inertia $\to 0$ as $dx,dy \to 0$):
$$M_1 = M_2$$

$$\tau_{xy} \cdot dy \cdot dx = \tau_{yx} \cdot dx \cdot dy$$

$$\boxed{\tau_{xy} = \tau_{yx}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is why the stress tensor is **symmetric** — only 6 independent components exist in 3D.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Generalized Hooke's Law from Uniaxial Case

### Step 1: Uniaxial Hooke's Law
For a bar loaded only in $x$:
$$\sigma_x = E\varepsilon_x \quad \Rightarrow \quad \varepsilon_x = \sigma_x/E$$

### Step 2: Superposition with Poisson Effect
If $\sigma_x$, $\sigma_y$, $\sigma_z$ all act simultaneously, the strain in $x$ has contributions from each:

$$\varepsilon_x = \underbrace{\frac{\sigma_x}{E}}_{\text{from }\sigma_x} \underbrace{-\frac{\nu\sigma_y}{E}}_{\text{Poisson from }\sigma_y} \underbrace{-\frac{\nu\sigma_z}{E}}_{\text{Poisson from }\sigma_z}$$

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)\right]}$$

By symmetry:
$$\varepsilon_y = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_y - \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_z)\right]$$
$$\varepsilon_z = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_z - \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_y)\right]$$

### Step 3: Shear Stress–Shear Strain Relation
For pure shear, experimental observation gives:
$$\tau_{xy} = G\gamma_{xy}$$

Substituting $G = E/[2(1+\nu)]$:
$$\gamma_{xy} = \frac{2(1+\nu)}{E}\tau_{xy}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Principal Strain from 2D Stress State

Given plane stress: $\sigma_x$, $\sigma_y$, $\tau_{xy}$, with $\sigma_z = 0$.

### Step 1: Compute Strains via Hooke's Law

$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}(\sigma_x - \nu\sigma_y)$$
$$\varepsilon_y = \frac{1}{E}(\sigma_y - \nu\sigma_x)$$
$$\gamma_{xy} = \frac{2(1+\nu)}{E}\tau_{xy}$$

### Step 2: Principal Stress Formula

$$\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$$

### Step 3: Transform to Principal Strains (using same rotation)

$$\varepsilon_{1,2} = \frac{\varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\varepsilon_x - \varepsilon_y}{2}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{\gamma_{xy}}{2}\right)^2}$$

### Step 4: Expand in Terms of Stress (Plane Stress)

Substituting Step 1 expressions:

$$\varepsilon_{1,2} = \frac{(1-\nu)}{2E}(\sigma_x + \sigma_y) \pm \frac{1+\nu}{E}\sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$$

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_{1,2} = \frac{1-\nu}{2E}(\sigma_x + \sigma_y) \pm \frac{1+\nu}{E}\sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Hydrostatic Strain (Volumetric Strain)

### Step 1: Apply Hydrostatic Stress
$$\sigma_x = \sigma_y = \sigma_z = -p$$

### Step 2: Substitute into Hooke's Law
$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}[-p - \nu(-p - p)] = \frac{-p}{E}[1 - 2\nu]$$

Similarly:
$$\varepsilon_y = \varepsilon_z = \frac{-p(1-2\nu)}{E}$$

### Step 3: Volumetric Strain (Dilatation)
$$e = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z = 3 \cdot \frac{-p(1-2\nu)}{E}$$

$$\boxed{e = \frac{3(1-2\nu)}{E}p = \frac{p}{K}}$$

where $K = E/[3(1-2\nu)]$ is the **Bulk Modulus**.

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Plane Strain Conditions

In plane strain: $\varepsilon_z = 0$, $\gamma_{xz} = \gamma_{yz} = 0$.

### Step 1: Set $\varepsilon_z = 0$ in Hooke's Law
$$0 = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_z - \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_y)\right]$$

### Step 2: Solve for $\sigma_z$
$$\boxed{\sigma_z = \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_y)}$$

### Step 3: Apply to Get 2D Effective Stresses
Substituting back into $\varepsilon_x$:
$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_x - \nu\sigma_y - \nu^2(\sigma_x + \sigma_y)\right]$$
$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_x(1-\nu^2) - \sigma_y(\nu+\nu^2)\right]$$
$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{1+\nu}{E}\left[\sigma_x(1-\nu) - \nu\sigma_y\right]$$

The effective plane-strain modulus is:
$$E' = \frac{E}{1-\nu^2}, \quad \nu' = \frac{\nu}{1-\nu}$$

So the plane strain Hooke's law is:
$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E'}(\sigma_x - \nu'\sigma_y)$$
$$\varepsilon_y = \frac{1}{E'}(\sigma_y - \nu'\sigma_x)$$

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Stress-Strain Relations Across States

| State | $\sigma_z$ | $\varepsilon_z$ | Hooke Form |
|-------|-----------|-----------------|------------|
| 3D General | $\sigma_z$ | $\frac{1}{E}[\sigma_z-\nu(\sigma_x+\sigma_y)]$ | Full 3D |
| Plane Stress | $0$ | $-\frac{\nu}{E}(\sigma_x+\sigma_y)$ | 2D with $\sigma_z=0$ |
| Plane Strain | $\nu(\sigma_x+\sigma_y)$ | $0$ | 2D with $\varepsilon_z=0$ |
| Hydrostatic | $-p$ | $-\frac{p(1-2\nu)}{E}$ | Equal triaxial |
| Uniaxial | $0$ | $-\frac{\nu}{E}\sigma_x$ | $\sigma = E\varepsilon$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** In plane strain, do not set $\sigma_z = 0$! It is **non-zero** but constrained by Poisson's ratio.

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Stress-Strain Relationship Framework

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[External Loading] --> B{Choose Coordinate System}
    B -->|Rectangular Geometry| C[Cartesian x,y,z]
    B -->|Axial Symmetry| D[Cylindrical r,theta,z]
    C --> E[Define Stress Tensor sigma_ij]
    D --> E
    E --> F{Identify Stress State}
    F -->|Thin Plate| G[Plane Stress]
    F -->|Long Body| H[Plane Strain]
    F -->|Pressure Vessel| I[Hydrostatic 3D]
    G --> J[Apply Hookes Law]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Compute Strains]
    K --> L[Principal Strains]

```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Coordinate System Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Point P in Space] --> B{Geometry Type}
    B -->|Rectangular| C[Cartesian: x,y,z]
    B -->|Cylindrical| D[Cylindrical: r,theta,z]
    C --> E[Position Vector: xi + yj + zk]
    D --> F[Position: r cos theta i + r sin theta j + z k]
    E --> G[Engineering Application]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Solid Mechanics Analysis]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Stress Tensor Decomposition

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Total Stress Tensor sigma_ij] --> B[Hydrostatic Component]
    A --> C[Deviatoric Component]
    B --> B1[Spherical: sigma_m * delta_ij]
    B --> B2[Causes Volume Change]
    B --> B3[Volumetric Strain e = 3 sigma_m 1-2nu / E]
    C --> C1[Deviator: s_ij = sigma_ij - sigma_m delta_ij]
    C --> C2[Causes Shape Distortion]
    C --> C3[Traceless: s_x + s_y + s_z = 0]
    B3 --> D[Material Behavior]
    C3 --> D
    D --> D1[Yielding uses Deviatoric]
    D --> D2[Fracture uses Hydrostatic]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Hooke's Law Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Given: sigma_x, sigma_y, sigma_z, tau_xy, tau_yz, tau_zx] --> B{State Identification}
    B -->|Plane Stress| C[Set sigma_z = 0]
    B -->|Plane Strain| D[Set eps_z = 0]
    B -->|3D General| E[Keep all terms]
    C --> F[Apply Generalized Hooke]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G[Compute eps_x, eps_y, eps_z, gamma_xy, gamma_yz, gamma_zx]
    G --> H[Compute Volumetric Strain e]
    H --> I[Compute Principal Strains eps_1, eps_2, eps_3]
    I --> J[Final Result]
```

## 4.5 Schematic: Stress Element at a Point

```
                +y
                 |
              ┌──┼──┐
              │ ↓│  │  ↑  τ_yx
         τ_xy→│  │  │←τ_xy
              │  │  │
              └──┼──┘
                 |
   ──── σ_y ↑   |    σ_y ↓ ────
       →       Origin       ←
   ──── σ_y ↓   |    σ_y ↑ ────
              ┌──┼──┐
              │  │  │
         τ_xy→│  │  │←τ_xy
              │ ↑│  │  ↓  τ_yx
              └──┼──┘
                 |
                +x direction (out of page: +z)

   Where:
   - σ_x, σ_y, σ_z : Normal stresses on x, y, z faces
   - τ_xy, τ_yz, τ_zx : Shear stresses (with τ_ij = τ_ji)
   - All 9 components fully describe the state of stress at a point
```

## 4.6 Schematic: Plane Stress vs Plane Strain

```
    PLANE STRESS (Thin Plate)              PLANE STRAIN (Long Body)
    ┌───────────────┐                       ┌───────────────┐
    │ → σ_x →       │                       │ → σ_x →       │
    │               │                       │               │
    │  σ_y          │  σ_z = 0              │  σ_y          │  σ_z = ν(σ_x+σ_y) ≠ 0
    │      ↑        │                       │      ↑        │
    │ → τ_xy →      │  ε_z ≠ 0              │ → τ_xy →      │  ε_z = 0
    └───────────────┘                       └───────────────┘
         ↓                                      ↓
    Free top/bottom surface              Constrained top/bottom (rigid)
    No out-of-plane loading              Long axis prevents ε_z
```
