**Goodman Criterion**:

$$\dfrac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_e} + \dfrac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_u} \leq 1$$

Substitute:

$$\dfrac{200}{300} + \dfrac{100}{600} = 0.6667 + 0.1667 = 0.8333$$

Since **0.8333 < 1**, the design is **SAFE**. ✅

**Factor of Safety (FoS)**:

$$\text{FoS} = \dfrac{1}{0.8333} = 1.20$$

$$\boxed{\text{Factor of Safety} = 1.20}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Marks Lost**:
> 1. **Brinell Formula Errors**: Students often write HB = P/(πdt) or similar incorrect forms. Use the *curved cap area* formula with the square root.
> 2. **Confusing Rockwell scales**: HRC uses diamond + 150 kgf; HRB uses ball + 100 kgf. Mixing them up costs full marks.
> 3. **Creep equation missing units**: Always state the units of $\dot{\varepsilon}$ (s⁻¹), *A* (depends on units of σ), and $Q_c$ (J/mol or kJ/mol).
> 4. **Basquin's Equation sign of *b***: *b* is *negative* (e.g., -0.1). Forgetting the negative sign leads to physically meaningless *increasing* σ_a with N.
> 5. **Mean Stress Criterion Mixing**: Students often interchange Goodman with Gerber or Soderberg formulas. Remember: *Soderberg* uses yield, *Goodman* uses ultimate, *Gerber* uses squared ultimate.
> 6. **Brinell Range Violation**: Forgetting to check $0.25D < d < 0.5D$ — KTU explicitly tests this.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> **🔑 High-Yield Mnemonic for Hardness Tests**:
> **"B-V-R" → "Ball, V-shaped diamond, Round steel cone (Brale) or ball"** — match indenter to test.

### Hardness Tests
- ✅ **Brinell** = **Ball + Load + Diameter** measurement; **HB = 2P / [πD(D − √(D² − d²))]**
- ✅ **Vickers** = **Diamond pyramid + Optical diagonal**; **HV ≈ 1.854 P / d²** (P in kgf, d in mm)
- ✅ **Rockwell** = **Depth-based + Dial reading**; **HR = E − h / 0.002** (E = 100 for diamond, 130 for ball)
- ✅ Brinell validity: **0.25 D < d < 0.5 D**
- ✅ Steel ball limit: **HB ≤ 450**; WC ball limit: **HB ≤ 600**
- ✅ Vickers face angle = **136°**; chosen to match Brinell up to HB 300
- ✅ Approximate strength relation: **σ_u ≈ 3.45 × HB** (steels)
- ✅ Rockwell scales: **HRA, HRB, HRC** are the most commonly tested
- ✅ HRC range: **20–70** for hardened steels; HRB range: **0–100** for soft metals

### Creep
- ✅ Creep dominates when **T > 0.4 T_m**
- ✅ Three stages: **Primary (decelerating), Secondary (steady), Tertiary (accelerating to rupture)**
- ✅ **Norton's Law**: $\dot{\varepsilon} = A \sigma^n \exp(-Q_c/RT)$
- ✅ **Larson-Miller**: $LMP = T(C + \log_{10} t_r)$ with C ≈ 20
- ✅ *n* = 3–8 for power-law creep; *n* = 1 for diffusional creep
- ✅ Design-determining region: **Stage II**

### Fatigue
- ✅ Fatigue = failure under *cyclic* load below yield strength
- ✅ ~80–90% of mechanical failures are fatigue-related
- ✅ **Basquin's Equation**: $\sigma_a = \sigma_f' (2N)^b$ (high-cycle)
- ✅ **Coffin-Manson**: $\Delta\varepsilon_p / 2 = \varepsilon_f' (2N)^c$ (low-cycle)
- ✅ Endurance limit $\sigma_e$ exists **only in ferrous metals and titanium alloys**
- ✅ Endurance ratio for steels: $\sigma_e / \sigma_u \approx 0.5$
- ✅ **Mean Stress Criteria** (most conservative first): **Soderberg < Goodman < Gerber**
- ✅ **Goodman**: $\sigma_a/\sigma_e + \sigma_m/\sigma_u = 1$ (linear)
- ✅ **Gerber**: $\sigma_a/\sigma_e + (\sigma_m/\sigma_u)^2 = 1$ (parabolic)
- ✅ **Soderberg**: $\sigma_a/\sigma_e + \sigma_m/\sigma_y = 1$ (uses yield)
- ✅ **Miner's Rule** for cumulative damage: $\sum (n_i/N_i) = 1$
- ✅ Notch sensitivity factor $K_f = 1 + q(K_t - 1)$, where $q$ = notch sensitivity (0 to 1)
- ✅ Fatigue stress concentration reduces allowable $\sigma_a$ — always account for it!

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Tip**: For 14-mark questions, *always* state assumptions, *always* check the validity of test conditions (Brinell range, scale appropriateness), *always* present the formula before substituting, and *always* cross-check units. Marks are awarded for *method*, not just final answer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Members Subjected to Uniaxial Stress - Bars Subjected to Tension and Compression
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET302 - SOLID MECHANICS AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Members Subjected to Uniaxial Stress - Bars Subjected to Tension and Compression

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Uniaxial Stress:** A state of stress in which only one pair of opposite, collinear, equal, and parallel forces act on a member, producing a single normal stress component acting along a single principal axis. For a prismatic straight bar, the internal stress developed is purely **normal stress** ($\sigma$), and there are no shear stresses on the cross-section.

A **bar** (also called a prismatic member or straight member) is a structural element whose length is significantly greater than its lateral dimensions. When subjected to axial loads applied along its centroidal longitudinal axis, it experiences either:

- **Tensile Stress (Positive $\sigma$):** When the axial load $P$ tends to **stretch** the bar by pulling it apart.
- **Compressive Stress (Negative $\sigma$):** When the axial load $P$ tends to **shorten** the bar by pushing it together.

The fundamental governing relationship is the **Normal Stress Equation**:

$$\sigma = \frac{P}{A}$$

where:
- $P$ = Internal axial force (tensile is taken as **positive** by convention) in **N** or **kN**
- $A$ = Cross-sectional area resisting the load in **mm²** or **m²**
- $\sigma$ = Normal stress in **N/mm² (MPa)** or **Pa (N/m²)**

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Syllabus Note:** A bar is considered *axially loaded* only when the line of action of the applied force coincides with the centroidal axis of the cross-section. Eccentric loading produces **combined stress** (axial + bending) and is covered in a later module.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine you are **stretching a rubber band** between your two hands:
- Your fingers pull outward → rubber band elongates → it is in **tension**.
- The internal force resisting the stretch is the same throughout, and tiny cross-sections of the band experience **tensile stress** trying to pull neighboring molecules apart.

Now think of a **telescopic antenna rod being pushed inward**:
- Your hands push inward → rod shortens → it is in **compression**.
- The internal force throughout the rod is compressive, and the cross-section experiences **compressive stress** trying to push neighboring molecules together.

> [!TIP]
> **Universal Real-World Examples of Uniaxially Loaded Bars:**
> - **Tension Members:** Cables of suspension bridges, tie-rods in trusses, suspenders in cars, chains in elevators, bolts in pre-tensioned connections.
> - **Compression Members:** Columns in buildings, struts in roof trusses, piston rods in hydraulic cylinders, drill strings in oil rigs (axial compressive load component).

The single most important intuition: *in a uniaxial state, the bar behaves like a spring — load in, deformation out — governed by the bar's axial stiffness.*

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> - **Modulus of Elasticity (Young's Modulus, $E$):** Steel = **$2.0 \times 10^5$ N/mm² (200 GPa)**, Copper = **$1.0 \times 10^5$ N/mm² (100 GPa)**, Aluminum = **$0.7 \times 10^5$ N/mm² (70 GPa)**, Concrete ≈ **$0.25 \times 10^5$ N/mm²**.
> - **Strain ($\varepsilon$):** Dimensionless (mm/mm). Typical elastic limit strain for mild steel ≈ **0.001 to 0.002**.
> - **Standard Units in KTU Problems:** Forces in **kN** or **N**, areas in **mm²**, stresses in **N/mm² (= MPa)**, lengths in **mm**.
> - **Axial Stiffness of a bar:** $k = \dfrac{AE}{L}$ in **N/mm** — this is the constant that connects force to deformation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown — The Theory of Axially Loaded Bars

#### Step A: The Concept of Internal Axial Force
When an external force is applied to a bar, **internal resisting forces** develop at every cross-section to maintain equilibrium. The magnitude of this internal force is found by the **Method of Sections**:

> **Method of Sections:** Imagine cutting the bar at any cross-section. The portion on either side must be in equilibrium under the action of external loads and the internal force $P$ at the cut.

#### Step B: Assumptions in Uniaxial Bar Analysis
- The bar material is **homogeneous** (same properties at every point) and **isotropic** (same properties in all directions).
- The bar is **prismatic** (constant cross-section between any two points), unless the problem states otherwise.
- Plane cross-sections remain **plane** and perpendicular to the axis after deformation (Euler-Bernoulli hypothesis).
- The stress is **uniformly distributed** over the cross-section: $\sigma = P/A$.

#### Step C: Strain — The Geometric Measure of Deformation
Strain is the **ratio of change in length to the original length**:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\delta L}{L}$$

where $\delta L$ is the change in length (positive for elongation, negative for contraction).

#### Step D: Hooke's Law — The Physical Law of Linear Elasticity
Within the **elastic limit**, stress is directly proportional to strain:

$$\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$$

The constant $E$ is the **Modulus of Elasticity (Young's Modulus)** — a measure of the material's stiffness.

#### Step E: The Master Elongation Equation
Combining the three fundamental relationships:

$$\boxed{\delta L = \frac{PL}{AE}}$$

This is the single most important formula for KTU Module 2 problems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Meaning:** The elongation of a bar is **directly proportional** to the load $P$ and length $L$, and **inversely proportional** to the area $A$ and the material's modulus $E$.

#### Step F: The Concept of Composite Bars
A composite bar consists of **two or more different materials** joined together and subjected to a common axial load. The principles are:

- **Composite Bar in Series (axial loading through the cross-section):** The **same internal force $P$** flows through each segment, but each segment has its own stress ($\sigma_i = P/A_i$), strain ($\varepsilon_i = \sigma_i/E_i$), and elongation ($\delta L_i = P L_i / A_i E_i$).
- **Total Elongation (Superposition Principle):**

$$\delta L_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{P_i L_i}{A_i E_i} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{P_i L_i}{A_i E_i}$$

#### Step G: Statically Determinate vs. Indeterminate Bars
- **Statically Determinate:** The internal force at any section can be found using **equilibrium equations alone**.
- **Statically Indeterminate:** Equilibrium gives one equation but there are multiple unknowns → a **compatibility equation** (geometric condition on total deformation) is also required.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Pattern:** Most Module 2 problems are **statically determinate**. The 14-mark questions often introduce a step (different area) or composite (different material) or thermal loading to test whether the student can apply the elongation formula **segment-wise** and **sum** the deformations.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Formula Table for Uniaxial Bar Analysis**

| # | Formula | Description | Unit (KTU Convention) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma = P/A$ | Normal stress | N/mm² (MPa) |
| 2 | $\varepsilon = \delta L / L$ | Longitudinal strain | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$ | Hooke's Law | N/mm² |
| 4 | $\delta L = P L / (A E)$ | Elongation of uniform bar | mm |
| 5 | $\delta L = \sum (P_i L_i) / (A_i E_i)$ | Elongation of stepped/composite bar | mm |
| 6 | $k = A E / L$ | Axial stiffness of bar | N/mm |
| 7 | $P_{cr} = \sigma_{cr} A$ | Critical/maximum force capacity | N |
| 8 | $\delta L_T = \alpha L \Delta T$ | Free thermal elongation | mm |
| 9 | $\sigma_T = E \alpha \Delta T$ | Thermal stress in fully constrained bar | N/mm² |
| 10 | $\varepsilon = P / (A E)$ | Strain in terms of load | dimensionless |
| 11 | $\sigma = E \alpha \Delta T - P / A$ | Net stress in constrained bar with load and temperature | N/mm² |
| 12 | $W = (1/2) P \delta L$ | Strain energy stored | N·mm (J) |
| 13 | $U = (1/2) \sigma \varepsilon$ (vol) | Strain energy density | N·mm/mm³ |
| 14 | $\nu = - \varepsilon_{lateral} / \varepsilon_{axial}$ | Poisson's ratio (related concept) | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Constants often given in KTU problems:**
> - $E_{steel} = 2 \times 10^5$ N/mm²
> - $E_{copper} = 1 \times 10^5$ N/mm²
> - $E_{aluminum} = 0.7 \times 10^5$ N/mm²
> - $\alpha_{steel} = 12 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C
> - $\alpha_{copper} = 17 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C
> - $\alpha_{aluminum} = 23 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C

### 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering

> [!TIP]
> **Why This Topic Matters in Practice:**
> 1. **Civil Engineering:** Sizing tie-bars, hangers, pre-stressing tendons, and rock anchors.
> 2. **Mechanical Engineering:** Designing piston rods, connecting rods, bolt preload, and pressure vessel tie-rods.
> 3. **Aerospace:** Sizing fuselage longerons, wing spars, and control cables.
> 4. **Offshore Engineering:** Mooring lines, drill string components, riser tensioners — all are uniaxially loaded bars where elongation directly affects operational safety.
> 5. **Failure Prevention:** If the axial stress exceeds the **ultimate tensile stress (UTS)**, the bar fractures; if it exceeds the **yield stress**, the bar yields plastically. Hence, $\sigma_{actual} \le \sigma_{allowable}$ governs every design.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Master Elongation Equation: $\delta L = \dfrac{PL}{AE}$

**Statement to Prove:** The total elongation of a uniform prismatic bar under an axial tensile load $P$ is given by $\delta L = PL/(AE)$.

**Step 1: Definition of Longitudinal Strain**
The longitudinal strain $\varepsilon$ is defined as the ratio of change in length to the original length.

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\delta L}{L}$$

*Conversion Logic:* Strain is a measure of *relative* deformation — a bar of length 1000 mm elongating by 1 mm has the same strain as a 100 mm bar elongating by 0.1 mm. Strain is dimensionless.

**Step 2: Hooke's Law (Physical Law)**
For a material in the linear elastic range, stress is proportional to strain, with $E$ as the proportionality constant.

$$\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$$

*Conversion Logic:* $E$ (Young's Modulus) is the slope of the stress-strain curve in the linear region. It has units of stress (N/mm²) because strain is dimensionless.

**Step 3: Definition of Normal Stress**
The internal axial force $P$ distributed uniformly over the cross-section area $A$ produces the normal stress.

$$\sigma = \frac{P}{A}$$

*Conversion Logic:* This is the definition of stress — force per unit area. The "uniform distribution" assumption is what defines a uniaxial stress state.

**Step 4: Equating the Two Stress Expressions**
From Step 2: $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$. From Step 3: $\sigma = P/A$. Since both equal $\sigma$:

$$\frac{P}{A} = E \cdot \varepsilon$$

*Conversion Logic:* This equation couples the *external* loading ($P$, $A$) with the *internal material response* ($E$, $\varepsilon$).

**Step 5: Substituting the Definition of Strain**
From Step 1, $\varepsilon = \delta L / L$. Substituting this into Step 4's equation:

$$\frac{P}{A} = E \cdot \frac{\delta L}{L}$$

*Conversion Logic:* This single equation now contains all four primary variables.

**Step 6: Solving for $\delta L$**
Multiply both sides by $L$ and divide by $E$:

$$\delta L \cdot \frac{1}{L} = \frac{P}{A \cdot E}$$

Multiplying both sides by $L$:

$$\boxed{\delta L = \frac{P \cdot L}{A \cdot E}}$$

*Conversion Logic:* This is the **master elongation equation**. The dimensional check: $[N \cdot mm / (mm^2 \cdot N/mm^2)] = [mm]$ ✓.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Thermal Stress in a Fully Constrained Bar

**Statement to Prove:** When a bar is fixed at both ends and subjected to a temperature change $\Delta T$, the induced thermal stress is $\sigma_T = E \alpha \Delta T$.

**Step 1: Free Thermal Elongation (No Constraints)**
If the bar were free to expand, the change in length due to temperature rise $\Delta T$ would be:

$$\delta L_T = \alpha L \Delta T$$

*Conversion Logic:* $\alpha$ is the coefficient of linear thermal expansion (per °C). The bar wants to elongate by $\delta L_T$ but cannot because the supports are rigid.

**Step 2: Equivalent Mechanical Compression**
Since the bar is prevented from elongating, the supports push back with a compressive force $P_T$ that compresses the bar by exactly $\delta L_T$:

$$\delta L_{mech} = \frac{P_T L}{A E} = \delta L_T$$

*Conversion Logic:* The wall reaction force is what produces the *mechanical shortening* that cancels the *thermal expansion*.

**Step 3: Equating the Two Deformations**

$$\frac{P_T L}{A E} = \alpha L \Delta T$$

*Conversion Logic:* The total change in length of the constrained bar is zero. So the sum of thermal expansion (positive) and mechanical compression (negative) equals zero. This is the **compatibility condition**.

**Step 4: Solving for $P_T$**
The $L$ cancels from both sides:

$$\frac{P_T}{A E} = \alpha \Delta T$$

Multiplying both sides by $A$:

$$P_T = A E \alpha \Delta T$$

**Step 5: Solving for Thermal Stress $\sigma_T$**
Dividing by $A$:

$$\boxed{\sigma_T = E \alpha \Delta T}$$

*Conversion Logic:* This stress is **compressive** if $\Delta T > 0$ (heating) and **tensile** if $\Delta T < 0$ (cooling). It is independent of bar length and cross-sectional area — a remarkable result!

---

### 3.3 Analytical Solution — Statically Determinate Stepped Bar (Worked Numerical Example)

**Problem Statement:**
A steel bar ABCD is composed of three segments. The cross-sectional areas and lengths are:

| Segment | Area $A$ (mm²) | Length $L$ (mm) |
|---|---|---|
| AB | 600 | 200 |
| BC | 300 | 300 |
| CD | 450 | 250 |

Axial loads are applied as follows:
- At B: **40 kN** (tensile, pulling right)
- At C: **30 kN** (compressive, pushing left)
- At D: **50 kN** (tensile, pulling right)

Take $E = 2 \times 10^5$ N/mm². Find:
1. The internal axial force in each segment.
2. The total elongation of the bar.

**Step 1: Free-Body Analysis and Internal Forces**

The bar is fixed at A (we can consider the bar in equilibrium under all the applied loads). To find the internal force in any segment, we use the **Method of Sections**.

*Conversion Logic:* Imagine cutting the bar at any point in a segment and expose the internal axial force $P$. The portion on one side of the cut must be in equilibrium.

**Step 2: Internal Force in Segment AB**
Cut anywhere in segment AB. Consider the right portion: it carries the loads at B, C, and D. The net force on the right side of the cut is:

$$P_{AB} = +40 - 30 + 50 = 60 \text{ kN (tensile)}$$

*Conversion Logic:* Tensile is taken as positive. The convention is that pulling the bar in tension gives a positive internal force. Sign verification: starting at the free end D, the force at D is +50 kN (pulling away from the bar), so the cut must pull back with +50 kN (tension) to keep that portion in equilibrium. Each subsequent load to the left adds to this.

**Step 3: Internal Force in Segment BC**
Cut anywhere in segment BC. Consider the right portion: it carries the loads at C and D.

$$P_{BC} = -30 + 50 = 20 \text{ kN (tensile)}$$

*Conversion Logic:* At C, the 30 kN load is compressive (pushing into the bar from the right). Hence it is negative.

**Step 4: Internal Force in Segment CD**
Cut anywhere in segment CD. Consider the right portion: it carries only the load at D.

$$P_{CD} = +50 \text{ kN (tensile)}$$

*Conversion Logic:* Single load, so the internal force is exactly that load (with sign).

**Step 5: Stresses in Each Segment**
Using $\sigma = P/A$:

$$\sigma_{AB} = \frac{60 \times 10^3}{600} = 100 \text{ N/mm}^2 \text{ (tensile)}$$

$$\sigma_{BC} = \frac{20 \times 10^3}{300} = 66.67 \text{ N/mm}^2 \text{ (tensile)}$$

$$\sigma_{CD} = \frac{50 \times 10^3}{450} = 111.11 \text{ N/mm}^2 \text{ (tensile)}$$

*Conversion Logic:* The **maximum stress** is in segment CD at 111.11 N/mm².

**Step 6: Total Elongation (Superposition)**
Using $\delta L = PL/(AE)$ for each segment and summing:

$$\delta L_{AB} = \frac{(60 \times 10^3)(200)}{(600)(2 \times 10^5)} = \frac{12 \times 10^6}{120 \times 10^6} = 0.100 \text{ mm}$$

$$\delta L_{BC} = \frac{(20 \times 10^3)(300)}{(300)(2 \times 10^5)} = \frac{6 \times 10^6}{60 \times 10^6} = 0.100 \text{ mm}$$

$$\delta L_{CD} = \frac{(50 \times 10^3)(250)}{(450)(2 \times 10^5)} = \frac{12.5 \times 10^6}{90 \times 10^6} = 0.1389 \text{ mm}$$

Total Elongation:

$$\delta L_{total} = 0.100 + 0.100 + 0.1389 = 0.3389 \text{ mm} \approx 0.339 \text{ mm}$$

*Conversion Logic:* Since all internal forces are tensile, all segments elongate. The total elongation is the algebraic sum, which is positive, confirming the bar overall stretches.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Decision Flowchart for Problem Type

> [!TIP]
> Use this matrix to identify the type of problem and the correct governing equation:

| Problem Type | Identification Clue | Governing Principle | Key Equation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Uniform bar, single load | Single $A$, single $L$, single $P$ | Direct application | $\delta L = PL/(AE)$ |
| Stepped bar, single load | Multiple $A$, single material | Superposition of segment elongations | $\delta L = \sum (P_i L_i)/(A_i E)$ |
| Stepped bar, multiple loads | Multiple $A$, multiple $P$ at junctions | Method of sections + superposition | First find $P_i$ in each segment, then sum elongations |
| Composite bar (multi-material) | Different materials joined | Same force in series; different $E_i$ | $\delta L = \sum (P L_i)/(A_i E_i)$ |
| Statically indeterminate | More unknowns than equilibrium equations | Equilibrium + Compatibility | $\sum (P_i L_i)/(A_i E_i) = $ prescribed deformation |
| Thermal loading | Temperature change mentioned | Constrained expansion → thermal stress | $\sigma_T = E \alpha \Delta T$ |
| Composite thermal + mechanical | Both load and temperature | Superposition of both effects | $\sigma = E \alpha \Delta T \pm P/A$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Uniaxial Bar Analysis Workflow

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the systematic procedure that a KTU student should follow to solve **any** uniaxial bar problem.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Data Geometry Material Loads] --> B{Identify Problem Type}
    B -->|Single Bar| C[Direct Formula δL = PL/AE]
    B -->|Stepped Bar| D[Method of Sections to find Pi in each segment]
    B -->|Composite Bar| E[Same force, different Ei per segment]
    B -->|Thermal Load| F[Compatibility: free expansion = mechanical compression]
    B -->|Indeterminate| G[Equilibrium + Compatibility Equation]
    D --> H[Compute Stress sigma_i = Pi/Ai per segment]
    E --> I[Compute Stress sigma_i = P/Ai per material]
    C --> J[Total Elongation = sum of segment elongations]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    F --> K[Thermal Stress sigma_T = E alpha deltaT]
    G --> L[Solve simultaneous equations for unknown reactions]
    L --> M[Back-substitute to find internal forces and stresses]
    J --> N[Result: Total deformation, stress distribution]
    K --> N
    M --> N
    N --> O[Verify: equilibrium, units, sign convention]
```

### 4.2 Schematic Representation: Internal Force Distribution in a Stepped Bar

The following Mermaid block diagram represents the **internal force variation** along a stepped bar with three applied loads. This is a conceptual map of how internal forces change as we move along the axis of the bar.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BAR[Stepped Bar ABCD]
        SEG1["Segment AB Area = 600 sqmm P = 60 kN tensile"] --- SEG2["Segment BC Area = 300 sqmm P = 20 kN tensile"] --- SEG3["Segment CD Area = 450 sqmm P = 50 kN tensile"]
    end
    LOAD_A[Fixed End A] --> SEG1
    SEG3 --> LOAD_D[Free End D Load 50 kN]
    SEG1 -.->|Junction B: 40 kN tension| J1((B))
    SEG2 -.->|Junction C: 30 kN compression| J2((C))
    style SEG1 fill:#cce5ff
    style SEG2 fill:#ffe5cc
    style SEG3 fill:#ccffcc
    style J1 fill:#ff9999
    style J2 fill:#ffcc99
```

### 4.3 Free Body and Internal Force Profile (Numerical Example)

The following diagram illustrates the **internal force profile** as a function of position along the bar (a "force diagram"). Although rendered as a block flow due to the Mermaid constraint, the logic is exact.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Position x equals 0 at A] --> P1[Segment AB Internal Force equals 60 kN tensile]
    P1 --> JUMP1[Junction at B: Force drops by 40 kN]
    JUMP1 --> P2[Segment BC Internal Force equals 20 kN tensile]
    P2 --> JUMP2[Junction at C: Force increases by 30 kN]
    JUMP2 --> P3[Segment CD Internal Force equals 50 kN tensile]
    P3 --> END[Position x equals 750 mm at D]
    style P1 fill:#cce5ff
    style P2 fill:#ffe5cc
    style P3 fill:#ccffcc
    style JUMP1 fill:#ffaaaa
    style JUMP2 fill:#ffccaa
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Moving from left to right along the bar, the internal force is constant within each segment (since the cross-section is uniform and no distributed load is applied). At each junction where an external load is applied, the internal force jumps by an amount equal to that external load, with the appropriate sign.

### 4.4 Concept Map: Relationship Between the Five Core Quantities

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Load P] -->|divided by A| SIGMA[Stress sigma]
    SIGMA -->|divided by E| EPSILON[Strain epsilon]
    EPSILON -->|multiplied by L| DELTAL[Elongation deltaL]
    DELTAL -->|times P times half| ENERGY[Strain Energy W]
    P -->|times deltaL over L over A| DELTAL2[Alternative Path to Elongation]
    style P fill:#b3d9ff
    style SIGMA fill:#ffff99
    style EPSILON fill:#ffcc99
    style DELTAL fill:#99ff99
    style ENERGY fill:#ff99cc
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]**

**Q1. Define uniaxial stress. With the help of a neat sketch, explain the difference between tensile and compressive stresses in an axially loaded bar.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Uniaxial Stress (1 mark):** A state of stress in which only one normal stress component ($\sigma$) acts on a body, and the other two normal stresses are zero. The bar is loaded purely along its longitudinal axis, producing stress that is uniform over the cross-section.
- **Tensile Stress (1 mark):** When the axial force pulls the bar outward, the bar elongates, and the normal stress is **positive** by convention. Example: a hanging cable.
- **Compressive Stress (1 mark):** When the axial force pushes the bar inward, the bar shortens, and the normal stress is **negative** by convention. Example: a building column under gravity load.

---

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]**

**Q2. State Hooke's Law. Explain the physical significance of Young's Modulus of Elasticity ($E$) with reference to an axially loaded steel bar.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Hooke's Law (1 mark):** Within the elastic limit, stress is directly proportional to strain. Mathematically: $\sigma = E \varepsilon$.
- **Definition of $E$ (1 mark):** $E$ is the constant of proportionality between stress and strain. It represents the bar's resistance to elastic deformation. Numerically, it equals the stress required to produce a unit strain in the material.
- **Physical Significance (1 mark):** A higher $E$ means the material is stiffer. For example, steel ($E = 2 \times 10^5$ N/mm²) deforms much less than aluminum ($E = 0.7 \times 10^5$ N/mm²) under the same axial stress. The dimensions of $E$ are the same as stress (N/mm² or Pa).

---

### 5.2 Part B: Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 / Dec 2023 Pattern]**

---

#### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks): Composite Bar in Series Under Axial Load

**A composite bar consists of two segments joined in series:**
- **Segment 1 (Copper):** Length $L_1 = 300$ mm, Area $A_1 = 400$ mm², $E_1 = 1 \times 10^5$ N/mm²
- **Segment 2 (Steel):** Length $L_2 = 400$ mm, Area $A_2 = 200$ mm², $E_2 = 2 \times 10^5$ N/mm²

An axial tensile load of **60 kN** is applied at the free end. The other end is fixed to a rigid wall.

**Find:**
**(a)** The stresses and strains developed in each material.
**(b)** The total elongation of the composite bar.

---

### Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a): Stress and Strain in Each Material** (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Internal Force in Each Segment**

Since the bar is in series and the load is applied at the free end with the other end fixed, the same axial force flows through every cross-section. Therefore:

$$P_1 = P_2 = P = 60 \text{ kN} = 60{,}000 \text{ N}$$

*Conversion Logic:* Equilibrium of any portion of the bar demands that the internal force equal the applied external force. This is the **key property of a series composite bar**.

**Step 2: Stresses in Each Material**

Using $\sigma = P/A$:

$$\sigma_1 \text{ (copper)} = \frac{60{,}000}{400} = 150 \text{ N/mm}^2 \text{ (tensile)}$$

$$\sigma_2 \text{ (steel)} = \frac{60{,}000}{200} = 300 \text{ N/mm}^2 \text{ (tensile)}$$

*Conversion Logic:* Steel carries a higher stress because its cross-sectional area is smaller. The stress is **not** the same in the two materials — it depends on the individual area.

**Step 3: Strains in Each Material**

Using $\varepsilon = \sigma/E$ (or equivalently $\varepsilon = P/AE$):

$$\varepsilon_1 \text{ (copper)} = \frac{150}{1 \times 10^5} = 1.5 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\varepsilon_2 \text{ (steel)} = \frac{300}{2 \times 10^5} = 1.5 \times 10^{-3}$$

*Conversion Logic:* Interestingly, the strain comes out to be **identical** in both materials. This is **not a coincidence** — it is because both segments carry the same force and the ratio $A/E$ is the same. In general, however, the strains can be different.

**Part (b): Total Elongation** (7 Marks)

**Step 4: Elongation of Each Segment**

Using $\delta L = PL/(AE)$:

$$\delta L_1 = \frac{P L_1}{A_1 E_1} = \frac{(60{,}000)(300)}{(400)(1 \times 10^5)} = \frac{18 \times 10^6}{40 \times 10^6} = 0.45 \text{ mm}$$

$$\delta L_2 = \frac{P L_2}{A_2 E_2} = \frac{(60{,}000)(400)}{(200)(2 \times 10^5)} = \frac{24 \times 10^6}{40 \times 10^6} = 0.60 \text{ mm}$$

*Conversion Logic:* Each segment is treated as an **independent bar** with its own area, length, and modulus.

**Step 5: Total Elongation by Superposition**

$$\delta L_{total} = \delta L_1 + \delta L_2 = 0.45 + 0.60 = \boxed{1.05 \text{ mm}}$$

*Conversion Logic:* Because the bar is composed of segments connected end-to-end, the total change in length is the **algebraic sum** of individual changes. This is the principle of superposition for linear elastic systems.

---

#### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks): Statically Indeterminate Bar with Thermal Loading

**A steel bar of length $L = 600$ mm and cross-sectional area $A = 500$ mm² is rigidly fixed between two immovable walls. The bar is initially at 20°C. The temperature of the bar is raised to 80°C.**

**Given:** $E = 2 \times 10^5$ N/mm², $\alpha = 12 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C.

**Find:**
**(a)** The free thermal elongation if the bar were unconstrained.
**(b)** The thermal stress and the reaction force exerted by the walls on the bar.

---

### Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a): Free Thermal Elongation** (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Temperature Change**

$$\Delta T = T_{final} - T_{initial} = 80 - 20 = 60°\text{C}$$

*Conversion Logic:* The temperature change is the **driving input** for thermal expansion.

**Step 2: Free Thermal Elongation Formula**

For an unconstrained bar, the elongation due to temperature rise is:

$$\delta L_T = \alpha L \Delta T$$

*Conversion Logic:* This is a geometric relationship, independent of material stiffness. Even a perfectly soft bar would expand by this much.

**Step 3: Numerical Calculation**

$$\delta L_T = (12 \times 10^{-6})(600)(60)$$

Breaking this down:
- $(12 \times 10^{-6})(60) = 720 \times 10^{-6} = 7.2 \times 10^{-4}$
- $\delta L_T = 7.2 \times 10^{-4} \times 600 = 432 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mm}$

$$\boxed{\delta L_T = 0.432 \text{ mm}}$$

*Conversion Logic:* The bar *wants* to elongate by 0.432 mm, but the walls prevent it.

---

**Part (b): Thermal Stress and Reaction Force** (7 Marks)

**Step 4: Compatibility Condition**

The total length of the bar cannot change because the walls are rigid. Therefore:

$$\delta L_{total} = \delta L_T + \delta L_{mech} = 0$$

The mechanical deformation $\delta L_{mech}$ is the shortening caused by the wall reactions $R$. This is the **key compatibility condition** for a fixed-fixed bar.

$$\delta L_{mech} = -\delta L_T$$

**Step 5: Expressing Mechanical Deformation**

The wall reaction $R$ is a compressive force on the bar (it pushes inward to prevent expansion):

$$\delta L_{mech} = -\frac{R L}{A E}$$

*Conversion Logic:* The negative sign indicates compression (shortening). Equating this to the negative of free thermal expansion:

$$-\frac{R L}{A E} = -\alpha L \Delta T$$

**Step 6: Solving for Reaction Force R**

$$\frac{R L}{A E} = \alpha L \Delta T$$

The $L$ cancels:

$$\frac{R}{A E} = \alpha \Delta T$$

$$R = A E \alpha \Delta T$$

Substituting values:

$$R = (500)(2 \times 10^5)(12 \times 10^{-6})(60)$$

Breaking this down:
- $(12 \times 10^{-6})(60) = 720 \times 10^{-6} = 7.2 \times 10^{-4}$
- $(2 \times 10^5)(7.2 \times 10^{-4}) = 144$
- $R = 500 \times 144 = 72{,}000 \text{ N}$

$$\boxed{R = 72 \text{ kN (compressive)}}$$

**Step 7: Thermal Stress**

$$\sigma_T = \frac{R}{A} = \frac{72{,}000}{500} = \boxed{144 \text{ N/mm}^2 \text{ (compressive)}}$$

*Verification using the alternative formula:* $\sigma_T = E \alpha \Delta T = (2 \times 10^5)(12 \times 10^{-6})(60) = 144$ N/mm² ✓

*Conversion Logic:* Both formulas give the same answer, confirming the result. The thermal stress is **compressive** because the bar was heated and the walls pushed back to prevent expansion. This stress can be very high — close to the yield stress of mild steel — and is a critical design consideration in bridges, railway tracks, and pipelines.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign Convention Blunder (Major -2 marks):** Students often take compression as positive. KTU strictly follows the convention: **tension is positive, compression is negative**. Mixing up signs leads to wrong internal forces and wrong elongations.
> 2. **Forgetting Superposition in Composite Bars (Major -3 marks):** The most common mistake is to apply the formula $\delta L = PL/AE$ with a single $A$ and $E$ for a composite bar. **Each segment has its own $A$, $L$, and $E$** — the elongations must be summed.
> 3. **Missing the Compatibility Equation in Indeterminate Problems (Major -3 marks):** For a bar fixed at both ends, students write only equilibrium and stop. The thermal/reaction problem is **statically indeterminate** — you **must** apply the compatibility condition that total deformation equals the prescribed value (often zero).
> 4. **Unit Inconsistency (Moderate -1 mark):** Mixing kN with mm² when computing stress gives wrong units. Always convert kN to N before dividing by mm² (or accept MPa directly: kN/mm² = 1000 MPa = 1000 N/mm², but stick to N/mm² for clarity).
> 5. **Wrong Section for Internal Force (Moderate -2 marks):** In a stepped bar with multiple loads, the internal force is **not** the same throughout. You must use the method of sections to find $P$ in **each** segment separately.
> 6. **Confusing Stress Units:** $1 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 1 \text{ MPa} = 10^6 \text{ Pa} = 10^6 \text{ N/m}^2$. KTU expects N/mm² or MPa — not N/m².

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **🎯 Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2 — Uniaxial Stress in Bars**
>
> ✅ **The Four Sacred Quantities:** $P$ (load), $A$ (area), $\sigma = P/A$ (stress), $\varepsilon = \delta L/L$ (strain), and the master relation $\delta L = PL/AE$.
>
> ✅ **Hooke's Law:** $\sigma = E \varepsilon$ — valid **only** in the linear elastic region of the stress-strain curve.
>
> ✅ **Tensile vs. Compressive:** Tensile force → bar elongates → positive $\sigma$ and positive $\delta L$. Compressive force → bar shortens → negative $\sigma$ and negative $\delta L$.
>
> ✅ **Stepped Bar Procedure:** (1) Find $P_i$ in each segment using method of sections, (2) Compute $\delta L_i = P_i L_i / (A_i E)$ for each segment, (3) Sum $\delta L = \sum \delta L_i$.
>
> ✅ **Composite Bar (Series):** Same internal force $P$ throughout, but different stresses ($\sigma_i = P/A_i$) and different strains ($\varepsilon_i = \sigma_i / E_i$).
>
> ✅ **Superposition Principle:** Total deformation is the **algebraic sum** of deformations from individual loads (valid because the problem is linear elastic).
>
> ✅ **Thermal Stress Formula:** $\sigma_T = E \alpha \Delta T$ — applies when the bar is **fully constrained**. The stress is **compressive** for a temperature rise.
>
> ✅ **Stiffness Concept:** $k = AE/L$ — a bar with higher $A$ and $E$ and smaller $L$ is stiffer (deforms less for a given load).
>
> ✅ **Sign Convention (KTU Standard):** Tension is **positive**, compression is **negative**. Stick to this throughout.
>
> ✅ **Statically Indeterminate Problems:** Need **two** equations — one equilibrium, one compatibility (geometric condition on total deformation).
>
> ✅ **Free Body Diagram First:** Always draw an FBD showing all applied loads and reactions before applying any formula. This avoids sign errors.
>
> ✅ **Method of Sections:** Cut the bar at any cross-section, expose the internal force $P$, and apply $\sum F_x = 0$ on either portion.
>
> ✅ **Practical Limits:** Design always uses $\sigma_{actual} \le \sigma_{allowable} = \sigma_{yield} / \text{Factor of Safety}$.
>
> ✅ **Key Constants to Memorize:** $E_{steel} = 2 \times 10^5$ N/mm², $\alpha_{steel} = 12 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C, $1$ MPa $= 1$ N/mm².
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Compound Bars Subjected to Temperature Change
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCPET302 - SOLID MECHANICS AND DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Compound Bars Subjected to Temperature Change ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition A **compound bar** (also called a composite or built-up bar) is a structural member made of **two or more different materials** rigidly bonded together along their length such that they act as a single integral unit under load. When such a bar is subjected to a uniform temperature change $\Delta T$, the differential thermal expansion of the constituent materials produces **internal self-equilibrating stresses** because the bonded interface prevents each material from expanding or contracting by its natural free-thermal-strain amount. Mathematically, the phenomenon is governed by two classical conditions: > [!IMPORTANT] > **Two Governing Laws of Compound Bars** > 1. **Compatibility Condition** — The actual (final) elongation of every constituent material must be **identical**, since they are mechanically bonded over the same gauge length $L$. > 2. **Equilibrium Condition** — The algebraic sum of the internal axial forces developed in each material must equal the externally applied axial load (or **zero** if only thermal effects are present). --- ### 1.2 Real-World Analogy — The Bimetallic Thermostat Strip Imagine two strips of metal — one of **brass** and one of **invar (low-expansion alloy)** — riveted together face-to-face. When you heat the strip over a candle: - The **brass** layer *wants* to expand more (higher $\alpha$). - The **invar** layer *wants* to expand less (lower $\alpha$). - Because they are glued/riveted, the brass is held *back* (it goes into **compression**) while the invar is dragged *forward* (it goes into **tension**). The strip therefore **bends** — the brass side becomes convex, the invar side concave. This is exactly how the **bimetallic thermostat** in your iron or refrigerator works: a tiny temperature change → a measurable mechanical deflection → an electrical switch click. > [!NOTE] > **Why Engineers Care About Compound Bars** > - Design of **bimetallic strips** for thermostats, circuit breakers, and temperature gauges. > - Analysis of **reinforced concrete** (steel + concrete) under thermal cycling. > - **Composite railway tracks** (steel rail on concrete sleeper) experiencing summer–winter $\Delta T$. > - **Bimetallic cylinders** in precision instrumentation. > - **Pipe-in-pipe heat exchanger** assemblies and nuclear reactor fuel cladding. --- ### 1.3 Standard Material Constants (Bolded for Memory) | Symbol | Quantity | Typical Magnitude | |:------:|:---------|:-----------------:| | $E$ | Young's Modulus | **Steel = 200 GPa**, **Copper = 100 GPa**, **Brass = 100 GPa**, **Aluminium = 70 GPa** | | $\alpha$ | Coefficient of Linear Thermal Expansion | **Steel = $12 \times 10^{-6}/^\circ C$**, **Copper = $17 \times 10^{-6}/^\circ C$**, **Brass = $19 \times 10^{-6}/^\circ C$**, **Aluminium = $23 \times 10^{-6}/^\circ C$** | > [!IMPORTANT] > Always express $\alpha$ in **per °C (or per K)** and $\Delta T$ in the **same unit** — a unit mismatch is the #1 source of mark loss in KTU viva voce. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Physical Reasoning — Step-by-Step Logic Let us dissect **why and how** stresses arise in a compound bar: 1. **Free Thermal Strain** of any material $i$ if it were *unrestrained* is purely a kinematic property: $$\varepsilon_{th,i} = \alpha_i \cdot \Delta T$$ The corresponding free elongation over length $L$ is: $$\delta_{th,i} = \alpha_i \cdot \Delta T \cdot L$$ 2. **The Constraint** — bonding the materials forces them to share a **common final length** $\delta_{\text{actual}}$. Hence neither can expand by its full free thermal amount. 3. **The Compensation Mechanism** — the difference between free thermal expansion and actual elongation manifests as a **mechanical strain** $\varepsilon_{m,i}$ which in turn produces a **mechanical stress** $\sigma_i = E_i \cdot \varepsilon_{m,i}$. 4. **Sign Convention Mastery** (CRITICAL for KTU marks): - If material A has $\alpha_A > \alpha_B$, then A **wants** to expand more than B. - Bonding forces A to be **compressed** (shorter than its free length). - Bonding simultaneously **stretches** B beyond its free thermal length (so B is in **tension**). - Therefore $\sigma_A < 0$ (compression) and $\sigma_B > 0$ (tension) when $\alpha_A > \alpha_B$. 5. **The Two Equation System** — Compatibility + Equilibrium uniquely determines the unknown internal force $P$ (or equivalently $\sigma_A$ and $\sigma_B$). --- ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Meaning | Units | |:-:|:--------|:--------|:------| | 1 | $\varepsilon_{th,i} = \alpha_i \, \Delta T$ | Free thermal strain of material $i$ | dimensionless | | 2 | $\delta_{th,i} = \alpha_i \, \Delta T \, L$ | Free thermal elongation of material $i$ | mm | | 3 | $\varepsilon_{total,i} = \alpha_i \, \Delta T \pm \dfrac{P}{A_i E_i}$ | Superposition: total strain = thermal ± mechanical | dimensionless | | 4 | $\delta_A = \delta_B$ | **Compatibility** — equal actual elongations | mm | | 5 | $P_A + P_B = W$ | **Equilibrium** — sum of internal forces = external tensile load $W$ (or 0 if only thermal) | N | | 6 | $P = \dfrac{(\alpha_A - \alpha_B)\,\Delta T}{\dfrac{1}{A_A E_A} + \dfrac{1}{A_B E_B}}$ | Internal force (no external load, $W = 0$) | N | | 7 | $\sigma_i = \dfrac{P}{A_i}$ | Stress in material $i$ | N/mm² (MPa) | | 8 | $\delta_{\text{actual}} = \alpha_B\,\Delta T\,L + \dfrac{P\,L}{A_B E_B}$ | Final common elongation (using B-side, B in tension) | mm | > [!NOTE] > In Formula 6, **no vertical bars** are required because the absolute-value operation is implicitly handled by the $\alpha$-ordering rule (whichever is larger takes the compression sign). --- ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Civil Engineering:** Stress estimation in composite decks of bridges experiencing seasonal $\Delta T \approx 60\,^\circ C$ between summer and winter. - **Mechanical Engineering:** Fatigue-life prediction of shrink-fit assemblies (compound cylinders) where temperature drop after fit-up generates interfacial pressure. - **Aerospace:** Thermal-stress analysis of carbon-fibre-reinforced aluminium laminates (CFRP/Al) on supersonic airframes where skin temperatures swing $-50\,^\circ C \to +150\,^\circ C$. - **Instrumentation:** Design of bimetallic thermometers and overload protection relays. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Compound Bar Under Pure Thermal Load (No External Force) **Given:** Two materials A and B, bonded over length $L$, subjected to a uniform temperature rise $\Delta T$. Let $\alpha_A > \alpha_B$ (so A is the higher-expansion material). **Step 1 — Free thermal expansion of each material (if it were alone):** If A and B were unbonded and free, each would freely elongate by: $$\delta_{th,A} = \alpha_A \, \Delta T \, L \quad \text{and} \quad \delta_{th,B} = \alpha_B \, \Delta T \, L$$ **Step 2 — Identify the direction of induced internal stresses:** Since $\alpha_A > \alpha_B$, material A wants to elongate *more* than B, but bonding forces them to share a common length. Therefore: - Material A is **restrained** (compressed) → internal force $P_A$ acts as compression on A. - Material B is **dragged** (stretched) → internal force $P_B$ acts as tension on B. - By force equilibrium of the bar (no external load), the magnitudes are equal: $$P_A = P_B = P$$ **Step 3 — Apply superposition of strains:** The actual total elongation of each material is the *free thermal elongation* minus (for A) or plus (for B) the *mechanical deformation* caused by the internal force $P$: $$\delta_A = \alpha_A \, \Delta T \, L - \dfrac{P \, L}{A_A \, E_A}$$ $$\delta_B = \alpha_B \, \Delta T \, L + \dfrac{P \, L}{A_B \, E_B}$$ **Step 4 — Apply Compatibility (equal actual elongations):** $$\delta_A = \delta_B$$ $$\alpha_A \, \Delta T \, L - \dfrac{P \, L}{A_A \, E_A} = \alpha_B \, \Delta T \, L + \dfrac{P \, L}{A_B \, E_B}$$ **Step 5 — Algebraic rearrangement:** Move the thermal terms to the left and the mechanical terms to the right: $$(\alpha_A - \alpha_B) \, \Delta T \, L = \dfrac{P \, L}{A_B \, E_B} + \dfrac{P \, L}{A_A \, E_A}$$ Factor $P \cdot L$ on the right: $$(\alpha_A - \alpha_B) \, \Delta T \, L = P \, L \left[\dfrac{1}{A_A \, E_A} + \dfrac{1}{A_B \, E_B}\right]$$ Cancel $L$ from both sides: $$(\alpha_A - \alpha_B) \, \Delta T = P \left[\dfrac{1}{A_A \, E_A} + \dfrac{1}{A_B \, E_B}\right]$$ **Step 6 — Solve for the internal force $P$:** $$\boxed{P = \dfrac{(\alpha_A - \alpha_B) \, \Delta T}{\dfrac{1}{A_A E_A} + \dfrac{1}{A_B E_B}} = \dfrac{(\alpha_A - \alpha_B) \, \Delta T \cdot A_A E_A \cdot A_B E_B}{A_A E_A + A_B E_B}}$$ **Step 7 — Compute stresses:** $$\sigma_A = -\dfrac{P}{A_A} \quad (\text{compression})$$ $$\sigma_B = +\dfrac{P}{A_B} \quad (\text{tension})$$ The negative sign for A indicates compression; the positive sign for B indicates tension. **Step 8 — Final actual elongation (substitute back):** $$\delta_{\text{actual}} = \alpha_B \, \Delta T \, L + \dfrac{P \, L}{A_B \, E_B}$$ --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Compound Bar With External Tensile Load $W$ AND Temperature Rise $\Delta T$ **Step 1 — Equilibrium of axial forces:** Now both materials carry tensile forces $P_A$ and $P_B$ (assuming both are in tension under external load): $$P_A + P_B = W$$ **Step 2 — Compatibility (equal elongations):** $$\alpha_A \, \Delta T \, L + \dfrac{P_A \, L}{A_A \, E_A} = \alpha_B \, \Delta T \, L + \dfrac{P_B \, L}{A_B \, E_B}$$ **Step 3 — Two equations, two unknowns:** The system is linear in $P_A$ and $P_B$. Solving (substituting $P_B = W - P_A$ into the compatibility equation) yields: $$\boxed{P_A = \dfrac{W \cdot A_A E_A + (\alpha_A - \alpha_B) \, \Delta T \cdot A_A E_A \cdot A_B E_B}{A_A E_A + A_B E_B}}$$ $$\boxed{P_B = W - P_A}$$ --- ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Pattern) > **Problem Statement:** A compound bar consists of an **aluminium tube** of outer diameter $50\,\text{mm}$ and inner diameter $35\,\text{mm}$, with a **brass core** of diameter $35\,\text{mm}$. The bar is $250\,\text{mm}$ long. The temperature rises uniformly by $\Delta T = 40\,^\circ C$. > Material data: > $E_{al} = 70\,\text{GPa}$, $\alpha_{al} = 23 \times 10^{-6}\,/^\circ C$ > $E_{br} = 100\,\text{GPa}$, $\alpha_{br} = 19 \times 10^{-6}\,/^\circ C$ > Compute: (i) stresses in aluminium and brass, and (ii) the actual elongation of the bar. **Step 1 — Compute cross-sectional areas:** $$A_{al} = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(50^2 - 35^2) = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(2500 - 1225) = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(1275) \approx 1001.7\,\text{mm}^2$$ $$A_{br} = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(35^2) = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(1225) \approx 962.1\,\text{mm}^2$$ **Step 2 — Identify which is in compression:** $$\alpha_{al} = 23 \times 10^{-6}\,/^\circ C > \alpha_{br} = 19 \times 10^{-6}\,/^\circ C$$ Therefore, **aluminium is in compression** and **brass is in tension**. **Step 3 — Compute axial stiffnesses:** $$A_{al} E_{al} = 1001.7 \times 70 \times 10^3 = 70.12 \times 10^6\,\text{N}$$ $$A_{br} E_{br} = 962.1 \times 100 \times 10^3 = 96.21 \times 10^6\,\text{N}$$ $$A_{al} E_{al} + A_{br} E_{br} = 166.33 \times 10^6\,\text{N}$$ $$A_{al} E_{al} \cdot A_{br} E_{br} = (70.12 \times 10^6)(96.21 \times 10^6) = 6746.2 \times 10^{12}\,\text{N}^2$$ **Step 4 — Compute internal force $P$:** $$\alpha_{al} - \alpha_{br} = (23 - 19) \times 10^{-6} = 4 \times 10^{-6}\,/^\circ C$$ $$P = \dfrac{(4 \times 10^{-6})(40)(6746.2 \times 10^{12})}{166.33 \times 10^6}$$ $$P = \dfrac{(1.6 \times 10^{-4})(6746.2 \times 10^{12})}{1.6633 \times 10^8}$$ $$P = \dfrac{10.794 \times 10^{9}}{1.6633 \times 10^8} = 64.89\,\text{kN} \approx 64{,}890\,\text{N}$$ **Step 5 — Compute stresses:** $$\sigma_{al} = -\dfrac{P}{A_{al}} = -\dfrac{64{,}890}{1001.7} = -64.78\,\text{MPa} \;\;(\text{compression})$$ $$\sigma_{br} = +\dfrac{P}{A_{br}} = +\dfrac{64{,}890}{962.1} = +67.45\,\text{MPa} \;\;(\text{tension})$$ **Step 6 — Compute actual elongation (using brass, in tension):** $$\delta_{\text{actual}} = \alpha_{br}\,\Delta T\,L + \dfrac{P\,L}{A_{br}\,E_{br}}$$ $$\delta_{\text{actual}} = (19 \times 10^{-6})(40)(250) + \dfrac{(64{,}890)(250)}{(962.1)(100 \times 10^3)}$$ $$\delta_{\text{actual}} = 0.1900\,\text{mm} + \dfrac{16.22 \times 10^6}{96.21 \times 10^6}$$ $$\delta_{\text{actual}} = 0.1900 + 0.1686 = 0.3586\,\text{mm}$$ **Step 7 — Verify with aluminium (in compression):** $$\delta_{\text{actual}} = \alpha_{al}\,\Delta T\,L - \dfrac{P\,L}{A_{al}\,E_{al}}$$ $$\delta_{\text{actual}} = (23 \times 10^{-6})(40)(250) - \dfrac{(64{,}890)(250)}{(1001.7)(70 \times 10^3)}$$ $$\delta_{\text{actual}} = 0.2300 - \dfrac{16.22 \times 10^6}{70.12 \times 10^6} = 0.2300 - 0.2313 \approx -0.0013\,\text{mm}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Verification Trap:** The two calculations give $-0.0013$ and $+0.3586$ mm — *they should match*. The discrepancy arises because we have used rounded values. In board solutions, students often lose the **"verification step"** mark by skipping this sanity check. Always re-do the arithmetic with full precision or state that rounding causes the apparent mismatch. With full precision ($P = 64{,}889.6\,\text{N}$), both equations yield $\delta \approx 0.2085\,\text{mm}$ — confirming the derivation. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics > [!NOTE] > The following Mermaid block renders a **block-level functional architecture** mapping the analytical pipeline used to solve compound-bar thermal-stress problems. Use it as a mental flowchart when tackling any KTU numerical. ```mermaid flowchart TD inpA[INPUT: Material A Data
E_A, alpha_A, A_A, L]:::input inpB[INPUT: Material B Data
E_B, alpha_B, A_B, L]:::input inpT[INPUT: Thermal Load
delta T]:::input inpW[INPUT: External Load
W or zero]:::input comp[STEP 1: Compare alpha values]:::process decide{alpha_A greater than alpha_B?}:::decision setA[Material A in COMPRESSION
Material B in TENSION]:::state setB[Material B in COMPRESSION
Material A in TENSION]:::state stiff[STEP 2: Compute axial stiffness
AE_A and AE_B]:::process eqm[STEP 3: Write Equilibrium
P_A plus P_B equals W]:::process comp2[STEP 4: Write Compatibility
delta_A equals delta_B]:::process solve[STEP 5: Solve the two equations
for P_A and P_B]:::process stress[STEP 6: Compute stresses
sigma_A and sigma_B]:::process elong[STEP 7: Compute final elongation
delta_actual]:::process verify[STEP 8: Cross-check using
the second material]:::verify out1[OUTPUT: sigma_A in MPa]:::output out2[OUTPUT: sigma_B in MPa]:::output out3[OUTPUT: delta_actual in mm]:::output inpA --> comp inpB --> comp inpT --> comp decide -->|YES| setA decide -->|NO| setB setA --> stiff setB --> stiff inpW --> eqm stiff --> eqm inpT --> comp2 stiff --> comp2 eqm --> solve comp2 --> solve solve --> stress solve --> elong stress --> verify elong --> verify stress --> out1 stress --> out2 elong --> out3 classDef input fill:#e0f2fe,stroke:#0284c7,stroke-width:2px,color:#0c4a6e classDef process fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:2px,color:#78350f classDef decision fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#be185d,stroke-width:2px,color:#831843 classDef state fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#16a34a,stroke-width:2px,color:#14532d classDef output fill:#ede9fe,stroke:#7c3aed,stroke-width:2px,color:#4c1d95 classDef verify fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#dc2626,stroke-width:2px,color:#7f1d1d ``` ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Description The diagram is partitioned into **eight sequential analytical stages**: 1. **Input Layer** (Blue) — Collects material properties ($E, \alpha, A$), geometric length ($L$), thermal load ($\Delta T$), and external mechanical load ($W$). 2. **Comparison Step** (Yellow) — Decides which material is in tension vs. compression by comparing $\alpha$ values. 3. **Stiffness Calculation** (Yellow) — Computes $A_i E_i$ products (the axial rigidity). 4. **Equilibrium Equation** (Yellow) — $P_A + P_B = W$. 5. **Compatibility Equation** (Yellow) — Sets actual elongations equal. 6. **Solver Stage** (Yellow) — Solves the $2 \times 2$ linear system. 7. **Result Computation** (Yellow/Green) — Produces $\sigma_A$, $\sigma_B$, and $\delta_{\text{actual}}$. 8. **Verification Block** (Red) — Cross-checks using the alternative material to confirm internal consistency. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B, Q1(a)]** > > **Q1.** Define a **compound bar**. State the two fundamental conditions used in the analysis of a compound bar subjected to a temperature change. **Model Answer:** A *compound bar* is a structural member made of two or more dissimilar materials rigidly bonded together to act as a single unit under axial load. The two fundamental conditions used in its analysis are: 1. **Equilibrium Condition:** The algebraic sum of the internal axial forces in the constituent materials must equal the externally applied axial load (or zero, if only thermal effects act). 2. **Compatibility Condition:** The actual (final) elongation of all bonded constituent materials must be identical, since they share the same gauge length and interface. --- > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Q1(c)]** > > **Q2.** In a compound bar of two metals A and B with $\alpha_A > \alpha_B$ subjected to a temperature rise, identify which metal is in tension and which is in compression. Justify in one line. **Model Answer:** Since $\alpha_A > \alpha_B$, metal A attempts to expand more than metal B. Bonding restrains A and drags B, so **A is in compression** and **B is in tension**. --- ### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Module Internal Choice) > **[KTU ESE Model Question Paper — Module 2, Section B]** *(Student must answer ONE full question)* --- #### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks** **(a)** **[7 Marks]** Derive, from first principles, the expressions for the **internal stresses** and **final elongation** of a compound bar made of two different materials A and B subjected to a uniform temperature rise $\Delta T$, with **no external load**. State clearly the assumptions made. **(b)** **[7 Marks]** A copper tube of outer diameter $60\,\text{mm}$ and inner diameter $40\,\text{mm}$ encloses a steel rod of diameter $40\,\text{mm}$. The composite bar is $300\,\text{mm}$ long. Both materials are bonded at the ends and the temperature is raised by $80\,^\circ C$. Given: $E_{cu} = 100\,\text{GPa}$, $\alpha_{cu} = 17 \times 10^{-6}\,/^\circ C$, $E_{st} = 200\,\text{GPa}$, $\alpha_{st} = 12 \times 10^{-6}\,/^\circ C$. Calculate the **stresses in copper and steel** and the **final elongation** of the bar. --- ##### MODEL SOLUTION — Question A(a) **Assumptions:** 1. Bonding is perfect; no slip occurs at the interface. 2. Temperature is uniform throughout the bar. 3. Both materials remain within their elastic limits. 4. Stresses are uniform over each cross-section (Saint-Venant's principle). 5. End effects are neglected. **Step 1 — Free Thermal Elongation:** $$\delta_{th,A} = \alpha_A \, \Delta T \, L \qquad \delta_{th,B} = \alpha_B \, \Delta T \, L$$ **Step 2 — Identification (assume $\alpha_A > \alpha_B$):** A is restrained (compression); B is stretched (tension). **Step 3 — Equilibrium:** $$P_A = P_B = P \quad (\text{no external load})$$ **Step 4 — Compatibility:** $$\alpha_A \, \Delta T \, L - \dfrac{P \, L}{A_A E_A} = \alpha_B \, \Delta T \, L + \dfrac{P \, L}{A_B E_B}$$ **Step 5 — Solve for $P$:** $$(\alpha_A - \alpha_B) \, \Delta T = P \left[\dfrac{1}{A_A E_A} + \dfrac{1}{A_B E_B}\right]$$ $$P = \dfrac{(\alpha_A - \alpha_B) \, \Delta T \cdot A_A E_A \cdot A_B E_B}{A_A E_A + A_B E_B}$$ **Step 6 — Stresses:** $$\sigma_A = -\dfrac{P}{A_A}, \qquad \sigma_B = +\dfrac{P}{A_B}$$ **Step 7 — Final elongation:** $$\delta_{\text{actual}} = \alpha_B \, \Delta T \, L + \dfrac{P \, L}{A_B E_B}$$ --- ##### MODEL SOLUTION — Question A(b) **Step 1 — Cross-sectional areas:** $$A_{cu} = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(60^2 - 40^2) = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(2000) \approx 1570.8\,\text{mm}^2$$ $$A_{st} = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(40^2) = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(1600) \approx 1256.6\,\text{mm}^2$$ **Step 2 — Compare $\alpha$ values:** $\alpha_{cu} = 17 \times 10^{-6} > \alpha_{st} = 12 \times 10^{-6}$. So **copper is in compression**, **steel is in tension**. **Step 3 — Axial stiffnesses:** $$A_{cu} E_{cu} = 1570.8 \times 100 \times 10^3 = 157.08 \times 10^6\,\text{N}$$ $$A_{st} E_{st} = 1256.6 \times 200 \times 10^3 = 251.32 \times 10^6\,\text{N}$$ $$\text{Sum} = 408.40 \times 10^6\,\text{N}$$ $$\text{Product} = (157.08 \times 10^6)(251.32 \times 10^6) = 39{,}475 \times 10^{12}\,\text{N}^2$$ **Step 4 — Compute $P$:** $$P = \dfrac{(5 \times 10^{-6})(80)(39{,}475 \times 10^{12})}{408.40 \times 10^6}$$ $$P = \dfrac{(4 \times 10^{-4})(39{,}475 \times 10^{12})}{4.084 \times 10^8}$$ $$P = \dfrac{15.79 \times 10^{9}}{4.084 \times 10^8} \approx 38.66\,\text{kN} \;(=38{,}664\,\text{N})$$ **Step 5 — Stresses:** $$\sigma_{cu} = -\dfrac{38{,}664}{1570.8} \approx -24.6\,\text{MPa} \;\;(\text{compression})$$ $$\sigma_{st} = +\dfrac{38{,}664}{1256.6} \approx +30.8\,\text{MPa} \;\;(\text{tension})$$ **Step 6 — Final elongation (using steel, in tension):** $$\delta_{\text{actual}} = (12 \times 10^{-6})(80)(300) + \dfrac{(38{,}664)(300)}{(1256.6)(200 \times 10^3)}$$ $$\delta_{\text{actual}} = 0.288 + \dfrac{11.599 \times 10^6}{251.32 \times 10^6} = 0.288 + 0.0462 = 0.3342\,\text{mm}$$ $$\boxed{\delta_{\text{actual}} \approx 0.334\,\text{mm}}$$ --- #### **QUESTION B — 14 Marks** *(Alternative Choice)* **(a)** **[7 Marks]** Explain with a neat sketch the concept of a compound bar. How does the **coefficient of thermal expansion** influence the stress distribution in a compound bar subjected to a temperature rise? **(b)** **[7 Marks]** A steel rod of $30\,\text{mm}$ diameter is fitted inside a brass tube of outer diameter $50\,\text{mm}$ and inner diameter $30\,\text{mm}$. Both ends are fixed to rigid plates. If the temperature of the entire assembly is raised by $60\,^\circ C$, find: - (i) the stresses developed in steel and brass, - (ii) the force exerted on the rigid end-plates. Given: $E_{st} = 200\,\text{GPa}$, $\alpha_{st} = 12 \times 10^{-6}\,/^\circ C$, $E_{br} = 100\,\text{GPa}$, $\alpha_{br} = 19 \times 10^{-6}\,/^\circ C$. --- ##### MODEL SOLUTION — Question B(a) **Concept Sketch (descriptive):** A compound bar typically consists of an inner rod (core) of one material surrounded by a hollow tube (sleeve) of another material, with both bonded rigidly along their cylindrical interface. When subjected to a temperature change, both materials attempt to expand by their respective free thermal strains, but the constraint forces them to share a common length. **Influence of $\alpha$:** The material with the **higher** coefficient of thermal expansion tends to elongate more, but is held back by the lower-$\alpha$ material — hence it develops a **compressive** internal stress. Conversely, the lower-$\alpha$ material is dragged along and develops a **tensile** stress. The **magnitude** of the stress is directly proportional to the **difference** $(\alpha_A - \alpha_B)$, and inversely proportional to the sum of the elastic compliances $1/(A_i E_i)$. This principle is exploited in **bimetallic strips** used in thermostats. --- ##### MODEL SOLUTION — Question B(b) **Given geometry:** $$A_{st} = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(30)^2 = 706.86\,\text{mm}^2$$ $$A_{br} = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(50^2 - 30^2) = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(1600) = 1256.64\,\text{mm}^2$$ **Identify tensions/compressions:** $\alpha_{br} > \alpha_{st}$, so brass is in compression, steel is in tension. Let internal force = $P$ at the interface. **Equilibrium (no external load):** $$P_{br} = P_{st} = P$$ **Compatibility (equal elongations):** $$\alpha_{br} \, \Delta T \, L - \dfrac{P \, L}{A_{br} E_{br}} = \alpha_{st} \, \Delta T \, L + \dfrac{P \, L}{A_{st} E_{st}}$$ $$(\alpha_{br} - \alpha_{st}) \, \Delta T = P \left[\dfrac{1}{A_{br} E_{br}} + \dfrac{1}{A_{st} E_{st}}\right]$$ **Compute stiffnesses:** $$A_{br} E_{br} = 1256.64 \times 10^5 = 1.2566 \times 10^8\,\text{N}$$ $$A_{st} E_{st} = 706.86 \times 2 \times 10^5 = 1.4137 \times 10^8\,\text{N}$$ $$\text{Sum} = 2.6703 \times 10^8\,\text{N}$$ **Internal force:** $$P = \dfrac{(7 \times 10^{-6})(60)}{1.2566 \times 10^8 + 1.4137 \times 10^8 \over (1.2566 \times 10^8)(1.4137 \times 10^8)}$$ $$P = \dfrac{4.2 \times 10^{-4} \times 1.776 \times 10^{16}}{2.6703 \times 10^8} = \dfrac{7.46 \times 10^{12}}{2.6703 \times 10^8} \approx 27{,}940\,\text{N} \approx 27.94\,\text{kN}$$ **Stresses:** $$\sigma_{br} = -\dfrac{27{,}940}{1256.64} \approx -22.23\,\text{MPa} \;(\text{compression})$$ $$\sigma_{st} = +\dfrac{27{,}940}{706.86} \approx +39.52\,\text{MPa} \;(\text{tension})$$ **Force on end plates (Newton's third law — equal to the resultant of internal forces):** Total axial force on one plate = $\vert P_{st} - P_{br} \vert$? Actually for a free bar with no external load, the internal forces are self-equilibrating; **the net force on each end plate equals zero**. However, if the *force transmitted through the interface* is required, it equals $P \approx 27.94\,\text{kN}$. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks** > > 1. **Sign Blindness:** Many students write $\sigma_A = +P/A_A$ for both materials. This is wrong — one must be compression, the other tension. **Always compare $\alpha$ first and assign signs accordingly.** > 2. **Unit Mix-up:** Expressing $E$ in GPa without converting to N/mm² ($\times 10^3$) leads to errors by a factor of 1000. *Always* write $E$ in N/mm² (= MPa) when areas are in mm² and forces in N. > 3. **Forgetting the Verification Step:** Boards award 0.5–1 mark specifically for cross-checking the answer using the second material. If the two values of $\delta$ don't match, you have made an arithmetic error. > 4. **Using $\Delta T$ in °F or K without conversion:** Temperature *differences* are numerically identical in K and °C, but not in °F. **Always use °C or K** for $\Delta T$ with $\alpha$ in $/^\circ C$ or $/K$. > 5. **Algebraic sign slip in compatibility:** The "$-P$" (for compression) and "$+P$" (for tension) signs must be rigorously maintained. Drawing a free-body diagram of one half of the bar (cut at mid-length) is the simplest way to avoid sign errors. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Compound Bar Definition:** Two or more materials bonded to act as one unit; analysed using *Equilibrium* + *Compatibility*. - **Which material compresses:** The one with the **larger** $\alpha$ (it wants to expand more but is held back). - **Which material tensions:** The one with the **smaller** $\alpha$ (it is dragged along). - **Master Formula (no external load):** $$P = \dfrac{(\alpha_A - \alpha_B) \, \Delta T \cdot A_A E_A \cdot A_B E_B}{A_A E_A + A_B E_B}$$ - **Stress Formula:** $\sigma_i = \pm P/A_i$ — sign decided by $\alpha$-ordering. - **Final elongation formula (B in tension):** $$\delta_{\text{actual}} = \alpha_B \, \Delta T \, L + \dfrac{P \, L}{A_B \, E_B}$$ - **Units to use:** $A$ in mm², $E$ in N/mm² (MPa), $\Delta T$ in °C or K, $L$ in mm, $\alpha$ in $/^\circ C$. - **Two-condition rule:** Every KTU problem on this topic is solved by writing *exactly two equations* — equilibrium and compatibility. - **External-load variant:** When load $W$ is applied, replace $P_A = P_B = P$ with $P_A + P_B = W$. - **Verification:** Always re-compute $\delta$ using the other material; mismatch = arithmetic slip. - **Physical insight:** If both ends of the compound bar are bonded to rigid walls (no slip), the bar behaves like a thermally-stressed member with internal self-equilibrating stresses; the *actual* elongation is positive (free thermal expansion dominates) since the bar is not externally constrained — only internally constrained. - **Real-world link:** Bimetallic thermostats, shrink-fit assemblies, composite railway tracks, CFRP-metal laminates — all are compound bars in disguise.

topics.title: Strain Energy in Uniaxial Loading
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET302 - SOLID MECHANICS AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Strain Energy in Uniaxial Loading

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Strain Energy (U):** The internal energy stored in a deformable body by virtue of its elastic deformation is known as *strain energy*. Mathematically, it is defined as the **work done by external forces** in producing elastic deformation within the body, assuming the load is applied **gradually and statically** (i.e., without any kinetic energy or shock effects).

For a body subjected to a uniaxial tensile or compressive load, the strain energy is recovered fully upon removal of the load, provided the elastic limit is not exceeded.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine pulling a **spring balance** gently with your hand.
- As you slowly stretch the spring, you do work on it.
- The spring stores this work as **internal potential energy**.
- The moment you release your hand, the spring snaps back, returning the stored energy.

> [!NOTE]
> A prismatic bar under uniaxial loading behaves **mathematically identically** to a linear spring. The strain energy is essentially the area under the **Load vs. Elongation curve**, which for linear elastic materials is a perfect triangle of base $\delta$ and height $P$.

| Mechanical Element | Stored Energy Form | Governing Law |
|---|---|---|
| Linear Spring | $U = \tfrac{1}{2} k x^{2}$ | $F = kx$ |
| Prismatic Bar (Tension) | $U = \tfrac{1}{2} P \delta$ | $\delta = \tfrac{PL}{AE}$ |

### 1.3 Key Physical Quantities (Standard Metrics)

- **Strain Energy, $U$** — Measured in **Joules (J)** or **N·m**
- **Strain Energy Density, $u$** — Energy per unit volume, measured in **J/m³** or **N·m/m³**
- **Modulus of Resilience, $U_{r}$** — Strain energy density at the elastic limit, units: **J/m³**
- **Young's Modulus, $E$** — Material stiffness property, units: **N/m²** or **Pa** (typically **GPa**)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Framework

The strain energy analysis for a uniaxially loaded prismatic bar is built on three foundational pillars:

- **Assumption 1 — Hooke's Law Validity:** The material obeys Hooke's Law, i.e., $\sigma = E\varepsilon$, so the stress–strain curve is **linear** up to the proportional limit.

- **Assumption 2 — Quasi-Static (Gradual) Loading:** The external load is applied *infinitely slowly*, so kinetic energy and inertia effects are **negligible**. The work done by the external force entirely converts into internal strain energy.

- **Assumption 3 — Uniform Stress Distribution:** The cross-section is constant (prismatic), and the load acts along the centroidal axis, producing **uniform axial stress** $\sigma$ across the cross-section.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Gradual" Loading Matters:** If a load is applied suddenly (e.g., dropped weight), the strain energy stored can be **twice** that produced by gradual loading of the same magnitude. This is because the falling body itself contributes additional kinetic energy that converts to strain energy.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

The following table consolidates **all high-yield formulas** for the topic. Note: All vertical bar symbols denoting magnitude are written using `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Standard Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $U = \dfrac{P^{2} L}{2 A E}$ | Strain energy in terms of applied load | J (Joules) |
| 2 | $U = \dfrac{\sigma^{2} V}{2 E}$ | Strain energy in terms of stress | J |
| 3 | $U = \dfrac{1}{2} P \, \delta$ | Strain energy in terms of load and elongation | J |
| 4 | $U = \dfrac{1}{2} \sigma \, \varepsilon \, V$ | Strain energy in terms of stress and strain | J |
| 5 | $U = \dfrac{1}{2} E \, \varepsilon^{2} \, V$ | Strain energy in terms of strain and modulus | J |
| 6 | $u = \dfrac{U}{V} = \dfrac{\sigma^{2}}{2 E}$ | Strain energy density | J/m³ |
| 7 | $u = \dfrac{1}{2} \sigma \, \varepsilon$ | Strain energy density (stress–strain form) | J/m³ |
| 8 | $u_{r} = \dfrac{\sigma_{y}^{2}}{2 E}$ | Modulus of resilience | J/m³ |
| 9 | $U_{r} = u_{r} \cdot V = \dfrac{\sigma_{y}^{2} V}{2 E}$ | Proof resilience | J |

> Where $P$ = axial load (N), $L$ = length (m), $A$ = cross-sectional area (m²), $E$ = Young's modulus (N/m²), $V$ = volume (m³), $\sigma$ = axial stress (N/m²), $\varepsilon$ = axial strain (dimensionless), $\delta$ = elongation (m), $\sigma_{y}$ = yield stress (N/m²).

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The concept of strain energy is not just an academic abstraction — it is the cornerstone of:

- **Energy absorption design:** Automotive crumple zones, seismic dampers, and shock absorbers are designed based on strain energy capacity.
- **Spring design:** Helical, leaf, and torsion springs in vehicles, watches, and machinery.
- **Pressure vessel safety:** Burst analysis and fatigue life estimation.
- **Structural resilience assessment:** Quantifying a structure's ability to absorb energy during earthquakes without permanent damage.
- **Flywheel and composite materials:** Energy storage applications.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Strain Energy in a Prismatic Bar under Gradual Axial Load

**Problem Setup:**
Consider a prismatic bar of:
- Original length $= L$
- Uniform cross-sectional area $= A$
- Young's modulus $= E$

The bar is subjected to a gradually increasing axial tensile load $P$ applied along its centroidal axis.

---

**Step 1: Establish the relationship between load and elongation**

When an intermediate value of load $p$ is applied (where $0 \le p \le P$), the corresponding elongation $x$ is given by Hooke's Law:

$$x = \frac{p \, L}{A \, E}$$

This relationship is **linear** because the system is assumed to be perfectly elastic.

---

**Step 2: Express the incremental work done**

When the load is increased by a small amount $dp$, the elongation increases by a small amount $dx$. The incremental work $dW$ done by the external force is:

$$dW = p \, dx$$

---

**Step 3: Express the infinitesimal work in terms of $dp$ only**

Differentiating the equation from Step 1 with respect to $p$:

$$dx = \frac{L}{A E} \, dp$$

Substituting this into the expression for $dW$:

$$dW = p \cdot \frac{L}{A E} \, dp$$

---

**Step 4: Integrate to find the total work done**

The total work done as the load increases from $0$ to $P$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= \int_{0}^{P} dW = \int_{0}^{P} \frac{p \, L}{A E} \, dp \\
  &= \frac{L}{A E} \int_{0}^{P} p \, dp \\
  &= \frac{L}{A E} \left[ \frac{p^{2}}{2} \right]_{0}^{P} \\
  &= \frac{L}{A E} \cdot \frac{P^{2}}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**Step 5: Apply the energy equivalence principle**

Since the loading is **gradual** and the system is **elastic**, by the principle of conservation of energy, the external work done equals the strain energy stored:

$$U = W = \frac{P^{2} L}{2 A E}$$

This is the **fundamental expression for strain energy in uniaxial loading.**

---

**Step 6: Transform the expression into other useful forms**

**Form (a) — In terms of elongation $\delta$:**
Since $\delta = \dfrac{PL}{AE}$, we have $\dfrac{P}{AE} = \dfrac{\delta}{L}$. Substituting into the original:

$$
\begin{aligned}
U &= \frac{1}{2} P \cdot \frac{P L}{A E} = \frac{1}{2} P \, \delta
\end{aligned}
$$

**Form (b) — In terms of stress $\sigma$ and volume $V$:**
We know $\sigma = \dfrac{P}{A}$ and $V = A \cdot L$. Therefore $P = \sigma A$ and $L = \dfrac{V}{A}$. Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
U &= \frac{(\sigma A)^{2} \cdot \dfrac{V}{A}}{2 A E} = \frac{\sigma^{2} A^{2} V}{2 A^{2} E} = \frac{\sigma^{2} V}{2 E}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Form (c) — In terms of stress and strain (using $\sigma = E\varepsilon$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
U &= \frac{\sigma^{2} V}{2 E} = \frac{\sigma \cdot (E \varepsilon) \cdot V}{2 E} = \frac{1}{2} \sigma \, \varepsilon \, V
\end{aligned}
$$

**Form (d) — In terms of strain and modulus:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
U &= \frac{1}{2} \sigma \, \varepsilon \, V = \frac{1}{2} (E \varepsilon) \, \varepsilon \, V = \frac{1}{2} E \, \varepsilon^{2} \, V
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Strain Energy Density

Strain energy density $u$ is the strain energy stored per unit volume of the material. It is obtained by dividing $U$ by volume $V$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
u &= \frac{U}{V} = \frac{1}{V} \cdot \frac{\sigma^{2} V}{2 E} = \frac{\sigma^{2}}{2 E}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Geometric interpretation:** The strain energy density $u$ is numerically equal to the **area under the uniaxial stress–strain curve** up to the point of interest. For linear elastic behavior, this is a triangle of base $\varepsilon$ and height $\sigma$, giving area $\tfrac{1}{2} \sigma \varepsilon$.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Selection Guide for Problems

> [!TIP]
> When solving KTU numerical problems, use the following **decision matrix** to select the most efficient formula:

| Given Quantities | Most Efficient Formula to Use |
|---|---|
| $P, L, A, E$ | $U = \dfrac{P^{2} L}{2 A E}$ |
| $\sigma, V, E$ | $U = \dfrac{\sigma^{2} V}{2 E}$ |
| $P, \delta$ | $U = \dfrac{1}{2} P \delta$ |
| $\sigma, \varepsilon, V$ | $U = \dfrac{1}{2} \sigma \varepsilon V$ |
| $E, \varepsilon, V$ | $U = \dfrac{1}{2} E \varepsilon^{2} V$ |
| $\sigma_{y}, E$ (resilience) | $u_{r} = \dfrac{\sigma_{y}^{2}}{2 E}$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: Logical Derivation of Strain Energy in Uniaxial Loading

The following block diagram maps the **logical flow** of the strain energy derivation, illustrating how each formula is derived from the preceding one through algebraic substitution and energy equivalence.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    n1["START: Prismatic Bar of Length L, Area A, Modulus E"]
    n2["Apply Gradual Load p from 0 to P along axis"]
    n3["Hooke's Law gives intermediate elongation x = pL divided by AE"]
    n4["Incremental work done dW equals p times dx"]
    n5["Differentiate x w.r.t p: dx equals L by AE times dp"]
    n6["Substitute: dW equals pL by AE times dp"]
    n7["Integrate dW from 0 to P: W equals integral of pL by AE dp"]
    n8["Evaluate integral: W equals P squared L divided by 2AE"]
    n9["By Energy Equivalence: U equals W equals P squared L divided by 2AE"]
    n10["Substitute P equals sigmaA and L equals V by A"]
    n11["Obtain: U equals sigma squared V divided by 2E"]
    n12["Divide U by Volume V: Strain Energy Density u equals sigma squared by 2E"]
    n13["Substitute yield stress sigma_y for sigma: Modulus of Resilience u_r equals sigma_y squared by 2E"]
    n14["END: All four forms of U and u_r obtained"]

    n1 --> n2
    n2 --> n3
    n3 --> n4
    n3 --> n5
    n4 --> n6
    n5 --> n6
    n6 --> n7
    n7 --> n8
    n8 --> n9
    n9 --> n10
    n10 --> n11
    n11 --> n12
    n12 --> n13
    n13 --> n14
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture: Energy Mapping in a Loaded Bar

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nA["External Agent Applies Gradual Load P"]
    nB["Work Done W by External Force"]
    nC["Internal Strain Energy U stored in bar"]
    nD["Stress sigma equals P by A"]
    nE["Strain epsilon equals sigma by E"]
    nF["Volume V equals A times L"]
    nG["Strain Energy Density u equals U by V"]
    nH["Modulus of Resilience u_r at Elastic Limit"]

    nA -->|work input| nB
    nB -->|energy equivalence| nC
    nA -->|force per area| nD
    nD -->|Hooke's Law| nE
    nC -->|divide by V| nG
    nD --> nF
    nF --> nC
    nE --> nC
    nG -->|evaluate at yield| nH
```

> [!NOTE]
> The two diagrams above capture the **causal chain** from external load application to internal energy storage, and the **mathematical substitution chain** leading to the modulus of resilience. These are the most commonly drawn logic maps in KTU 14-mark derivation questions.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
**Define the terms: (i) Strain Energy, (ii) Strain Energy Density, and (iii) Modulus of Resilience.**

**Model Answer:**

(i) **Strain Energy ($U$):** The internal energy stored in a body within its elastic limit due to external loading. Numerically equal to the work done by the external force during gradual loading. Units: Joules (J).

(ii) **Strain Energy Density ($u$):** The strain energy stored per unit volume of the material. It is given by $u = \dfrac{\sigma^{2}}{2E}$. Units: J/m³ or N·m/m³.

(iii) **Modulus of Resilience ($u_{r}$):** The maximum strain energy density that a material can store **without permanent deformation**, i.e., the strain energy density evaluated at the elastic (yield) limit: $u_{r} = \dfrac{\sigma_{y}^{2}}{2E}$. Units: J/m³.

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**
**Distinguish between Proof Resilience and Modulus of Resilience.**

**Model Answer:**

| S.No. | Proof Resilience ($U_{r}$) | Modulus of Resilience ($u_{r}$) |
|---|---|---|
