| 1 | It is the **total** strain energy stored in a member up to the elastic limit. | It is the strain energy stored **per unit volume** of the member up to the elastic limit. |
| 2 | Units: Joules (J) or N·m | Units: J/m³ or N·m/m³ |
| 3 | Formula: $U_{r} = \dfrac{\sigma_{y}^{2} V}{2 E}$ | Formula: $u_{r} = \dfrac{\sigma_{y}^{2}}{2 E}$ |
| 4 | Depends on the **volume** of the member. | Depends only on **material properties** ($\sigma_{y}$ and $E$). |
| 5 | Indicates total energy-absorption capacity. | Indicates intrinsic material toughness (within elastic range). |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and features a **Module-Internal Choice** (attempt either Option A or Option B in full). Each option is split into two sub-parts: **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

### **Option A** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a) Derive an expression for the strain energy stored in a prismatic bar of uniform cross-section subjected to a gradually applied axial tensile load. State clearly the assumptions made. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Assumptions:**
1. The material is homogeneous, isotropic, and obeys Hooke's Law.
2. The load is applied **gradually** (quasi-statically), so kinetic effects are negligible.
3. The bar is prismatic with constant cross-section $A$ and length $L$.
4. The load acts along the centroidal axis, producing uniform axial stress.
5. The stress does not exceed the proportional limit.

**Derivation:**

Consider a prismatic bar subjected to a gradually increasing load. At an intermediate load $p$, the elongation is:

$$x = \frac{p \, L}{A E}$$

Incremental work done for an additional elongation $dx$:

$$dW = p \, dx = p \cdot \frac{L}{A E} \, dp$$

Total work done (i.e., strain energy stored) as $p$ goes from $0$ to $P$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
U &= \int_{0}^{P} \frac{p \, L}{A E} \, dp = \frac{L}{A E} \cdot \frac{P^{2}}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{U = \frac{P^{2} L}{2 A E}}$$

This is the **fundamental expression** for strain energy in uniaxial loading.

---

**(b) A steel rod of 20 mm diameter and 2 m length is subjected to an axial tensile pull of 50 kN. Calculate the strain energy stored in the rod. Take $E = 200$ GPa. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- Diameter, $d = 20 \text{ mm} = 0.020 \text{ m}$
- Length, $L = 2 \text{ m}$
- Axial load, $P = 50 \text{ kN} = 50\,000 \text{ N}$
- Young's modulus, $E = 200 \text{ GPa} = 200 \times 10^{9} \text{ N/m}^{2}$

**Step 1: Calculate the cross-sectional area**

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4} d^{2} = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.020)^{2} = 3.1416 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^{2}$$

**Step 2: Apply the strain energy formula**

$$U = \frac{P^{2} L}{2 A E}$$

**Step 3: Substitute the numerical values**

$$
\begin{aligned}
U &= \frac{(50\,000)^{2} \times 2}{2 \times (3.1416 \times 10^{-4}) \times (200 \times 10^{9})} \\
  &= \frac{(2.5 \times 10^{9}) \times 2}{(1.2566 \times 10^{8})} \\
  &= \frac{5.0 \times 10^{9}}{1.2566 \times 10^{8}} \\
  &= 39.79 \text{ J}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{U \approx 39.79 \text{ J}}$$

---

### **Option B** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

**(a) What is Modulus of Resilience? Derive the expression for strain energy density in a body subjected to uniaxial loading. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:**
The **Modulus of Resilience** is defined as the maximum strain energy density that a material can absorb **without undergoing permanent deformation**, i.e., within its elastic limit. It is a measure of the material's ability to absorb energy in the elastic region.

**Derivation:**

Consider a prismatic bar of volume $V$ subjected to a uniaxial stress $\sigma$ within the elastic limit. The strain energy stored in the bar is:

$$U = \frac{\sigma^{2} V}{2 E}$$

Dividing by the volume $V$, the strain energy density is:

$$u = \frac{U}{V} = \frac{\sigma^{2}}{2 E}$$

Using Hooke's Law ($\sigma = E \varepsilon$), this can also be written as:

$$u = \frac{1}{2} \sigma \varepsilon = \frac{1}{2} E \varepsilon^{2}$$

At the elastic (yield) limit, the modulus of resilience is:

$$u_{r} = \frac{\sigma_{y}^{2}}{2 E}$$

where $\sigma_{y}$ is the yield stress of the material.

---

**(b) A circular bar of length 1.5 m and diameter 25 mm is subjected to a tensile load of 80 kN. If $E = 210$ GPa and yield stress $\sigma_{y} = 250$ MPa, determine: (i) the strain energy stored, (ii) the strain energy density, and (iii) the modulus of resilience. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- Length, $L = 1.5 \text{ m}$
- Diameter, $d = 25 \text{ mm} = 0.025 \text{ m}$
- Tensile load, $P = 80 \text{ kN} = 80\,000 \text{ N}$
- Young's modulus, $E = 210 \text{ GPa} = 210 \times 10^{9} \text{ N/m}^{2}$
- Yield stress, $\sigma_{y} = 250 \text{ MPa} = 250 \times 10^{6} \text{ N/m}^{2}$

**Step 1: Calculate the cross-sectional area and volume**

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.025)^{2} = 4.9087 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^{2}$$

$$V = A \times L = 4.9087 \times 10^{-4} \times 1.5 = 7.3631 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^{3}$$

**Step 2: Calculate the axial stress**

$$\sigma = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{80\,000}{4.9087 \times 10^{-4}} = 162.97 \times 10^{6} \text{ N/m}^{2} = 162.97 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3 (i): Strain energy stored**

$$U = \frac{\sigma^{2} V}{2 E}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
U &= \frac{(162.97 \times 10^{6})^{2} \times (7.3631 \times 10^{-4})}{2 \times (210 \times 10^{9})} \\
  &= \frac{(2.6559 \times 10^{16}) \times (7.3631 \times 10^{-4})}{4.20 \times 10^{11}} \\
  &= \frac{1.9554 \times 10^{13}}{4.20 \times 10^{11}} \\
  &= 46.56 \text{ J}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{U \approx 46.56 \text{ J}}$$

**Step 4 (ii): Strain energy density**

$$u = \frac{U}{V} = \frac{46.56}{7.3631 \times 10^{-4}} = 63\,236 \text{ J/m}^{3}$$

$$\boxed{u \approx 63.24 \text{ kJ/m}^{3}}$$

**Step 5 (iii): Modulus of resilience**

$$u_{r} = \frac{\sigma_{y}^{2}}{2 E} = \frac{(250 \times 10^{6})^{2}}{2 \times (210 \times 10^{9})}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
u_{r} &= \frac{6.25 \times 10^{16}}{4.20 \times 10^{11}} = 1.4881 \times 10^{5} \text{ J/m}^{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{u_{r} \approx 148.81 \text{ kJ/m}^{3}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch Trap:** Students often substitute $E$ in MPa while $A$ is in mm² and $L$ in m. Always convert **all quantities to SI units (N, m, Pa)** before substitution. Mixed units yield **zero marks** in the final answer.
> 2. **"Gradual" Assumption Oversight:** The formula $U = P^{2}L/(2AE)$ is **strictly valid only for gradual (quasi-static) loading**. For sudden/dynamic loading, multiply by 2 (i.e., $U_{sudden} = P^{2}L/(AE)$). Examiners **deduct up to 2 marks** if this distinction is missed.
> 3. **Strain Energy vs. Strain Energy Density Confusion:** Examiners strictly differentiate $U$ (Joules) from $u$ (J/m³). Writing $u$ where $U$ is asked, or vice versa, results in **partial credit loss**.
> 4. **Missing Assumption in Derivations:** In 7-mark derivation questions, failing to list the **assumptions** typically costs 1–2 marks. Always state: (a) Hooke's Law validity, (b) gradual loading, (c) prismatic section, (d) uniform stress.
> 5. **Algebraic Sign Errors in Compression:** For compressive loads, the formulas remain valid, but the stress and strain carry a **negative sign**. Strain energy is always a **positive scalar quantity**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Strain Energy in Uniaxial Loading:**

- **Strain Energy ($U$):** Internal energy stored in an elastic body due to deformation. $U = \dfrac{P^{2}L}{2AE}$
- **Gradual Loading:** External work done = Strain energy stored (kinetic effects negligible).
- **Sudden Loading:** Strain energy stored = $2 \times$ gradual value (i.e., $U_{sudden} = \dfrac{P^{2}L}{AE}$).
- **Strain Energy Density ($u$):** Energy per unit volume. $u = \dfrac{\sigma^{2}}{2E} = \dfrac{1}{2}\sigma\varepsilon = \dfrac{1}{2}E\varepsilon^{2}$
- **Geometric Interpretation:** $u$ = area under the linear elastic portion of the $\sigma$-$\varepsilon$ curve.
- **Modulus of Resilience ($u_{r}$):** $u$ evaluated at the **elastic (yield) limit**. $u_{r} = \dfrac{\sigma_{y}^{2}}{2E}$. It measures a material's intrinsic capacity to absorb energy elastically.
- **Proof Resilience ($U_{r}$):** Total strain energy at the elastic limit. $U_{r} = u_{r} \cdot V = \dfrac{\sigma_{y}^{2}V}{2E}$.
- **Units Discipline:** $U$ in J, $u$ and $u_{r}$ in J/m³. Always convert to **SI base units** before calculation.
- **Material Property:** $u_{r}$ depends only on the material (via $\sigma_y$ and $E$), whereas $U_r$ depends additionally on the **volume** of the component.
- **High-Resilience Materials:** Spring steels and rubber-like elastomers exhibit high modulus of resilience, making them suitable for energy-storage applications.
- **Limitation of the Formulas:** All expressions above assume **uniaxial stress** and **linear elastic** behavior. They are **not** applicable in the plastic region or for multiaxial stress states (which require the generalized strain energy expression involving all six stress/strain components).
- **Hooke's Law Prerequisite:** $\sigma = E\varepsilon$ must hold; this implies the material is operating **below the proportional limit**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Moduli of Resilience and Toughness
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET302 - SOLID MECHANICS AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Moduli of Resilience and Toughness

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Module 2 — Moduli of Resilience and Toughness

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU-Style Definitions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Modulus of Resilience (Ur):**
> It is defined as the maximum amount of strain energy per unit volume that a material can absorb **without undergoing any permanent (plastic) deformation**. In simpler terms, it represents the capacity of a material to **store energy elastically** up to its yield point.
> - **Unit:** Joules per cubic metre (J/m³) or N·m/m³ (dimensionally equivalent to Pascal, Pa).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Modulus of Toughness (Ut):**
> It is defined as the total amount of strain energy per unit volume that a material can absorb **before it finally fractures**. It is a measure of the material's ability to withstand both elastic and plastic deformation prior to failure.
> - **Unit:** Joules per cubic metre (J/m³) or Pascal (Pa).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine you are stretching a rubber band:
- **Modulus of Resilience** → Think of a **spring**. If you pull and release within its elastic limit, it bounces back perfectly and stores energy temporarily. The energy density stored here = Resilience.
- **Modulus of Toughness** → Think of a **taffy/chewing gum**. You can stretch it a lot, deform it permanently, and it will absorb a huge amount of energy before snapping. The total energy density absorbed till snapping = Toughness.

**Engineering takeaway:**
- A material with **high resilience** (e.g., **spring steel**) is suitable for **springs, shock absorbers, and machine components** that must return to their original shape.
- A material with **high toughness** (e.g., **mild steel, copper**) is required for **structural members, bridges, gears, and crash-resistant components** that must absorb impact loads without sudden failure.

### 1.3 Graphical Interpretation from the Stress-Strain Curve

When we plot the engineering stress-strain curve of a ductile material, the areas under the curve have the following physical meaning:

| Region of Stress-Strain Curve | Strain Energy Per Unit Volume | Property |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Area under elastic portion (0 → yield point) | Resilience | Ur |
| Area under entire curve (0 → fracture point) | Toughness | Ut |
| Area under plastic portion only (yield → fracture) | Strain-hardening capacity | Ut − Ur |

> [!NOTE]
> **For a perfectly brittle material**, the stress-strain curve is essentially a straight line up to fracture. In this case, **Modulus of Resilience ≈ Modulus of Toughness**, because there is no plastic region.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Strain Energy Density

The **strain energy per unit volume** (also called strain energy density) stored in a material subjected to uniaxial stress is mathematically the area under the stress-strain diagram up to the strain of interest:

$$
U \;=\; \int_{0}^{\varepsilon} \sigma \, d\varepsilon
$$

Where:
- $U$ = Strain energy per unit volume (J/m³)
- $\sigma$ = Engineering stress at any instant (N/m²)
- $\varepsilon$ = Engineering strain at that instant

If we **upper-bound** the integral to the elastic limit strain $\varepsilon_y$, we get the Modulus of Resilience.
If we **upper-bound** the integral to the fracture strain $\varepsilon_f$, we get the Modulus of Toughness.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarises **every standard formula** that can be asked in KTU exams on this topic. Memorise this table thoroughly.

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Use |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | $U \;=\; \int \sigma \, d\varepsilon$ | General strain energy per unit volume (definition) | Any material (elastic + plastic) |
| 2 | $U_r \;=\; \dfrac{1}{2}\,\sigma_y \,\varepsilon_y$ | Modulus of Resilience (area of triangle under elastic region) | Linearly elastic materials |
| 3 | $U_r \;=\; \dfrac{\sigma_y^{\,2}}{2E}$ | Modulus of Resilience in terms of yield stress and Young's modulus | Linearly elastic materials |
| 4 | $U_r \;=\; \dfrac{1}{2}\,E\,\varepsilon_y^{\,2}$ | Modulus of Resilience in terms of yield strain and Young's modulus | Linearly elastic materials |
| 5 | $U_t \;=\; \int_{0}^{\varepsilon_f} \sigma \, d\varepsilon$ | Modulus of Toughness (total area under curve up to fracture) | Any material |
| 6 | $U_t \;=\; U_r \;+\; \int_{\varepsilon_y}^{\varepsilon_f} \sigma \, d\varepsilon$ | Decomposition: Toughness = Resilience + Plastic energy | Ductile materials |
| 7 | $W_{\text{total}} \;=\; U \times V$ | Total strain energy stored in a member of volume V | Numerical problems |

**Where:**
- $\sigma_y$ = Yield stress (N/m² or Pa)
- $\varepsilon_y$ = Strain at yield point (dimensionless)
- $E$ = Young's Modulus (N/m² or Pa)
- $\varepsilon_f$ = Strain at fracture (dimensionless)
- $V$ = Volume of the member (m³)

### 2.3 Why These Quantities Matter in Real Engineering

| Field | Why Resilience/Toughness Matters |
|:---|:---|
| **Automotive Crash Design** | Vehicle crumple zones must absorb kinetic energy = high toughness needed |
| **Spring Manufacturing** | Springs must store and release energy without permanent set = high resilience needed |
| **Aerospace Structures** | Materials must resist impact and fatigue = balanced toughness required |
| **Earthquake Engineering** | Ductile materials (high toughness) used so structures deform without collapse |
| **Pressure Vessel Design** | Must undergo small elastic strains repeatedly = high resilience helps prevent fatigue |

### 2.4 Step-by-Step Logic of Computation

**Step A:** Identify whether the material behaves **linearly elastic** up to yield (most metals in elastic region follow Hooke's law).
**Step B:** For resilience, integrate only up to $\varepsilon_y$. Use $U_r = \sigma_y^2/(2E)$.
**Step C:** For toughness, integrate up to $\varepsilon_f$. If curve is complex, use graphical method (counting squares) or trapezoidal numerical integration.
**Step D:** Convert area (in MPa·strain = MJ/m³) into standard units (J/m³).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Modulus of Resilience for a Linearly Elastic Material

We want to find the strain energy density stored in a material that is loaded **only within its elastic limit**, assuming linear elastic behaviour (Hooke's Law applicable).

**Given:**
- Hooke's Law: $\sigma = E\varepsilon$ (valid in the elastic region only)
- Yield point stress = $\sigma_y$
- Yield point strain = $\varepsilon_y$
- Young's Modulus = $E$

**Step 1:** Start from the universal definition of strain energy per unit volume.

$$
U \;=\; \int_{0}^{\varepsilon} \sigma \, d\varepsilon
$$

This represents the **area under the stress-strain curve** from zero strain up to strain $\varepsilon$.

**Step 2:** Substitute Hooke's Law ($\sigma = E\varepsilon$) into the integrand. Since $E$ is constant for a linearly elastic material, it can be taken outside the integral.

$$
U \;=\; \int_{0}^{\varepsilon_y} E\varepsilon \, d\varepsilon \;=\; E \int_{0}^{\varepsilon_y} \varepsilon \, d\varepsilon
$$

**Step 3:** Perform the integration. The integral of $\varepsilon$ with respect to $\varepsilon$ is $\varepsilon^2/2$.

$$
U \;=\; E \left[\frac{\varepsilon^{2}}{2}\right]_{0}^{\varepsilon_y} \;=\; E \left[\frac{\varepsilon_y^{2}}{2} \;-\; \frac{0^{2}}{2}\right]
$$

**Step 4:** Simplify the result.

$$
U \;=\; \frac{E\,\varepsilon_y^{2}}{2}
$$

**Step 5:** Since we are interested in the energy stored up to the **elastic limit**, this value equals the **Modulus of Resilience**.

$$
U_r \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\,E\,\varepsilon_y^{2} \quad \text{...(Equation 1)}
$$

**Step 6:** Use Hooke's Law again to eliminate strain. At yield point: $\varepsilon_y = \sigma_y / E$.

Substituting $\varepsilon_y = \sigma_y/E$ into Equation 1:

$$
U_r \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\,E\left(\frac{\sigma_y}{E}\right)^{2} \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\,E \cdot \frac{\sigma_y^{2}}{E^{2}}
$$

**Step 7:** The $E$ cancels out partially with $E^2$ in the denominator, leaving:

$$
U_r \;=\; \frac{\sigma_y^{2}}{2E} \quad \text{...(Equation 2 — Most common form)}
$$

**Step 8:** We can also derive the form involving $\sigma_y$ and $\varepsilon_y$ directly using the area of the triangle.

Since the stress-strain curve in the elastic region is a straight line, the area under it is a **triangle** with base $\varepsilon_y$ and height $\sigma_y$.

$$
\text{Area of triangle} \;=\; \frac{1}{2} \times \text{base} \times \text{height} \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\,\varepsilon_y\,\sigma_y
$$

Therefore:

$$
U_r \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\,\sigma_y\,\varepsilon_y \quad \text{...(Equation 3)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **All three equations (1), (2), and (3) for $U_r$ are mathematically equivalent** for linearly elastic materials. The choice of which to use depends on the data given in the problem.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Modulus of Toughness

Unlike resilience, the modulus of toughness does **not** have a closed-form algebraic expression in general, because the plastic region of the stress-strain curve is **non-linear** and **material-specific**.

**General Definition:**

$$
U_t \;=\; \int_{0}^{\varepsilon_f} \sigma \, d\varepsilon
$$

**Decomposition Approach:**

$$
U_t \;=\; \underbrace{\int_{0}^{\varepsilon_y} \sigma \, d\varepsilon}_{U_r \,=\, \text{Resilience}} \;+\; \underbrace{\int_{\varepsilon_y}^{\varepsilon_f} \sigma \, d\varepsilon}_{\text{Plastic Energy Absorbed}}
$$

**Method 1 — Direct Graphical Integration:**
Draw the complete stress-strain curve on graph paper. Count the number of complete and partial squares between the curve and the strain axis. Multiply by the area each square represents. This gives $U_t$.

**Method 2 — Trapezoidal Numerical Integration (for tabular data):**
If stress-strain data points $(\varepsilon_i, \sigma_i)$ are available:

$$
U_t \;\approx\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{2}\,(\sigma_i + \sigma_{i-1})\,(\varepsilon_i - \varepsilon_{i-1})
$$

**Method 3 — Idealised Curve Approximation:**
Some textbooks approximate the actual curve using a bilinear (or trilinear) idealised curve and compute the areas of resulting trapezoids and triangles.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Derivation-Style Proof)

**Problem:** A steel rod of length 1.5 m and cross-sectional area 400 mm² is subjected to an axial tensile load of 60 kN. The yield stress of steel is 250 MPa and Young's modulus is 200 GPa. Determine:
1. The strain energy stored in the rod (within elastic range).
2. The modulus of resilience of the material.
3. The total strain energy if the rod is stressed up to the elastic limit.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute stress in the rod.

$$
\sigma \;=\; \frac{P}{A} \;=\; \frac{60 \times 10^{3}\, \text{N}}{400 \times 10^{-6}\, \text{m}^{2}} \;=\; 150 \times 10^{6}\, \text{Pa} \;=\; 150\, \text{MPa}
$$

**Step 2:** Check if the rod is within elastic limit. Since $\sigma = 150$ MPa < $\sigma_y = 250$ MPa, the rod is **safely elastic**.

**Step 3:** Compute strain using Hooke's law.

$$
\varepsilon \;=\; \frac{\sigma}{E} \;=\; \frac{150 \times 10^{6}}{200 \times 10^{9}} \;=\; 7.5 \times 10^{-4}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute strain energy per unit volume (resilience at this stress).

$$
U \;=\; \frac{\sigma^{2}}{2E} \;=\; \frac{(150 \times 10^{6})^{2}}{2 \times 200 \times 10^{9}}
$$

Evaluating the numerator:

$$
(150 \times 10^{6})^{2} \;=\; 22500 \times 10^{12} \;=\; 2.25 \times 10^{16}\, \text{Pa}^{2}
$$

Now dividing:

$$
U \;=\; \frac{2.25 \times 10^{16}}{4.0 \times 10^{11}} \;=\; 5.625 \times 10^{4}\, \text{J/m}^{3} \;=\; 56.25\, \text{kJ/m}^{3}
$$

**Step 5:** Compute volume of the rod.

$$
V \;=\; A \times L \;=\; (400 \times 10^{-6}) \times 1.5 \;=\; 6.0 \times 10^{-4}\, \text{m}^{3}
$$

**Step 6:** Compute total strain energy stored.

$$
W_{\text{total}} \;=\; U \times V \;=\; 5.625 \times 10^{4} \times 6.0 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
W_{\text{total}} \;=\; 33.75\, \text{J}
$$

**Step 7:** Modulus of resilience (max energy density at yield).

$$
U_r \;=\; \frac{\sigma_y^{2}}{2E} \;=\; \frac{(250 \times 10^{6})^{2}}{2 \times 200 \times 10^{9}}
$$

$$
U_r \;=\; \frac{6.25 \times 10^{16}}{4.0 \times 10^{11}} \;=\; 1.5625 \times 10^{5}\, \text{J/m}^{3} \;=\; 156.25\, \text{kJ/m}^{3}
$$

**Step 8:** Total strain energy at elastic limit.

$$
W_{y} \;=\; U_r \times V \;=\; 1.5625 \times 10^{5} \times 6.0 \times 10^{-4} \;=\; 93.75\, \text{J}
$$

**Summary of Results:**
- Strain energy at 60 kN load = **33.75 J**
- Modulus of resilience = **156.25 kJ/m³**
- Total strain energy up to yield = **93.75 J**

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Stress-Strain Curve Showing Resilience and Toughness Zones

This block-diagram models the stress-strain curve and clearly distinguishes the regions corresponding to Modulus of Resilience and Modulus of Toughness.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    O[Origin: zero stress, zero strain] -->|Linear elastic loading| A
    A[Point A: Proportional Limit] -->|Elastic region continues| B
    B[Point B: Elastic Limit] -->|Slight non-linearity| C
    C[Point C: Upper Yield Point] -->|Drops slightly| D
    D[Point D: Lower Yield Point] -->|Plastic region begins| E
    E[Point E: Ultimate Stress Peak] -->|Necking starts| F
    F[Point F: Fracture Point]

    subgraph RESILIENCE_ZONE [AREA EQUALS MODULUS OF RESILIENCE]
        direction LR
        Z1[Triangle from O to C under the curve]
    end

    subgraph TOUGHNESS_ZONE [AREA EQUALS MODULUS OF TOUGHNESS]
        direction LR
        Z2[Entire area from O to F under the curve]
    end

    subgraph PLASTIC_ZONE [PLASTIC ENERGY ONLY]
        direction LR
        Z3[Area between C and F under the curve]
    end

    Z1 -->|Toughness minus this| Z3
    Z2 -->|Includes both elastic and plastic| Z1
```

**Interpretation of the diagram:**
- The **triangle O → C** under the curve = Modulus of Resilience $U_r$
- The **entire region O → F** under the curve = Modulus of Toughness $U_t$
- The **shaded region C → F** under the curve = Plastic energy = $U_t - U_r$

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Material Comparison: Brittle vs Ductile

This block-level architecture maps how resilience and toughness differ across material classes.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M[Material Classification] --> BR[Brittle Materials]
    M --> DU[Ductile Materials]

    BR --> BR1[Example: Cast Iron, Glass, Concrete]
    BR1 --> BR2[Stress-strain curve: nearly straight up to fracture]
    BR2 --> BR3[Ur approximately equal to Ut]
    BR3 --> BR4[Very low plastic zone]

    DU --> DU1[Example: Mild Steel, Copper, Aluminium alloys]
    DU1 --> DU2[Stress-strain curve: long plastic plateau]
    DU2 --> DU3[Ut is much greater than Ur]
    DU3 --> DU4[High plastic energy absorption]

    BR4 --> SUM[Engineering Implication: Brittle fails suddenly]
    DU4 --> SUM2[Engineering Implication: Ductile gives warning before failure]
```

---

### 4.3 Block-Level Flow — Energy Absorption Process in a Member

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Apply External Load P] --> B{Is stress below yield?}
    B -- YES --> C[Member deforms elastically]
    C --> D[Strain Energy stored equals Ur]
    D --> E[Remove Load: Member returns to original shape]
    E --> F[NO permanent deformation]
    B -- NO --> G[Plastic deformation begins]
    G --> H[Energy absorbed in plastic region equals Ut minus Ur]
    H --> I{Load still increasing?}
    I -- YES --> J[Continue absorbing energy up to ultimate point]
    J --> K{Necking or fracture starts?}
    K -- YES --> L[Final fracture occurs]
    L --> M[Total energy absorbed equals Ut]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define the terms:**
**(a)** Modulus of Resilience
**(b)** Modulus of Toughness
**State their SI units.**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Modulus of Resilience:** It is the maximum strain energy per unit volume that a material can absorb **without undergoing permanent deformation**. It is the area under the stress-strain curve from the origin up to the elastic limit.

**Unit:** $\text{J/m}^{3}$ or equivalently $\text{N·m/m}^{3}$ or Pascal (Pa).

**(b) Modulus of Toughness:** It is the total strain energy per unit volume that a material can absorb **before it fractures**. It is the total area under the complete stress-strain curve from origin to the fracture point.

**Unit:** $\text{J/m}^{3}$ or Pascal (Pa).

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Distinguish between modulus of resilience and modulus of toughness with suitable stress-strain diagrams.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Modulus of Resilience | Modulus of Toughness |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Definition | Energy density absorbed up to elastic limit | Energy density absorbed up to fracture |
| Graphical region | Area under curve from 0 to yield point | Total area under curve from 0 to fracture |
| Formula (linear elastic) | $U_r = \sigma_y^2 / (2E)$ | $U_t = \int_0^{\varepsilon_f} \sigma\, d\varepsilon$ |
| Plastic deformation involved | No | Yes |
| Typical magnitude | Lower (only elastic region) | Higher (elastic + plastic region) |
| Used for design of | Springs, elastic members | Ductile structural members, impact-resistant parts |

For a **perfectly brittle material**, both values are nearly identical, since there is no plastic region.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

## Question A (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Modified]`**

**(a)** Derive an expression for the modulus of resilience of a linearly elastic material in terms of yield stress and Young's modulus. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A mild steel component has yield stress of 320 MPa and Young's modulus 210 GPa. If the component has a volume of $2000\,\text{cm}^{3}$, calculate:
- (i) The modulus of resilience of the material.
- (ii) The maximum strain energy that the component can store without permanent deformation.
- (iii) The strain energy stored if it is subjected to a stress of 200 MPa. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)**

*Already derived in detail in Section 3.1 of these notes. Students should write it step-by-step as:*

1. Start with strain energy per unit volume definition: $U = \int \sigma\, d\varepsilon$
2. Apply Hooke's Law: $\sigma = E\varepsilon$
3. Integrate from $0$ to $\varepsilon_y$: $U = \frac{1}{2}E\varepsilon_y^2$
4. Apply boundary condition at yield: $\varepsilon_y = \sigma_y/E$
5. Substitute to obtain: $\boxed{U_r = \dfrac{\sigma_y^{2}}{2E}}$

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $\sigma_y = 320$ MPa $= 320 \times 10^{6}$ Pa
- $E = 210$ GPa $= 210 \times 10^{9}$ Pa
- $V = 2000\,\text{cm}^{3} = 2000 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}^{3} = 2 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}^{3}$

**(i) Modulus of Resilience:**

$$
U_r \;=\; \frac{\sigma_y^{2}}{2E} \;=\; \frac{(320 \times 10^{6})^{2}}{2 \times 210 \times 10^{9}}
$$

Numerator:

$$
(320 \times 10^{6})^{2} \;=\; 102400 \times 10^{12} \;=\; 1.024 \times 10^{17}\, \text{Pa}^{2}
$$

Dividing:

$$
U_r \;=\; \frac{1.024 \times 10^{17}}{4.2 \times 10^{11}} \;=\; 2.4381 \times 10^{5}\, \text{J/m}^{3} \;\approx\; 243.81\, \text{kJ/m}^{3}
$$

**(ii) Maximum strain energy at elastic limit:**

$$
W_{\max} \;=\; U_r \times V \;=\; 2.4381 \times 10^{5} \times 2 \times 10^{-3} \;=\; 487.62\, \text{J}
$$

**(iii) Strain energy at $\sigma = 200$ MPa:**

$$
U_{200} \;=\; \frac{\sigma^{2}}{2E} \;=\; \frac{(200 \times 10^{6})^{2}}{2 \times 210 \times 10^{9}} \;=\; \frac{4 \times 10^{16}}{4.2 \times 10^{11}}
$$

$$
U_{200} \;=\; 9.5238 \times 10^{4}\, \text{J/m}^{3} \;\approx\; 95.24\, \text{kJ/m}^{3}
$$

Total energy stored in the component:

$$
W_{200} \;=\; U_{200} \times V \;=\; 9.5238 \times 10^{4} \times 2 \times 10^{-3} \;=\; 190.48\, \text{J}
$$

---

## Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**(a)** Define modulus of toughness. Explain how it is determined from a stress-strain diagram. Why is toughness considered an important property for ductile materials? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In a tension test on a cylindrical steel specimen, the following data were obtained:
- Diameter of specimen = 25 mm
- Gauge length = 60 mm
- Load at yield point = 98 kN
- Ultimate load = 130 kN
- Load at fracture = 95 kN
- Final gauge length at fracture = 72 mm

Assuming the material is linearly elastic up to yield, calculate the modulus of resilience and estimate the modulus of toughness using a graphical average method (assume average stress in the plastic region = 0.85 × ultimate stress). **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — Conceptual (7 marks)**

**Definition:** Modulus of Toughness is the total strain energy per unit volume absorbed by a material up to the point of fracture.

**Determination from stress-strain diagram:**
Plot the complete engineering stress-strain curve obtained from a tension test. The modulus of toughness is the **total area enclosed between the stress-strain curve and the strain (x) axis**, from zero strain up to the strain at fracture. This area can be computed using:
- Planimeter (mechanical instrument)
- Counting squares on graph paper
- Numerical integration (Trapezoidal/Simpson's rule)

**Why toughness is important for ductile materials:**
- Ductile materials undergo large plastic deformation before fracture.
- This deformation absorbs a large amount of energy, providing **warning before failure**.
- Critical for structures subjected to impact or dynamic loads (bridges, vehicles, pressure vessels).
- Reduces risk of catastrophic brittle fracture.

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- Diameter $d = 25$ mm → Area $A = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(25)^2 = 490.87\,\text{mm}^{2}$
- Yield load $P_y = 98$ kN → $\sigma_y = \dfrac{98 \times 10^3}{490.87 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 199.65$ MPa
- Ultimate load $P_u = 130$ kN → $\sigma_u = \dfrac{130 \times 10^3}{490.87 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 264.85$ MPa
- Final length = 72 mm, Original = 60 mm
- $\varepsilon_f = \dfrac{72 - 60}{60} = 0.20$
- $\varepsilon_y = \sigma_y / E$. Assume $E = 210$ GPa (standard for steel):

$$
\varepsilon_y \;=\; \frac{199.65 \times 10^{6}}{210 \times 10^{9}} \;=\; 9.507 \times 10^{-4}
$$

**Modulus of Resilience:**

$$
U_r \;=\; \frac{\sigma_y^{2}}{2E} \;=\; \frac{(199.65 \times 10^{6})^{2}}{2 \times 210 \times 10^{9}} \;=\; \frac{3.986 \times 10^{16}}{4.2 \times 10^{11}}
$$

$$
U_r \;\approx\; 9.49 \times 10^{4}\, \text{J/m}^{3} \;=\; 94.9\, \text{kJ/m}^{3}
$$

**Modulus of Toughness (Approximate):**

Assume average plastic-region stress = $0.85 \times \sigma_u = 0.85 \times 264.85 \approx 225.12$ MPa.

Plastic strain range: $\varepsilon_f - \varepsilon_y \approx 0.20 - 0.00095 \approx 0.1991$

Plastic energy density:

$$
U_{\text{plastic}} \;\approx\; \sigma_{\text{avg,plastic}} \times (\varepsilon_f - \varepsilon_y) \;=\; 225.12 \times 10^{6} \times 0.1991
$$

$$
U_{\text{plastic}} \;\approx\; 4.481 \times 10^{7}\, \text{J/m}^{3} \;\approx\; 44.81\, \text{MJ/m}^{3}
$$

Adding elastic portion:

$$
U_t \;\approx\; U_r + U_{\text{plastic}} \;\approx\; 9.49 \times 10^{4} + 4.481 \times 10^{7} \;\approx\; 4.490 \times 10^{7}\, \text{J/m}^{3}
$$

$$
\boxed{U_t \;\approx\; 44.9\, \text{MJ/m}^{3}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Blunder (MOST COMMON MARK-LOSS):** Students often forget to convert MPa to Pa or mm² to m². Always convert **all units to SI base** (Pa, m², m³, J) before calculation. A 1000× error will cost full marks.
> 2. **Formula Mix-up:** Using $U_r$ formula for toughness problems or vice versa. Note: $U_r = \sigma_y^2/(2E)$ works **only** for linearly elastic materials up to yield. For toughness, the formula is an **integral**, not a closed expression in general.
> 3. **Resilience vs Toughness Confusion:** Resilience is **elastic** energy only; toughness is **total** energy (elastic + plastic).
> 4. **Missing $V$ in total energy:** Strain energy density ($U$) and total strain energy ($W = U \times V$) are different — read the question carefully.
> 5. **Brittle Material Trap:** For brittle materials, since there is no yielding, $U_r \approx U_t$. Don't apply plasticity-related formulas blindly.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorise Before Exam:**

- [x] **Modulus of Resilience** = Strain energy per unit volume up to **elastic limit** only.
- [x] **Modulus of Toughness** = Total strain energy per unit volume up to **fracture**.
- [x] **Master Formula (Resilience):** $\;U_r = \dfrac{\sigma_y^{2}}{2E} = \dfrac{1}{2}\sigma_y\varepsilon_y = \dfrac{1}{2}E\varepsilon_y^{2}$
- [x] **Master Formula (Toughness):** $\;U_t = \displaystyle\int_{0}^{\varepsilon_f} \sigma \, d\varepsilon$ (no simple closed form for non-linear curves).
- [x] **Graphical Region:** $U_r$ = triangle area; $U_t$ = entire area under stress-strain curve.
- [x] **Units:** J/m³ = Pa = N/m². Always report answers in **SI units**.
- [x] **Total strain energy:** $W = U \times V$. Multiply by volume to get total energy in Joules.
- [x] **Ductile materials:** $U_t \gg U_r$ (because of plastic zone).
- [x] **Brittle materials:** $U_t \approx U_r$ (no plastic zone).
- [x] **Real-world pairing:** Springs → High $U_r$; Crash zones → High $U_t$.
- [x] **Decomposition identity:** $U_t = U_r + U_{\text{plastic}}$.
- [x] **For a perfectly elastic (Hookean) material** up to fracture: $U_t = U_r = \sigma_f^{2}/(2E)$.
- [x] **Numerical/graphical methods** for toughness: Trapezoidal rule, Simpson's rule, counting squares, planimeter.
- [x] **Always verify** whether the applied stress is below yield before applying $U_r = \sigma_y^2/(2E)$ to a specific stress value.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Shear Force and Bending Moment Diagrams - Sign Convention
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET302 - SOLID MECHANICS AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Shear Force and Bending Moment Diagrams - Sign Convention

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Shear Force (V)

**Shear Force (V)** at any cross-section of a beam is defined as the *algebraic sum of the vertical forces* (transverse loads and reaction components) acting on **either side** of the section. It represents the internal resisting force parallel to the cross-section that prevents the two halves of the beam from sliding vertically relative to each other.

### 1.2 Formal Definition of Bending Moment (M)

**Bending Moment (M)** at any cross-section is defined as the *algebraic sum of the moments* (about the section) of all the forces and couples acting on either side of the section. It represents the internal resisting couple that prevents relative rotation (bending) between the two halves of the beam.

### 1.3 The Need for a Sign Convention

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU 2024 Scheme examinations, drawing an SFD/BMD with the **wrong sign convention** results in an **automatic loss of 4 to 6 marks** out of 14, even if the magnitudes are computed correctly. The university strictly follows the *Beam Sign Convention* (also called the *New Convention*).

A **Sign Convention** is a standardized set of rules that assigns a positive or negative algebraic sign to internal forces and moments. Without a universal sign rule, every textbook, designer, and examiner would interpret "positive" differently, leading to ambiguous diagrams. KTU follows the **IIT/KTU Standard Beam Sign Convention**.

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The "Snapped Bamboo" Intuition

Imagine a long, freshly cut bamboo stick held horizontally on two supports. If you push the **left half upward** with your palm while pushing the **right half downward**, the bamboo wants to *shear* (slide vertically) at any imaginary vertical cut.

- The internal resistance to this vertical sliding is called **Shear Force**. By convention, this particular loading direction is called **POSITIVE shear**.

Now imagine sitting exactly in the middle of the bamboo. The bamboo sags down, making a "U" shape (like a smile 🙂). The top fibers stretch (tension) and the bottom fibers compress.

- The internal moment that causes this **sagging (smile-shaped) curvature** is called **POSITIVE Bending Moment**.

If instead the bamboo curves the other way — like a frown 🙁 (hogging) — the moment is **NEGATIVE**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Trick (KTU Style):**
> - Sagging = **Smile** = Positive Bending Moment (Tension at bottom)
> - Hogging = **Frown** = Negative Bending Moment (Tension at top)

### 1.5 Standard Physical Constants / Metrics (Bold)

- **Unit of Shear Force:** **Newton (N)** in SI, or **kN** for typical beams
- **Unit of Bending Moment:** **Newton-metre (N·m)** in SI, or **kN·m**
- **Plotting Convention:** Shear Force on **Y-axis vs. Beam Length (X-axis)** → SFD
- **Plotting Convention:** Bending Moment on **Y-axis vs. Beam Length (X-axis)** → BMD
- **Standard Direction:** SFD/BMD are drawn **below the beam** in KTU board exam answer sheets for clarity.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Accepted Sign Conventions — KTU Board Verdict

| Convention | Also Known As | Used By |
|---|---|---|
| **Beam (New) Convention** | Engineering Convention | **KTU, IIT, NPTEL, GATE, IS 800** |
| Cantilever (Old) Convention | Classical Convention | Some legacy textbooks |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme strictly mandates the BEAM (NEW) CONVENTION.** This is non-negotiable in board examinations.

### 2.2 Sign Convention Rules (Beam Convention) — The Master Table

| Internal Effect | Positive ( + ) | Negative ( − ) |
|---|---|---|
| **Shear Force (V)** | Left portion pushed **UP** OR Right portion pushed **DOWN** | Left portion pushed **DOWN** OR Right portion pushed **UP** |
| **Bending Moment (M)** | Causes **SAGGING** (concave up, smile 🙂) — tension at **bottom** fibre | Causes **HOGGING** (concave down, frown 🙁) — tension at **top** fibre |

### 2.3 Mathematical Definition (Left-to-Right Reading — LTR)

When we consider the **left portion** of the section as the free body:

$$
V(x) = \sum (\text{Upward forces on left}) - \sum (\text{Downward forces on left})
$$

$$
M(x) = \sum (\text{Clockwise moments about section from left loads}) - \sum (\text{Anticlockwise moments about section from left loads})
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Clockwise moments on the left of the section produce POSITIVE Bending Moment.** This single statement answers 80% of KTU questions on sign convention.

### 2.4 Mathematical Definition (Right-to-Left Reading — RTL)

When we consider the **right portion** of the section as the free body:

$$
V(x) = \sum (\text{Downward forces on right}) - \sum (\text{Upward forces on right})
$$

$$
M(x) = \sum (\text{Anticlockwise moments about section from right loads}) - \sum (\text{Clockwise moments about section from right loads})
$$

### 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V = \dfrac{dM}{dx}$ | Slope of BMD = Shear Force | N or kN |
| 2 | $w = \dfrac{dV}{dx} = \dfrac{d^{2}M}{dx^{2}}$ | Slope of SFD = Load intensity | N/m or kN/m |
| 3 | $\Delta M = \int V \, dx$ | Change in BM = Area under SFD | N·m |
| 4 | $\Delta V = \int w \, dx$ | Change in SF = Area under load diagram | N |
| 5 | At a **point load P** | SFD has a **jump of magnitude P** | — |
| 6 | At a **couple C₀** | BMD has a **jump of magnitude C₀** | — |
| 7 | At free end with no load | **V = 0, M = 0** | — |
| 8 | At simply supported end | **M = 0** (pin/roller) | — |
| 9 | At fixed end | V = Reaction; M = Fixing moment | — |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The sign convention directly determines:
- **Reinforcement placement in RCC beams:** Positive BM → tension steel at bottom; Negative BM → tension steel at top.
- **Steel beam flanges:** Top flange in compression, bottom in tension for sagging regions.
- **Bridge deck design:** Continuous bridges have alternating sagging/hogging zones — the sign convention distinguishes these regions for shear connectors and tendon profiles in prestressed concrete.
- **Aerospace wing spars:** Bending moment sign dictates which skin is loaded in tension (critical for fatigue).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Shear Force at a Section

Consider a simply supported beam AB of length **L** carrying a point load **P** at midspan. We need to find shear force at a section X-X located at distance **x** from support A (where $x < L/2$).

**Step 1 — Calculate Reactions**

By symmetry of the beam (load P is at center):

$$
R_A = R_B = \frac{P}{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Make an Imaginary Cut at Distance x from A**

We cut the beam vertically at section X-X, creating a left free body (length x) and a right free body (length L − x).

**Step 3 — Analyze the Left Free Body**

Forces acting on the left free body:
- Reaction $R_A = P/2$ acting **upward** at A
- No other load in this region (since $x < L/2$)

For equilibrium of the left free body (vertical forces must sum to zero):

$$
R_A - V(x) = 0
$$

where $V(x)$ is the internal shear force acting **downward** on the right face of the left free body (by Newton's 3rd law, the beam material on the right pushes down on the left segment at the cut).

Solving:

$$
V(x) = R_A = +\frac{P}{2}
$$

> **Sign Logic:** Upward force $R_A$ on the **left** portion → **POSITIVE** (per rule: left pushed up = +ve).

**Step 4 — Verify Using the Right Free Body**

Forces on the right free body (length L − x):
- Reaction $R_B = P/2$ acting **upward** at B
- Point load P acting **downward** at midspan

Net vertical force on the right portion (taking up as positive):

$$
R_B - P = \frac{P}{2} - P = -\frac{P}{2}
$$

The shear force on the left face of the right free body is the negative of the left free body's shear (Newton's 3rd law):

$$
V(x) = -(-\frac{P}{2}) = +\frac{P}{2} \quad \checkmark
$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Bending Moment at the Same Section

**Step 1 — Take Moments of Left Free Body About Section X-X**

The reaction $R_A$ acts at distance x from the section, creating a **clockwise moment** about X-X:

$$
M_{R_A} = R_A \cdot x = \frac{P}{2} \cdot x
$$

No other forces/couples exist on the left free body, so for rotational equilibrium:

$$
\frac{P}{2} \cdot x - M(x) = 0
$$

where $M(x)$ is the internal moment acting **counter-clockwise** on the right face of the left free body (resisting the clockwise external moment).

Solving:

$$
M(x) = +\frac{Px}{2}
$$

> **Sign Logic:** Clockwise moment on the **left** of the section → **POSITIVE Bending Moment** (sagging).

**Step 2 — Verify Using Right Free Body**

Forces on the right free body (from midspan to B, length $L/2 - x$):
- $R_B = P/2$ at B, distance from section = $L - x$
- $P$ downward at midspan, distance from section = $L/2 - x$

Taking moments about X-X (counter-clockwise positive):

$$
M_{R_B} = \frac{P}{2}(L-x) \quad (\text{counter-clockwise about X-X})
$$

$$
M_P = P \cdot \left(\frac{L}{2} - x\right) \quad (\text{clockwise about X-X})
$$

Net anticlockwise moment:

$$
\frac{P}{2}(L-x) - P\left(\frac{L}{2} - x\right) = \frac{PL}{2} - \frac{Px}{2} - \frac{PL}{2} + Px = +\frac{Px}{2} \quad \checkmark
$$

### 3.3 Derivation: Maximum Bending Moment Formula

For this simply supported beam with central point load, maximum moment occurs at midspan ($x = L/2$):

$$
M_{max} = \frac{P \cdot (L/2)}{2} = \frac{PL}{4}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Favourite Result:** $M_{max} = \dfrac{PL}{4}$ for a simply supported beam with a central point load.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Sign Convention Decision Flowchart Logic

| Loading Situation | Effect on Left Free Body | Sign of V | Effect of Moment on Left | Sign of M |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Upward force on left (reaction) | Pushes left up | + | Creates clockwise moment | + |
| Downward point load on left | Pushes left down | − | Creates counter-clockwise moment | − |
| Upward UDL on left | Pushes left up | + | Creates clockwise moment | + |
| Couple (anticlockwise) on left | — | 0 | Direct addition | − |
| Couple (clockwise) on left | — | 0 | Direct addition | + |

### 3.5 Derivation: Slope Relationship $V = dM/dx$

Consider an infinitesimal beam element of length **dx** subjected to:
- Shear force $V$ on the left face (downward on the right face of left element)
- Shear force $V + dV$ on the right face (downward)
- Distributed load $w(x) \cdot dx$ acting downward
- Moments $M$ and $M + dM$ on left and right faces

Vertical equilibrium of the element:

$$
V - (V + dV) - w \cdot dx = 0
$$

Simplifying:

$$
-dV = w \cdot dx \implies \frac{dV}{dx} = -w
$$

(Note: Some textbooks define w as positive upward, flipping the sign.)

Rotational equilibrium about the right face (taking counter-clockwise positive):

$$
M - (M + dM) + V \cdot dx - w \cdot dx \cdot \frac{dx}{2} = 0
$$

The term $w \cdot dx \cdot dx/2$ is a second-order differential and is neglected for small dx:

$$
-dM + V \cdot dx = 0
$$

$$
\boxed{V = \frac{dM}{dx}}
$$

Combining both results:

$$
w = \frac{dV}{dx} = \frac{d^{2}M}{dx^{2}}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Sign Convention Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start: Cut beam at section X-X]
    startB[Choose left OR right free body]
    rule1{Force on LEFT pushing UP?}
    rule2{Force on LEFT pushing DOWN?}
    rule3{Force on RIGHT pushing DOWN?}
    rule4{Force on RIGHT pushing UP?}
    
    s1[Shear Force = POSITIVE]
    s2[Shear Force = NEGATIVE]
    s3[Shear Force = POSITIVE]
    s4[Shear Force = NEGATIVE]
    
    endA[Plot SFD at section X-X]
    
    startA --> startB
    startB --> rule1
    rule1 -->|Yes| s1
    rule1 -->|No| rule2
    rule2 -->|Yes| s2
    startB --> rule3
    rule3 -->|Yes| s3
    rule3 -->|No| rule4
    rule4 -->|Yes| s4
    
    s1 --> endA
    s2 --> endA
    s3 --> endA
    s4 --> endA
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Sign Convention Architecture (Block-Level Functional Mapping)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input: External Load System]
        L1[Vertical Forces]
        L2[Couples Moments]
        L3[UDL or UVL]
    end
    
    subgraph ANALYSIS[Analysis Engine]
        C1[Cut Section X-X]
        C2[Choose Free Body: Left or Right]
        C3[Apply Sign Rules]
    end
    
    subgraph OUTPUT[Output: Internal Force State]
        O1[Shear Force V with sign]
        O2[Bending Moment M with sign]
        O3[Sagging vs Hogging identification]
    end
    
    subgraph PLOT[Diagrammatic Output]
        P1[SFD]
        P2[BMD]
    end
    
    L1 --> C1
    L2 --> C1
    L3 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
    C3 --> O1
    C3 --> O2
    C3 --> O3
    O1 --> P1
    O2 --> P2
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Visual Mapping — Sagging vs Hogging

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Beam Cross Section with Internal Forces]
    
    A --> P[POSITIVE M: Sagging - Smile]
    A --> N[NEGATIVE M: Hogging - Frown]
    
    P --> P1[Beam shape: Concave UP]
    P --> P2[Top fiber: COMPRESSION]
    P --> P3[Bottom fiber: TENSION]
    P --> P4[RCC steel placement: BOTTOM]
    
    N --> N1[Beam shape: Concave DOWN]
    N --> N2[Top fiber: TENSION]
    N --> N3[Bottom fiber: COMPRESSION]
    N --> N4[RCC steel placement: TOP]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Point Load vs Couple Effect on Diagrams

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PL[Effect of Point Load P]
        SFD_P[SFD: Jump of magnitude P]
        BMD_P[BMD: Kinks - slope changes by P]
    end
    
    subgraph CL[Effect of Applied Couple C0]
        SFD_C[SFD: No change - zero jump]
        BMD_C[BMD: Vertical jump of magnitude C0]
    end
    
    inputP[Point Load P] --> PL
    inputC[Couple C0] --> CL
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visualization Tip for KTU Exams:** When drawing SFD, the diagram always lies on the **same side as the resultant of the left-side vertical forces**. The SFD "lives" above the beam for upward-left forces and below for downward-left forces.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*State and explain the sign convention for shear force as per the KTU/standard beam convention used in engineering mechanics.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

According to the standard **beam (new) sign convention** followed by KTU:

> The shear force at a section is considered **POSITIVE** if the resultant of the vertical forces (transverse forces and reactions) acting on the **left portion** of the beam tends to push the left part **UPWARD** (or equivalently, the right part **DOWNWARD**). It is **NEGATIVE** if the left portion is pushed **DOWNWARD** (or right portion pushed upward).

**Diagram for clarity:** Draw a small element showing an upward arrow on the left face and downward arrow on the right face with a "+V" label. (1 mark for definition, 1 mark for left-pushed-up rule, 1 mark for diagram.)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Define positive bending moment. How does it differ from a negative bending moment in terms of deformation?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> A **Positive Bending Moment** at a section causes the beam to **sag** at that location (concavity facing upward, resembling a "smile" shape 🙂). This produces **tension in the bottom fibres** and **compression in the top fibres**.

> A **Negative Bending Moment** causes the beam to **hog** (concavity facing downward, resembling a "frown" shape 🙁), producing **tension in the top fibres** and **compression in the bottom fibres**.

In RCC design, positive BM requires main tensile steel at the **bottom**, while negative BM requires main tensile steel at the **top**. (1 mark definition, 1 mark deformation, 1 mark fibre stress / design implication.)

---

### Part B Question A (14 Marks)

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]**
**(a)** A simply supported beam AB of span **6 m** carries a uniformly distributed load (UDL) of **20 kN/m** over its entire span. Draw the Shear Force Diagram (SFD) and Bending Moment Diagram (BMD) using the standard KTU sign convention. Mark all critical values. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the same beam in part (a), determine the position and magnitude of **maximum bending moment**. Also state the relationship $V = dM/dx$ and verify it at $x = 3$ m from support A. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Calculate Reactions**

Total load = $20 \times 6 = 120$ kN

By symmetry:

$$
R_A = R_B = \frac{120}{2} = 60 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 2: Shear Force at Distance x from A (using left free body)**

For a UDL of intensity $w$ over the left portion of length $x$:

$$
V(x) = R_A - w \cdot x = 60 - 20x
$$

At $x = 0$ (point A): $V = +60$ kN
At $x = 6$ m (point B): $V = 60 - 120 = -60$ kN

**Step 3: Bending Moment at Distance x from A**

$$
M(x) = R_A \cdot x - w \cdot x \cdot \frac{x}{2} = 60x - 10x^2
$$

At $x = 0$: $M = 0$ ✓
At $x = 6$ m: $M = 360 - 360 = 0$ ✓

**Step 4: SFD and BMD Sketches**

SFD: Linear straight line from +60 kN at A to −60 kN at B, crossing zero at $x = 3$ m.
BMD: Parabolic curve (since $w$ is constant, BMD is a second-degree parabola), starting at 0, rising to maximum, returning to 0 at B.

Maximum BM occurs where $V = 0$, i.e., at $x = 3$ m.

(Allocate: 2 marks reactions, 2 marks V(x) expression and values, 2 marks M(x) expression, 1 mark SFD/BMD sketch.)

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Locate Maximum BM**

Set $V(x) = 0$:

$$
60 - 20x = 0 \implies x = 3 \text{ m (midspan)}
$$

**Step 2: Magnitude of Maximum BM**

$$
M_{max} = M(3) = 60(3) - 10(3)^2 = 180 - 90 = +90 \text{ kN·m}
$$

**Step 3: State the Relationship**

From strength of materials derivation:

$$
V = \frac{dM}{dx}
$$

Differentiating $M(x) = 60x - 10x^2$:

$$
\frac{dM}{dx} = 60 - 20x = V(x) \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 4: Verify at $x = 3$ m**

$$
\left.\frac{dM}{dx}\right|_{x=3} = 60 - 20(3) = 60 - 60 = 0
$$

Since $V(3) = 0$, the verification is complete. This is consistent with the fact that **maximum moment occurs where shear force is zero** (slope of BMD is zero at the peak).

(Allocate: 1 mark location, 2 marks M_max calculation, 2 marks derivative verification, 2 marks slope interpretation.)

---

### Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]**
**(a)** A cantilever beam of length **4 m** is fixed at A and free at B. It carries a point load of **30 kN** at the free end B. Using the **KTU standard beam sign convention**, draw SFD and BMD. Mark the values at the fixed end. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A simply supported beam of span **8 m** carries a couple of **40 kN·m** (clockwise) at a distance of **3 m from the left support A**. Find and draw SFD and BMD. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Calculate Reactions at Fixed End A**

Vertical reaction (upward):

$$
R_A = 30 \text{ kN}
$$

Fixing moment (counter-clockwise to balance clockwise load moment):

$$
M_A = 30 \times 4 = 120 \text{ kN·m (anticlockwise / hogging)}
$$

**Step 2: Shear Force at Distance x from B (using right free body — easier for cantilever)**

The right free body (length $4 - x$) has only the 30 kN downward load at B:

$$
V(x) = -(30) = -30 \text{ kN (constant)}
$$

(Using right free body rule: downward force on right = negative shear.)

**Step 3: Bending Moment at Distance x from B**

Moment of 30 kN load about section X-X (distance x from B):

The 30 kN load creates a **counter-clockwise** moment on the right free body about X-X:

$$
M(x) = -(30) \cdot x = -30x \text{ kN·m}
$$

At free end B ($x = 0$): $M = 0$ ✓
At fixed end A ($x = 4$ m): $M = -30 \times 4 = -120$ kN·m

**Step 4: SFD and BMD Sketches**

- **SFD:** Constant horizontal line at $-30$ kN throughout.
- **BMD:** Straight line varying from $0$ at B to $-120$ kN·m at A. (Negative throughout → entire beam is in **HOGGING**, typical of cantilevers.)

(Allocate: 2 marks reactions, 2 marks V(x), 2 marks M(x), 1 mark diagram.)

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Calculate Reactions**

Sum of vertical forces:

$$
R_A + R_B = 0 \text{ (no vertical loads, only a couple)}
$$

Sum of moments about A (counter-clockwise positive):

$$
R_B \cdot 8 + 40 = 0 \implies R_B = -5 \text{ kN (i.e., 5 kN downward)}
$$

Therefore:

$$
R_A = +5 \text{ kN (upward)}
$$

**Step 2: Shear Force**

For $0 < x < 3$ m (between A and the couple, using left free body):

$$
V(x) = R_A = +5 \text{ kN}
$$

For $3 < x < 8$ m (between the couple and B):

$$
V(x) = R_A = +5 \text{ kN (couples do not affect shear)}
$$

**Step 3: Bending Moment**

For $0 < x < 3$ m:

$$
M(x) = R_A \cdot x = 5x
$$

At $x = 0$: $M = 0$ ✓
At $x = 3^-$: $M = 15$ kN·m

At the location of the couple ($x = 3$ m), there is a **jump of 40 kN·m (downward)** in the BMD:

$$
M(3^+) = 15 - 40 = -25 \text{ kN·m}
$$

For $3 < x < 8$ m:

$$
M(x) = 5x - 40
$$

At $x = 8$ m: $M = 40 - 40 = 0$ ✓

**Step 4: SFD and BMD Sketches**

- **SFD:** Constant rectangular block at $+5$ kN throughout.
- **BMD:** Linear line from 0 at A, rising to $+15$ kN·m at $x = 3^-$; sudden drop of 40 kN·m to $-25$ kN·m at $x = 3^+$; linear back to 0 at B.

(Allocate: 2 marks reactions, 2 marks shear, 2 marks moment with jump, 1 mark diagram.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the jump in BMD at a couple location** — students treat the couple as if it were a point load and get continuous BMD. Deduct 3–4 marks.
> 2. **Using the wrong sign convention** — if a student uses the cantilever (old) convention, the entire SFD/BMD will be sign-flipped, and **6 out of 14 marks** are forfeited even if magnitudes are correct.
> 3. **Not verifying M = 0 at simple supports** — at pin/roller ends, M MUST equal zero. If your calculated M is non-zero, a calculation error has occurred.
> 4. **Forgetting to label units (kN, kN·m)** on the diagrams — KTU deducts 1 mark.
> 5. **Conflating the load direction sign with the shear sign** — an upward reaction is positive regardless of whether it is $R_A$ or $R_B$. The sign comes from the FREE BODY side, not the force's location on the beam.
> 6. **Plotting BMD on the wrong side** — KTU accepts SFD/BMD drawn below the beam (standard practice). Plotting on top without shading is acceptable but marking ordinates is mandatory.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Sign Convention followed by KTU:** Beam (New) Convention — left-up = +V; clockwise-on-left = +M.
- **Sagging = Smile = +M = Tension at bottom.**
- **Hogging = Frown = −M = Tension at top.**
- **Point load → vertical jump in SFD; couple → vertical jump in BMD.**
- **$V = dM/dx$** → shear is the slope of the bending moment diagram.
- **$w = dV/dx$** → load intensity is the slope of the SFD.
- **$M_{max}$ occurs where $V = 0$** (or where V changes sign).
- **Simply supported end:** $M = 0$ (always).
- **Free end with no load:** $V = 0$ and $M = 0$ (always).
- **Fixed end:** V = reaction; M = fixing moment (anticlockwise reaction moment for downward loads).
- **UDL → SFD is linear; BMD is parabolic (2nd degree).**
- **UVL (triangular load) → SFD is parabolic (2nd degree); BMD is cubic (3rd degree).**
- **Always check:** at $x = 0$ and $x = L$ for boundary consistency — if not satisfied, recompute.
- **Units to use in KTU exams:** kN for forces, kN·m for moments, kN/m for UDL.
- **Draw diagrams neatly** with proper hatching for UDL, arrows for point loads, and clearly mark ordinates at all critical sections.
- **Self-check formula for simply supported beam with central point load P over span L:** $V_{max} = \pm P/2$; $M_{max} = PL/4$.
- **Self-check formula for simply supported beam with UDL w over span L:** $V_{max} = \pm wL/2$; $M_{max} = wL^2/8$.
- **Self-check formula for cantilever with point load P at free end, span L:** $V = -P$ (constant); $M_{fixed} = -PL$ (hogging).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Relationship Between Load, Shear Force and Bending Moment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET302 - SOLID MECHANICS AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Relationship Between Load, Shear Force and Bending Moment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In Strength of Materials, the **Relationship Between Load, Shear Force, and Bending Moment** constitutes the **First Fundamental Theorem of Beam Theory**. It establishes the differential calculus linkage governing how an applied transverse load (force per unit length) on a beam generates the internal shear force ($V$) and bending moment ($M$) distributions along its length.

For a beam subjected to a distributed load $w(x)$ (measured in **N/m** or **kN/m**), the three governing relationships using the standard KTU sign convention are:

$$\frac{dV}{dx} = -w(x)$$

$$\frac{dM}{dx} = V(x)$$

$$\frac{d^{2}M}{dx^{2}} = -w(x)$$

where $x$ is measured along the neutral axis of the beam from a chosen origin.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This topic is a **core analytical foundation** for Module 2. Questions frequently appear as 3-mark derivations (Part A) and as integrated 14-mark problems where you must find equations for $V$ and $M$ given a loading function.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a long, flexible wooden plank (a beam) resting on two stools. When you place a heavy bag (a point load) at the center:

- The **plank bends** (this bending is quantified by the **Bending Moment, $M$**).
- The wood fibers on either side of the cut-section **slide** vertically relative to each other (this sliding tendency is quantified by the **Shear Force, $V$**).
- The **weight of the bag** is the **Load**, $w$.

If you were to "cut" the plank with an imaginary vertical saw at any location and look at the left half, you would find:
- A vertical force is needed to keep that half in equilibrium → this is the **Shear Force**.
- A rotational couple (moment) is needed to keep it from rotating → this is the **Bending Moment**.

The relationship tells us that the **rate of change of shear** along the beam equals the negative of the load intensity. In plain words: where the load is heavier, the shear force changes more rapidly.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Intuition Box:**
> - **No load region** ($w = 0$): Shear force is **constant** (a horizontal line).
> - **Uniform load** ($w = $ constant): Shear force varies **linearly**.
> - **Linear load** ($w$ is a linear function of $x$): Shear force varies **parabolically**.
> - In every case, the **slope of the Bending Moment diagram equals the Shear Force**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Breakdown — The Logic Chain

To derive these relationships, consider an elemental slice of a beam of length $dx$ located at a distance $x$ from the origin. The elemental segment is in **static equilibrium** under the action of the distributed load and the internal forces at its two faces.

**Step 1 — Element Selection:**
- Cut a small element of length $dx$ from the beam at coordinate $x$.
- Let the left face of this element carry shear $V$ and moment $M$.
- The right face carries shear $V + dV$ and moment $M + dM$ (these differ by infinitesimals because of the load).
- The distributed load $w$ acts downward (positive convention) over the length $dx$, producing a total downward force $w \cdot dx$.

**Step 2 — Vertical Force Equilibrium ($\Sigma F_y = 0$):**
- Sum of vertical forces = $V - (V + dV) - w \cdot dx = 0$.
- This simplifies to: $-dV - w \cdot dx = 0$, hence $\dfrac{dV}{dx} = -w$.

**Step 3 — Moment Equilibrium about the Right Face ($\Sigma M = 0$):**
- The moment of $V$ about the right face = $V \cdot dx$.
- The moment of the distributed load $w \cdot dx$ about the right face = $w \cdot dx \cdot \dfrac{dx}{2}$ (a second-order infinitesimal, negligible).
- Equilibrium gives: $M + dM - M - V \cdot dx = 0$.
- Hence $\dfrac{dM}{dx} = V$.

**Step 4 — Combining the Two:**
- Differentiating $\dfrac{dM}{dx} = V$ with respect to $x$: $\dfrac{d^2M}{dx^2} = \dfrac{dV}{dx}$.
- Substituting $\dfrac{dV}{dx} = -w$ yields: $\dfrac{d^2M}{dx^2} = -w$.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Relationship

- **Why $\dfrac{dV}{dx} = -w$?** Because shear force must "absorb" or "balance" the cumulative load. Wherever load is added (positive $w$ downward), the shear decreases as we move rightward.
- **Why $\dfrac{dM}{dx} = V$?** Because the bending moment at any section is essentially the integral (area under the curve) of all the shear forces to its left. The rate at which $M$ accumulates equals the current value of $V$.
- **Why $\dfrac{d^2M}{dx^2} = -w$?** Because it combines both — the concavity (curvature) of the bending moment diagram is dictated directly by the load intensity. This is extremely powerful: **knowing $w(x)$ alone lets you sketch the $M$ diagram qualitatively without integration.**

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Relationship | Differential Form | Integral Form | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Load to Shear | $\dfrac{dV}{dx} = -w$ | $V_2 - V_1 = -\int_{x_1}^{x_2} w \, dx$ | Slope of SFD = $-w$ |
| 2 | Shear to Moment | $\dfrac{dM}{dx} = V$ | $M_2 - M_1 = \int_{x_1}^{x_2} V \, dx$ | Slope of BMD = $V$ |
| 3 | Load to Moment | $\dfrac{d^{2}M}{dx^{2}} = -w$ | $M_2 - M_1 = -\int_{x_1}^{x_2}\!\!\int w \, dx \, dx$ | Concavity of BMD = $-w$ |
| 4 | Local Maxima/Minima | $\dfrac{dM}{dx} = 0 \Rightarrow V = 0$ | — | Point of contraflexure / maxima |
| 5 | Point Load $P$ | $\Delta V = -P$ (downward) | — | Sudden jump in SFD |
| 6 | Couple $C$ applied | $\Delta M = -C$ | — | Sudden jump in BMD |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign Convention Used (KTU Standard):**
> - Downward distributed load $w$ is **positive**.
> - Shear force $V$ causing **clockwise rotation** of the element is **positive**.
> - Bending moment $M$ causing **concave-up (sagging)** deformation is **positive**.
> - Always mark the **origin ($x = 0$)** clearly in derivations to avoid integration constant errors.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Civil Engineering:** Quick estimation of maximum bending moment in a bridge girder under wheel loads (UDL approximations) without solving equilibrium equations.
- **Mechanical Engineering:** Design of crane jibs, machine tool beds, and pressure vessel nozzles.
- **Aerospace Engineering:** Wing spar analysis where distributed aerodynamic loads are integrated to find critical bending stress sections.
- **Quick Verification Tool:** During KTU exams, you can **cross-check** your computed $V$ and $M$ diagrams by verifying that (a) the SFD slope equals $-w$ at every point, and (b) the BMD slope equals $V$.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Governing Relationships

Consider a simply supported beam of length $L$. We isolate an infinitesimal element of length $dx$ at a distance $x$ from the left support $A$.

**Visual Setup of the Element:**

- Left face: Internal shear force $V$ acting **upward**, internal bending moment $M$ acting **counter-clockwise** (positive convention).
- Right face: Internal shear force $V + dV$ acting **downward**, internal bending moment $M + dM$ acting **clockwise**.
- Distributed load $w$ (intensity, units: N/m) acting **downward** across the element.

#### Derivation A — Vertical Force Equilibrium ($\Sigma F_y = 0$)

Taking upward as positive:

$$V - (V + dV) - w \, dx = 0$$

Expand and cancel $V$:

$$V - V - dV - w \, dx = 0$$

$$-dV - w \, dx = 0$$

$$\boxed{\dfrac{dV}{dx} = -w}$$

**Logic Note:** The negative sign indicates that as $x$ increases (moving rightward), the shear force decreases when the load $w$ is downward (positive). This is the **First Fundamental Relationship**.

#### Derivation B — Moment Equilibrium about the Right Face ($\Sigma M_{\text{right}} = 0$)

Taking counter-clockwise moments as positive. The forces acting on the element are:
- $V$ on the left face, at a perpendicular distance $dx$ from the right face.
- $w \cdot dx$ (resultant of distributed load), acting at the centroid of the element, at a distance $\dfrac{dx}{2}$ from the right face.
- $M$ on the left face (counter-clockwise positive).
- $M + dM$ on the right face (clockwise positive, hence appears as negative in CCW summation).

Moment equilibrium:

$$M + dM - M - V \cdot dx + \left(w \cdot dx\right) \cdot \dfrac{dx}{2} = 0$$

The term $w \cdot dx \cdot \dfrac{dx}{2} = \dfrac{w \, (dx)^2}{2}$ is a **second-order infinitesimal** and vanishes in the limit $dx \to 0$:

$$dM - V \cdot dx + \underbrace{\dfrac{w \, (dx)^2}{2}}_{\to \, 0} = 0$$

$$dM - V \cdot dx = 0$$

$$\boxed{\dfrac{dM}{dx} = V}$$

**Logic Note:** The rate of change of bending moment with respect to position equals the shear force at that section. This is the **Second Fundamental Relationship**.

#### Derivation C — Combined Second-Order Relationship

Differentiate Derivation B with respect to $x$:

$$\dfrac{d}{dx}\left(\dfrac{dM}{dx}\right) = \dfrac{dV}{dx}$$

$$\dfrac{d^{2}M}{dx^{2}} = -w$$

$$\boxed{\dfrac{d^{2}M}{dx^{2}} = -w}$$

**Logic Note:** This is a **single integrated equation** that, given a load function $w(x)$ and two boundary conditions (e.g., moment values at supports), directly yields $M(x)$ without separately computing $V(x)$.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Shape Recognition Cheat Sheet

| Load Type $w(x)$ | SFD Shape $V(x)$ | BMD Shape $M(x)$ | Slopes at Endpoints |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Zero** ($w = 0$) | Constant (horizontal line) | Linear (straight slope) | SFD slope = 0 |
| **Uniformly Distributed Load (UDL)** ($w = $ const) | Linear (straight slope) | Parabolic (second-degree curve) | SFD slope = $-w$ |
| **Triangular Load** ($w = kx$) | Parabolic (second-degree) | Cubic (third-degree) | SFD slope = $-kx$ |
| **Point Load** ($P$ at $x = a$) | Sudden jump of $-P$ at $x = a$ | Kink (change in slope) at $x = a$ | Discontinuous SFD |
| **Pure Couple** ($C$ at $x = a$) | No change in SFD | Sudden jump of $-C$ at $x = a$ | Discontinuous BMD |

### 3.3 Worked Derivation Example — Semi-Infinite Beam with UDL

**Problem:** A cantilever beam of length $L$ carries a uniformly distributed load $w$ over its entire span. Using only the load-moment relationship $\dfrac{d^{2}M}{dx^{2}} = -w$, derive $M(x)$ measured from the fixed end.

**Step 1 — Set up the differential equation:**

$$\dfrac{d^{2}M}{dx^{2}} = -w$$

**Step 2 — First integration (gives shear):**

$$\dfrac{dM}{dx} = -wx + C_1$$

By definition, $\dfrac{dM}{dx} = V$, so $V(x) = -wx + C_1$.

**Step 3 — Second integration (gives moment):**

$$M(x) = -\dfrac{wx^{2}}{2} + C_1 x + C_2$$

**Step 4 — Apply boundary conditions:**

- At the free end ($x = L$): $V(L) = 0 \Rightarrow -wL + C_1 = 0 \Rightarrow C_1 = wL$.
- At the free end ($x = L$): $M(L) = 0 \Rightarrow -\dfrac{wL^{2}}{2} + wL \cdot L + C_2 = 0 \Rightarrow C_2 = -\dfrac{wL^{2}}{2}$.

**Step 5 — Final Result:**

$$V(x) = w(L - x)$$

$$M(x) = -\dfrac{wx^{2}}{2} + wLx - \dfrac{wL^{2}}{2}$$

$$\boxed{M(x) = -\dfrac{w}{2}(x - L)^{2} = -\dfrac{w(L-x)^{2}}{2}}$$

**Verification:** At the fixed end ($x = 0$): $M(0) = -\dfrac{wL^{2}}{2}$, which matches the standard textbook result for a cantilever with UDL. Maximum bending moment magnitude is $\dfrac{wL^{2}}{2}$ at the fixed end, and shear force varies linearly from $wL$ at the fixed end to $0$ at the free end. ✓

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Infinitesimal Beam Element — Free Body Diagram

The following Mermaid block-diagram represents the functional equilibrium model of a beam element of length $dx$, illustrating the load–shear–moment interactions:

```mermaid
graph LR
    elem["BEAM ELEMENT OF LENGTH dx"] --> faceL["LEFT FACE x"]
    elem --> faceR["RIGHT FACE x plus dx"]
    elem --> load["DISTRIBUTED LOAD w DOWNWARD"]

    faceL --> shearL["SHEAR V UPWARD"]
    faceL --> momentL["MOMENT M COUNTERCLOCKWISE"]

    faceR --> shearR["SHEAR V plus dV DOWNWARD"]
    faceR --> momentR["MOMENT M plus dM CLOCKWISE"]

    load --> resultant["RESULTANT w times dx AT CENTROID"]

    equilibrium1["EQUILIBRIUM Sigma Fy equals 0 gives dV by dx equals minus w"]
    equilibrium2["EQUILIBRIUM Sigma M equals 0 gives dM by dx equals V"]

    shearL --> equilibrium1
    shearR --> equilibrium1
    load --> equilibrium1

    shearL --> equilibrium2
    momentL --> equilibrium2
    momentR --> equilibrium2
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture Flow — Integration Strategy

This block-level flowchart maps the logical decision path a student should follow during the KTU exam when given any loading scenario:

```mermaid
graph TD
    start["START GIVEN LOADING w OF x"] --> identify{"IDENTIFY LOAD TYPE"}

    identify --> udl["UDL CONSTANT w"]
    identify --> point["POINT LOAD P AT x EQUALS a"]
    identify --> couple["APPLIED COUPLE C AT x EQUALS a"]
    identify --> variable["VARIABLE LOAD w OF x"]

    udl --> integrate1["INTEGRATE dV by dx equals minus w ONCE GIVES V OF x LINEAR"]
    integrate1 --> integrate2["INTEGRATE dM by dx EQUALS V ONCE AGAIN GIVES M OF x PARABOLIC"]

    point --> jump1["V JUMPS BY minus P AT x EQUALS a"]
    jump1 --> kink1["M HAS KINK SLOPE CHANGE AT x EQUALS a"]

    couple --> noJump["NO JUMP IN V AT x EQUALS a"]
    noJump --> jump2["M JUMPS BY minus C AT x EQUALS a"]

    variable --> multi["REPEAT INTEGRATION AS MANY TIMES AS POWER OF x IN w"]

    integrate2 --> boundary["APPLY BOUNDARY CONDITIONS"]
    kink1 --> boundary
    jump2 --> boundary
    multi --> boundary

    boundary --> final["FINAL EQUATIONS V OF x AND M OF x"]
    final --> sketch["SKETCH SFD AND BMD AND LABEL KEY VALUES"]
```

### 4.3 Qualitative Shape Recognition Schematic

```mermaid
graph LR
    caseA["CASE 1 NO LOAD w EQUALS 0"] --> shapeA["SFD HORIZONTAL LINE AND BMD STRAIGHT LINE WITH SLOPE V"]
    caseB["CASE 2 UDL w CONSTANT"] --> shapeB["SFD STRAIGHT SLOPING LINE AND BMD PARABOLA CONCAVE UP IF w DOWNWARD"]
    caseC["CASE 3 TRIANGULAR w EQUALS kx"] --> shapeC["SFD PARABOLA AND BMD CUBIC CURVE"]
    caseD["CASE 4 POINT LOAD P"] --> shapeD["SFD HAS VERTICAL JUMP OF MINUS P AND BMD HAS KINK OR PEAK"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Each block in the Mermaid schematics represents a **functional state** in the derivation or analysis. The arrows indicate the logical flow of equilibrium conditions or integration operations. KTU examiners reward clearly-labeled free-body diagrams even in purely analytical problems — drawing the infinitesimal element is a 1–2 mark "freebie."

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**

State and prove the relationship between the intensity of loading, shear force, and bending moment for a beam.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Statement:** The rate of change of shear force with respect to $x$ equals the negative of the load intensity, and the rate of change of bending moment with respect to $x$ equals the shear force.

**Proof:** Consider an elemental slice of length $dx$ at distance $x$ from the origin. Let $V$ and $M$ act on the left face and $V + dV$, $M + dM$ act on the right face. The distributed load $w$ acts downward.

Applying $\Sigma F_y = 0$:

$$V - (V + dV) - w \, dx = 0 \implies \dfrac{dV}{dx} = -w \quad \text{...(1 mark)}$$

Applying $\Sigma M_{\text{right}} = 0$ (neglecting second-order term):

$$M + dM - M - V \cdot dx = 0 \implies \dfrac{dM}{dx} = V \quad \text{...(1 mark)}$$

Combining both:

$$\dfrac{d^{2}M}{dx^{2}} = -w \quad \text{...(1 mark)}$$

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

A beam is subjected to a uniformly distributed load of intensity $w$ over its entire span. Sketch the shape of the Shear Force Diagram (SFD) and Bending Moment Diagram (BMD), justifying the shapes using the load-shear-moment relationships.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- Since $w$ is constant, $\dfrac{dV}{dx} = -w$ is **constant** (negative slope), so the SFD is a **straight line** with constant negative slope. **(1 mark)**
- Since $\dfrac{dM}{dx} = V$ and $V$ is linear in $x$, the BMD is the integral of a linear function, hence a **parabolic curve** (second-degree). **(1 mark)**
- The parabolic BMD is **concave-up** (since $\dfrac{d^{2}M}{dx^{2}} = -w < 0$ when $w$ is downward positive... *corrected by sign*: concave-down for downward load). The maximum bending moment occurs where the SFD crosses zero. **(1 mark)**

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Question]**

**(a)** A simply supported beam of length $L$ carries a uniformly distributed load $w$ over its entire span. Using the load-shear-moment relationships $\dfrac{dV}{dx} = -w$ and $\dfrac{dM}{dx} = V$, derive the equations for $V(x)$ and $M(x)$ measured from the left support. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A cantilever beam of length $L$ carries a triangular load that varies from zero at the fixed end to $w_0$ at the free end. Derive $V(x)$ and $M(x)$ using the second-order relationship $\dfrac{d^{2}M}{dx^{2}} = -w$, where $x$ is measured from the fixed end. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **MODEL SOLUTION — Question A**

**(a) Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Define load function:** The load intensity is constant: $w(x) = w$.

**Step 2 — Integrate to find V(x):** From $\dfrac{dV}{dx} = -w$:

$$V(x) = -wx + C_1 \quad \text{...(1 mark for integration setup)}$$

**Step 3 — Apply boundary condition for V:** At $x = 0$ (left support), $V(0) = +\dfrac{wL}{2}$ (reaction force is upward):

$$C_1 = \dfrac{wL}{2}$$

Hence:

$$\boxed{V(x) = \dfrac{wL}{2} - wx} \quad \text{...(1 mark)}$$

**Step 4 — Integrate to find M(x):** From $\dfrac{dM}{dx} = V$:

$$M(x) = \dfrac{wL}{2}x - \dfrac{wx^{2}}{2} + C_2 \quad \text{...(1 mark for integration)}$$

**Step 5 — Apply boundary condition for M:** At $x = 0$, $M(0) = 0$ (simply supported end):

$$C_2 = 0$$

Hence:

$$\boxed{M(x) = \dfrac{wx}{2}(L - x)} \quad \text{...(1 mark)}$$

**Step 6 — State key values (verification):**

- Maximum bending moment at $x = \dfrac{L}{2}$: $M_{\max} = \dfrac{wL^{2}}{8}$. **(1 mark)**
- Shear is zero at $x = \dfrac{L}{2}$ (confirming the point of maximum moment). **(1 mark)**

---

**(b) Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Define load function:** Measured from the fixed end ($x = 0$), the triangular load varies linearly:

$$w(x) = \dfrac{w_0}{L} \cdot x \quad \text{...(1 mark for expression)}$$

(At $x = 0$, $w = 0$; at $x = L$, $w = w_0$.)

**Step 2 — Set up the differential equation:**

$$\dfrac{d^{2}M}{dx^{2}} = -w(x) = -\dfrac{w_0}{L}x$$

**Step 3 — First integration (gives shear):**

$$\dfrac{dM}{dx} = V(x) = -\dfrac{w_0}{2L}x^{2} + C_1 \quad \text{...(1 mark)}$$

**Step 4 — Second integration (gives moment):**

$$M(x) = -\dfrac{w_0}{6L}x^{3} + C_1 x + C_2 \quad \text{...(1 mark)}$$

**Step 5 — Apply boundary conditions:**

- At the free end ($x = L$), $V(L) = 0$ (no shear at the tip of a cantilever):

$$0 = -\dfrac{w_0}{2L}L^{2} + C_1 = -\dfrac{w_0 L}{2} + C_1 \implies C_1 = \dfrac{w_0 L}{2}$$

- At the free end ($x = L$), $M(L) = 0$ (no moment at the tip):

$$0 = -\dfrac{w_0}{6L}L^{3} + \dfrac{w_0 L}{2} \cdot L + C_2 = -\dfrac{w_0 L^{2}}{6} + \dfrac{w_0 L^{2}}{2} + C_2$$

$$C_2 = \dfrac{w_0 L^{2}}{6} - \dfrac{w_0 L^{2}}{2} = -\dfrac{w_0 L^{2}}{3}$$

**Step 6 — Final equations:**
