$$\boxed{V(x) = \dfrac{w_0 L}{2} - \dfrac{w_0 x^{2}}{2L}} \quad \text{...(1 mark)}$$

$$\boxed{M(x) = -\dfrac{w_0 x^{3}}{6L} + \dfrac{w_0 L x}{2} - \dfrac{w_0 L^{2}}{3}} \quad \text{...(1 mark)}$$

**Step 7 — Verification at the fixed end ($x = 0$):**

$$V(0) = \dfrac{w_0 L}{2}, \quad M(0) = -\dfrac{w_0 L^{2}}{3}$$

The negative sign indicates **hogging** (concave-down) at the fixed end, consistent with a cantilever under downward load. ✓ **(1 mark)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Alternative Model]**

**(a)** Derive the relationships $\dfrac{dV}{dx} = -w$ and $\dfrac{dM}{dx} = V$ from the equilibrium of an infinitesimal beam element of length $dx$. Clearly state the sign convention used. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A simply supported beam of length $6 \text{ m}$ carries a point load of $30 \text{ kN}$ at its mid-span. Using the load-shear-moment relationships, determine the equations of shear force and bending moment. Also find the maximum bending moment. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **MODEL SOLUTION — Question B**

**(a) Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Sign Convention (1 mark):**
- Downward distributed load $w$ is **positive**.
- Left-side shear $V$ acting **upward** is **positive**.
- Left-side bending moment $M$ causing **sagging** (concave-up) is **positive**.

**Step 2 — Free Body Diagram Description (2 marks):**
Consider an element of length $dx$ at distance $x$. Left face carries $V$ (up) and $M$ (CCW). Right face carries $V + dV$ (down) and $M + dM$ (CW). Distributed load $w$ acts downward over the element.

**Step 3 — Vertical Equilibrium (2 marks):**

$$V - (V + dV) - w \, dx = 0 \implies \dfrac{dV}{dx} = -w$$

**Step 4 — Moment Equilibrium about Right Face (2 marks):**

$$M + dM - M - V \cdot dx = 0 \implies \dfrac{dM}{dx} = V$$

---

**(b) Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Reactions:** By symmetry, each support carries $R_A = R_B = 15 \text{ kN}$.

**Step 2 — Region 1 ($0 \leq x < 3 \text{ m}$):** No load between $x = 0$ and $x = 3$, so $w = 0$. Hence $\dfrac{dV}{dx} = 0$, meaning $V$ is constant.

From $\Sigma F_y = 0$ on left portion: $V(x) = R_A = +15 \text{ kN}$ for $0 < x < 3$. **(1 mark)**

From $\dfrac{dM}{dx} = V$: $M(x) = 15x + C_1$. With $M(0) = 0$: $C_1 = 0$.

$$M(x) = 15x \quad \text{for } 0 \leq x \leq 3 \quad \text{...(1 mark)}$$

**Step 3 — Region 2 ($3 < x \leq 6 \text{ m}$):** After the point load, $V$ jumps down by $30 \text{ kN}$:

$$V(x) = 15 - 30 = -15 \text{ kN}$$

$M(x) = -15x + C_2$. Continuity at $x = 3$: $M(3^-) = 15(3) = 45$ and $M(3^+) = -15(3) + C_2 = 45$, so $C_2 = 90$.

$$M(x) = -15x + 90 \quad \text{for } 3 \leq x \leq 6 \quad \text{...(1 mark)}$$

Verify: $M(6) = -90 + 90 = 0$ ✓. **(1 mark)**

**Step 4 — Maximum Bending Moment:** $V$ changes sign at $x = 3 \text{ m}$, which is the location of maximum $M$.

$$M_{\max} = 15 \times 3 = 45 \text{ kN·m} \quad \text{...(1 mark)}$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion (1 mark):** The SFD is a step function (rectangular block from +15 to −15 at mid-span), and the BMD is a triangle peaking at $45 \text{ kN·m}$.

---

### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Mistakes — AVOID THESE:**
> 1. **Sign Convention Errors (–2 marks typical):** Students often write $\dfrac{dV}{dx} = +w$ instead of $-w$ because they confuse the load direction. Always explicitly state: "Taking upward forces as positive."
> 2. **Skipping the FBD (–1 mark):** Even for analytical problems, KTU examiners expect a labeled free-body diagram of the infinitesimal element. Draw it even if it's a small sketch.
> 3. **Ignoring Second-Order Terms Incorrectly:** When deriving $\dfrac{dM}{dx} = V$, the term $w \cdot dx \cdot \dfrac{dx}{2}$ vanishes because it is second-order. State this explicitly — examiners want to see the reasoning, not just the cancellation.
> 4. **Forgetting Integration Constants:** Two boundary conditions are needed to find $C_1$ and $C_2$. A common error is using only one condition or applying it at the wrong location.
> 5. **No Origin Declaration:** Always declare where $x = 0$ (left support? fixed end? point of load application?). Examiners deduct marks when the coordinate system is ambiguous.
> 6. **Confusing Point Load vs. Couple Behavior:** A point load causes a **jump in SFD** but only a **kink in BMD**. A couple causes **no change in SFD** but a **jump in BMD**. Mixing these up loses 1–2 marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Master These Before the Exam:**
>
> - **Three Core Equations (MUST MEMORIZE):**
>   - $\dfrac{dV}{dx} = -w$
>   - $\dfrac{dM}{dx} = V$
>   - $\dfrac{d^{2}M}{dx^{2}} = -w$
>
> - **Shape Recognition Rules:**
>   - Constant load → Linear SFD → Parabolic BMD
>   - Linear load → Parabolic SFD → Cubic BMD
>   - Zero load → Constant SFD → Linear BMD
>
> - **Boundary Conditions — Always Two Needed:**
>   - At a **simple support**: $M = 0$
>   - At a **fixed support**: $V = $ reaction, $M = $ reaction moment
>   - At a **free end**: $V = 0$, $M = 0$
>
> - **Special Pointers:**
>   - **Point of zero shear** = Location of **maximum/minimum moment**
>   - **Sign of $w$** controls the **concavity** of the BMD
>   - **Point load $P$**: $\Delta V = -P$ (downward) at that location
>   - **Applied couple $C$**: $\Delta M = -C$ at that location
>
> - **Quick Verification After Solving:**
>   1. Check that $V$ changes correctly at point loads.
>   2. Verify $M$ satisfies both boundary conditions.
>   3. Confirm that $dM/dx = V$ at any chosen $x$.
>   4. Confirm that $dV/dx = -w$ at any chosen $x$.
>
> - **Units Vigilance:**
>   - $w$ → N/m or kN/m
>   - $V$ → N or kN
>   - $M$ → N·m or kN·m
>   - $x$ → m
>
> - **Time-Saver Tip:** When asked to "sketch" SFD/BMD without full equations, immediately:
>   1. Compute reactions.
>   2. Identify load regions.
>   3. Use the shape recognition rules above.
>   4. Mark critical values (max $M$, zero crossings of $V$).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3: FLEXURAL AND SHEAR STRESSES IN BEAMS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Flexural Stress (Bending Stress):** When a structural member (beam) is subjected to transverse loads acting perpendicular to its longitudinal axis, internal resisting moments develop within the cross-section. These internal moments produce normal stresses that vary linearly across the depth of the section. The intensity of such longitudinal stress at any fibre located at a distance $y$ from the neutral axis is given by the **Flexure Formula** (also called the **Euler–Bernoulli Bending Equation**).

$$\sigma = \frac{M \, y}{I}$$

where:
- $M$ = Bending moment at the section (N·m)
- $I$ = Second moment of area about the neutral axis (m$^4$)
- $y$ = Distance of the fibre from the neutral axis (m)
- $\sigma$ = Bending (flexural) stress (N/m$^2$ or Pa)

**Shear Stress in Beams:** Transverse loading also produces internal shear forces at any cross-section. These shear forces are resisted by tangential (shear) stresses distributed over the cross-section. The shear stress at any level in a beam is given by the **Shear Stress Formula** (commonly attributed to Jourawski):

$$\tau = \frac{V \, Q}{I \, b}$$

where:
- $V$ = Shear force at the section (N)
- $Q$ = First moment of area of the portion of the cross-section beyond the level considered, about the neutral axis (m$^3$)
- $I$ = Second moment of area of the whole section about the neutral axis (m$^4$)
- $b$ = Width of the section at the level where shear stress is computed (m)
- $\tau$ = Shear stress (N/m$^2$ or Pa)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Examiner Insight:** A beam subjected *purely* to bending moments (no shear) is in **Pure Bending**. When transverse loads cause *both* shear and bending, the condition is **Non-Uniform / Ordinary Bending**. The flexure formula is strictly valid for *Pure Bending*, but for slender beams ($L/d > 10$) the error introduced under ordinary bending is negligible (less than 1.5%).

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

**Real-World Analogy — The Diving Board:**
Imagine a diver standing at the free end of a long, flexible diving board. The board bends downward. If we take a thin cross-section slice, the **top fibres of the board get compressed (squashed)** while the **bottom fibres get stretched (pulled apart)**. Somewhere in between — at the **Neutral Axis** — there is **no change in length**, hence no longitudinal stress.

- Compression on top → **Compressive Flexural Stress** ($\sigma_c$)
- Tension on bottom → **Tensile Flexural Stress** ($\sigma_t$)
- Middle line → **Neutral Axis** (zero longitudinal strain and stress)

**The Deck of a Loaded Ship:** When cargo is loaded unevenly, the ship's deck bends. The steel plating on the top experiences compression, and on the bottom, tension. The shear stress is what holds the *layers* of the deck from sliding over one another — like a pack of playing cards where each card slides slightly relative to the next.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction for Examiners:**
> 1. **Flexural stress $\sigma$** acts **perpendicular** to the cross-section (NORMAL stress) and varies **linearly** with $y$.
> 2. **Shear stress $\tau$** acts **parallel** to the cross-section (TANGENTIAL stress) and varies **parabolically** (for rectangular sections) or by a complex curve (for I-sections).
> 3. The **maximum bending stress** occurs at the **extreme fibre** ($y = y_{max}$), while the **maximum shear stress** for rectangular sections occurs at the **neutral axis**.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard SI Unit | Field Convention |
|---|---|---|
| Bending Stress ($\sigma$) | **N/m² (Pa)**, often **MPa** = 10⁶ Pa | **MPa** or **N/mm²** in design |
| Shear Stress ($\tau$) | **N/m² (Pa)**, often **MPa** | **MPa** in design |
| Bending Moment ($M$) | **N·m** | **kN·m** for civil/structural |
| Shear Force ($V$) | **N** | **kN** in practice |
| Second Moment of Area ($I$) | **m⁴** | **mm⁴** or **cm⁴** in design tables |
| First Moment of Area ($Q$) | **m³** | **mm³** or **cm³** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Tip:** Always convert cross-section dimensions from **mm to m** (or maintain consistency in mm throughout). Mixing units is the #1 reason students lose 2–3 marks in numericals.

---

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Assumptions of the Simple Bending Theory (Euler–Bernoulli Theory)

The classical flexure formula is derived under the following **five fundamental assumptions**:

1. **Plane Sections Remain Plane:** Cross-sections that are plane before bending remain plane and normal to the longitudinal axis after bending (no warping).
2. **Homogeneity:** The material is uniform in composition throughout.
3. **Isotropy:** The material has identical elastic properties in all directions.
4. **Linearity (Hookean Behaviour):** The material obeys Hooke's Law — stress is proportional to strain ($\sigma = E\varepsilon$).
5. **No Transverse Strain:** Fibres do not exert pressure on each other laterally, so each fibre behaves as if it were under simple axial stress alone.

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Reality Check:** For **deep beams** ($L/d < 5$), assumption 1 fails because shear deformation causes cross-sections to **warp** (not remain plane). In such cases, **Timoshenko Beam Theory** must be used.

---

## 2.2 Derivation Logic — Step-by-Step Reasoning

### Step 1: Geometry of Deformation
A small element of length $dx$ of the beam, originally straight, bends into an arc of radius $R$ after deformation. The neutral axis (length unchanged) has length $dx$. A fibre at distance $y$ from the neutral axis now has length $(R+y)d\theta$, where $d\theta = dx/R$.

### Step 2: Longitudinal Strain
The change in length of the fibre is $(R+y)d\theta - dx = y\,d\theta$. Hence strain:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{y\,d\theta}{dx} = \frac{y}{R}$$

### Step 3: Stress via Hooke's Law
Since each fibre is under uniaxial stress, $\sigma = E\varepsilon$:

$$\sigma = \frac{E \, y}{R}$$

### Step 4: Equilibrium — Moment Resistance
The internal moment must equal the moment of the stress distribution about the neutral axis:

$$M = \int \sigma \, y \, dA = \int \frac{E\,y}{R} \cdot y \, dA = \frac{E}{R}\int y^2 dA = \frac{E\,I}{R}$$

### Step 5: Combine to Eliminate $E/R$
From Step 3: $\dfrac{E}{R} = \dfrac{M}{I}$. Substituting:

$$\boxed{\sigma = \frac{M\,y}{I}}$$

---

## 2.3 Section Modulus ($Z$)

For design purposes, the **maximum bending stress** (at extreme fibre $y = c$) is:

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{M \, c}{I} = \frac{M}{Z}$$

where $Z = I/c$ is the **Section Modulus** (units: m³).

**Resisting Moment of a Section:**

$$M_R = \sigma_{allow} \times Z$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Implication:** To carry a given moment with minimum material, a section should have the **highest possible $Z$ for a given cross-sectional area**. This is why I-sections are used in beams — most material is placed *far from the neutral axis*, where its contribution to $I$ (and hence $Z$) is maximised (since $I \propto y^2$).

---

## 2.4 Shear Stress Derivation Logic

Consider a small element of length $dx$ in a beam. Bending moment changes from $M$ to $M + dM$ across the element. At a level located at distance $y_1$ from the neutral axis:

- On face 1: longitudinal force on the area $A_1$ above the level = $F_1 = \int_{A_1} \sigma \, dA = \int_{A_1} \frac{M y}{I} dA = \frac{M}{I} \cdot Q_1$
- On face 2: similar force $F_2 = \dfrac{M + dM}{I} \cdot Q_1$

The net unbalanced longitudinal force $dF = F_2 - F_1 = \dfrac{dM \cdot Q_1}{I}$ must be resisted by horizontal shear force on the cross-section area ($b \cdot dx$):

$$\tau \cdot b \cdot dx = \frac{dM \cdot Q_1}{I}$$

Since $\dfrac{dM}{dx} = V$ (shear force):

$$\boxed{\tau = \frac{V \, Q}{I \, b}}$$

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Key Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma = \dfrac{M\,y}{I}$ | Flexural stress at fibre $y$ | Bending stress at any level |
| 2 | $\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{M}{Z}$ | Maximum flexural stress | Design of beams against failure |
| 3 | $Z = \dfrac{I}{c}$ | Section modulus | Selection of cross-section |
| 4 | $M_R = \sigma_b \cdot Z$ | Moment of resistance | Capacity check |
| 5 | $\tau = \dfrac{V\,Q}{I\,b}$ | Shear stress at any level | Shear design |
| 6 | $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{V \cdot Q_{max}}{I \cdot b_{min}}$ | Maximum shear stress | Web shear in I-sections |
| 7 | $Q = A\bar{y}$ | First moment of area | Compute shear stress |
| 8 | $\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{E}{R} = \dfrac{\sigma}{y}$ | Curvature relation | Curvature, deflection |
| 9 | $\dfrac{1}{R} = \dfrac{M}{EI}$ | Radius of curvature | Slope and deflection integration |
| 10 | $\tau_{max,rect} = 1.5 \cdot \tau_{avg} = \dfrac{3V}{2A}$ | Max shear, rectangular | Direct application |
| 11 | $\tau_{max,circ} = \dfrac{4V}{3A}$ | Max shear, solid circle | Shaft design |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **NOTE on $\vert$ usage in tables:** All modulus/absolute bars in this table use $\vert \cdot \vert$ form to avoid breaking Markdown. In LaTeX, always write $\vert$ or $\mid$ inside a table row, not the vertical pipe.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application |
|---|---|
| **Civil/Structural Engineering** | Design of building beams, bridge girders, floor joists |
| **Mechanical Engineering** | Shaft design under transverse loading, machine tool frames |
| **Aerospace** | Wing spar analysis, helicopter rotor blades |
| **Marine** | Ship hull girder strength, deck plating |
| **Railway** | Rail stress analysis under wheel loads |
| **Automotive** | Chassis frame, leaf-spring design |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters:** Almost every load-bearing structure is fundamentally a beam. The flexure and shear formulas form the *very backbone* of structural design codes like **IS 800 (Steel), IS 456 (Concrete), AISC 360, and Eurocode 3**.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Flexure Formula

**Starting Assumptions (recapitulated):**
- Beam initially straight with uniform cross-section
- Material homogeneous, isotropic, linearly elastic
- Pure bending: constant bending moment, zero shear
- Plane sections remain plane and normal to the axis

**Geometric Setup:**
Consider a beam segment of original length $L$ along the centroidal axis. After bending, the centroidal axis forms an arc of radius $R$ (the radius of curvature). A fibre at distance $y$ above the neutral axis (positive upward) has its centre-line on a circle of radius $(R+y)$.

**Step 1 — Length of the Neutral Axis Fibre:**

The neutral axis fibre (at $y = 0$) is unchanged in length, so:

$$L = R \cdot \theta$$

where $\theta$ is the angle subtended at the centre of curvature.

**Step 2 — Length of a Fibre at Distance $y$:**

A fibre at distance $y$ from the neutral axis has radius $(R + y)$, so its length is:

$$L' = (R + y)\theta$$

**Step 3 — Longitudinal Strain in the Fibre:**

Strain = change in length / original length:

$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{L' - L}{L} = \frac{(R+y)\theta - R\theta}{R\theta} = \frac{y\theta}{R\theta} = \frac{y}{R}$$

**Step 4 — Apply Hooke's Law:**

For uniaxial stress in the fibre:

$$\sigma_x = E \cdot \varepsilon_x = \frac{E \cdot y}{R}$$

**Step 5 — Resultant Force on the Cross-Section:**

The longitudinal force on the cross-section must be zero (no axial force in pure bending):

$$F_x = \int_A \sigma_x \, dA = \int_A \frac{E\,y}{R} \, dA = \frac{E}{R}\int_A y \, dA = 0$$

This is satisfied *automatically* if the neutral axis passes through the centroid (where $\int y\,dA = 0$). This is why **the neutral axis coincides with the centroidal axis** in pure bending.

**Step 6 — Resultant Moment about the Neutral Axis:**

The internal resisting moment equals the bending moment $M$:

$$M = \int_A \sigma_x \cdot y \, dA = \int_A \frac{E\,y}{R} \cdot y \, dA = \frac{E}{R}\int_A y^2 \, dA = \frac{E\,I}{R}$$

**Step 7 — Eliminate $E/R$:**

From Step 6: $\dfrac{E}{R} = \dfrac{M}{I}$

Substituting into Step 4:

$$\sigma_x = \frac{M\,y}{I}$$

**Step 8 — Curvature Relationship:**

Rearranging Step 6:

$$\frac{1}{R} = \frac{M}{E\,I}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The curvature $1/R$ of the bent beam is **directly proportional** to the bending moment $M$ and **inversely proportional** to the **Flexural Rigidity** $EI$. A higher $EI$ means a "stiffer" beam that bends less.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of the Shear Stress Formula

**Setup:** Consider a beam segment of length $dx$ between two cross-sections. Bending moment increases from $M$ on the left face to $M + dM$ on the right face. We compute shear stress at a horizontal level at distance $y_1$ from the neutral axis.

**Step 1 — Bending Stress on Left Face at Level $y_1$:**

The longitudinal (bending) stress on the left face at distance $y_1$ is:

$$\sigma_1 = \frac{M\,y_1}{I}$$

**Step 2 — Resultant Longitudinal Force on Area $A_1$ Above Level $y_1$:**

Integrate the bending stress over the area $A_1$ (the portion of the cross-section *above* the level $y_1$):

$$F_1 = \int_{A_1} \sigma_1 \, dA = \int_{A_1} \frac{M\,y}{I} dA = \frac{M}{I}\int_{A_1} y \, dA = \frac{M \cdot Q}{I}$$

where $Q = \int_{A_1} y \, dA = A_1 \bar{y}$ is the **first moment of area** of $A_1$ about the neutral axis, and $\bar{y}$ is the distance from the neutral axis to the centroid of $A_1$.

**Step 3 — Resultant Longitudinal Force on Right Face:**

The bending moment is $M + dM$, so the force is:

$$F_2 = \frac{(M + dM) Q}{I}$$

**Step 4 — Unbalanced Longitudinal Force:**

$$dF = F_2 - F_1 = \frac{dM \cdot Q}{I}$$

**Step 5 — Equilibrium — Horizontal Shear on the Cut:**

This unbalanced force must be balanced by a horizontal shear force acting on the longitudinal cut at level $y_1$ over the area $b \cdot dx$:

$$\tau \cdot b \cdot dx = dF = \frac{dM \cdot Q}{I}$$

**Step 6 — Use $V = dM/dx$:**

$$\tau \cdot b \cdot dx = \frac{V \cdot dx \cdot Q}{I}$$

Dividing both sides by $b \cdot dx$:

$$\boxed{\tau = \frac{V \, Q}{I \, b}}$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — Shear Stress Distribution in a Rectangular Section

**Given:** A rectangular beam of width $b$ and depth $d$ subjected to a shear force $V$.

**Required:** Shear stress at the neutral axis, at the top, and the distribution shape.

**Solution:**

For a rectangular section, take a level at distance $y$ from the neutral axis (where $-d/2 \le y \le d/2$).

**Step 1 — Area Above Level $y$:**

$$A_1 = b \cdot \left(\frac{d}{2} - y\right)$$

**Step 2 — Centroid of $A_1$ from Neutral Axis:**

The area $A_1$ extends from $y$ to $d/2$. Its centroid is at the midpoint:

$$\bar{y} = \frac{y + d/2}{2}$$

**Step 3 — First Moment of Area $Q$:**

$$Q = A_1 \cdot \bar{y} = b \cdot \left(\frac{d}{2} - y\right) \cdot \frac{1}{2}\left(y + \frac{d}{2}\right) = \frac{b}{2}\left[\left(\frac{d}{2}\right)^2 - y^2\right]$$

**Step 4 — Second Moment of Area:**

$$I = \frac{b d^3}{12}$$

**Step 5 — Shear Stress:**

$$\tau = \frac{V \cdot Q}{I \cdot b} = \frac{V \cdot \frac{b}{2}\left[\left(\frac{d}{2}\right)^2 - y^2\right]}{\frac{bd^3}{12} \cdot b} = \frac{6V}{bd^3}\left[\frac{d^2}{4} - y^2\right]$$

**Step 6 — Maximum Shear Stress (at $y = 0$):**

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{6V}{bd^3} \cdot \frac{d^2}{4} = \frac{3V}{2bd} = \frac{3V}{2A}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Result:** $\tau_{max,rect} = 1.5 \cdot \tau_{avg} = \dfrac{3V}{2A}$. This is a **favourite KTU numerical** — students must memorise this result.

**Step 7 — Shear Stress at Top and Bottom ($y = \pm d/2$):**

$$\tau_{top} = \tau_{bottom} = \frac{6V}{bd^3}\left[\frac{d^2}{4} - \frac{d^2}{4}\right] = 0$$

**Step 8 — Distribution Shape:** The expression $\tau \propto \left(\dfrac{d^2}{4} - y^2\right)$ is a **parabola** in $y$, with maximum at $y = 0$ and zero at the extreme fibres.

---

## 3.4 Shear Stress in a Circular Section (Solid)

For a solid circular section of diameter $d$:

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{4V}{3A} = \frac{16V}{3\pi d^2}$$

This corresponds to a **factor of $4/3$** above the average shear stress $V/A$.

---

## 3.5 Shear Stress in an I-Section

For an I-beam with flange width $b_f$, flange thickness $t_f$, web height $h_w$, and web thickness $t_w$:

**Step 1 — Compute $I$** about the neutral axis using parallel axis theorem for the entire section.

**Step 2 — Compute $Q$ at the Neutral Axis:**

Take the area $A_1$ as the upper flange + half the web (or simply the entire top half). For symmetric I:

$$Q_{NA} = A_{flange} \cdot \bar{y}_{flange} + A_{half-web} \cdot \bar{y}_{half-web}$$

For a standard I-section with $b_f$, $t_f$, $h_w$, $t_w$:

$$Q_{NA} = b_f t_f \cdot \left(\frac{h_w}{2} + \frac{t_f}{2}\right) + t_w \cdot \frac{h_w}{2} \cdot \frac{h_w}{4}$$

**Step 3 — Maximum Shear Stress in the Web:**

$$\tau_{max,web} = \frac{V \cdot Q_{NA}}{I \cdot t_w}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Insight:** In an I-section, shear stress in the **flanges is small and nearly horizontal**, while in the **web it is vertical and very large**. This is why the web is made **thinner but reinforced with stiffeners** at points of high shear (e.g., near supports).

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Summary of Results

| Cross-Section | $\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{M}{Z}$ | $\tau_{max} = k \cdot \dfrac{V}{A}$ | Distribution of $\tau$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Rectangle $b \times d$ | $\dfrac{6M}{bd^2}$ | $1.5 \cdot \dfrac{V}{A}$ | Parabolic |
| Solid Circle (diameter $d$) | $\dfrac{32M}{\pi d^3}$ | $\dfrac{4}{3} \cdot \dfrac{V}{A}$ | Parabolic |
| Hollow Circle (ID $d_i$, OD $d_o$) | $\dfrac{32M}{\pi(d_o^4 - d_i^4)} \cdot d_o$ | $\dfrac{16 V d_o}{3\pi (d_o^4 - d_i^4)}(d_o^2 + d_i^2)$ wait, use $\dfrac{4V}{3A}\left(\dfrac{d_o^2 + d_i^2}{d_o^2 - d_i^2}\right)$ | Parabolic |
| I-Section (built-up) | $\dfrac{M}{Z_e}$ where $Z_e = I/c$ | Computed via $\dfrac{V Q_{NA}}{I t_w}$ | Nearly uniform in web |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Stress States in a Beam Under Flexure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[External Transverse Load Applied on Beam]:::load --> B[Internal Reactions at Cross-Section]
    B --> C[Internal Bending Moment M]
    B --> D[Internal Shear Force V]
    C --> E[Flexure Formula M y over I]
    E --> F[Bending Stress sigma_x]
    F --> G[Top Fibre: Compression sigma_c]
    F --> H[Bottom Fibre: Tension sigma_t]
    F --> I[Neutral Axis: Zero Longitudinal Stress]
    D --> J[Shear Formula V Q over I b]
    J --> K[Shear Stress tau]
    K --> L[At Neutral Axis: tau_max for Rectangular]
    K --> M[At Extreme Fibres: tau Approx Zero]
    J --> N[Shear Distribution]
    N --> O[Rectangle: Parabolic]
    N --> P[I-Section: Web carries Most Shear]
    N --> Q[Circle: Parabolic with k 4 by 3]
    classDef load fill:#f9c,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

```mermaid
    B --> C[Cross-Section Behaviour]
    C --> D[Top Fibre Shortened - Compression]
    C --> E[Bottom Fibre Elongated - Tension]
    C --> F[Neutral Axis: NA No Length Change]
    subgraph Stress_Distribution
        S1[Linear Variation of sigma with y]
        S2[sigma max at y equals plus d over 2]
        S3[sigma equals zero at Neutral Axis y equals 0]
        S4[sigma min at y equals minus d over 2 Negative Compression]
    end

```

## 4.3 Schematic — Shear Stress Distribution Across Various Sections

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Beam Cross-Section] --> B{Section Type}
    B -->|Rectangle| C[Shear Distribution: Parabolic]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**State the assumptions made in the theory of simple bending.**

**Model Answer:**

The Euler–Bernoulli theory of simple bending is based on the following assumptions:

1. The beam material is **homogeneous** (uniform composition) and **isotropic** (same elastic properties in all directions).
2. The material obeys **Hooke's Law** — stress is proportional to strain ($\sigma = E\varepsilon$).
3. The beam is initially **straight** and has a **uniform cross-section**.
4. **Plane sections remain plane and normal to the longitudinal axis** before and after bending (no warping).
5. The beam is subjected to **pure bending** — only bending moment acts, shear force is zero.
6. **Self-weight of the beam is neglected** compared to applied loads (or treated separately).
7. **No lateral stresses** exist between adjacent fibres; each fibre is treated as a uniaxial test specimen.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**Distinguish between flexural stress and shear stress in a beam.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Flexural Stress ($\sigma$) | Shear Stress ($\tau$) |
|---|---|---|
| **Direction** | Normal (perpendicular) to cross-section | Tangential (parallel) to cross-section |
| **Cause** | Internal **bending moment** $M$ | Internal **shear force** $V$ |
| **Variation with $y$** | **Linear** — $\sigma = My/I$ | **Parabolic** (for rectangle) — $\tau = VQ/Ib$ |
| **Magnitude — Max location** | At **extreme fibres** ($y = \pm d/2$) | At **neutral axis** ($y = 0$) for rectangular sections |
| **Magnitude — Min location** | At **neutral axis** ($y = 0$) — equals zero | At **extreme fibres** — equals zero |
| **Sign convention** | Tension on one side, compression on the other | Always acts in the same rotational sense as $V$ |
| **Design use** | Governs design of **flexural members** (beams in bending) | Governs design in **webs of beams, shear connections, bolts** |

---

## PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice Format

### **QUESTION A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 3]**

**A simply supported beam of span 4 m carries a uniformly distributed load (UDL) of 30 kN/m over its entire span. The beam is of rectangular cross-section, 200 mm wide and 400 mm deep. Determine:**

**(a)** The maximum bending stress and the maximum shear stress in the beam. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The position and magnitude of the absolute maximum flexural stress, and verify the parabolic nature of the shear stress distribution by computing the shear stress at $y = 100$ mm from the neutral axis. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Question A:**

**Given:**
- Span $L = 4$ m
- UDL $w = 30$ kN/m
- Width $b = 200$ mm = 0.2 m
- Depth $d = 400$ mm = 0.4 m

**Reactions (by symmetry):**
$$R_A = R_B = \frac{wL}{2} = \frac{30 \times 4}{2} = 60 \text{ kN}$$

**Maximum Bending Moment (at midspan):**
$$M_{max} = \frac{wL^2}{8} = \frac{30 \times 4^2}{8} = 60 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Maximum Shear Force (at supports):**
$$V_{max} = \frac{wL}{2} = 60 \text{ kN}$$

**Section Properties:**
$$I = \frac{bd^3}{12} = \frac{200 \times 400^3}{12} = \frac{200 \times 64 \times 10^6}{12} = 1.0667 \times 10^9 \text{ mm}^4$$

$$Z = \frac{bd^2}{6} = \frac{200 \times 400^2}{6} = 5.333 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^3$$

---

##### **Part (a) Solution:**

**Maximum Bending Stress:**

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{M_{max}}{Z} = \frac{60 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}}{5.333 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^3} = 11.25 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 11.25 \text{ MPa}$$

**Maximum Shear Stress (rectangular section):**

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{3V_{max}}{2A} = \frac{3 \times 60 \times 10^3}{2 \times (200 \times 400)} = \frac{180000}{160000} = 1.125 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 1.125 \text{ MPa}$$

---

##### **Part (b) Solution:**

**Absolute Maximum Flexural Stress:**

The maximum flexural stress occurs at the **midspan** (where $M = M_{max}$) and at the **extreme fibre** ($y = \pm d/2 = \pm 200$ mm).

- **Position:** Midspan ($x = L/2 = 2$ m from support), top or bottom fibre.
- **Magnitude:** $\sigma_{max} = 11.25$ MPa (compressive at top, tensile at bottom — for a sagging beam).

**Shear Stress at $y = 100$ mm from Neutral Axis:**

The maximum shear force occurs at the supports ($V = 60$ kN at $x = 0$ or $x = L$). Take $V = 60$ kN at the support section.

**Compute $Q$ at $y = 100$ mm:**

Area above $y = 100$ mm is the rectangle from $y = 100$ to $y = 200$:
$$A_1 = b \times (d/2 - y) = 200 \times (200 - 100) = 20{,}000 \text{ mm}^2$$

Centroid of $A_1$ from neutral axis:
$$\bar{y} = \frac{y + d/2}{2} = \frac{100 + 200}{2} = 150 \text{ mm}$$

First moment of area:
$$Q = A_1 \cdot \bar{y} = 20{,}000 \times 150 = 3 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^3$$

**Shear Stress:**
$$\tau = \frac{V \cdot Q}{I \cdot b} = \frac{60 \times 10^3 \times 3 \times 10^6}{1.0667 \times 10^9 \times 200}$$

$$\tau = \frac{1.8 \times 10^{11}}{2.133 \times 10^{11}} = 0.8438 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 0.8438 \text{ MPa}$$

**Verification of Parabolic Distribution:**

Using the derived formula:
$$\tau = \frac{6V}{bd^3}\left[\frac{d^2}{4} - y^2\right] = \frac{6 \times 60 \times 10^3}{200 \times 400^3}\left[\frac{400^2}{4} - 100^2\right]$$

$$= \frac{3.6 \times 10^5}{1.28 \times 10^{10}}\left[40000 - 10000\right] = \frac{3.6 \times 10^5 \times 30000}{1.28 \times 10^{10}} = 0.8438 \text{ MPa} \checkmark$$

At $y = 0$: $\tau = \dfrac{6 \times 60000}{200 \times 64 \times 10^6} \times 40000 = 1.125$ MPa (matches $\tau_{max}$) ✓

At $y = 200$ mm: $\tau = \dfrac{6V}{bd^3}\left[40000 - 40000\right] = 0$ ✓ (zero at extreme fibre)

The distribution is confirmed to be **parabolic** with maximum at the neutral axis and zero at the extreme fibres.

---

### **QUESTION B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 3]**

**A T-beam has the following cross-sectional dimensions:**
- **Flange:** 200 mm wide × 40 mm thick
- **Web:** 40 mm wide × 200 mm deep (below the flange)
- **Total depth:** 240 mm

**The beam carries a maximum shear force of 80 kN. Determine:**

**(a)** The position of the **neutral axis** from the top fibre, and the **second moment of area** $I$ about the neutral axis. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The **maximum shear stress** in the section and the **shear stress at the junction** of the flange and the web. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Question B:**

**Given:**
- Flange: $b_f = 200$ mm, $t_f = 40$ mm
- Web: $b_w = 40$ mm, $h_w = 200$ mm
- Total depth $D = 240$ mm
- $V = 80$ kN = 80,000 N

**Step 1 — Areas and Centroids of Component Parts (measured from TOP of section):**

| Part | Area $A$ (mm²) | Centroid $\bar{y}$ from top (mm) | $A \cdot \bar{y}$ (mm³) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Flange | $200 \times 40 = 8000$ | $40/2 = 20$ | $8000 \times 20 = 160{,}000$ |
| Web | $40 \times 200 = 8000$ | $40 + 200/2 = 140$ | $8000 \times 140 = 1{,}120{,}000$ |
| **Total** | **16,000** | — | **1,280,000** |

**Step 2 — Neutral Axis Position (from top):**

$$\bar{y}_{NA} = \frac{\sum A \bar{y}}{\sum A} = \frac{1{,}280{,}000}{16{,}000} = 80 \text{ mm from top}$$

Distance from bottom: $240 - 80 = 160$ mm.

---

##### **Part (a) Solution — Second Moment of Area $I$:**

Using the **parallel axis theorem** for each part: $I = I_{self} + A d^2$ where $d$ is the distance from the part's centroid to the neutral axis.

**Flange:**
$$I_1 = \frac{200 \times 40^3}{12} + 8000 \times (80 - 20)^2 = \frac{200 \times 64{,}000}{12} + 8000 \times 3600$$
$$= 1{,}066{,}667 + 28{,}800{,}000 = 29{,}866{,}667 \text{ mm}^4$$

**Web:**
$$I_2 = \frac{40 \times 200^3}{12} + 8000 \times (80 - 140)^2 = \frac{40 \times 8 \times 10^6}{12} + 8000 \times 3600$$
$$= 26{,}666{,}667 + 28{,}800{,}000 = 55{,}466{,}667 \text{ mm}^4$$

**Total Second Moment of Area:**
$$I = I_1 + I_2 = 29{,}866{,}667 + 55{,}466{,}667 = 85{,}333{,}333 \text{ mm}^4 = 8.533 \times 10^7 \text{ mm}^4$$

---

##### **Part (b) Solution — Shear Stresses:**

**Step 1 — Maximum Shear Stress (at Neutral Axis):**

At the neutral axis ($y = 80$ mm from top, which is 160 mm from bottom), compute $Q$ for the area **above the neutral axis**:

Above NA consists of the entire flange + part of the web from $y = 40$ to $y = 80$ (i.e., 40 mm of web below the flange).

**Flange contribution to $Q$:**
$$Q_{flange} = 8000 \times (80 - 20) = 8000 \times 60 = 480{,}000 \text{ mm}^3$$

**Web portion contribution to $Q$:**
- Area = $40 \times 40 = 1600$ mm²
- Centroid from NA: $\bar{y} = (80 + 40)/2 = 60$ mm

$$Q_{web,partial} = 1600 \times 60 = 96{,}000 \text{ mm}^3$$

**Total $Q$ at NA:**
$$Q_{NA} = 480{,}000 + 96{,}000 = 576{,}000 \text{ mm}^3$$

**Maximum Shear Stress (in web, at neutral axis):**
$$\tau_{max} = \frac{V \cdot Q_{NA}}{I \cdot b_w} = \frac{80{,}000 \times 576{,}000}{85{,}333{,}333 \times 40}$$

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{4.608 \times 10^{10}}{3.4133 \times 10^9} = 13.5 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 13.5 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2 — Shear Stress at Flange-Web Junction (at $y = 40$ mm from top, the bottom of the flange):**

Compute $Q$ for the area **above the junction** (this is just the flange):
$$Q_{junction} = 8000 \times (80 - 20) = 480{,}000 \text{ mm}^3$$

$$\tau_{junction} = \frac{V \cdot Q_{junction}}{I \cdot b_f} = \frac{80{,}000 \times 480{,}000}{85{,}333{,}333 \times 200}$$

$$\tau_{junction} = \frac{3.84 \times 10^{10}}{1.7067 \times 10^{10}} = 2.25 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 2.25 \text{ MPa}$$

**Observation:** The shear stress is **6 times higher** in the web (13.5 MPa) than at the flange-web junction (2.25 MPa), because the web is **5 times thinner** than the flange.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign of $y$:** Students often use $y = +d/2$ at the top and $y = -d/2$ at the bottom but forget that $\sigma$ is a **signed quantity** (compressive negative, tensile positive). Always mention the sign.
> 2. **Neutral Axis ≠ Centroidal Axis in Asymmetric Bending:** For symmetric sections (I, rectangle), the two coincide. For unsymmetric sections (T, L, channel), the neutral axis is still the centroidal axis under *symmetric* bending (no axial force), but the **maximum tensile and compressive stresses are unequal**.
> 3. **Forgetting to use $\tau_{max} = 1.5V/A$ for rectangle:** Many students compute average shear and call it maximum — losing 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Confusing $I$ and $Z$:** $I$ has units of length⁴, $Z$ has units of length³. Mixing them up in $\sigma = M/Z$ versus $\sigma = My/I$ is a frequent error.
> 5. **Missing the $Q$ calculation:** $Q$ is the first moment of the area *above* (or *below*) the level considered. Students often use the wrong area, leading to wrong answers.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST**

### Flexural Stresses (Bending)

- ✅ **Flexure Formula:** $\sigma = \dfrac{M y}{I}$ — normal stress varying linearly with $y$.
- ✅ **Maximum at extreme fibres** ($y = c$): $\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{M}{Z}$.
- ✅ **Neutral Axis:** The locus of zero longitudinal stress; passes through the **centroid** of the cross-section in pure bending.
- ✅ **Section Modulus $Z = I/c$:** Measures bending efficiency; higher $Z$ means less material needed.
- ✅ **Assumptions:** Homogeneous, isotropic, Hookean, plane sections remain plane.
- ✅ **Curvature:** $\dfrac{1}{R} = \dfrac{M}{EI}$ — basis for slope and deflection integration.

### Shear Stresses in Beams

- ✅ **Shear Formula:** $\tau = \dfrac{V Q}{I b}$ — tangential stress.
- ✅ **Maximum at the neutral axis** for rectangular sections; equals $\dfrac{3V}{2A} = 1.5 \tau_{avg}$.
- ✅ **Zero at extreme fibres** (free surface boundary condition).
- ✅ **For circular (solid):** $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{4V}{3A}$.
- ✅ **For I-section:** Maximum in the web; web carries ~90–95% of shear in typical proportions.
- ✅ **First Moment of Area $Q$:** Always the area *above* (or below) the level where $\tau$ is calculated, taken about the neutral axis.

### Key Design Insights

- ✅ **Material placement:** For maximum $I$ at minimum weight, place material as **far from the neutral axis as possible** (I-section, hollow circular).
- ✅ **Failure modes:** Bending → extreme fibres; Shear → web of I-section; both must be checked in design.
- ✅ **Deflection check:** Even if stresses are within limits, **serviceability** (deflection $< L/250$ to $L/360$ for floors) must be verified.
- ✅ **Unit consistency:** Always use **N, mm, MPa** OR **N, m, Pa** — never mix.

### Common KTU Numerical Format

- ✅ **Pattern 1:** Find $\sigma_{max}$ and $\tau_{max}$ for a given loading and cross-section.
- ✅ **Pattern 2:** Design a cross-section for a given allowable $\sigma$ (compute $Z$, then choose section).
- ✅ **Pattern 3:** Compare efficiencies of two cross-sections (which carries more moment for same weight?).
- ✅ **Pattern 4:** For a T or I section, find $I$, $\sigma_{max}$, and $\tau_{max}$ — multi-step problem.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Note for KTU Aspirants:** Module 3 carries high weightage in KTU ESE (End Semester Examination) and Module Test. Master the derivation of both formulas, memorise the shear coefficients ($3/2$ for rectangle, $4/3$ for circle), and practise at least 5–6 numerical problems on I, T, and rectangular sections. The 14-mark questions almost always involve a **T or I section with both bending and shear computations**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pure Bending
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET302 - SOLID MECHANICS AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Pure Bending

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Pure Bending** is defined as the *flexural deformation* of a straight beam in which a **constant bending moment** acts along a finite length of the beam, while the **transverse shear force** is identically **zero** over that same region.

In more formal terms, the cross-section of the beam is subjected only to a **bending moment vector** (no axial force and no shear force). Under this condition, the **radius of curvature** of the deflected beam remains *constant* over the pure bending region, and the **neutral axis**, **centroidal axis**, and **axis of symmetry** all coincide.

Mathematically, the loading condition is expressed as:

$$V(x) = \dfrac{dM}{dx} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad M(x) = \text{constant} = M_0$$

This relationship establishes that wherever the shear force diagram (SFD) shows a **zero plateau**, the corresponding segment in the bending moment diagram (BMD) is a **horizontal straight line**, indicating a *pure bending zone*.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition Check (The Diving Board Analogy):** Imagine you stand on the free end of a diving board. The board is *fixed* at one end (a wall) and *loaded* at the other. Between your feet and the wall, there is a continuous, smoothly changing bending moment — this is **ordinary bending**.
>
> Now picture a **see-saw (teeter-totter)** with two equal children sitting exactly at symmetric positions. The pivot is in the middle. The board does not twist or shear, it just *arcs* uniformly. Each cross-section experiences the **same moment** from one end to the other — this is **pure bending**.

A simpler classroom visualization:

- Take a flexible plastic ruler.
- Place it between your two palms, palms facing each other, with the ruler extending equally on both sides.
- Apply an **equal and opposite couple** at each end.
- The ruler bends into a **perfect circular arc**.
- Anywhere you cut the ruler, the internal moment is the **same magnitude and sign** — this is the textbook definition of pure bending.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Significance:** Pure bending produces the *cleanest possible stress state* for theoretical study. It allows us to isolate flexural stress from shear stress and is the foundation upon which beam-design codes (IS 800, IS 456, Eurocode 3) compute bending capacities.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following **standard elastic constants** govern pure bending analysis:

- **Young's Modulus of Elasticity, E** (units: N/mm² or GPa). For structural steel: **E = 2 × 10⁵ N/mm²**. For concrete: **E ≈ 25 × 10³ N/mm²**.
- **Poisson's Ratio, ν** — typically **0.3** for metals, **0.15–0.20** for concrete.
- **Radius of Curvature, R** (units: mm or m) — geometric measure of bent curvature.
- **Second Moment of Area (Moment of Inertia), I** (units: mm⁴ or m⁴).
- **Section Modulus, Z** (units: mm³ or m³).

## 1.4 Governing Assumptions of the Pure Bending Theory (Euler–Bernoulli)

> [!NOTE]
> These are the *classical assumptions* — the same assumptions that KTU examiners expect you to *state before solving* any bending problem:

1. The beam material is **homogeneous, isotropic, and linearly elastic** (obeys Hooke's Law).
2. The beam is initially **straight** and has a **uniform cross-section**.
3. Plane cross-sections **remain plane and perpendicular to the neutral axis** *after bending* (Euler–Bernoulli hypothesis).
4. The beam is subjected to **pure bending only** — no axial, shear, or torsional loads.
5. **Self-weight is neglected** unless explicitly included in the loading.
6. **Radius of curvature is large** compared to beam dimensions, so that **plane sections remain plane** (small-deflection theory).
7. The **neutral axis** passes through the **centroid** of the cross-section.
8. **Stress does not exceed the proportional limit** so the material remains elastic.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Logic Flow — From Loading to Stress

The path from external loading to internal stress under pure bending proceeds in **five structured steps**:

### Step 1 — Identify the Pure Bending Region
Locate the segment where **V = 0** and **M = constant**. Only this region admits the classical pure-bending stress equation in its simplest form.

### Step 2 — Strain Distribution (Kinematic Step)
A fiber at a distance **y** from the neutral axis has its original length **dx** stretched (or compressed) to **(R + y)·dθ**. Therefore the longitudinal strain is:

$$\varepsilon_{xx} = \dfrac{(R + y)\,d\theta - R\,d\theta}{R\,d\theta} = \dfrac{y}{R}$$

This shows strain varies **linearly** with distance from the neutral axis.

### Step 3 — Stress Distribution (Constitutive Step)
Apply Hooke's Law in its uniaxial form, $\sigma = E\varepsilon$:

$$\sigma_{xx} = E\,\dfrac{y}{R}$$

Stress therefore also varies **linearly with y** — zero at the neutral axis, maximum at the extreme fiber.

### Step 4 — Equilibrium — Zero Net Axial Force
Since pure bending is *moment only*, no net axial force is permitted:

$$\int_A \sigma\,dA = \dfrac{E}{R}\int_A y\,dA = 0$$

This proves that the **neutral axis coincides with the centroidal axis** of the cross-section (because $\int y\,dA = 0$ *only* if measured from the centroid).

### Step 5 — Moment Equilibrium — The Core Bending Equation
Take moments of the elemental stresses about the neutral axis:

$$M = \int_A \sigma \cdot y \, dA = \dfrac{E}{R}\int_A y^2 \, dA = \dfrac{EI}{R}$$

This is the **canonical flexure formula** of Euler–Bernoulli beam theory. Combining it with Step 3 yields the famous triple-equality:

$$\boxed{\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R}}$$

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Tip:** Memorize this table verbatim. Most KTU Part-B (14-mark) questions on pure bending will need 3–4 of these relations in a single problem.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Flexure Formula (triple relation) | $\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R}$ | M in N·mm; I in mm⁴; $\sigma$ in N/mm²; y in mm; E in N/mm²; R in mm | Master equation for bending |
| 2 | Bending Stress at a Fiber | $\sigma = \dfrac{M \cdot y}{I}$ | Same as above | Stress at distance y from N.A. |
| 3 | Extreme Fiber Stress | $\sigma_{\max} = \dfrac{M}{Z} = \dfrac{M \cdot y_{\max}}{I}$ | Z = section modulus in mm³ | Design check against $\sigma_{\text{allowable}}$ |
| 4 | Section Modulus | $Z = \dfrac{I}{y_{\max}}$ | mm³ | Property listed in steel tables |
| 5 | Radius of Curvature | $R = \dfrac{E \cdot I}{M}$ | mm | Geometric measure of flexure |
| 6 | Curvature | $\kappa = \dfrac{1}{R} = \dfrac{M}{E \cdot I}$ | 1/mm | Flexural stiffness indicator |
| 7 | Neutral Axis Position | $\bar{y} = \dfrac{\sum A_i y_i}{\sum A_i}$ | mm | For unsymmetric sections |
| 8 | Moment of Resistance | $M_R = \sigma_{\text{allow}} \cdot Z$ | N·mm | Capacity check in design |
| 9 | Strain at Distance y | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{y}{R}$ | dimensionless | Used in deflection problems |
| 10 | Bending Equation (Deflection) | $\dfrac{1}{R} = \dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2}$ for small slopes | — | Derives $EI\,\dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2} = M(x)$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Sign Convention Pitfall:** In KTU valuation, *tension below the neutral axis* (sagging beam, +ve moment) produces *tensile* $\sigma$ at the bottom fiber. Always state the sign of $\sigma$ and the location of the neutral axis explicitly in your answer.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Pure bending theory underpins virtually every *flexural design* in structural and mechanical engineering:

- **Steel I-beam design (IS 800:2007):** Section modulus Z is the primary design parameter — chosen so $M_{\text{applied}} \leq \sigma_{\text{yield}} \cdot Z / \gamma_{M0}$.
- **Reinforced Concrete Beams (IS 456:2000):** Under-reinforced sections are designed so steel yields first; the lever-arm analysis uses pure-bending kinematics.
- **Machine Shafts:** Bending stress $\sigma_b = \dfrac{32 M}{\pi d^3}$ is obtained directly by substituting the solid-circular moment of inertia $I = \dfrac{\pi d^4}{64}$ into $\sigma = \dfrac{M \cdot y}{I}$ with $y_{\max} = d/2$.
- **Springs, Bridges, Crane Girders, Aircraft Wings, Leaf Springs** — all use flexure-formula as a starting point.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Pure Bending Equation

We derive the **canonical flexure formula** $\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R}$ from first principles, following the Euler–Bernoulli approach. Every algebraic step is shown explicitly, suitable for a 14-mark KTU Part-B question.

---

### Step 1 — Consider an Infinitesimal Beam Element

Take a small element of length **dx** cut from a beam in the pure-bending region. After bending, the element subtends an angle **dθ** at the centre of curvature O. The neutral axis lies at a distance **R** from O, and the axis of symmetry coincides with the neutral axis.

Original length of an elemental fiber at distance **y** from the neutral axis:
$$L_{\text{original}} = R\,d\theta$$

Length of the same fiber after bending (arc length on a circle of radius $R + y$):
$$L_{\text{deformed}} = (R + y)\,d\theta$$

---

### Step 2 — Compute the Longitudinal Strain

By definition of normal strain:
$$\varepsilon = \dfrac{L_{\text{deformed}} - L_{\text{original}}}{L_{\text{original}}}$$

Substituting:
$$\varepsilon = \dfrac{(R + y)\,d\theta - R\,d\theta}{R\,d\theta}$$

Simplifying the numerator (the $R\,d\theta$ terms cancel):
$$\varepsilon = \dfrac{y\,d\theta}{R\,d\theta}$$

Cancelling $d\theta$ in numerator and denominator:
$$\boxed{\varepsilon = \dfrac{y}{R}}$$

**Interpretation:** Strain is purely a geometric quantity, *linear in y*, *independent of material*.

---

### Step 3 — Apply Hooke's Law (Uniaxial Constitutive Relation)

For a material within its elastic limit:
$$\sigma = E\,\varepsilon$$

Substitute the strain from Step 2:
$$\boxed{\sigma = \dfrac{E\,y}{R}}$$

**Interpretation:** The longitudinal bending stress varies **linearly** with the distance y from the neutral axis. It is **zero** at y = 0 (neutral axis) and **maximum** at the extreme fiber $y = y_{\max}$.

---

### Step 4 — Prove the Neutral Axis Coincides with the Centroidal Axis

For *pure bending*, no net axial force is permitted. Equilibrium of horizontal forces gives:
$$\int_A \sigma\,dA = 0$$

Substitute the stress from Step 3:
$$\int_A \dfrac{E\,y}{R}\,dA = 0$$

Factor out the constants:
$$\dfrac{E}{R}\int_A y\,dA = 0$$

Since $E/R$ is non-zero:
$$\int_A y\,dA = 0$$

This integral is, by definition, the **first moment of area** about the neutral axis. The condition $\int y\,dA = 0$ is *only* satisfied when the axis passes through the **centroid** of the cross-section. Hence:

$$\boxed{\text{Neutral Axis} \equiv \text{Centroidal Axis}}$$

---

### Step 5 — Apply Moment Equilibrium

The internal stresses $\sigma$ acting on the cross-section must produce a resultant moment equal in magnitude to the externally applied bending moment **M**. Take moments about the neutral axis:

$$M = \int_A \sigma \cdot y \, dA$$

Substitute $\sigma$ from Step 3:
$$M = \int_A \left(\dfrac{E\,y}{R}\right) y \, dA$$

Combine the two y terms:
$$M = \dfrac{E}{R}\int_A y^2\,dA$$

By definition, $\int y^2\,dA$ is the **second moment of area** (moment of inertia) **I**:
$$M = \dfrac{E\,I}{R}$$

Rearrange to solve for the radius of curvature:
$$\boxed{R = \dfrac{E\,I}{M}}$$

---

### Step 6 — Combine to Get the Flexure Formula

From Step 3: $\sigma = \dfrac{E\,y}{R} \;\Rightarrow\; \dfrac{E}{R} = \dfrac{\sigma}{y}$

From Step 5: $\dfrac{E}{R} = \dfrac{M}{I}$

Equating the two expressions for $E/R$:
$$\dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{M}{I}$$

Solving for $\sigma$ gives the **bending stress equation**:
$$\boxed{\sigma = \dfrac{M \cdot y}{I}}$$

Combining all three gives the **canonical flexure formula** (the full triple relation):

$$\boxed{\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R}}$$

**This is the most important equation in Module 3 of Solid Mechanics and Design.**

---

### Step 7 — Section Modulus and Extreme-Fiber Stress

The maximum bending stress occurs at the extreme fiber $y = y_{\max}$. Substituting:
$$\sigma_{\max} = \dfrac{M \cdot y_{\max}}{I} = \dfrac{M}{I/y_{\max}} = \dfrac{M}{Z}$$

where the **section modulus** is defined as:
$$\boxed{Z = \dfrac{I}{y_{\max}}}$$

For a *symmetric* cross-section, $y_{\max}$ is the distance from the neutral axis to the *outermost* fiber on either side. Standard values:

- **Solid rectangle (b × h):** $I = \dfrac{bh^3}{12},\;\; y_{\max} = h/2,\;\; Z = \dfrac{bh^2}{6}$
- **Solid circle (diameter d):** $I = \dfrac{\pi d^4}{64},\;\; y_{\max} = d/2,\;\; Z = \dfrac{\pi d^3}{32}$
- **Hollow circle (outer d_o, inner d_i):** $I = \dfrac{\pi(d_o^4 - d_i^4)}{64},\;\; Z = \dfrac{\pi(d_o^4 - d_i^4)}{32\,d_o}$
- **Standard I-section:** Use tabulated values; for symmetric I with $I_{xx}$ and depth h, $Z_{xx} = I_{xx}/(h/2)$.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Worked Example Method (KTU-Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard 14-Mark KTU Problem Pattern:** A simply-supported beam of span L carries a load. You must (a) find the maximum bending moment, (b) determine the cross-section dimensions such that the maximum bending stress does not exceed the allowable value, and (c) find the radius of curvature in the pure-bending zone.

| Step | Task | Governing Equation | Common Mistake |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Compute Reactions | $\sum F_y = 0,\;\sum M = 0$ | Forgetting moment of distributed load ($wL^2/2$) |
| 2 | Draw SFD & BMD | $V = dM/dx$ | Confusing point load vs. UDL diagram shape |
| 3 | Identify Pure Bending Region | V = 0, M = constant | Marking the wrong region |
| 4 | Compute $M_{\max}$ | From BMD | Using $M_{\max}$ from wrong span for cantilever |
| 5 | Compute I or Z of Section | Standard formula | Mixing $I_{xx}$ with $I_{yy}$ |
| 6 | Check Bending Stress | $\sigma_{\max} = M_{\max}/Z \leq \sigma_{\text{allow}}$ | Forgetting the factor of safety |
| 7 | Compute Radius of Curvature | $R = EI / M_{\max}$ | Using E in GPa without unit conversion |
| 8 | State Sign of Stress | Tension/compression at top/bottom | Skipping sign convention |

---

## 3.3 Verification of the Pure-Bending Equation — Sanity Check

A quick dimensional check that KTU examiners sometimes award a 1-mark bonus for:

$$\left[\dfrac{M}{I}\right] = \dfrac{\text{N·mm}}{\text{mm}^4} = \dfrac{\text{N}}{\text{mm}^3}$$

$$\left[\dfrac{\sigma}{y}\right] = \dfrac{\text{N/mm}^2}{\text{mm}} = \dfrac{\text{N}}{\text{mm}^3}$$

$$\left[\dfrac{E}{R}\right] = \dfrac{\text{N/mm}^2}{\text{mm}} = \dfrac{\text{N}}{\text{mm}^3}$$

All three terms have **identical units** $\dfrac{\text{N}}{\text{mm}^3}$ — confirming the equation is dimensionally consistent.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram — Pure Bending Analysis Workflow

The following flowchart shows the **step-by-step analytical process** a student should follow when solving a KTU pure-bending problem.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Given Beam and Loading] --> B[Compute Support Reactions]
    B --> C[Draw SFD and BMD]
    C --> D{Is there a Zone with V equals 0?}
    D -- No --> E[Pure Bending Does Not Exist]
    D -- Yes --> F[Identify Pure Bending Region]
    F --> G[Read Constant M from BMD]
    G --> H[Compute Section Properties: I and Z]
    H --> I[Apply Flexure Formula: M over I equals sigma over y equals E over R]
    I --> J[Compute Maximum Bending Stress]
    J --> K{sigma max less than sigma allow?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Design is Safe]
    K -- No --> M[Revise Cross Section]
    M --> H
    I --> N[Compute Radius of Curvature: R equals EI over M]
    I --> O[Compute Curvature kappa equals 1 over R]
    N --> P[Report Final Answer with Units]
    O --> P
    J --> P
    L --> P
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Concept Map of Pure Bending Theory

The following diagram maps the *interconnections* between the **three primary relations** that govern pure bending: the geometric relation, the constitutive relation, and the equilibrium relation.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Geometric: strain equals y over R] --> C[Triple Flexure Formula]
    B[Constitutive: Hookes Law sigma equals E epsilon] --> C
    D[Equilibrium: M equals integral of sigma y dA] --> C
    C --> E[Bending Stress sigma equals M y over I]
    C --> F[Curvature 1 over R equals M over EI]
    C --> G[Section Modulus Z equals I over y max]
    E --> H[Extreme Fiber Stress sigma max equals M over Z]
    G --> H
    F --> I[Radius of Curvature R equals EI over M]
    H --> J[Design Check against sigma allow]
    I --> K[Deflection Calculations]
    J --> L[Final Safe Section]
    K --> L
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Stress Distribution Under Pure Bending

This diagram visualizes the **linear variation of bending stress** across a rectangular cross-section, distinguishing the **tension zone** (bottom, for a sagging moment) from the **compression zone** (top).

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Rectangular Cross Section
        direction TB
        A1[Top Fiber: Maximum Compression sigma c] --> A2[Linear Decrease through Depth]
        A2 --> A3[Neutral Axis at Centroid: sigma equals 0]
        A3 --> A4[Linear Increase toward Bottom]
        A4 --> A5[Bottom Fiber: Maximum Tension sigma t]
    end
    A1 --> B[Stress varies linearly with y]
    A3 --> C[Neutral Axis coincides with Centroid]
    A5 --> D[Tension below NA for Sagging Moment]
    B --> E[Flexure Formula: sigma equals M y over I]
    C --> E
    D --> E
    E --> F[sigma max equals M over Z]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Architecture — Pure Bending vs. Ordinary Bending

The following block diagram clarifies the **distinction** between *pure bending* and *ordinary (non-uniform) bending* that KTU frequently tests.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Beam Subjected to Transverse Loading] --> B{Is Shear Force Zero?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Pure Bending]
    B -- No --> D[Ordinary Bending Combined with Shear]
    C --> E[Bending Moment M is Constant]
    C --> F[Radius of Curvature R is Constant]
    C --> G[Stress Distribution is Purely Linear]
    D --> H[Bending Moment M varies with x]
    D --> I[Shear Stress Exists: tau equals VQ over Ib]
    D --> J[Deflection y from EI d2y over dx2 equals M of x]
    E --> K[Equation: M over I equals sigma over y equals E over R]
    F --> K
    G --> K
    H --> L[Equation: EI d2y over dx2 equals M of x]
    I --> L
    J --> L
```
