<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Definition of Pure Bending `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q.** Define *pure bending*. State the assumptions made in the theory of pure bending.

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Pure Bending** is a state of flexural loading in which a *constant bending moment* acts over a finite length of a beam while the *shear force* is zero over that region.

**Assumptions of the theory of pure bending:**

1. The beam material is **homogeneous, isotropic, and linearly elastic** (obeys Hooke's Law up to the proportional limit).
2. The beam is **initially straight** and has a **uniform cross-section**.
3. **Plane cross-sections remain plane** and **perpendicular to the neutral axis** after bending (Euler–Bernoulli hypothesis).
4. The beam is subjected to **pure bending only** — no axial, shear, or torsional loads.
5. **Self-weight is neglected** unless explicitly included.
6. The **radius of curvature is large** compared to the cross-sectional dimensions (small-deflection theory).
7. The **neutral axis** passes through the **centroid** of the cross-section.
8. The material is **elastic** throughout — stress does not exceed the **proportional limit**.

**(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for any 4 of the above assumptions in brief.)**

---

### Question 2: Significance of Section Modulus `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q.** What is *section modulus*? State its physical significance and write its units.

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** The *section modulus* (denoted **Z**) of a beam cross-section is the ratio of the *second moment of area* (moment of inertia) **I** about the neutral axis to the *distance* $y_{\max}$ of the extreme fiber from the neutral axis.

$$Z = \dfrac{I}{y_{\max}}$$

**Physical Significance:**

- Section modulus is a *purely geometric property* of the cross-section — it depends *only* on the shape and size, not on the material.
- It is the *direct measure of bending strength* of a beam. A higher Z means a higher moment-carrying capacity for the same material.
- In the flexure formula, $\sigma_{\max} = M/Z$, so for a given applied moment, the section that has the **largest Z** will experience the **lowest stress**.

**Units:**

- SI: $\text{mm}^3$ (or $\text{m}^3$).
- FPS: $\text{in}^3$.

**Examples:**

- Solid rectangle (b × h): $Z = \dfrac{b h^2}{6}$.
- Solid circle (diameter d): $Z = \dfrac{\pi d^3}{32}$.

**(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for significance, 1 for unit + example.)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Format Reminder:** Each Part B question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each. Both Part A and Part B questions carry full marks only when units and sign conventions are correctly mentioned.

---

### Part B — Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q. (a)** A simply-supported steel beam of span **6 m** carries a **uniformly distributed load (UDL) of 30 kN/m** over the entire span. The beam has a **rectangular cross-section 200 mm wide × 400 mm deep**. Determine:

1. The maximum bending moment.
2. The maximum bending stress in the beam.
3. The radius of curvature in the pure bending region.

Take **E = 2 × 10⁵ N/mm²** for steel.

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute Reactions:**
By symmetry, $R_A = R_B = \dfrac{wL}{2} = \dfrac{30 \times 6}{2} = 90 \text{ kN}$.

**Step 2 — Bending Moment Equation:**
At a distance $x$ from the left support:
$$M(x) = R_A \cdot x - \dfrac{w x^2}{2} = 90x - 15x^2 \quad (\text{kN·m})$$

**Step 3 — Maximum Bending Moment:**
Maximum moment occurs at the centre where $dM/dx = 0$:
$$90 - 30x = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x = 3 \text{ m}$$

$$M_{\max} = 90(3) - 15(3)^2 = 270 - 135 = 135 \text{ kN·m}$$

$$M_{\max} = 135 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 4 — Section Properties (Rectangle 200 mm × 400 mm):**

$$I = \dfrac{b h^3}{12} = \dfrac{200 \times 400^3}{12} = \dfrac{200 \times 64 \times 10^6}{12} = 1.0667 \times 10^9 \text{ mm}^4$$

$$Z = \dfrac{b h^2}{6} = \dfrac{200 \times 400^2}{6} = \dfrac{200 \times 160000}{6} = 5.333 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^3$$

**Step 5 — Maximum Bending Stress:**

$$\sigma_{\max} = \dfrac{M_{\max}}{Z} = \dfrac{135 \times 10^6}{5.333 \times 10^6} = 25.31 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_{\max} \approx 25.31 \text{ MPa}}$$

**Step 6 — Radius of Curvature:**

$$R = \dfrac{E I}{M_{\max}} = \dfrac{(2 \times 10^5)(1.0667 \times 10^9)}{135 \times 10^6}$$

$$R = \dfrac{2.1333 \times 10^{14}}{1.35 \times 10^8} = 1.5802 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}$$

$$\boxed{R \approx 1580 \text{ m}}$$

---

**Q. (b)** Derive the **bending equation** $\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R}$ for a beam subjected to pure bending. State clearly the assumptions used.

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

*(Full derivation as in Section 3.1 of these notes is to be reproduced in the answer script. Key checkpoints that the examiner looks for:)*

1. Consider a beam element of length dx in pure bending. **(0.5 mark)**
2. State **Euler–Bernoulli assumption** (plane sections remain plane). **(0.5 mark)**
3. Show geometric derivation: $\varepsilon = y/R$. **(1 mark)**
4. Apply Hooke's Law: $\sigma = E y / R$. **(1 mark)**
5. Prove $\int y\,dA = 0$ ⇒ **neutral axis passes through centroid**. **(1 mark)**
6. Take moment equilibrium: $M = \int \sigma y\,dA = EI/R$. **(1.5 marks)**
7. Combine to obtain the final **triple flexure formula**. **(1 mark)**
8. State all 7–8 assumptions in a separate bullet list. **(0.5 mark)**

---

### Part B — Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q. (a)** A cast-iron beam of **T-section** has the following dimensions:

- Flange: **100 mm wide × 20 mm thick**
- Web: **20 mm wide × 80 mm deep** (below the flange)

The beam is subjected to a **bending moment of 5 kN·m** in such a direction that the **flange is in compression** and the **web tip is in tension**.

Compute:

1. The position of the neutral axis from the top of the flange.
2. The maximum tensile and compressive stresses.

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Set Up Reference Axis at Top of Flange:**

Let the top of the flange be the reference axis. We have two rectangles:

| Part | Area (mm²) | $y_i$ (mm) | $A_i \cdot y_i$ (mm³) | $I_{i,\text{own}}$ (mm⁴) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Flange (100 × 20) | $2000$ | $10$ | $20000$ | $\dfrac{100 \times 20^3}{12} = 66{,}667$ |
| Web (20 × 80) | $1600$ | $20 + 40 = 60$ | $96000$ | $\dfrac{20 \times 80^3}{12} = 853{,}333$ |
| **Total** | $\sum A = 3600$ | — | $\sum A_i y_i = 116000$ | $\sum I_{i,\text{own}} = 920000$ |

**Step 2 — Locate Centroidal Axis (Neutral Axis) from Top:**

$$\bar{y} = \dfrac{\sum A_i y_i}{\sum A_i} = \dfrac{116000}{3600} = 32.22 \text{ mm from top}$$

**Step 3 — Total Depth of Section:**

$$h_{\text{total}} = 20 + 80 = 100 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Distances from Neutral Axis:**

- $y_{\text{top}}$ (top of flange) = $32.22$ mm — compression side.
- $y_{\text{bottom}}$ (tip of web) = $100 - 32.22 = 67.78$ mm — tension side.

**Step 5 — Compute Moment of Inertia about Neutral Axis (Parallel Axis Theorem):**

$$I_{\text{NA}} = \sum \left[I_{i,\text{own}} + A_i (\bar{y} - y_i)^2\right]$$

For flange: $I_{f} = 66667 + 2000(32.22 - 10)^2 = 66667 + 2000(22.22)^2 = 66667 + 987{,}580 = 1{,}054{,}247 \text{ mm}^4$

For web: $I_{w} = 853333 + 1600(60 - 32.22)^2 = 853333 + 1600(27.78)^2 = 853333 + 1{,}234{,}664 = 2{,}087{,}997 \text{ mm}^4$

$$I_{\text{NA}} = 1{,}054{,}247 + 2{,}087{,}997 = 3{,}142{,}244 \text{ mm}^4 \approx 3.142 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^4$$

**Step 6 — Compute Maximum Stresses:**

$M = 5 \text{ kN·m} = 5 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}$

Maximum compression (at top, flange):
$$\sigma_c = \dfrac{M \cdot y_{\text{top}}}{I_{\text{NA}}} = \dfrac{5 \times 10^6 \times 32.22}{3.142 \times 10^6} = 51.27 \text{ N/mm}^2 \;(\text{compression})$$

Maximum tension (at bottom, web tip):
$$\sigma_t = \dfrac{M \cdot y_{\text{bottom}}}{I_{\text{NA}}} = \dfrac{5 \times 10^6 \times 67.78}{3.142 \times 10^6} = 107.86 \text{ N/mm}^2 \;(\text{tension})$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_c \approx 51.3 \text{ MPa (compression)}; \quad \sigma_t \approx 107.9 \text{ MPa (tension)}}$$

---

**Q. (b)** Explain the concept of **moment of resistance**. How is it used in the *design of a beam*? Derive the relation $M_R = \sigma_b \cdot Z$ for a rectangular cross-section.

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (2 marks):**
The **moment of resistance** $M_R$ of a beam cross-section is the *internal resisting moment* developed by the bending stresses acting over the cross-section. It is the moment that the cross-section can *safely* resist without exceeding the allowable bending stress $\sigma_b$.

**Conceptual Explanation (2 marks):**
- When an external moment $M$ is applied, the beam develops internal stresses $\sigma$ that vary linearly from $-y_{\max}$ to $+y_{\max}$.
- The resultant of these stresses forms an internal couple whose moment equals $M_R$.
- For safe design: $M_{\text{applied}} \leq M_R$.

**Derivation (3 marks):**

Consider a rectangular cross-section of width **b** and depth **h**. The moment of resistance is obtained by integrating the elemental stresses:

$$M_R = \int_A \sigma \cdot y \, dA$$

The stress at distance y is $\sigma = \dfrac{M \cdot y}{I}$. For *moment of resistance*, we use the **maximum allowable stress** $\sigma_b$ at $y = h/2$:

$$\sigma_b = \dfrac{M_R \cdot (h/2)}{I}$$

The moment of inertia of a rectangle is $I = \dfrac{bh^3}{12}$, so:

$$\sigma_b = \dfrac{M_R \cdot (h/2)}{bh^3/12} = \dfrac{6 M_R}{b h^2}$$

Rearranging for $M_R$:

$$M_R = \dfrac{\sigma_b \cdot b h^2}{6}$$

For a rectangle, section modulus is $Z = \dfrac{bh^2}{6}$, so:

$$\boxed{M_R = \sigma_b \cdot Z}$$

**Design Use (additional credit):**
- Choose $\sigma_b$ based on material and factor of safety.
- Compute required $Z_{\text{req}} = M_{\text{applied}} / \sigma_b$.
- Select a standard section from steel tables with $Z_{\text{provided}} \geq Z_{\text{required}}$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in Pure Bending Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting units in $\sigma_{\max} = M/Z$:** If $M$ is in kN·m and $Z$ is in mm³, you *must* convert to N·mm first. A common error is to mix units and end up with a stress 1000× too small.
> 2. **Mixing up $I$ and $Z$:** Many students write $\sigma = M/I$ (wrong!) instead of $\sigma = M y/I$ or $\sigma_{\max} = M/Z$. KTU examiners *will* deduct a full mark for this.
> 3. **Using the wrong $y$ value for unsymmetric sections:** For a T-section or channel, the neutral axis is *not* at mid-depth. Always compute $\bar{y}$ from the top first.
> 4. **Forgetting the parallel axis theorem:** When computing I of a built-up section, you cannot simply add $I_{\text{own}}$ values — you must add the $A_i d_i^2$ term.
> 5. **Not stating the assumptions:** In any 7-mark derivation, the assumptions carry 0.5–1 mark. Skipping them costs easy marks.
> 6. **Skipping the sign convention:** A *sagging* moment is conventionally positive; tension occurs *below* the neutral axis. Always state it.
> 7. **Confusing curvature and radius of curvature:** $R = EI/M$ is the radius; $\kappa = 1/R = M/EI$ is the curvature. Don't interchange them.
> 8. **Forgetting that pure bending requires V = 0:** A common conceptual mistake in case-study questions is to compute $M_{\max}$ from a non-pure-bending region.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision Card — Pure Bending:**

- ☐ **Pure bending = constant M, V = 0.** Look for *flat plateau* in BMD.
- ☐ **Triple flexure formula:** $\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R}$ — memorize verbatim.
- ☐ **Bending stress:** $\sigma = \dfrac{M \cdot y}{I}$ (at distance y from NA).
- ☐ **Extreme fiber stress:** $\sigma_{\max} = \dfrac{M}{Z} = \dfrac{M}{I/y_{\max}}$.
- ☐ **Section modulus:** $Z = I / y_{\max}$ — listed in steel tables; geometric property only.
- ☐ **Radius of curvature:** $R = \dfrac{EI}{M}$ — large R = small deflection; small R = sharp bend.
- ☐ **Curvature:** $\kappa = \dfrac{1}{R} = \dfrac{M}{EI}$.
- ☐ **Neutral axis** passes through the **centroid** of the cross-section.
- ☐ **Assumptions** (Euler–Bernoulli): homogeneous, isotropic, elastic, plane sections remain plane, no shear.
- ☐ **Standard sections:**
  - Rectangle: $I = bh^3/12$, $Z = bh^2/6$
  - Circle: $I = \pi d^4 / 64$, $Z = \pi d^3 / 32$
  - Hollow circle: $I = \pi(d_o^4 - d_i^4)/64$
  - Built-up: Use parallel axis theorem $I_{\text{NA}} = \sum [I_{\text{own}} + A d^2]$
- ☐ **Sign convention:** Sagging moment → top compression, bottom tension.
- ☐ **Units:** Convert all quantities to N·mm, N/mm², mm³, mm⁴ *before* substituting.
- ☐ **Pure bending vs ordinary bending:** Pure has V = 0, M = constant; ordinary has V ≠ 0 and shear stress $\tau = VQ/(Ib)$ also exists.
- ☐ **Design check:** $M_{\text{applied}} \leq \sigma_{\text{allow}} \cdot Z = M_R$.
- ☐ **Curvature-deformation relation:** $EI \dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2} = M(x)$ — foundation of deflection theory in Module 4.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Derivation of Elastic Flexural Formula
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET302 - SOLID MECHANICS AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Derivation of Elastic Flexural Formula

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Elastic Flexural Formula** (also known as the **Bending Equation** or **Euler–Bernoulli Beam Equation**) is the fundamental governing relationship in the strength of materials that connects the **bending moment** acting on a beam to the **flexural stress** induced in its cross-section, while the beam material remains within its elastic limit.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Standard):**
> The Elastic Flexural Formula is mathematically expressed as:
> $$\frac{M}{I} = \frac{\sigma}{y} = \frac{E}{R}$$
> where every term has a precise physical meaning and SI units. This is the most heavily tested equation in Module 3 of PCPET302.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are holding a **steel ruler** (a cantilever beam) firmly at one end on a table edge and pressing down on the free end with your thumb.

- The **top fibers** of the ruler **stretch** (because the beam curves downward, opening the top surface).
- The **bottom fibers** of the ruler **compress** (because the beam squeezes together on the bottom).
- Somewhere in between, there is a layer that experiences **zero change in length** — this is the **Neutral Axis (NA)**.

The bending stress $\sigma$ at any fiber is directly proportional to its **distance $y$ from the Neutral Axis**. The further a fiber sits from the NA, the more it stretches or compresses, and the higher the stress it carries. The Elastic Flexural Formula quantifies exactly this distribution.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants & Units Reminder**
> - **Young's Modulus ($E$)**: $\text{N/m}^2$ or **Pa** (a material stiffness property)
> - **Moment of Inertia ($I$)**: $\text{m}^4$ or $\text{mm}^4$ (a geometric property of the cross-section)
> - **Bending Moment ($M$)**: $\text{N·m}$ or $\text{N·mm}$
> - **Bending Stress ($\sigma$)**: $\text{N/m}^2$ or **Pa** or **MPa**
> - **Distance from Neutral Axis ($y$)**: $\text{m}$ or $\text{mm}$
> - **Radius of Curvature ($R$)**: $\text{m}$ or $\text{mm}$

## 1.3 Where It Is Used in Real Engineering

| Engineering Field | Practical Application |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Civil Engineering** | Design of RCC beams, steel girders in bridges, slabs in buildings |
| **Mechanical Engineering** | Design of machine shafts, crane jibs, leaf springs, pressure vessel heads |
| **Aerospace** | Wing spar analysis, fuselage frames in aircraft |
| **Automotive** | Chassis frame design, leaf-spring suspension, drive shafts |
| **Marine** | Ship hull girder bending, deck plating under wave loads |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Assumptions of Pure Bending (Euler–Bernoulli)

Before the derivation is valid, the following **five classical assumptions** must be satisfied. Examiners frequently award marks simply for stating these clearly.

1. **Homogeneous & Isotropic Material:** The beam material has identical elastic properties in all directions.
2. **Linear Elasticity:** The material obeys **Hooke's Law** ($\sigma = E\varepsilon$). Stress remains well below the proportional limit.
3. **Pure Bending:** The bending moment is constant along the section under consideration; shear force is zero, so no warping occurs.
4. **Plane Sections Remain Plane & Perpendicular to the Neutral Axis** (kinematic assumption of Euler–Bernoulli theory). Cross-sections rotate as rigid discs without distortion.
5. **No Transverse Compression of Fibers:** Fibers only stretch or compress longitudinally; they do not push into each other radially.

> [!NOTE]
> If assumption 4 is relaxed (sections can shear), we obtain **Timoshenko Beam Theory** — outside the scope of KTU Module 3 but conceptually important.

## 2.2 The Three-Stage Logic of the Derivation (Why & How)

**Stage 1 — Geometric Compatibility (Strain Distribution):**
- *Why:* Because plane sections rotate rigidly, the longitudinal strain $\varepsilon_x$ at any fiber must be linearly proportional to its distance $y$ from the Neutral Axis.
- *How:* The arc length differential gives $\varepsilon_x = \dfrac{y}{R}$, where $R$ is the local radius of curvature.

**Stage 2 — Constitutive Law (Hooke's Law Application):**
- *Why:* Because we are in the elastic regime, stress is linearly proportional to strain.
- *How:* $\sigma = E\varepsilon = E \cdot \dfrac{y}{R}$, which gives the **Flexure Formula** in its stress form: $\dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R}$.

**Stage 3 — Static Equilibrium (Force & Moment Resultants):**
- *Why:* Because the beam has no net axial force and the internal moment $M$ must equal the integral of stress times the moment arm.
- *How:* Integrating $\sigma \, dA$ over the area gives zero axial force (defines NA), and integrating $\sigma y \, dA$ gives the moment $M$, which produces $\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R}$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are examinable. Memorize the units and limiting cases.**

| # | Formula | Description / Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R}$ | Stress distribution across a section (Hooke + Geometry) |
| 2 | $\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{\sigma}{y}$ | **Principal Flexure Formula** (used in ~80% of KTU problems) |
| 3 | $\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{E}{R}$ | Used to compute curvature or radius of bending |
| 4 | $M = \dfrac{EI}{R}$ | Defines **Flexural Rigidity** $EI$ ($\text{N·m}^2$) |
| 5 | $\sigma_{\max} = \dfrac{M}{I/y_{\max}} = \dfrac{M}{Z}$ | Maximum bending stress (where $Z = I/y_{\max}$) |
| 6 | $I = \displaystyle\int_A y^2 \, dA$ | Second moment of area about NA |
| 7 | $Z = \dfrac{I}{y_{\max}}$ | **Section Modulus** ($\text{m}^3$ or $\text{mm}^3$) |
| 8 | $\int_A y \, dA = 0$ | Defines the location of the Neutral Axis through the centroid |
| 9 | $\dfrac{1}{R} = \dfrac{M}{EI}$ | **Curvature Equation** — basis of deflection analysis |
| 10 | $R = \dfrac{EI}{M}$ | Radius of curvature (used when $E$, $I$, $M$ are known) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Section Modulus Quick Values (KTU-favorite shapes):**
> - Rectangle ($b \times d$): $Z = \dfrac{bd^2}{6}$, with $d$ being depth in bending direction.
> - Solid Circle (diameter $D$): $Z = \dfrac{\pi D^3}{32}$.
> - Hollow Circle (outer $D$, inner $d$): $Z = \dfrac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{32 D}$.
> - I-section: $Z = \dfrac{I_{xx}}{d/2}$ (where $d$ is total flange-to-flange depth).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivation (Exhaustive)

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation follows the classical **Popov / Timoshenko / Beer–Johnston** methodology that KTU examiners expect verbatim. Every algebraic step is shown explicitly. **No steps are skipped.**

## 3.1 Setup: Consider a Beam Element Under Pure Bending

Take a small element of length $dx$ cut from a beam subjected to a constant bending moment $M$. Before bending, the element is rectangular. After bending, its neutral surface curves into an arc of radius $R$ subtending an angle $d\theta$ at the center of curvature.

## 3.2 Geometric Compatibility — Strain Derivation

Let us focus on a fiber at a distance $y$ from the Neutral Axis (NA).

**Step 1:** The original length of the fiber (along the NA) is:
$$L_0 = dx$$

**Step 2:** After bending, the NA itself becomes an arc of length:
$$L_0^{\text{new}} = R \, d\theta$$

**Step 3:** A fiber at distance $y$ from the NA (where the radius of its own arc is $R + y$ if $y$ is measured on the convex side, or $R - y$ if on the concave side) will have an arc length of:
$$L_y = (R + y) \, d\theta$$

**Step 4:** The change in length of the fiber is:
$$\Delta L = L_y - L_0 = (R + y) d\theta - R \, d\theta = y \, d\theta$$

**Step 5:** The longitudinal strain at distance $y$ is:
$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0} = \frac{y \, d\theta}{dx}$$

**Step 6:** Since $R \, d\theta = dx$, we can substitute $d\theta = \dfrac{dx}{R}$, giving:
$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{y \cdot (dx / R)}{dx} = \frac{y}{R}$$

**Geometric Result (Bending Strain Distribution):**
$$\boxed{\varepsilon_x = \frac{y}{R}}$$

> **Interpretation:** Strain varies **linearly** with distance $y$ from the NA. Tensile on one side, compressive on the other, zero at the NA.

## 3.3 Constitutive Law — Apply Hooke's Law

Since the material is in the elastic range:
$$\sigma_x = E \cdot \varepsilon_x$$

Substituting the strain from Step 3.2:
$$\sigma_x = E \cdot \frac{y}{R}$$

Rearranging:
$$\boxed{\frac{\sigma_x}{y} = \frac{E}{R}}$$

> **This is the *stress distribution* form of the flexure formula.** It tells us bending stress is also linear in $y$.

## 3.4 Static Equilibrium — Force and Moment Resultants

**Step A (Axial Force):** The internal axial force on the cross-section must be zero (pure bending, no net pull/push). Therefore:
$$F_x = \int_A \sigma_x \, dA = 0$$

Substituting $\sigma_x = E y / R$:
$$F_x = \int_A \frac{E y}{R} \, dA = \frac{E}{R} \int_A y \, dA = 0$$

Since $E/R \neq 0$, this gives the **Neutral Axis condition**:
$$\int_A y \, dA = 0 \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \text{NA passes through the CENTROID of the section.}$$

**Step B (Moment Resultant):** The internal moment $M$ must equal the integral of $\sigma_x \cdot y$ (force times moment arm) over the area:
$$M = \int_A \sigma_x \cdot y \, dA = \int_A \frac{E y}{R} \cdot y \, dA = \frac{E}{R} \int_A y^2 \, dA$$

**Step C (Define Moment of Inertia):** By definition, the second moment of area about the neutral axis is:
$$I = \int_A y^2 \, dA$$

Substituting back:
$$M = \frac{E I}{R}$$

Rearranging:
$$\boxed{\frac{M}{I} = \frac{E}{R}}$$

## 3.5 Combine to Get the Master Equation

From Step 3.3 we have $\dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R}$ and from Step 3.4 we have $\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{E}{R}$. Since both equal the same quantity, we can equate them to obtain the **Unified Elastic Flexural Formula**:

$$\boxed{\frac{M}{I} = \frac{\sigma}{y} = \frac{E}{R}}$$

## 3.6 Final Form — Maximum Bending Stress

The maximum bending stress occurs at the fiber farthest from the neutral axis, i.e., at $y = y_{\max}$. Substituting:
$$\sigma_{\max} = \frac{M \cdot y_{\max}}{I} = \frac{M}{Z}$$

where the **Section Modulus** is defined as:
$$Z = \frac{I}{y_{\max}}$$

This compact form is the form used in 90% of KTU design problems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Master Equation (in KTU board exam language):**
> $$\frac{M}{I} = \frac{\sigma}{y} = \frac{E}{R} \quad \text{(Elastic Flexural Formula)}$$
> $$\sigma_{\max} = \frac{M}{Z} \quad \text{(Maximum bending stress form)}$$
> **Q.E.D. — Derivation complete.**

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Derivation

The diagram below maps the logical flow of the three-pillar derivation (Geometry → Constitutive → Equilibrium) leading to the master equation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Beam under Pure Bending] --> B[Step 1: Geometric Compatibility]
    B --> B1[Plane sections remain plane and perpendicular to NA]
    B1 --> B2[Fiber length change: dL = y dtheta]
    B2 --> B3[Bending strain: eps = y / R]
    
    A --> C[Step 2: Constitutive Law]
    C --> C1[Apply Hooke Law: sigma = E times eps]
    C1 --> C2[Stress distribution: sigma = E y / R]
    C2 --> C3[Result: sigma per y = E per R]
    
    A --> D[Step 3: Static Equilibrium]
    D --> D1[Axial force = 0 leads to NA through centroid]
    D1 --> D2[Moment integral: M = E per R times integral of y squared dA]
    D2 --> D3[Define moment of inertia I = integral of y squared dA]
    D3 --> D4[Result: M per I = E per R]
    
    B3 --> E[Master Equation Assembly]
    C3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    E --> F[Final Result: M per I = sigma per y = E per R]
    F --> G[Max stress form: sigma max = M per Z]
    
    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px
    style F fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#00802b,stroke-width:3px
    style G fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc8800,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 Cross-Section Stress Distribution Block Diagram

This block diagram represents the **linear stress distribution** that arises from the flexure formula, viewed as a functional mapping of distance $y$ to stress $\sigma$.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Neutral Axis y = 0] -->|sigma = 0| B[Zero stress point]
    C[Fiber at y equals ymax top] -->|Tensile sigma max| B
    D[Fiber at y equals ymax bottom] -->|Compressive sigma max| B
    
    subgraph Distribution Profile
    E[Linear Variation: sigma = M y per I]
    end
    
    B --> E
    E --> F[Section Modulus Z = I per ymax]
    E --> G[Design check: sigma max must be less than sigma allow]
    
    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style E fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc8800,stroke-width:2px
    style G fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000
```

## 4.3 Schematic: Cantilever Beam with Bending Stress Profile

The following schematic represents the physical behavior of a cantilever beam under tip load, showing the **uniform curvature region** and the **linear stress distribution** across the depth.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Cantilever Beam Schematic
    direction TB
    S1[Fixed Support at Left Wall] -->|Encastered end| S2[Beam of length L]
    S2 -->|Free end| S3[Tip Load P acting downward]
    S2 -->|Constant EI| S4[Radius of Curvature R is uniform]
    S4 -->|Neutral Axis at centroid| S5[Top fibers in TENSION]
    S4 --> S6[Bottom fibers in COMPRESSION]
    S5 -->|Linear sigma varies as y| S7[sigma max at y equals c top]
    S6 -->|Linear sigma varies as y| S8[sigma max at y equals c bottom]
    end
    
    S7 --> O1[Top Extreme Fiber Stress equals Mc per I]
    S8 --> O2[Bottom Extreme Fiber Stress equals Mc per I]
    O1 --> OUT[Governing Design Equation: sigma max = M per Z]
    O2 --> OUT
    
    style S1 fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000
    style S3 fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000
    style S4 fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc8800,stroke-width:2px
    style OUT fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#00802b,stroke-width:3px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read these diagrams for the exam:** In the KTU board exam, when asked to "sketch the bending stress distribution," draw a rectangular cross-section, mark the NA through the centroid, and show a **straight-line stress profile** crossing zero at the NA. The slope of this line is $M/(EI)$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1.** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> State the **assumptions** used in the derivation of the **pure bending equation** $\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R}$.

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Allocation: 1 mark per valid assumption, 3 of 5 expected):**

The classical Euler–Bernoulli assumptions for pure bending are:
1. The material is **homogeneous and isotropic** and obeys **Hooke's Law** (stress $\propto$ strain) within the elastic limit.
2. The beam is initially **straight** and has a **uniform cross-section**.
3. The beam is subjected to **pure bending** (constant bending moment, zero transverse shear).
4. **Plane cross-sections remain plane and perpendicular to the neutral axis** after bending (no warping).
5. **Modulus of Elasticity $E$ is the same in tension and compression**, and fibers do not press laterally on each other (no transverse strain).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall (Common Mark Loss):** Students often write *only the formula* without stating the assumptions. The KTU valuation key allocates **2 out of 3 marks** to the assumption statements. The formula alone gets only partial credit.

---

> **Question 2.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> Define **Section Modulus** and state its units. How is it related to the **moment of inertia**?

**Model Answer:**

**Section Modulus ($Z$):** It is the ratio of the second moment of area ($I$) about the neutral axis to the distance ($y_{\max}$) of the outermost fiber from the neutral axis.

$$Z = \frac{I}{y_{\max}}$$

**Units:** $\text{mm}^3$ (or $\text{m}^3$ in SI).

**Significance:** It is a purely geometric property that quantifies the **strength efficiency** of a cross-section. A higher $Z$ means lower bending stress for the same applied moment. It directly enables the compact design equation:
$$\sigma_{\max} = \frac{M}{Z}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Important KTU Pattern:** Part B questions in Module 3 carry **internal choice**. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each part (a) and (b) carries 7 marks.

---

### ✅ **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set A]**

**(a)** **[7 Marks]** Derive the **elastic flexural formula** $\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R}$ starting from first principles, clearly stating all **assumptions** and explaining the role of **Hooke's Law**.

**(b)** **[7 Marks]** A simply supported beam of span **4 m** carries a uniformly distributed load (UDL) of **10 kN/m** over its entire length. The cross-section is rectangular with width $b = 120 \text{ mm}$ and depth $d = 240 \text{ mm}$. If $E = 200 \text{ GPa}$, find:
1. The **maximum bending moment**.
2. The **maximum bending stress** ($\sigma_{\max}$).
3. The **radius of curvature** at the section of maximum moment.

---

#### 📝 **Model Solution for Question A**

### Part (a) — Derivation [7 Marks]

*(See SECTION 3 for the full exhaustive derivation. The student should write:)*

**Step 1 — Assumptions [1 mark]:** State all five assumptions listed in SECTION 2.1.

**Step 2 — Geometric Relation [2 marks]:** Show that
$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{y}{R}$$
by analyzing the arc-length change of a fiber at distance $y$ from the NA (full algebra as in SECTION 3.2).

**Step 3 — Hooke's Law [1 mark]:** Apply $\sigma = E\varepsilon$ to obtain
$$\frac{\sigma}{y} = \frac{E}{R}$$

**Step 4 — Equilibrium [2 marks]:**
- $\sum F_x = 0$ gives $\int_A y \, dA = 0$ → NA passes through centroid.
- $\sum M = M = \int_A \sigma y \, dA$ gives $M = \dfrac{EI}{R}$, hence $\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{E}{R}$.

**Step 5 — Combine [1 mark]:** Write the unified equation:
$$\frac{M}{I} = \frac{\sigma}{y} = \frac{E}{R}$$

### Part (b) — Numerical Problem [7 Marks]

**Given:** Span $L = 4 \text{ m}$, UDL $w = 10 \text{ kN/m}$, $b = 120 \text{ mm} = 0.12 \text{ m}$, $d = 240 \text{ mm} = 0.24 \text{ m}$, $E = 200 \text{ GPa} = 200 \times 10^9 \text{ Pa}$.

**Step 1 — Maximum Bending Moment** [2 marks]

For a simply supported beam with UDL, the maximum moment occurs at mid-span:
$$M_{\max} = \frac{w L^2}{8} = \frac{10 \times 4^2}{8} = \frac{10 \times 16}{8} = 20 \text{ kN·m}$$

Converting: $M_{\max} = 20 \times 10^3 \text{ N·m} = 20 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}$.

**Step 2 — Section Modulus and Maximum Stress** [3 marks]

For a rectangle bending about its horizontal centroidal axis:
$$I = \frac{b d^3}{12} = \frac{120 \times 240^3}{12} = \frac{120 \times 1.3824 \times 10^7}{12} = 1.3824 \times 10^8 \text{ mm}^4$$

$$Z = \frac{I}{y_{\max}} = \frac{I}{d/2} = \frac{b d^2}{6} = \frac{120 \times 240^2}{6} = \frac{120 \times 57600}{6} = 1.152 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^3$$

Maximum bending stress:
$$\sigma_{\max} = \frac{M_{\max}}{Z} = \frac{20 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}}{1.152 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^3} = 17.36 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 17.36 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3 — Radius of Curvature** [2 marks]

Using $\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{E}{R}$, solve for $R$:
$$R = \frac{E I}{M_{\max}} = \frac{200 \times 10^3 \text{ N/mm}^2 \times 1.3824 \times 10^8 \text{ mm}^4}{20 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}}$$

$$R = \frac{2.7648 \times 10^{13}}{2.0 \times 10^7} = 1.3824 \times 10^6 \text{ mm} = 1382.4 \text{ m}$$

**Final Answers (boxed for KTU valuation):**
- $M_{\max} = 20 \text{ kN·m}$
- $\sigma_{\max} = 17.36 \text{ MPa}$
- $R = 1382.4 \text{ m}$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Question A):**
> 1. **Unit conversion mismatch** — Mixing kN·m and mm³ causes silent order-of-magnitude errors. Always convert moment to N·mm **before** dividing by $Z$ in mm³.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert $E$ to consistent units** — $E = 200$ GPa must be written as $200 \times 10^3 \text{ N/mm}^2$ when using mm.
> 3. **Using $b$ and $d$ interchangeably** — In a rectangle, $d$ is always the dimension **parallel to the load** (depth), and $b$ is the dimension **perpendicular** to load. Using $b = 240$ instead of $d = 240$ gives a stress off by a factor of 4. This is the **#1 mark-killer** in KTU papers.

---

### ✅ **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set B]**

**(a)** **[7 Marks]** A timber beam of rectangular cross-section $100 \text{ mm} \times 200 \text{ mm}$ is simply supported over a span of **3 m** and carries a **point load of 15 kN at the center**. Determine:
1. The **maximum bending stress** induced in the beam.
2. The **radius of curvature** of the neutral surface at the location of maximum moment.
   Take $E = 12 \text{ GPa}$.

**(b)** **[7 Marks]** State and explain the **Euler–Bernoulli theory of bending**. With the help of a neat sketch, show the **linear variation of bending stress** across the depth of a rectangular cross-section. Indicate the position of the **neutral axis**.

---

#### 📝 **Model Solution for Question B**

### Part (a) — Numerical Problem [7 Marks]

**Given:** $b = 100 \text{ mm}$, $d = 200 \text{ mm}$, $L = 3 \text{ m}$, $P = 15 \text{ kN}$ at center, $E = 12 \text{ GPa} = 12 \times 10^3 \text{ N/mm}^2$.

**Step 1 — Maximum Bending Moment** [1 mark]

For a simply supported beam with a central point load:
$$M_{\max} = \frac{P L}{4} = \frac{15 \times 3}{4} = 11.25 \text{ kN·m} = 11.25 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 2 — Section Modulus** [2 marks]

$$Z = \frac{b d^2}{6} = \frac{100 \times 200^2}{6} = \frac{100 \times 40000}{6} = 6.667 \times 10^5 \text{ mm}^3$$

**Step 3 — Maximum Bending Stress** [2 marks]
$$\sigma_{\max} = \frac{M_{\max}}{Z} = \frac{11.25 \times 10^6}{6.667 \times 10^5} = 16.875 \text{ N/mm}^2 \approx 16.88 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4 — Radius of Curvature** [2 marks]

First compute $I$:
$$I = \frac{b d^3}{12} = \frac{100 \times 200^3}{12} = \frac{100 \times 8 \times 10^6}{12} = 6.667 \times 10^7 \text{ mm}^4$$

Then:
$$R = \frac{E I}{M_{\max}} = \frac{12 \times 10^3 \times 6.667 \times 10^7}{11.25 \times 10^6} = \frac{8.0 \times 10^{11}}{1.125 \times 10^7} = 7.11 \times 10^4 \text{ mm} = 71.11 \text{ m}$$

**Final Answers (boxed for KTU valuation):**
- $\sigma_{\max} \approx 16.88 \text{ MPa}$
- $R \approx 71.11 \text{ m}$

### Part (b) — Theory and Sketch [7 Marks]

**Marks Distribution:**
- [2 marks] Statement of all five Euler–Bernoulli assumptions (write verbatim from SECTION 2.1).
- [2 marks] The bending equation $\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R}$ and a brief explanation of each term.
- [3 marks] Neat sketch (described below) showing linear stress variation, NA through centroid, with top fiber in tension/compression labeled.

**Sketch Description (for the answer script):**
- Draw a rectangle (cross-section). Mark the centroidal horizontal line as **NA**.
- Draw a horizontal axis (vertical position $y$) and a vertical axis (stress $\sigma$).
- Plot a straight line passing through the origin (NA), with positive $\sigma$ above (tension) and negative $\sigma$ below (compression).
- Mark $+y_{\max}$ and $-y_{\max}$ at the top and bottom fibers, and label $\pm\sigma_{\max}$ at the extremes.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Question B):**
> 1. **Forgetting to draw the NA explicitly** — KTU valuation key allots at least 1 mark for clearly showing the NA on the sketch.
> 2. **Showing a curved stress profile** — A common error. Stress varies **linearly** with $y$, NOT parabolically.
> 3. **Using $I = bd^3/12$ for radius of curvature but $Z = bd^2/6$ for stress without verifying depth direction** — make sure $d$ is the bending depth in **both** formulas.

---

## 5.3 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Master These Before the Exam:**

- ✅ The **Elastic Flexural Formula** is $\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R}$.
- ✅ The formula is **valid only within the elastic limit** — never use it for plastic analysis.
- ✅ The **Neutral Axis always passes through the centroid** of the cross-section (proved via $\int_A y\,dA = 0$).
- ✅ **Bending stress is maximum at the fiber farthest from the NA**, given by $\sigma_{\max} = M/Z$ where $Z = I/y_{\max}$.
- ✅ The **Section Modulus $Z$** is a purely geometric property: Rectangle $bd^2/6$, Solid Circle $\pi D^3/32$, Hollow Circle $\pi(D^4-d^4)/(32D)$.
- ✅ **Flexural Rigidity $EI$** has units of $\text{N·m}^2$ — it measures the beam's resistance to bending deformation.
- ✅ **Radius of Curvature** is $R = EI/M$ — small $R$ means a sharply curved beam; large $R$ means nearly straight.
- ✅ For **symmetric cross-sections**, the NA coincides with the axis of symmetry. For **unsymmetric sections** (e.g., a T-beam), compute $\bar{y}$ from the extreme fiber using $\bar{y} = \dfrac{\sum A_i y_i}{\sum A_i}$.
- ✅ **Common error to avoid:** Do not confuse the **elastic section modulus $Z_e$** with the **plastic section modulus $Z_p$** — they are different and the formulas above apply only to elastic design.
- ✅ **Memory trick for the three ratios:** *M-I* (like the chemical element), *σ-y* (like the Greek sigma and y), and *E-R* — all equal.
- ✅ **Exam hack:** In numerical problems, **always box the final answer with units**. KTU examiners explicitly reward unit-correct final answers and often deduct 0.5–1 mark for missing units.

> **End of Module 3 — Derivation of Elastic Flexural Formula Notes**
> **Prepared under KTU 2024 Scheme — PCPET302 / Solid Mechanics and Design**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Shear Stress in Beams of Typical Cross Sections
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCPET302 - SOLID MECHANICS AND DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Shear Stress in Beams of Typical Cross Sections ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Shear Stress in Beams** refers to the vertical (transverse) shear stress distribution across the cross-section of a beam subjected to transverse loading. It arises from the internal shear force $V$ acting on the cross-section and is mathematically expressed by **Jourawski's (or Zhuravskii's) Shear Formula**: $$\tau = \frac{V \cdot Q}{I \cdot b}$$ Where: - $\tau$ = Shear stress at a fiber located at distance $y$ from the Neutral Axis (NA) — measured in **N/mm² (MPa)**. - $V$ = Transverse shear force at the section — measured in **N (Newtons)**. - $Q = \bar{y} \cdot A$ = First Moment of Area of the portion of the cross-section lying above (or below) the fiber about the Neutral Axis — measured in **mm³**. - $I$ = Second Moment of Area (Moment of Inertia) of the entire cross-section about the Neutral Axis — measured in **mm⁴**. - $b$ = Width of the cross-section at the fiber where shear stress is computed — measured in **mm**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This formula assumes a beam of *prismatic cross-section*, with loads applied in a plane of symmetry, and the material is *homogeneous, isotropic, and obeys Hooke's Law*. The shear stress distribution is **parabolic** for solid rectangular and circular sections and becomes more complex for built-up sections like I-beams, T-sections, and channels. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **deck of playing cards** held horizontally between your palms. If you try to slide the top half of the deck relative to the bottom half, friction between the cards resists this sliding. Now imagine that "deck" is actually a beam, and the sliding force is the internal shear force. Each horizontal fiber of the beam experiences a longitudinal shear stress that balances the vertical shear force. When we sum up these longitudinal stresses across a vertical plane, they resolve into the **transverse shear stress** $\tau$ acting on the cross-section. A more practical real-world analogy: think of **plywood**. Plywood is stronger than ordinary wood in bending because the glue between layers resists the **horizontal shearing** that would otherwise cause the layers to slide over one another. This horizontal shear is exactly what Jourawski's formula captures — the tendency of horizontal fibers in a beam to slide past each other when the beam bends. > [!NOTE] > **Key Physical Insight:** Shear stress is **maximum at the Neutral Axis** (where $b$ is typically smallest and $Q$ is largest) and **zero at the top and bottom extreme fibers** (where $Q = 0$). This is why beams often fail first in shear along the neutral axis. ### 1.3 Sign Convention & Assumptions (KTU Standard) - The transverse shear stress $\tau$ at any point is **assumed uniformly distributed** across the width $b$ at that horizontal level. - The shear stress direction is **parallel to the applied shear force** $V$ (i.e., vertical for a vertical shear). - Complementary shear stresses of equal magnitude act on **horizontal planes** (perpendicular to the cross-section). - The cross-section must remain **plane after deformation** (Euler-Bernoulli assumption). - **Units in KTU examinations:** $V$ in N, dimensions in mm → $\tau$ in **MPa (N/mm²)**. Always convert carefully. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Why Shear Stress Varies When a beam bends under transverse loading, horizontal fibers at different levels experience **different longitudinal strains** (since strain $\varepsilon = y/\rho$, where $\rho$ is radius of curvature). This means adjacent fibers want to slip relative to each other. To maintain **compatibility** (no slip), internal shear stresses develop. The resultant of these vertical shear stresses across the width $b$ must equal the applied shear force $V$. ### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic Behind Jourawski's Formula 1. **Equilibrium of a segment** of the beam between two cross-sections separated by $dx$ — the bending stresses on the two faces differ by $dM$. 2. **Resultant longitudinal force** on the segment above the fiber level must be balanced by **shear force on the horizontal plane** at that level. 3. The horizontal shear force per unit length along the beam equals $VQ/I$. 4. Dividing by the width $b$ gives the shear stress $\tau = VQ/(Ib)$. ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **MASTER THESE EQUATIONS — These are the most-tested formulas in KTU ESE for this topic.** | # | Formula | Description | Typical Use | |---|---------|-------------|-------------| | 1 | $\tau = \dfrac{VQ}{Ib}$ | Jourawski's Shear Formula | General shear stress at any fiber | | 2 | $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{V \cdot Q_{max}}{I \cdot b_{min}}$ | Maximum shear stress in cross-section | Occurs at NA, where $b$ is minimum | | 3 | $\tau_{avg} = \dfrac{V}{A}$ | Average shear stress over full section | Quick estimation | | 4 | $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{3V}{2A}$ | For **Rectangular** section ($\tau_{max} = 1.5\,\tau_{avg}$) | Rectangular beams | | 5 | $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{4V}{3A}$ | For **Solid Circular** section ($\tau_{max} = \tfrac{4}{3}\,\tau_{avg}$) | Solid shafts/shafts | | 6 | $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{V}{b \cdot d_w}$ | For **I-section** (approx., web-dominated) | I-beam shear check | | 7 | $\tau_{flange} = \dfrac{V}{I \cdot b_f} \cdot Q_{flange}(y)$ | I-section flange contribution | Flange shear in I-beams | | 8 | $\tau = \dfrac{V}{I \cdot t} \cdot Q(y)$ | For **Thin-walled** sections (constant thickness $t$) | Channels, thin tubes | ### 2.4 Shear Stress Distribution in Typical Cross-Sections (KTU Focus Areas) #### (a) Rectangular Cross-Section ($b \times d$) - **Distribution shape:** Parabolic - **Maximum:** At Neutral Axis - **Minimum:** Zero at top and bottom fibers - $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{3V}{2bd} = 1.5\,\tau_{avg}$ #### (b) Solid Circular Cross-Section (Diameter $D$) - **Distribution shape:** Parabolic (along diameter) - **Maximum:** At Neutral Axis - $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{4V}{3 \cdot \pi D^2/4} = \dfrac{16V}{3\pi D^2} \approx \dfrac{4}{3}\,\tau_{avg}$ #### (c) Hollow Circular Cross-Section (Outer $D$, Inner $d$) - $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{4V}{3A} \cdot \dfrac{D^2 + Dd + d^2}{D^2 + d^2}$ (for thin-walled, simplifies to $\approx \frac{2V}{A}$) #### (d) Standard I-Section (Flange + Web) - **Distribution shape:** Nearly uniform in the **web**, parabolic-like in **flanges** - $\tau$ **jumps** at the flange-web junction due to abrupt change in width - Most shear (~90–95%) is carried by the **web** - Maximum shear stress is computed at the Neutral Axis using web width $b_w$ #### (e) T-Section - Distribution is **asymmetric** since the Neutral Axis does not bisect the section - $\tau_{max}$ occurs at the NA (in the web, just below the flange-web junction) - Computed carefully using $Q_{max}$ above the NA #### (f) Channel Section - Shear stress distribution is **non-symmetric** about the horizontal axis through the centroid - Introduces concept of **Shear Centre** (point where load must act to produce bending without twisting) ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Design of Steel I-Beams:** Web thickness is chosen so that $\tau_{max}$ in the web does not exceed allowable shear stress (typically $0.4 f_y$ as per IS 800). - **Wooden Beams:** Need to check horizontal shear failure along the grain (a common failure mode). - **Riveted/Welded Plate Girders:** Web shear buckling is governed by $\tau_{max}$. - **Shaft Design:** Circular shafts transmit torque and shear; $\tau_{max}$ at NA determines torque capacity. - **Concrete Beams:** Shear reinforcement (stirrups) is designed based on $\tau_v = V/(bd)$, the *nominal* shear stress. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Complete Derivation of Jourawski's Shear Formula **Setup:** Consider a beam segment of length $dx$ between two cross-sections. Let the bending moments at the two ends be $M$ and $M + dM$. Let $V$ be the constant shear force. **Step 1:** Bending stress on a fiber at distance $y$ from NA: $$\sigma = \frac{M \cdot y}{I}$$ **Step 2:** Bending stress on the same fiber at the section $dx$ away: $$\sigma + d\sigma = \frac{(M + dM) \cdot y}{I}$$ **Step 3:** Difference in longitudinal stress on the two faces of the segment (above a fiber at distance $y_1$ from NA): $$d\sigma \cdot dA = \frac{dM \cdot y}{I} \cdot dA = \frac{V \cdot dx \cdot y}{I} \cdot dA$$ (Since $dM = V \cdot dx$ from beam equilibrium.) **Step 4:** Total horizontal force on the area $A'$ above the fiber at $y_1$: $$\begin{aligned} dF_H &= \int_{A'} d\sigma \cdot dA = \frac{V \cdot dx}{I} \int_{A'} y \cdot dA \\ &= \frac{V \cdot dx}{I} \cdot Q \end{aligned}$$ Where $Q = \bar{y} \cdot A'$ is the first moment of area $A'$ about the NA. **Step 5:** This horizontal force is resisted by shear stress $\tau'$ acting on the horizontal plane of area $b \cdot dx$: $$dF_H = \tau' \cdot b \cdot dx$$ **Step 6:** Equating the two expressions for $dF_H$: $$\tau' \cdot b \cdot dx = \frac{V \cdot dx \cdot Q}{I}$$ **Step 7:** Solving for $\tau'$: $$\tau' = \frac{V \cdot Q}{I \cdot b}$$ **Step 8:** By **complementary shear theorem** (equality of shear stresses on perpendicular planes), the **transverse shear stress** on the vertical cross-section equals the longitudinal shear stress: $$\boxed{\tau = \frac{V \cdot Q}{I \cdot b}}$$ This is **Jourawski's Formula**. --- ### 3.2 Derivation for Rectangular Section ($b \times d$) **Step 1:** Section properties: - Area $A = b \cdot d$ - Moment of Inertia $I = \dfrac{bd^3}{12}$ - Distance from NA to extreme fiber: $d/2$ **Step 2:** Consider a fiber at distance $y$ from the NA. The area above this fiber: $$A' = b \cdot \left(\frac{d}{2} - y\right)$$ **Step 3:** Centroid of this area (distance from NA): $$\bar{y} = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{d}{2} + y\right) = \frac{d}{4} + \frac{y}{2}$$ Wait — more carefully, the centroid is at the mid-height of the upper portion: $$\bar{y} = \frac{\frac{d}{2} + y}{2}$$ **Step 4:** First moment of area above the fiber: $$\begin{aligned} Q &= \bar{y} \cdot A' = \frac{\frac{d}{2} + y}{2} \cdot b \cdot \left(\frac{d}{2} - y\right) \\ &= \frac{b}{2} \cdot \left(\frac{d^2}{4} - y^2\right) \\ &= \frac{b}{8}\left(d^2 - 4y^2\right) \end{aligned}$$ **Step 5:** Substitute into Jourawski's formula: $$\begin{aligned} \tau &= \frac{V}{I \cdot b} \cdot \frac{b}{8}\left(d^2 - 4y^2\right) \\ &= \frac{V}{8I}\left(d^2 - 4y^2\right) \\ &= \frac{V}{8 \cdot \frac{bd^3}{12}}\left(d^2 - 4y^2\right) \\ &= \frac{12V}{8bd^3}\left(d^2 - 4y^2\right) \\ &= \frac{3V}{2bd^3}\left(d^2 - 4y^2\right) \end{aligned}$$ **Step 6:** At NA ($y = 0$): $$\boxed{\tau_{max} = \frac{3V}{2bd} = 1.5\,\tau_{avg}}$$ **Step 7:** At extreme fiber ($y = \pm d/2$): $$\tau = \frac{3V}{2bd^3}\left(d^2 - 4 \cdot \frac{d^2}{4}\right) = 0 \;\; ✓$$ --- ### 3.3 Derivation for Solid Circular Section (Diameter $D$) **Step 1:** Geometry — fiber at distance $y$ from center. Width at that level: $$b(y) = 2\sqrt{\left(\frac{D}{2}\right)^2 - y^2} = \sqrt{D^2 - 4y^2}$$ **Step 2:** Area above the fiber is a circular segment. First moment of area $Q(y)$: $$Q(y) = \frac{2}{3}\left(\frac{D}{2}\right)^3 \left[1 - \left(\frac{2y}{D}\right)^2\right]^{3/2}$$ Or more simply: $$Q(y) = \frac{D^3}{12}\left(1 - \frac{4y^2}{D^2}\right)^{3/2}$$ **Step 3:** Substituting into Jourawski's formula (with $I = \pi D^4/64$): $$\begin{aligned} \tau &= \frac{V \cdot Q}{I \cdot b} \\ &= \frac{V \cdot \dfrac{D^3}{12}\left(1 - \dfrac{4y^2}{D^2}\right)^{3/2}}{\dfrac{\pi D^4}{64} \cdot \sqrt{D^2 - 4y^2}} \\ &= \frac{V \cdot D^3 \cdot 64 \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{4y^2}{D^2}\right)^{3/2}}{12 \cdot \pi D^4 \cdot \sqrt{D^2 - 4y^2}} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4:** Simplify using $\left(1 - \frac{4y^2}{D^2}\right)^{3/2} / \sqrt{D^2-4y^2}$: $$= \frac{\left(1 - 4y^2/D^2\right) \cdot D^2}{D^3 \cdot D^2} \cdot \text{terms...}$$ After careful simplification: $$\tau(y) = \frac{4V}{3A}\left[1 - \left(\frac{2y}{D}\right)^2\right]$$ where $A = \pi D^2/4$. **Step 5:** At NA ($y = 0$): $$\boxed{\tau_{max} = \frac{4V}{3A} = \frac{4}{3}\,\tau_{avg} = \frac{16V}{3\pi D^2}}$$ **Step 6:** At extreme fiber ($y = \pm D/2$): $$\tau = 0 \;\; ✓$$ --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Shear Stress in Standard I-Section **Geometry:** Flange width $B$, Flange thickness $t_f$, Web height $h_w$, Web thickness $t_w$, Overall depth $D$. **Step 1:** Locate the Neutral Axis (by symmetry, at mid-depth of the web): $$\bar{Y} = \frac{D}{2} \text{ from top}$$ **Step 2:** Compute Moment of Inertia: $$I = \frac{BD^3}{12} - \frac{(B - t_w) \cdot h_w^3}{12}$$ Or equivalently: $$I = \frac{B \cdot D^3 - (B - t_w) \cdot h_w^3}{12}$$ Where $h_w = D - 2t_f$. **Step 3:** Maximum shear stress at NA: - $Q_{max} = Q_{flange} + Q_{web, \,above\, NA}$ - $Q_{flange} = B \cdot t_f \cdot \left(\frac{D}{2} - \frac{t_f}{2}\right)$ - $Q_{web, \,above\, NA} = t_w \cdot \frac{h_w}{2} \cdot \frac{h_w}{4}$ $$Q_{max} = B t_f \left(\frac{D - t_f}{2}\right) + t_w \cdot \frac{h_w^2}{8}$$ $$\tau_{max} = \frac{V \cdot Q_{max}}{I \cdot t_w}$$ **Step 4:** Shear stress in flange at depth $y$ from top (where $0 \leq y \leq t_f$): Area of flange above the fiber $= B \cdot y$ Centroid distance from NA $= \dfrac{D}{2} - \dfrac{y}{2}$ $$Q_{flange}(y) = B \cdot y \cdot \left(\frac{D}{2} - \frac{y}{2}\right)$$ $$\tau_{flange}(y) = \frac{V}{I \cdot B} \cdot B \cdot y \cdot \left(\frac{D - y}{2}\right) = \frac{V \cdot y(D-y)}{2I}$$ This is **parabolic** in the flange, maximum at $y = D/2$? No — at the bottom of the flange ($y = t_f$). **Step 5:** Shear stress in web at depth $y$ from top (where $t_f \leq y \leq D/2$): $$Q(y) = B t_f \left(\frac{D}{2} - \frac{t_f}{2}\right) + t_w \left(y - t_f\right)\left[\frac{D}{2} - \frac{(y - t_f)}{2}\right]$$ $$\tau_{web}(y) = \frac{V \cdot Q(y)}{I \cdot t_w}$$ **Step 6:** At flange-web junction ($y = t_f$): $$\tau_{junction} = \frac{V \cdot B t_f \cdot (D-t_f)/2}{I \cdot B} = \frac{V t_f (D-t_f)}{2I}$$ There is a **jump** because the width changes from $B$ (flange) to $t_w$ (web). --- ### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — I-Section > **Problem (KTU-style):** An I-section has flange width $B = 120$ mm, flange thickness $t_f = 12$ mm, web thickness $t_w = 8$ mm, and overall depth $D = 200$ mm. The beam carries a shear force $V = 60$ kN. Compute: > (a) Maximum shear stress > (b) Shear stress at the flange-web junction > (c) Percentage of shear carried by web vs. flange **Solution:** **Step 1:** Web height: $$h_w = D - 2 t_f = 200 - 24 = 176 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 2:** Moment of Inertia: $$\begin{aligned} I &= \frac{120 \times 200^3}{12} - \frac{(120-8) \times 176^3}{12} \\ &= \frac{120 \times 8 \times 10^6}{12} - \frac{112 \times 5.45 \times 10^6}{12} \\ &= 80 \times 10^6 - 50.92 \times 10^6 \\ &= 29.08 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^4 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3:** $Q_{max}$ at NA: $$\begin{aligned} Q_{max} &= B t_f \cdot \frac{D-t_f}{2} + t_w \cdot \frac{h_w^2}{8} \\ &= 120 \times 12 \times \frac{200-12}{2} + 8 \times \frac{176^2}{8} \\ &= 120 \times 12 \times 94 + 1 \times 30976 \\ &= 135360 + 30976 \\ &= 166336 \text{ mm}^3 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4:** Maximum shear stress: $$\begin{aligned} \tau_{max} &= \frac{V \cdot Q_{max}}{I \cdot t_w} = \frac{60 \times 10^3 \times 166336}{29.08 \times 10^6 \times 8} \\ &= \frac{9.98 \times 10^9}{2.326 \times 10^8} \\ &\approx 42.91 \text{ MPa} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 5:** Shear at junction: $$\begin{aligned} Q_{junction} &= B t_f \cdot \frac{D-t_f}{2} = 120 \times 12 \times 94 = 135360 \text{ mm}^3 \\ \tau_{junction} &= \frac{60 \times 10^3 \times 135360}{29.08 \times 10^6 \times B} \\ &= \frac{8.12 \times 10^9}{29.08 \times 10^6 \times 120} \\ &= \frac{8.12 \times 10^9}{3.49 \times 10^9} \approx 2.33 \text{ MPa} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 6:** Shear carried by web (approximate): The web carries shear from NA to the junction, integrated over the web height. A reasonable approximation is that **the web carries ~90–95% of total shear**. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** The web, despite being much smaller in cross-sectional area than the flanges, carries the majority of the shear. This is why I-beam webs are sometimes thickened (e.g., in plate girders) or stiffened to prevent shear buckling. --- ### 3.6 Shear Centre (Brief Overview for Channel Sections) For unsymmetric sections like **Channel (C-section)**, if the load is applied through the centroid, the beam will **twist** as well as bend because the resultant shear stress distribution does not pass through the centroid. The **Shear Centre** is the point through which the load must act so that the beam bends **without twisting**. For a standard channel of flange width $b$, flange thickness $t_f$, web height $h_w$, and web thickness $t_w$: $$e = \frac{3 b^2}{h_w + 6 b} \cdot \frac{t_w}{t_w} = \frac{b^2 h_w^2 t_w}{4 I_x}$$ This is a higher-level concept; KTU usually asks only the definition and a basic diagram. --- ### 3.7 Comparison Table — Typical Sections | Cross-Section | Shape of $\tau$ Distribution | $\tau_{max}/\tau_{avg}$ | Location of $\tau_{max}$ | |---|---|---|---| | Rectangular | Parabolic | $1.5$ | Neutral Axis | | Solid Circular | Parabolic (along diameter) | $4/3 \approx 1.333$ | Neutral Axis | | Hollow Circular | Parabolic (approx.) | $\sim 1.27$ (thin) | Neutral Axis | | I-Section | Parabolic in flange, near-uniform in web | $\sim 1.0\text{–}1.2$ | NA in web | | T-Section | Parabolic (asymmetric) | Varies | Neutral Axis | | Thin-walled Tube | Near-uniform | $\sim 2.0$ | Entire wall | | Triangular | Parabolic | $1.5$ | At $h/2$ from apex | ## 4.
