# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Setup: The 2D Stress Element

Consider a 2D plane stress element of unit thickness. The element has:
- A face whose **outward normal is along the +x axis** (called the "x-face") → carries σₓ (normal) and τₓᵧ (shear).
- A face whose **outward normal is along the +y axis** (called the "y-face") → carries σᵧ (normal) and τᵧₓ (shear).
- By **complementary shear principle**: **τₓᵧ = τᵧₓ** (always).

Now we cut the element with an **inclined plane** whose normal makes an angle **θ** (measured CCW from x-axis) with the x-axis. The objective is to determine the **σₙ** (normal stress) and **τₙ** (shear stress) acting on this inclined plane.

## 2.2 The Five-Step Logical Framework

1. **Step 1 — Free Body of the Wedge:** Cut the element with a plane whose outward normal is at angle θ. The wedge-shaped free body has three faces: x-face (area = ds·1), y-face (area = ds·sinθ·1), and inclined face (area = ds·cosθ·1, where ds is the inclined face length).

2. **Step 2 — Resolve Forces on x and y faces:** Multiply each stress by its respective area to get the forces. Resolve these forces into components along and perpendicular to the inclined plane.

3. **Step 3 — Equilibrium Equations:** Sum forces in two directions (along inclined normal and along inclined plane) and equate to zero.

4. **Step 4 — Eliminate Geometric Variables:** Use trigonometric identities (sin²θ = (1−cos2θ)/2, cos²θ = (1+cos2θ)/2, 2sinθcosθ = sin2θ) to obtain compact forms.

5. **Step 5 — Identify Special Planes:** Differentiate σₙ and τₙ with respect to 2θ to find the planes of **principal stress** (dσₙ/dθ = 0) and **maximum shear** (dτₙ/dθ = 0).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------|
| 1 | Normal stress on inclined plane | $\sigma_n = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\sin 2\theta$ | σₙ is the normal stress on plane at angle θ |
| 2 | Shear stress on inclined plane | $\tau_n = -\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\cos 2\theta$ | τₙ is the tangential stress on plane at angle θ |
| 3 | Principal stresses (general 2D) | $\sigma_{1,2} = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$ | σ₁ ≥ σ₂ (always) |
| 4 | Center of Mohr's circle | $\sigma_{avg} = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2}$ | Independent of θ |
| 5 | Radius of Mohr's circle | $R = \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$ | Also = τ_max in pure 2D |
| 6 | Principal plane angle | $\tan 2\theta_p = \dfrac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}$ | θₚ is the angle of plane on which σ₁ or σ₂ acts |
| 7 | Max shear stress plane angle | $\tan 2\theta_s = -\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2\tau_{xy}}$ | θₛ is offset by 45° from θₚ |
| 8 | Maximum shear stress | $\tau_{max} = \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$ | Acts on plane of max shear |
| 9 | Stress on perpendicular plane (θ + 90°) | $\sigma_{\theta+90} = \sigma_x + \sigma_y - \sigma_\theta$ | Quick check formula |
| 10 | Invariant of stress (2D) | $I_1 = \sigma_x + \sigma_y$ | Sum of normal stresses is invariant |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU Board Convention:** All angles (θₚ, θₛ) must be quoted in the **range 0° to 180°** and must be **clearly marked on a diagram**. If a problem gives σₓ = σᵧ, then the principal plane angle formula has a **0/0 indeterminate form** — in this case, the **principal planes are at θₚ = 45° and 135°** (special case to memorize).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Field | Application of Combined Stress Theory |
|-------------------|---------------------------------------|
| **Mechanical Machine Design** | Design of **crankshafts, axles, propeller shafts** subjected to combined bending + torsion. |
| **Pressure Vessel Design** | Hoop and longitudinal stresses in cylindrical shells create biaxial stress states. |
| **Civil/Structural Engineering** | Analysis of **welded and riveted joints** in trusses and bridges. |
| **Aerospace Engineering** | Combined flight loads (bending, torsion, axial) on aircraft wings. |
| **Failure Analysis** | Predicting **yielding (ductile)** or **fracture (brittle)** using **von Mises** or **Maximum Shear Stress** criteria. |

## 2.5 The Mohr's Circle (Graphical Power Tool)

Mohr's Circle, developed by **Christian Otto Mohr (1882)**, is a 2D graphical representation of the stress transformation equations. Every point on the circle represents a different inclined plane's stress state (σₙ, τₙ). The power of Mohr's Circle lies in:

- **Visual clarity:** Principal stresses and maximum shear appear as the extreme points of the circle.
- **Quick problem-solving:** Especially helpful for composite stress states.
- **No algebra errors:** Eliminates sign confusion common in direct substitution.

**Construction Steps (must memorize for KTU 4-mark questions):**
1. Plot a coordinate system with σ on x-axis (positive to the right) and τ on y-axis (positive downward, by convention).
2. Mark **Point X** at coordinates (σₓ, τₓᵧ).
3. Mark **Point Y** at coordinates (σᵧ, −τₓᵧ). [Note the sign flip on τ!]
4. Connect X and Y. The line XY intersects the σ-axis at **C = (σ_avg, 0)**.
5. Draw a circle with center C and radius CX = CY = R.
6. Principal stresses = σ_avg ± R (extremes on x-axis).
7. Maximum shear stress = R (extremes on y-axis).
8. **Angle on Mohr's Circle is 2θ** (double the physical angle).

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Stresses on an Inclined Plane (Foundation Derivation)

**Statement to prove:** Derive the expressions for normal stress σₙ and shear stress τₙ on a plane inclined at angle θ to the x-axis for a 2D stress element carrying σₓ, σᵧ, and τₓᵧ.

**Setup:** Consider a wedge ABC cut from the rectangular stress element. The inclined face AB has outward normal at angle θ (CCW) from the x-axis. Let the length AB = ds (along the inclined plane). Then:
- Area of face AB (inclined face) = ds × 1 = ds
- Area of face BC (vertical, x-face... wait — let me redefine): Let the inclined face be the hypotenuse. The wedge has:
  - Face 1 (along x-face of original element): length ds·cosθ → Area = ds·cosθ
  - Face 2 (along y-face of original element): length ds·sinθ → Area = ds·sinθ
  - Inclined face: length ds → Area = ds

### Step 1: Forces on Each Face (assume all stresses are positive as drawn)

- **On x-face** (area = ds·cosθ): Normal force = σₓ·(ds·cosθ), Shear force = τₓᵧ·(ds·cosθ)
- **On y-face** (area = ds·sinθ): Normal force = σᵧ·(ds·sinθ), Shear force = τᵧₓ·(ds·sinθ)
- **On inclined face** (area = ds): Normal force = σₙ·ds, Shear force = τₙ·ds

### Step 2: Resolve Forces Along Inclined Normal Direction (n-direction, at angle θ from x)

The unit vector along inclined normal = (cosθ, sinθ).
The unit vector along inclined plane (t-direction, perpendicular to n) = (−sinθ, cosθ).

**Forces in n-direction:**
- σₓ·(ds·cosθ) contributes σₓ·ds·cosθ · cosθ (component along n)
- τₓᵧ·(ds·cosθ) contributes τₓᵧ·ds·cosθ · sinθ (shear resolved along n)
- σᵧ·(ds·sinθ) contributes σᵧ·ds·sinθ · sinθ
- τᵧₓ·(ds·sinθ) contributes τᵧₓ·ds·sinθ · cosθ
- Equilibrium: σₙ·ds = sum of all components

Dividing by ds and using τₓᵧ = τᵧₓ:

$$\sigma_n = \sigma_x \cos^2\theta + \sigma_y \sin^2\theta + 2\tau_{xy}\sin\theta\cos\theta$$

### Step 3: Resolve Forces Along Inclined Plane Direction (t-direction)

**Forces in t-direction:**
- σₓ·ds·cosθ contributes σₓ·ds·cosθ · (−sinθ) [n is at +θ, t is at θ+90°, so t·x-component = −sinθ]
- τₓᵧ·ds·cosθ contributes τₓᵧ·ds·cosθ · cosθ
- σᵧ·ds·sinθ contributes σᵧ·ds·sinθ · cosθ
- τᵧₓ·ds·sinθ contributes τᵧₓ·ds·sinθ · sinθ
- Equilibrium: τₙ·ds = sum

Dividing by ds:

$$\tau_n = -(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)\sin\theta\cos\theta + \tau_{xy}(\cos^2\theta - \sin^2\theta)$$

### Step 4: Apply Double-Angle Identities

Using: $\cos^2\theta = \dfrac{1+\cos 2\theta}{2}$, $\sin^2\theta = \dfrac{1-\cos 2\theta}{2}$, $2\sin\theta\cos\theta = \sin 2\theta$, $\cos^2\theta - \sin^2\theta = \cos 2\theta$:

$$\sigma_n = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\sin 2\theta$$

$$\tau_n = -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\cos 2\theta$$

**Q.E.D.** ✅

## 3.2 Derivation: Principal Stresses

**Statement:** Find the value of θ at which σₙ is **maximum** (principal angle) and the corresponding σₙ (principal stress).

### Step 1: Differentiate σₙ with respect to 2θ

Let φ = 2θ. Then:

$$\sigma_n = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos\phi + \tau_{xy}\sin\phi$$

### Step 2: Set derivative to zero for extremum

$$\frac{d\sigma_n}{d\phi} = -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin\phi + \tau_{xy}\cos\phi = 0$$

### Step 3: Solve for tan(2θₚ)

$$\tan 2\theta_p = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}$$

This gives two solutions for 2θₚ differing by 180°, i.e., two solutions for θₚ differing by 90° — one corresponds to σ₁ (max), the other to σ₂ (min).

### Step 4: Substitute back to get principal stress values

We need to find sin(2θₚ) and cos(2θₚ) in terms of given stresses. From the right-triangle construction:
- Adjacent side = (σₓ − σᵧ)/2
- Opposite side = τₓᵧ
- Hypotenuse = R = √[((σₓ − σᵧ)/2)² + τₓᵧ²]

Therefore:
- $\cos 2\theta_p = \dfrac{(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)/2}{R}$
- $\sin 2\theta_p = \dfrac{\tau_{xy}}{R}$

Substituting into σₙ:

$$\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \left[\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2} \cdot \frac{(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)/2}{R} + \tau_{xy} \cdot \frac{\tau_{xy}}{R}\right]$$

$$\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \frac{1}{R}\left[\frac{(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)^2}{4} + \tau_{xy}^2\right]$$

Since $R^2 = \left(\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2 = \dfrac{(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)^2}{4} + \tau_{xy}^2$:

$$\boxed{\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}}$$

**Q.E.D.** ✅

## 3.3 Derivation: Maximum Shear Stress

**Statement:** Find the plane on which τₙ is maximum and its value.

### Step 1: Differentiate τₙ with respect to 2θ

$$\frac{d\tau_n}{d\phi} = -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos\phi - \tau_{xy}\sin\phi = 0$$

### Step 2: Solve for tan(2θₛ)

$$\tan 2\theta_s = -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2\tau_{xy}}$$

Notice that $\tan(2\theta_s) = \tan(2\theta_p + 90°)$... **wait, let me verify**:

Using $\tan(2\theta_p) = \dfrac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}$ and $\tan(2\theta_s) = -\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2\tau_{xy}}$:

$$\tan 2\theta_p \cdot \tan 2\theta_s = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y} \cdot \left(-\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2\tau_{xy}}\right) = -1$$

Since the product of tangents is −1, we have $2\theta_s = 2\theta_p + 90°$ → $\theta_s = \theta_p + 45°$.

**Conclusion:** The plane of maximum shear is always at **45°** to the principal plane. ✅

### Step 3: Substitute to find τ_max

Using the analog of the construction:
- $\cos 2\theta_s = \dfrac{-\tau_{xy}}{R}$
- $\sin 2\theta_s = \dfrac{(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)/2}{R}$

Substituting into τₙ expression:

$$\tau_{max} = -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2} \cdot \frac{(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)/2}{R} + \tau_{xy} \cdot \frac{-\tau_{xy}}{R}$$

$$\tau_{max} = -\frac{1}{R}\left[\frac{(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)^2}{4} + \tau_{xy}^2\right] = -\frac{R^2}{R} = -R$$

Taking magnitude (since shear stress magnitude is what we care about for yielding):

$$\boxed{\tau_{max} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check:** The magnitude τ_max = R (the Mohr's circle radius). This makes geometric sense — the extreme y-coordinate on a circle of radius R is ±R.

## 3.4 Special Case: Uniaxial Stress State (Pure Tension/Compression)

If σₓ = σ, σᵧ = 0, τₓᵧ = 0:
- σ₁,₂ = σ/2 ± σ/2 → σ₁ = σ, σ₂ = 0
- tan(2θₚ) = 0/σ = 0 → 2θₚ = 0° or 180° → θₚ = 0° or 90°
- This says: principal planes are at 0° and 90°. The maximum normal stress σ acts on the cross-section perpendicular to loading. ✓
- τ_max = √(σ/2)² = σ/2 → occurs at θ = 45° (i.e., plane of max shear in pure tension is at 45° to the cross-section). This is why **ductile tensile specimens fail along a 45° plane** (slip band theory).

## 3.5 Special Case: Pure Shear (No Direct Stress)

If σₓ = σᵧ = 0, τₓᵧ = τ:
- σ₁,₂ = 0 ± τ → σ₁ = +τ, σ₂ = −τ
- tan(2θₚ) = 2τ/0 → 2θₚ = 90° → θₚ = 45°
- Principal planes are at 45° to the original faces.
- τ_max = τ (the same as input — already a state of pure shear, no larger shear exists).
- This is the basis for **torsion of circular shafts**: a fiber on the surface in pure torsion sees pure shear, equivalent to biaxial tension-compression at 45°.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Stress Transformation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Given 2D Stress Element] --> B[Identify sigma_x, sigma_y, tau_xy]
    B --> C[Compute Average Stress: sigma_avg equals sigma_x + sigma_y over 2]
    C --> D[Compute Radius: R equals sqrt of sigma_x minus sigma_y over 2 squared plus tau_xy squared]
    D --> E[Draw Mohrs Circle]
    E --> F[Mark Principal Stress Points on Sigma Axis]
    E --> G[Mark Maximum Shear Stress Points on Tau Axis]
    F --> H[Compute Principal Angles from Circle Geometry]
    G --> I[Compute Max Shear Plane Angles: theta_s equals theta_p plus 45 degrees]
    H --> J[Output Principal Stresses sigma_1 and sigma_2]
    I --> K[Output Maximum Shear Stress tau_max]
    J --> L[Application: Design Verification]
    K --> L
    L --> M[Compare with Material Yield Strength]
    L --> N[Apply Failure Theory: Von Mises or Tresca]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: 2D Stress Element with Inclined Plane

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Original X-Face] -->|carries sigma_x and tau_xy| Q1[Stress Element]
    P2[Original Y-Face] -->|carries sigma_y and tau_yx| Q1
    Q1 --> R1[Cut at angle theta]
    R1 --> S1[Inclined Plane with normal n at angle theta CCW from X axis]
    S1 --> T1[Normal Stress sigma_n on inclined plane]
    S1 --> T2[Shear Stress tau_n on inclined plane]
    T1 --> U1[Equilibrium equations and transformation formulas]
    T2 --> U1
    U1 --> V1[Principal Stress: d sigma_n over d theta equals 0]
    U1 --> V2[Max Shear Stress: d tau_n over d theta equals 0]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Mohr's Circle Architecture (Block-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Stress State Input: sigma_x, sigma_y, tau_xy] --> B[Plot Point X at sigma_x, tau_xy on Mohr Plane]
    A --> C[Plot Point Y at sigma_y, negative tau_xy on Mohr Plane]
    B --> D[Connect X and Y to find center C on sigma axis]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Center C is at sigma_avg equals sigma_x plus sigma_y over 2, 0]
    D --> F[Radius R equals distance from C to X equals distance from C to Y]
    E --> G[Draw Circle of radius R centered at C]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Principal Stress sigma_1 is rightmost point on sigma axis: sigma_avg plus R]
    G --> I[Principal Stress sigma_2 is leftmost point on sigma axis: sigma_avg minus R]
    G --> J[Max Shear Stress tau_max equals R, topmost and bottommost points]
    H --> K[Angle from X to sigma_1 point on circle equals 2 theta_p]
    I --> K
    J --> L[Angle from X to tau_max point on circle equals 2 theta_s]
    K --> M[Output: Physical principal angle theta_p equals half of angle on circle]
    L --> N[Output: Physical max shear angle theta_s equals half of angle on circle]
```

## 4.4 Conceptual Mermaid: Real-World Application Flow (Drive Shaft)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Engine Torque T] --> D[Shaft Cross Section]
    B[Vehicle Weight W] --> D
    C[Bending Moment M from W] --> D
    A --> E[Torsional Shear Stress: tau equals 16T over pi d cubed]
    C --> F[Bending Normal Stress: sigma equals 32M over pi d cubed]
    D --> G[Surface Fiber of Shaft]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Combined Stress State: sigma and tau simultaneously]
    H --> I[Apply Principal Stress Formula]
    I --> J[Compute sigma_1 and sigma_2]
    J --> K[Apply von Mises or Maximum Shear Failure Theory]
    K --> L[Shaft Safety Factor Verification]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Visualization Tip:** When drawing Mohr's circle in the KTU exam:
> 1. **σ-axis** is horizontal (positive to the right)
> 2. **τ-axis** is vertical (positive **downward** by the standard Mohr convention — this is opposite to the mathematical convention)
> 3. Plot **X-face point first** as (σₓ, +τₓᵧ) and **Y-face point** as (σᵧ, −τₓᵧ)
> 4. The **angle 2θ** is measured on Mohr's circle, then divided by 2 to get the physical angle θ.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2023]
**Define principal stresses and principal planes. What is the significance of these quantities in design?**

**Model Answer:**

**Principal Stresses:** The maximum and minimum normal stresses that occur on a particular pair of perpendicular planes at a point in a stressed material are called **principal stresses**. They are denoted as **σ₁** (maximum) and **σ₂** (minimum) for a 2D stress state, with σ₁ ≥ σ₂. The corresponding shear stresses on these planes are **zero**.

**Principal Planes:** The planes on which the principal stresses act (i.e., the planes on which shear stress vanishes) are called **principal planes**.

**Significance in Design:**
- Principal stresses govern **brittle material fracture** (e.g., cast iron, concrete) via the **Maximum Normal Stress Theory (Rankine's Theory)**.
- They form the basis for **failure theories** like Maximum Shear Stress (Tresca) and Distortion Energy (von Mises).
- Knowing principal stresses allows engineers to **orient components optimally** so that the working stress aligns with the principal stress direction.
- They are essential for **fatigue analysis**, where the principal stress range determines crack initiation.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]
**State the expression for maximum shear stress in a 2D stress system. On what plane does it act?**

**Model Answer:**

For a 2D stress system with normal stresses σₓ, σᵧ and shear stress τₓᵧ on mutually perpendicular planes, the **maximum shear stress** is given by:

$$\tau_{max} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$$

This maximum shear stress acts on a plane that is inclined at an angle **θₛ** to the x-axis, where:

$$\tan 2\theta_s = -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2\tau_{xy}}$$

**Key observation:** The plane of maximum shear is always at **45°** to the principal plane (i.e., θₛ = θₚ ± 45°). On this plane, the normal stress equals the **average stress** (σₓ + σᵧ)/2.

> [!NOTE]
> **3-mark KTU Note:** Always mention both the formula and the plane angle expression for full marks. A 2-mark answer is acceptable if you only write the formula.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]

**(a)** A steel shaft of diameter 60 mm is subjected to a bending moment of 4 kN·m and a torsional moment of 6 kN·m. Determine:
   (i) The normal and shear stresses on the surface of the shaft. **[4 marks]**
   (ii) The magnitudes and directions of the principal stresses at the surface. **[5 marks]**
   (iii) The maximum shear stress at the surface. **[3 marks]**
   (iv) The diameter of the shaft required if the maximum shear stress is not to exceed 60 MPa. **[2 marks]**

**(b)** A point in a machine component is subjected to the following stresses on two perpendicular planes: σₓ = 80 MPa (tensile), σᵧ = 40 MPa (compressive), τₓᵧ = 30 MPa. Using **Mohr's circle**, determine the principal stresses and the planes on which they act. **[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(a):

**Given:**
- Diameter d = 60 mm = 0.06 m → Radius r = 0.03 m
- Bending moment M = 4 kN·m = 4 × 10⁶ N·mm
- Torsional moment T = 6 kN·m = 6 × 10⁶ N·mm

**Step 1: Bending normal stress (σ) on the surface**

The bending stress formula: $\sigma = \dfrac{32M}{\pi d^3}$

$$\sigma = \frac{32 \times 4 \times 10^6}{\pi \times (60)^3} = \frac{128 \times 10^6}{\pi \times 216000} = \frac{128 \times 10^6}{678584}$$

$$\sigma = 188.62 \text{ MPa (tensile)}$$

**Step 2: Torsional shear stress (τ) on the surface**

The torsion formula: $\tau = \dfrac{16T}{\pi d^3}$

$$\tau = \frac{16 \times 6 \times 10^6}{\pi \times (60)^3} = \frac{96 \times 10^6}{678584}$$

$$\tau = 141.47 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3: Set up the 2D stress element**

At the surface of the shaft (fiber experiencing max stresses), we have:
- σₓ = 188.62 MPa, σᵧ = 0 (lateral direction unstressed)
- τₓᵧ = 141.47 MPa

**Step 4: Principal Stresses**

Using $\sigma_{1,2} = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$:

$$\frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} = \frac{188.62 + 0}{2} = 94.31 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\sqrt{\left(\frac{188.62}{2}\right)^2 + (141.47)^2} = \sqrt{(94.31)^2 + (141.47)^2} = \sqrt{8894.4 + 20013.8}$$

$$= \sqrt{28908.2} = 170.02 \text{ MPa}$$

Therefore:
- σ₁ = 94.31 + 170.02 = **264.33 MPa**
- σ₂ = 94.31 − 170.02 = **−75.71 MPa** (compressive)

**Step 5: Principal Plane Angle**

$$\tan 2\theta_p = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y} = \frac{2 \times 141.47}{188.62} = 1.500$$

$$2\theta_p = \tan^{-1}(1.500) = 56.31° \Rightarrow \theta_p = 28.15°$$

**Step 6: Maximum Shear Stress**

$$\tau_{max} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{188.62}{2}\right)^2 + (141.47)^2} = 170.02 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 7: Required Diameter for τ_max = 60 MPa**

The new torsion formula: $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{16}{\pi d^3}\sqrt{M^2 + T^2}$

$$d^3 = \frac{16}{\pi \times 60} \sqrt{(4 \times 10^6)^2 + (6 \times 10^6)^2}$$

$$d^3 = \frac{16}{188.496} \sqrt{16 \times 10^{12} + 36 \times 10^{12}} = \frac{16}{188.496} \times \sqrt{52 \times 10^{12}}$$

$$d^3 = \frac{16}{188.496} \times 7.211 \times 10^6 = 6.121 \times 10^5 \text{ mm}^3$$

$$d = (6.121 \times 10^5)^{1/3} = 84.95 \text{ mm} \approx 85 \text{ mm}$$

**Answer Summary:** σ = 188.62 MPa, τ = 141.47 MPa, σ₁ = 264.33 MPa, σ₂ = −75.71 MPa, θₚ = 28.15°, τ_max = 170.02 MPa, d_required = 85 mm.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(b):

**Given:** σₓ = +80 MPa, σᵧ = −40 MPa, τₓᵧ = +30 MPa

**Step 1: Compute average stress (center of Mohr's circle)**

$$\sigma_{avg} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} = \frac{80 + (-40)}{2} = 20 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2: Compute radius of Mohr's circle**

$$R = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{80-(-40)}{2}\right)^2 + (30)^2}$$

$$R = \sqrt{(60)^2 + (30)^2} = \sqrt{3600 + 900} = \sqrt{4500} = 67.08 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3: Principal stresses**

- σ₁ = σ_avg + R = 20 + 67.08 = **87.08 MPa**
- σ₂ = σ_avg − R = 20 − 67.08 = **−47.08 MPa**

**Step 4: Principal plane angle**

$$\tan 2\theta_p = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y} = \frac{2 \times 30}{80 - (-40)} = \frac{60}{120} = 0.5$$

$$2\theta_p = \tan^{-1}(0.5) = 26.57° \Rightarrow \theta_p = 13.28°$$

**Step 5: Verification on Mohr's circle**

Plot X = (80, 30) and Y = (−40, −30). Center C = (20, 0). Distance from C to X:

$$\sqrt{(80-20)^2 + (30)^2} = \sqrt{3600 + 900} = \sqrt{4500} = 67.08 \text{ MPa} ✓$$

The angle 2θₚ on Mohr's circle from X to σ₁ point:

$$\tan 2\theta_p = \frac{30}{60} = 0.5 \Rightarrow 2\theta_p = 26.57° \Rightarrow \theta_p = 13.28° ✓$$

**Answer Summary:** σ₁ = 87.08 MPa at θₚ = 13.28°, σ₂ = −47.08 MPa at θₚ + 90° = 103.28°.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Alternative Choice]

**(a)** A circular bar of 50 mm diameter is subjected to an axial tensile load of 100 kN and a torque of 2 kN·m. Calculate:
   (i) The normal and shear stresses on a plane inclined at 30° to the axis of the bar. **[6 marks]**
   (ii) The maximum and minimum principal stresses. **[4 marks]**
   (iii) The factor of safety according to the Maximum Normal Stress Theory, if the material has an ultimate tensile strength of 400 MPa. **[4 marks]**

**(b)** The stresses on two perpendicular planes through a point in a stressed material are: σₓ = 60 MPa, σᵧ = 20 MPa, τₓᵧ = 25 MPa. Find the stresses on a plane inclined at 30° to the x-axis. Also, find the maximum shear stress and the plane on which it acts. **[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(a):

**Given:** d = 50 mm, A = π/4 × 50² = 1963.5 mm²
- Axial load P = 100 kN = 100,000 N
- Torque T = 2 kN·m = 2 × 10⁶ N·mm

**Step 1: Axial normal stress**

$$\sigma_x = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{100000}{1963.5} = 50.93 \text{ MPa (tensile)}$$

**Step 2: Torsional shear stress**

$$\tau_{xy} = \frac{16T}{\pi d^3} = \frac{16 \times 2 \times 10^6}{\pi \times (50)^3} = \frac{32 \times 10^6}{392699} = 81.49 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3: Stresses on inclined plane (θ = 30° from x-axis)**

Using transformation formulas with σₓ = 50.93, σᵧ = 0, τₓᵧ = 81.49:

$$\sigma_n = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\sin 2\theta$$

$$\sigma_n = \frac{50.93}{2} + \frac{50.93}{2}\cos 60° + 81.49 \times \sin 60°$$

$$\sigma_n = 25.465 + 25.465 \times 0.5 + 81.49 \times 0.866$$

$$\sigma_n = 25.465 + 12.732 + 70.570 = 108.77 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\tau_n = -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\cos 2\theta$$

$$\tau_n = -25.465 \times 0.866 + 81.49 \times 0.5 = -22.05 + 40.75 = 18.70 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4: Principal stresses**

$$\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{50.93 + 0}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{50.93}{2}\right)^2 + (81.49)^2}$$

$$\sigma_{1,2} = 25.465 \pm \sqrt{648.5 + 6640.7} = 25.465 \pm \sqrt{7289.2} = 25.465 \pm 85.38$$

- σ₁ = **110.84 MPa**
- σ₂ = **−59.92 MPa** (compressive)

**Step 5: Factor of Safety (Maximum Normal Stress Theory)**

According to Rankine's theory, FOS = σ_ultimate / σ_max (where σ_max is the maximum principal stress):

$$FOS = \frac{400}{110.84} = 3.61$$

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(b):

**Given:** σₓ = 60 MPa, σᵧ = 20 MPa, τₓᵧ = 25 MPa, θ = 30°

**Step 1: Normal and shear stresses at θ = 30°**

2θ = 60°, cos(60°) = 0.5, sin(60°) = 0.866

$$\sigma_n = \frac{60+20}{2} + \frac{60-20}{2}\cos 60° + 25\sin 60°$$

$$\sigma_n = 40 + 20 \times 0.5 + 25 \times 0.866 = 40 + 10 + 21.65 = 71.65 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\tau_n = -\frac{60-20}{2}\sin 60° + 25\cos 60° = -20 \times 0.866 + 25 \times 0.5 = -17.32 + 12.5 = -4.82 \text{ MPa}$$

(The negative sign indicates the actual shear acts opposite to the assumed direction.)

**Step 2: Maximum shear stress**

$$\tau_{max} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{60-20}{2}\right)^2 + (25)^2} = \sqrt{400 + 625} = \sqrt{1025} = 32.02 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3: Plane of maximum shear**

$$\tan 2\theta_s = -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2\tau_{xy}} = -\frac{40}{50} = -0.8$$

$$2\theta_s = \tan^{-1}(-0.8) = -38.66° \text{ (or equivalently } 141.34°\text{)}$$

$$\theta_s = -19.33° \text{ (or } 70.67°\text{)}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **COMMON MISTAKES THAT COST MARKS IN KTU EXAMS:**
>
> 1. **Sign Error on Shear Stress in Mohr's Circle (–2 to –3 marks):** Many students forget to **negate** the shear stress when plotting the y-face point. **Remember: Y-point = (σᵧ, −τₓᵧ)**, NOT (σᵧ, +τₓᵧ). This single sign error can corrupt the entire principal stress calculation.
>
> 2. **Confusion Between τ on Inclined Plane vs τ_max (–1 mark):** A common trap is to equate the shear stress on the inclined plane (τₙ) with the maximum shear stress (τ_max). They are equal ONLY when θ = θₛ. Always check the plane angle.
>
> 3. **Forgetting to Convert Units (–1 mark):** kN·m must be converted to N·mm (multiply by 10⁶) before plugging into formulas that use mm. The same applies for cm, m, etc.
>
> 4. **Indeterminate 0/0 Case (–2 marks):** When σₓ = σᵧ (e.g., in a pressure vessel under equal biaxial tension), the principal angle formula gives 0/0. The correct answer is **θₚ = 45°** (not 0°). This is a frequently tested special case.
>
> 5. **Principal Stress Sign Convention:** σ₁ is the **algebraically largest** (most positive) and σ₂ is the smallest (most negative). Don't just assign "major" to whichever is in tension.
>
> 6. **Drawing Mohr's Circle Without Scale (–1 mark):** Always show a properly scaled plot with axes labeled, points marked, and the center and radius identified. A "sketch" without a scale gets partial credit only.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ☐ **State of stress at a point in 2D** is defined by 3 components: σₓ, σᵧ, τₓᵧ (with τₓᵧ = τᵧₓ by complementary shear).
- ☐ **Transformation equations** for stress on a plane at angle θ:
   - $\sigma_n = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\sin 2\theta$
   - $\tau_n = -\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\cos 2\theta$
- ☐ **Principal stresses formula:** $\sigma_{1,2} = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$
- ☐ **Principal plane angle:** $\tan 2\theta_p = \dfrac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}$ (special case σₓ=σᵧ gives θₚ=45°)
- ☐ **Maximum shear stress:** $\tau_{max} = \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$ (always equal to Mohr's circle radius R)
- ☐ **Plane of max shear is 45° from principal plane:** $\theta_s = \theta_p + 45°$
- ☐ **Mohr's Circle construction order:** Plot X-face (σₓ, +τₓᵧ), Y-face (σᵧ, −τₓᵧ), find center C on σ-axis, draw circle, read off σ₁, σ₂, τ_max.
- ☐ **Invariants:** σₓ + σᵧ = σ₁ + σ₂ (sum is invariant), useful for verification.
- ☐ **Real-world applications:** Drive shafts (bending + torsion), pressure vessels, riveted joints, welded structures.
- ☐ **Failure theories used with combined stresses:** Maximum Shear Stress (Tresca) and Distortion Energy (von Mises) are most common for ductile materials.
- ☐ **Quick fact check:** Pure uniaxial tension gives σ₁ = σ, σ₂ = 0, τ_max = σ/2, and max shear plane is at 45° to the cross-section (explains slip bands in ductile tensile specimens).
- ☐ **Quick fact check:** Pure shear gives σ₁ = +τ, σ₂ = −τ, principal planes at 45°, τ_max = τ (the same input shear).
- ☐ **Always state units** (MPa or N/mm²) in the final answer.
- ☐ **Always draw the stress element diagram** with clearly labeled positive directions before applying formulas.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Board Strategy:** In a 14-mark question, expect the problem to test (a) the inclined plane stress formula directly (≈6 marks), (b) principal stresses calculation (≈4–5 marks), and (c) a Mohr's circle graphical verification or a design safety check (≈3–4 marks). Practice 5–7 numerical problems of this combination **before the exam** for guaranteed full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mohr's Circle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET302 - SOLID MECHANICS AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Mohr's Circle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Mohr's Circle** is a two-dimensional graphical representation of the **state of stress at a point**, developed by the German civil engineer **Christian Otto Mohr in 1882**. It provides a complete geometric picture of the stress transformation equations and offers a powerful visual method to determine:

- **Principal stresses** (maximum and minimum normal stresses on mutually perpendicular planes)
- **Maximum shear stress** (and the planes on which it acts)
- **Normal and shear stresses on any arbitrarily inclined plane**
- The **orientation of principal planes**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Mohr's circle is a **mandatory graphical and analytical tool** in the module on *Combined Stress and Failure Theories*. It links the **2D stress state** with the **geometry of a circle**, and the KTU board consistently tests its construction, interpretation, and the **2θ-to-θ angle relationship convention**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Stress Compass

Imagine a stress element as a **small building block** with tiny arrows on each face (normal and shear forces). If you rotate the block, the arrows change direction and magnitude. **Mohr's circle acts like a "stress compass"**: each point on the circumference of the circle represents **one complete stress state** of a corresponding rotated element. The center of the circle is the **average stress** (a property of the material at that point that never changes), and the radius is the **maximum swing** of the stress.

**Real-world application analogy:** Just as a clock face helps you visualize the position of the hour, minute, and second hands through rotation, Mohr's circle helps a mechanical or civil engineer visualize how stresses **rotate** as the plane of interest changes — without having to perform tedious trigonometric calculations every time.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The **angle on Mohr's circle is 2θ**, where **θ** is the physical angle of rotation of the element. This **doubling effect** is a defining hallmark of Mohr's circle and a frequent source of marks lost in KTU exams.

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants / Conventions

- **Coordinate Convention:** Standard right-handed Cartesian system with **tensile stresses as positive** and **clockwise shear on the x-face as positive** (some texts use the opposite — always check your sign convention in KTU).
- **Angle Convention:** All angles on Mohr's circle are measured **counter-clockwise positive**, and physical angles are **doubled** when plotted.
- **Unit:** Stresses are in **MPa (N/mm²)** or **N/m² (Pa)**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Theory — How Mohr's Circle Works

Mohr's circle is built upon three foundational pillars:

1. **Equilibrium of a 2D Stress Element**
   - Consider a small element rotated by an angle **θ** counter-clockwise. By applying static equilibrium of forces in x' and y' directions, the **stress transformation equations** emerge.

2. **Stress Transformation Equations (Foundations of Mohr's Circle)**
   - These are the algebraic heart of Mohr's circle. They describe how stresses change with rotation.

3. **Geometric Interpretation**
   - The transformation equations can be rearranged to fit the **general equation of a circle** in the **σ-τ** plane: $(x-a)^2 + y^2 = R^2$.
   - This mathematical "trick" is what gives Mohr's circle its elegance and graphical power.

### 2.2 The Core Logic — Step by Step

- **Step 1:** Identify the known stress state: $\sigma_x$, $\sigma_y$, $\tau_{xy}$.
- **Step 2:** Compute the **center of Mohr's circle**, which lies on the σ-axis at the average normal stress:
  $$C = \left( \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2}, \, 0 \right)$$
- **Step 3:** Compute the **radius of Mohr's circle**:
  $$R = \sqrt{\left( \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2} \right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$$
- **Step 4:** Plot the two stress points: **X** = $(\sigma_x, -\tau_{xy})$ and **Y** = $(\sigma_y, +\tau_{xy})$ on the σ-τ plane. These lie diametrically opposite on the circle.
- **Step 5:** Draw the circle with center C and radius R. **Read off** principal stresses, max shear stress, and any other inclined plane stress state.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is your **high-yield revision sheet**. Memorize it before attempting any KTU numerical.

| **Parameter** | **Formula** | **Description** |
|---------------|-------------|-----------------|
| Center of circle (x-coord) | $C_{\sigma} = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2}$ | Average normal stress |
| Center of circle (y-coord) | $C_{\tau} = 0$ | Lies on σ-axis |
| Radius of Mohr's circle | $R = \sqrt{\left( \dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$ | Max shear stress magnitude |
| Principal stress 1 | $\sigma_1 = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + R$ | Maximum normal stress |
| Principal stress 2 | $\sigma_2 = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} - R$ | Minimum normal stress |
| Maximum shear stress | $\tau_{\max} = R$ | Occurs at 45° to principal planes |
| Principal plane angle | $\tan 2\theta_p = \dfrac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}$ | Angle of $\sigma_1$ plane |
| Max shear plane angle | $\theta_s = \theta_p \pm 45°$ | At 45° to principal plane |
| Stress on inclined plane (θ) | $\sigma_{\theta} = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\sin 2\theta$ | Normal stress on rotated plane |
| Stress on inclined plane (θ) | $\tau_{\theta} = -\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\cos 2\theta$ | Shear stress on rotated plane |
| 3D Mohr's circle radius | $R_{3D} = \dfrac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2}$ | Used for 3D stress analysis |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL sign convention:** In KTU 2024 scheme, on the x-face, **shear stress causing clockwise moment is positive**. So when plotting on Mohr's circle, the point X is plotted as $(\sigma_x, -\tau_{xy})$ and Y as $(\sigma_y, +\tau_{xy})$ for a **standard convention**. Always confirm with the question.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Mechanical Design:** Used in design of shafts, pressure vessels, and crankshafts subjected to combined loading.
- **Civil/Structural Engineering:** Essential for analysis of beams under combined bending and torsion, and for stress analysis in dams and retaining walls.
- **Geotechnical Engineering:** Used in the analysis of soil and rock failure using the **Mohr-Coulomb failure criterion**, which is a direct extension of Mohr's circle concept.
- **Aerospace and Automotive:** Used for stress analysis in wing spars, fuselage panels, and chassis components.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the 2D Stress Transformation Equations

**Setup:** Consider a 2D stress element with faces perpendicular to the x and y axes, having stresses $\sigma_x$, $\sigma_y$, and $\tau_{xy}$. We rotate the element by an angle **θ (counter-clockwise)** to obtain a new element with faces perpendicular to x' and y' axes.

Forces on the inclined plane (per unit thickness) of an element with side **dx = ds·cosθ** and **dy = ds·sinθ**:

- Force on x-face: $F_x = \sigma_x \, dy + \tau_{xy} \, dx$
- Force on y-face: $F_y = \sigma_y \, dx + \tau_{xy} \, dy$

These forces resolve into normal and tangential components on the inclined face.

**Equilibrium along x'-axis (normal to inclined face):**

$$\sum F_{x'} = 0$$

$$\sigma_{x'} \cdot ds = \sigma_x \, dy \cos\theta + \tau_{xy} \, dx \cos\theta + \sigma_y \, dx \sin\theta + \tau_{xy} \, dy \sin\theta$$

Substitute $dx = ds \cos\theta$ and $dy = ds \sin\theta$:

$$\sigma_{x'} = \sigma_x \sin\theta \cos\theta + \tau_{xy} \cos^2\theta + \sigma_y \cos\theta \sin\theta + \tau_{xy} \sin^2\theta$$

Using identities: $\sin\theta\cos\theta = \dfrac{\sin 2\theta}{2}$ and $\cos^2\theta - \sin^2\theta = \cos 2\theta$:

$$\sigma_{x'} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\sin 2\theta$$

**Equilibrium along y'-axis (tangential to inclined face):**

$$\sum F_{y'} = 0$$

After similar trigonometric manipulation:

$$\tau_{x'y'} = -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\cos 2\theta$$

These two equations are the **stress transformation equations** — the algebraic basis of Mohr's circle.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Mohr's Circle Equation

**Starting Point:** Use the two stress transformation equations above.

$$\sigma_{x'} - \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} = \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\sin 2\theta \quad \text{...(i)}$$

$$\tau_{x'y'} = -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\cos 2\theta \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

**Step 1:** Square both equations (i) and (ii).

**Step 2:** Add the two squared equations.

**Step 3:** Apply the trigonometric identity: $\sin^2 2\theta + \cos^2 2\theta = 1$.

**Step 4:** The result simplifies elegantly to:

$$\left(\sigma_{x'} - \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{x'y'}^2 = \left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2$$

This is the **equation of a circle** in the $(\sigma, \tau)$ plane with:

- **Center:** $\left( \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2}, \, 0 \right)$
- **Radius:** $R = \sqrt{\left( \dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2} \right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$

### 3.3 Derivation of Principal Stresses

The principal stresses are the **maximum and minimum values of $\sigma_{x'}$**, which occur when the radius vector in Mohr's circle aligns horizontally with the σ-axis. This corresponds to the **leftmost and rightmost points** of the circle.

**Setting $\dfrac{d\sigma_{x'}}{d\theta} = 0$:**

$$-(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)\sin 2\theta + 2\tau_{xy}\cos 2\theta = 0$$

$$\tan 2\theta_p = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}$$

The values of $\sigma$ at the extreme points are:

$$\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$$

### 3.4 Derivation of Maximum Shear Stress

The maximum shear stress is the **largest value of $\tau_{x'y'}$**, which occurs at the **top and bottom of Mohr's circle**, 90° away from the principal points (i.e., $2\theta_s = 2\theta_p \pm 90°$):

$$\theta_s = \theta_p \pm 45°$$

The maximum value is simply the **radius** of the circle:

$$\tau_{\max} = R = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$$

**Important note:** On the planes of maximum shear stress, there is a **non-zero normal stress** (equal to the center coordinate) **unless** the center is at the origin (i.e., pure shear case). This is a frequent KTU exam trap.

$$\sigma_{\text{on max-shear plane}} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2}$$

### 3.5 Worked Example — Full Numerical Walkthrough

**Problem:** For a 2D stress element with $\sigma_x = 80$ MPa, $\sigma_y = 40$ MPa, $\tau_{xy} = 30$ MPa (clockwise on x-face), construct Mohr's circle and determine the principal stresses, maximum shear stress, and the orientation of the principal planes.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the given stresses:**

$$\sigma_x = 80 \text{ MPa}, \quad \sigma_y = 40 \text{ MPa}, \quad \tau_{xy} = 30 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the center:**

$$C = \left( \frac{80 + 40}{2}, 0 \right) = (60, 0) \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the radius:**

$$R = \sqrt{\left(\frac{80 - 40}{2}\right)^2 + 30^2} = \sqrt{20^2 + 30^2} = \sqrt{400 + 900} = \sqrt{1300}$$

$$R = 36.06 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4 — Principal stresses:**

$$\sigma_1 = 60 + 36.06 = 96.06 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_2 = 60 - 36.06 = 23.94 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 5 — Maximum shear stress:**

$$\tau_{\max} = R = 36.06 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 6 — Orientation of principal planes:**

$$\tan 2\theta_p = \frac{2 \times 30}{80 - 40} = \frac{60}{40} = 1.5$$

$$2\theta_p = \tan^{-1}(1.5) = 56.31°$$

$$\theta_p = 28.15° \text{ (counter-clockwise from x-axis)}$$

**Step 7 — Maximum shear plane orientation:**

$$\theta_s = 28.15° - 45° = -16.85° \text{ (or } 73.15° \text{ CCW)}$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Mohr's Circle Construction Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Given 2D stress state] --> B[Identify sigma_x, sigma_y, tau_xy]
    B --> C[Compute Center C on sigma-axis]
    C --> C1["C = (sigma_x + sigma_y)/2 , 0"]
    B --> D[Compute Radius R of Mohr's circle]
    D --> D1["R = sqrt of half diff squared + tau_xy squared"]
    C --> E[Plot Point X on circle]
    D --> E
    E --> E1["X = sigma_x , minus tau_xy"]
    C --> F[Plot Point Y on circle]
    D --> F
    F --> F1["Y = sigma_y , plus tau_xy"]
    E --> G[Draw circle with center C and radius R]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Locate extreme points of circle]
    H --> H1[Principal stresses sigma_1 and sigma_2]
    H --> H2[Maximum shear stress tau_max]
    G --> I[Measure angles 2 theta from X to required plane]
    I --> I1[Convert to physical angle theta]
    I1 --> J[Read normal and shear stress at any plane]
    J --> K[End: Complete stress state known]

    style A fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style K fill:#15803d,color:#ffffff
    style C1 fill:#fef3c7
    style D1 fill:#fef3c7
    style H1 fill:#fee2e2
    style H2 fill:#fee2e2
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Relationships Between Stress Quantities

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Stress State sigma_x, sigma_y, tau_xy] --> B[Average Stress sigma_avg]
    A --> C[Deviatoric Component]
    B --> B1["sigma_avg = sigma_x + sigma_y over 2"]
    C --> C1["D = sigma_x minus sigma_y over 2"]
    B --> D[Center of Mohr Circle C]
    C --> D
    A --> D
    D --> D1["C lies on sigma-axis"]
    A --> E[Radius of Mohr Circle R]
    C1 --> E
    A --> E
    E --> E1["R = sqrt of D squared plus tau_xy squared"]
    D --> F[Principal Stresses]
    E --> F
    F --> F1["sigma_1 = sigma_avg plus R"]
    F --> F2["sigma_2 = sigma_avg minus R"]
    E --> G[Max Shear Stress]
    G --> G1["tau_max = R"]
    D --> H[Normal stress on max-shear plane]
    H --> H1["sigma = sigma_avg (center)"]
    F --> I[Principal Plane Angle]
    I --> I1["tan of 2 theta_p equals 2 tau_xy over sigma_x minus sigma_y"]

    style A fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style F fill:#fee2e2
    style G fill:#dcfce7
    style I fill:#e0e7ff
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Mermaid Diagram — Physical Element vs. Mohr's Circle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Physical Element - x-axis vertical, y-axis horizontal] --> Q1[Plot Point X on Mohr Circle]
    P2[Physical Element - y-axis vertical, x-axis horizontal] --> Q2[Plot Point Y on Mohr Circle]
    P1 --> P3[Rotate by angle theta CCW]
    P3 --> P4[New physical element]
    P4 --> Q3[Plot Point X-dash on Mohr Circle]
    Q1 --> M[Center C on sigma-axis]
    Q2 --> M
    Q3 --> M
    Q1 --> Q2
    Q2 --> Q3
    Q1 -. 2 theta angle on Mohr circle .-> Q3
    P3 -. theta angle on physical element .-> P4

    style P1 fill:#dbeafe
    style P4 fill:#dbeafe
    style M fill:#fde68a
    style Q1 fill:#fecaca
    style Q2 fill:#fecaca
    style Q3 fill:#fecaca
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** The point **X** corresponds to the x-face of the physical element, and the point **Y** corresponds to the y-face. Diametrically opposite on the Mohr's circle, they represent the two perpendicular faces. Rotation by **θ** on the physical element corresponds to rotation by **2θ** on the Mohr's circle — this is the **2θ-to-θ rule**.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
Define Mohr's circle. Write the expressions for the center, radius, and principal stresses of a 2D stress state $\sigma_x$, $\sigma_y$, $\tau_{xy}$.

**Model Answer:**

**Mohr's Circle Definition:** Mohr's circle is a graphical representation of the 2D stress transformation equations. It is a circle drawn in the $\sigma$–$\tau$ plane that represents all possible stress states on different planes passing through a point.

**Center:**
$$C = \left( \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2}, \, 0 \right)$$

**Radius:**
$$R = \sqrt{\left( \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2} \right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$$

**Principal Stresses:**
$$\sigma_1 = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + R, \quad \sigma_2 = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} - R$$

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
State the relationship between the angle on Mohr's circle and the actual physical angle of rotation of a stress element. Justify your answer briefly.

**Model Answer:**

The angle on Mohr's circle is **twice** the physical angle of rotation of the stress element. That is, if the physical element is rotated by an angle $\theta$, the corresponding point on Mohr's circle moves through an angle of $2\theta$.

**Justification:** The stress transformation equations contain $\sin 2\theta$ and $\cos 2\theta$ terms (not $\sin\theta$ and $\cos\theta$). When these are plotted in the $\sigma$–$\tau$ plane, the parametric equations trace a circle with parameter $2\theta$, hence the angle traversed on the circle is $2\theta$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students write "angle is half" or confuse the convention. Always remember: **Mohr's circle angle = 2 × physical angle**. Marks are routinely lost here.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### **Question A (Choice 1)**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 3]**

**(a)** A 2D stress element has $\sigma_x = 60$ MPa, $\sigma_y = 20$ MPa, and $\tau_{xy} = 25$ MPa (CCW positive). Construct Mohr's circle and determine the **principal stresses** and the **orientation of the principal planes**.

**(7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Center of Mohr's circle:**

$$C = \left( \frac{60 + 20}{2}, \, 0 \right) = (40, 0) \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2 — Radius of Mohr's circle:**

$$R = \sqrt{\left( \frac{60 - 20}{2} \right)^2 + 25^2} = \sqrt{20^2 + 25^2} = \sqrt{400 + 625} = \sqrt{1025}$$

$$R = 32.02 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3 — Principal stresses:**

$$\sigma_1 = 40 + 32.02 = 72.02 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_2 = 40 - 32.02 = 7.98 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4 — Principal plane angle:**

$$\tan 2\theta_p = \frac{2 \times 25}{60 - 20} = \frac{50}{40} = 1.25$$

$$2\theta_p = 51.34° \Rightarrow \theta_p = 25.67° \text{ CCW from x-axis}$$

**Step 5 — Construction Summary:**
- Plot X = (60, −25) and Y = (20, +25).
- Center C = (40, 0), radius R = 32.02 MPa.
- $\sigma_1 = 72.02$ MPa, $\sigma_2 = 7.98$ MPa.

---

**(b)** For the same stress element in (a), find the **normal and shear stresses on a plane whose normal makes an angle of 30° counter-clockwise from the x-axis**.

**(7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Substitute in stress transformation equation:**

$$\sigma_{x'} = \frac{60 + 20}{2} + \frac{60 - 20}{2}\cos(2 \times 30°) + 25\sin(2 \times 30°)$$

$$\sigma_{x'} = 40 + 20\cos 60° + 25\sin 60°$$

$$\sigma_{x'} = 40 + 20(0.5) + 25(0.866)$$

$$\sigma_{x'} = 40 + 10 + 21.65 = 71.65 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2 — Shear stress:**

$$\tau_{x'y'} = -\frac{60 - 20}{2}\sin 60° + 25\cos 60°$$

$$\tau_{x'y'} = -20(0.866) + 25(0.5)$$

$$\tau_{x'y'} = -17.32 + 12.5 = -4.82 \text{ MPa}$$

**Result:**
- Normal stress on the plane: $\sigma_{x'} = 71.65$ MPa
- Shear stress on the plane: $\tau_{x'y'} = -4.82$ MPa (negative indicates direction opposite to assumed positive)

---

#### **Question B (Choice 2)**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3]**

**(a)** Derive the **2D stress transformation equations** for normal and shear stresses on an inclined plane, starting from the equilibrium of a stress element.

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (Concise Steps):**

1. Consider a 2D element of unit thickness, with sides dx and dy, rotated by angle θ.
2. The x-face has area $\sin\theta$ (per unit depth), y-face has area $\cos\theta$.
3. Resolve forces along x' (normal) and y' (tangential) directions.
4. Apply equilibrium: $\sum F_{x'} = 0$ and $\sum F_{y'} = 0$.
5. Substitute $\sin\theta\cos\theta = \dfrac{\sin 2\theta}{2}$ and $\cos^2\theta - \sin^2\theta = \cos 2\theta$.
6. Final equations:

$$\sigma_{x'} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\sin 2\theta$$

$$\tau_{x'y'} = -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\cos 2\theta$$

(Full derivation already shown in Section 3.1 above.)

---

**(b)** The stresses at a point in a structure are $\sigma_x = 100$ MPa, $\sigma_y = 20$ MPa, $\tau_{xy} = 40$ MPa. Using Mohr's circle, find the **stresses on a plane inclined at 30° to the vertical** (i.e., $\theta = 60°$ from the x-axis).

**(7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Center and Radius:**

$$C = \left( \frac{100 + 20}{2}, 0 \right) = (60, 0) \text{ MPa}$$

$$R = \sqrt{\left( \frac{100 - 20}{2} \right)^2 + 40^2} = \sqrt{40^2 + 40^2} = 40\sqrt{2} = 56.57 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2 — Reference angle from X-point to the horizontal on Mohr's circle:**

The initial point on Mohr's circle from X (x-face) is at angle $\alpha$ from σ-axis where:

$$\tan\alpha = \frac{\tau_{xy}}{(\sigma_x - C)} = \frac{40}{100 - 60} = \frac{40}{40} = 1 \Rightarrow \alpha = 45°$$

So point X is at $2\theta_X = -45°$ (below σ-axis since $\tau_{xy}$ is taken as $-\tau_{xy}$ for plot).

**Step 3 — For plane inclined at $\theta = 60°$ (CCW from x-axis):**

Move $2\theta = 120°$ counter-clockwise from point X along the circle.

The new angle from σ-axis = $-45° + 120° = 75°$.

**Step 4 — Compute stresses using radius vector at 75°:**

$$\sigma_{x'} = C + R\cos(75°) = 60 + 56.57 \times 0.2588 = 60 + 14.64 = 74.64 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\tau_{x'y'} = R\sin(75°) = 56.57 \times 0.9659 = 54.64 \text{ MPa}$$

**Verification using direct equations:**

$$\sigma_{x'} = 60 + 40\cos 120° + 40\sin 120° = 60 - 20 + 34.64 = 74.64 \text{ MPa} \checkmark$$

$$\tau_{x'y'} = -40\sin 120° + 40\cos 120° = -34.64 - 20 = -54.64 \text{ MPa}$$

(The sign depends on convention; the magnitude is $54.64$ MPa.)

**Result:** $\sigma_{x'} = 74.64$ MPa, $\tau_{x'y'} = 54.64$ MPa.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Angle Doubling Error:** Forgetting the **2θ rule** — always remember Mohr's circle uses **2θ**, not θ.
> 2. **Sign Convention:** Mixing up CW/CCW signs. If a question gives $\tau_{xy}$ as "positive" without specifying direction, **assume CCW positive on x-face** unless told otherwise.
> 3. **Plotting Errors:** The point X should be plotted as $(\sigma_x, -\tau_{xy})$ and Y as $(\sigma_y, +\tau_{xy})$ in the standard convention. Reversing this gives inverted results.
> 4. **Rounding Too Early:** Keep at least **4 significant figures** during intermediate steps. KTU examiners deduct marks for premature rounding.
> 5. **Forgetting Normal Stress on Max-Shear Plane:** Many students wrongly assume $\tau_{\max}$ occurs on planes with zero normal stress. In general, $\sigma = C$ on max-shear planes (not zero).
> 6. **Confusing Principal Stresses Order:** $\sigma_1$ is always the **algebraically larger** (most tensile) and $\sigma_2$ is the smaller. If both are negative, $\sigma_1$ is the "less negative" one.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision for KTU 2024 Exam:**

- ✅ Mohr's circle is a **graphical tool** in the **σ-τ plane** representing the 2D state of stress at a point.
- ✅ **Center** lies on the σ-axis at $C = \left( \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2}, 0 \right)$ — this is the **average normal stress**.
- ✅ **Radius** is $R = \sqrt{\left( \dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2} \right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$ — also the **maximum shear stress magnitude**.
- ✅ **Principal stresses** are at the extreme horizontal points: $\sigma_1 = C + R$ and $\sigma_2 = C - R$.
- ✅ **Maximum shear stress** occurs at the top/bottom of the circle: $\tau_{\max} = R$.
- ✅ **Angle convention:** Mohr's circle uses **2θ** (TWICE the physical angle of element rotation).
- ✅ **Principal plane angle:** $\tan 2\theta_p = \dfrac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}$.
- ✅ **Max-shear plane angle:** $\theta_s = \theta_p \pm 45°$ (i.e., $2\theta_s = 2\theta_p \pm 90°$ on Mohr's circle).
- ✅ On planes of **maximum shear**, the normal stress is **NOT zero** in general — it equals the **center coordinate** $C$.
- ✅ **Plotting rule (standard convention):** Point X = $(\sigma_x, -\tau_{xy})$, Point Y = $(\sigma_y, +\tau_{xy})$ — they are **diametrically opposite** on the circle.
- ✅ **Sign convention:** Tensile stresses are **positive**; compressive are **negative**.
- ✅ Mohr's circle extends to **3D stress** using three Mohr's circles — the maximum shear stress is $R_{3D} = \dfrac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2}$ where $\sigma_1 \geq \sigma_2 \geq \sigma_3$.
- ✅ Mohr's circle is foundational to the **Mohr-Coulomb failure theory** used widely in geotechnical and materials engineering.
- ✅ The **diameter of Mohr's circle** is $\sigma_1 - \sigma_2$, the **stress range** experienced by the element under rotation.
- ✅ Always **label units** (MPa or N/mm²) — KTU examiners deduct marks for missing units.
- ✅ **Practice at least 3–4 numerical problems** before the exam — Mohr's circle is a **high-weightage topic** in Module 3 and consistently appears in KTU ESE.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Factor of Safety
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET302 - SOLID MECHANICS AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Factor of Safety

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 MODULE 4: FACTOR OF SAFETY — CORE CONCEPTUAL FOUNDATION

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the framework of **APJ Abdul Kalam Technological University (KTU) 2024 Scheme** under the course *PCPET302 — Solid Mechanics and Design*, the **Factor of Safety (FoS)**, denoted mathematically by the symbol **$N$** or **$F.S.$**, is defined as the **ratio of the maximum stress a material can sustain (the failure stress) to the working stress (or allowable stress) actually applied in service**.

Mathematically expressed in its most general form:

$$N = \frac{\sigma_{failure}}{\sigma_{working}} = \frac{\sigma_{failure}}{\sigma_{applied}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board-Standard Definition:** *Factor of Safety is the ratio of the ultimate (or yield) strength of a material to the permissible (working) stress, used as a multiplier of uncertainty in design to prevent failure under unexpected loading, material defects, or environmental degradation.*

The **failure stress** $\sigma_{failure}$ can be:
- The **ultimate tensile strength** $\sigma_u$ (for brittle materials) → $N = \sigma_u / \sigma_{working}$
- The **yield strength** $\sigma_y$ (for ductile materials) → $N = \sigma_y / \sigma_{working}$
- The **endurance limit** $\sigma_e$ (for fatigue/fluctuating loads) → $N = \sigma_e / \sigma_{working}$
- The **creep rupture stress** (for high-temperature applications)

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are constructing a **rope bridge** across a Himalayan river to carry tourists. You know the bridge must hold 100 kg (a heavy trekker with backpack). Will you use a rope rated for exactly 100 kg? **Absolutely not!** You will use a rope rated for, say, 500 kg.

- The **rope's rated load** = failure stress (what the rope can just barely handle before snapping).
- The **trekker's actual load** = working/applied stress.
- The ratio $500/100 = 5$ is your **Factor of Safety**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Summary:** The Factor of Safety is a **"design cushion"** — a built-in margin of safety that protects the structure, machine, or component from uncertainties such as:
> - Unexpected overloads
> - Material imperfections (inclusions, voids, micro-cracks)
> - Fabrication errors
> - Aggressive environmental conditions (corrosion, temperature)
> - Inaccurate loading assumptions
> - Stress concentrations at geometric discontinuities

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Unit | Notes |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| Factor of Safety | $N$ | Dimensionless ratio | Always $\geq 1.0$ for safe design |
| Working Stress | $\sigma_w$ | $\text{MPa}$ or $\text{N/mm}^2$ | Also called *allowable* or *design* stress |
| Ultimate Strength | $\sigma_u$ | $\text{MPa}$ or $\text{N/mm}^2$ | Maximum stress before fracture |
| Yield Strength | $\sigma_y$ | $\text{MPa}$ or $\text{N/mm}^2$ | Onset of permanent deformation |
| Endurance Limit | $\sigma_e$ | $\text{MPa}$ or $\text{N/mm}^2$ | For fatigue-critical components |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The Course Outcome (CO) alignment for this topic falls under **CO4 — Design of Machine Elements**, where students are expected to *apply appropriate Factor of Safety values to design safe mechanical components against various modes of failure (static, fatigue, buckling, deflection).*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 🔬 DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Operational Logic of Factor of Safety — Why and How

The Factor of Safety is not an arbitrary number. It is a **deliberate engineering decision** rooted in the uncertainty budget of the design. Here is the structured logic behind its application:

### Step 1: Identify the Governing Failure Mode
- **Static loading (single, sustained load)** → Failure by yielding → Use $\sigma_y$
- **Brittle fracture (cast iron, ceramics)** → Failure by fracture → Use $\sigma_u$
- **Fluctuating/cyclic loading** → Failure by fatigue → Use $\sigma_e$ (endurance limit)
- **High temperature** → Failure by creep → Use creep rupture strength
- **Impact loading** → Use impact-modified stress formula

### Step 2: Quantify the Working Stress
- Compute the nominal stress using the applied load and the cross-sectional geometry.
- For example, for an axial member: $\sigma = P/A$
- For bending: $\sigma = M c / I$
- For torsion: $\tau = T r / J$

### Step 3: Account for Stress Concentrations
- At holes, fillets, keyways, and sharp corners, local stresses far exceed the nominal stress.
- The **stress concentration factor** $K_t$ (theoretical) or $K_f$ (fatigue) modifies the stress:

$$\sigma_{max} = K_t \cdot \sigma_{nominal}$$

### Step 4: Apply the Factor of Safety
- Design stress = failure stress / Factor of Safety:

$$\sigma_{allowable} = \frac{\sigma_{failure}}{N}$$

- The cross-section is then sized so that $\sigma_{working} \leq \sigma_{allowable}$.

### Step 5: Verify and Iterate
- Check deflection, buckling, fatigue, and stability criteria.
- Adjust $N$ upward if consequences of failure are catastrophic (aircraft, nuclear, pressure vessels).

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Selection of N

| Application Domain | Typical FoS Range | Reasoning |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| Aircraft structures | $1.2 - 1.5$ | Weight-critical, well-characterized materials |
| Pressure vessels / boilers | $3.0 - 4.0$ | Catastrophic failure risk |
| Bridges / civil structures | $2.0 - 3.0$ | Public safety, variable loads |
| Machine components (general) | $1.5 - 3.0$ | Balance of weight and reliability |
| Lifting equipment (cranes) | $5.0 - 7.0$ | Human life dependent |
| Cast iron components | $5.0 - 8.0$ | Brittle, variable properties |
| Storage tanks | $3.0 - 5.0$ | Environmental hazard |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Application | Notes |
| :--: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $N = \sigma_u / \sigma_w$ | Brittle materials (static) | Ultimate strength basis |
| 2 | $N = \sigma_y / \sigma_w$ | Ductile materials (static) | Yield strength basis |
| 3 | $N = \sigma_e / \sigma_w$ | Fatigue loading | Endurance limit basis |
| 4 | $\sigma_w = \sigma_y / N$ | Design stress for ductile material | Most common in KTU problems |
| 5 | $\sigma_w = \sigma_u / N$ | Design stress for brittle material |  |
| 6 | $\sigma_{max} = K_t \cdot \sigma_{nom}$ | Stress concentration | $K_t$ from charts / Peterson |
| 7 | $K_f = 1 + q(K_t - 1)$ | Fatigue stress concentration | $q$ = notch sensitivity |
| 8 | $\sigma_{max} = K_f \cdot \sigma_{nom}$ | Fatigue strength check |  |
| 9 | $A_{req} = P / \sigma_w$ | Axial design | $P$ = applied tensile load |
| 10 | $d_{min} = \left( 32 M / (\pi \sigma_w) \right)^{1/3}$ | Bending design (solid shaft) | For circular cross-section |
| 11 | $d_{min} = \left( 16 T / (\pi \tau_w) \right)^{1/3}$ | Torsion design (solid shaft) | $\tau_w = \tau_y / N$ |
| 12 | $\sigma_{eq,von} = \left( \sigma^2 + 3\tau^2 \right)^{1/2}$ | Combined loading (DE-Goodman) | Von Mises equivalent stress |
| 13 | $\sigma_{eq,max} = \left( \sigma^2 + 4\tau^2 \right)^{1/2}$ | Combined loading (Rankine/Max-shear) | Maximum principal stress |
| 14 | $N = \tau_y / \tau_w$ | Factor of safety in shear | $\tau_y = 0.577 \sigma_y$ (von Mises) |
| 15 | $\tau_y = 0.5 \sigma_y$ | Shear yield (Tresca) | Conservative estimate |
| 16 | $\sigma_a / \sigma_e + \sigma_m / \sigma_u = 1/N$ | Goodman fatigue line |  |
| 17 | $\sigma_a / \sigma_e + \sigma_m / \sigma_y = 1/N$ | Soderberg fatigue line | Most conservative |
| 18 | $N = \sigma_{-1} / \sigma_a$ | Pure fluctuating load | $\sigma_m = 0$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always express $N$ in the form $\frac{\text{Failure Stress}}{\text{Working Stress}}$ explicitly in your answer scripts. Examiners look for this canonical ratio.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Factor of Safety is the **cornerstone of mechanical design** in industries ranging from:
- **Automotive**: Crankshafts, connecting rods (FoS $\approx 2 - 3$)
- **Aerospace**: Wing spars, landing gear (FoS $\approx 1.2 - 1.5$, tight margins)
- **Pressure vessel fabrication**: ASME Boiler Code (FoS $\approx 4$ on tensile strength)
- **Civil**: Bridges, columns, beams (FoS $\approx 2 - 3$)
- **Biomedical**: Prosthetic implants, surgical instruments (FoS $\approx 2 - 5$)

It enables engineers to **quantify reliability**, **standardize designs**, and **account for unknowns** in a rigorous mathematical framework.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🧮 EXHAUSTIVE STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Working Stress from Factor of Safety

**Statement:** Show that the allowable working stress equals the failure stress divided by the Factor of Safety.

### Given:
- Failure stress: $\sigma_{failure}$ (e.g., $\sigma_y$ or $\sigma_u$)
- Working stress: $\sigma_w$
- Factor of safety: $N$

### To Prove:
$$\sigma_w = \frac{\sigma_{failure}}{N}$$

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

Starting with the **definition** of the Factor of Safety as the ratio of failure stress to working stress:

$$N = \frac{\sigma_{failure}}{\sigma_w}$$

This is the *direct* definition. To isolate the working stress on the left-hand side, multiply both sides by $\sigma_w$:

$$N \cdot \sigma_w = \frac{\sigma_{failure}}{\sigma_w} \cdot \sigma_w$$

The $\sigma_w$ on the right-hand side cancels:

$$N \cdot \sigma_w = \sigma_{failure}$$

Now divide both sides by $N$ to solve for $\sigma_w$:

$$\frac{N \cdot \sigma_w}{N} = \frac{\sigma_{failure}}{N}$$

$$\sigma_w = \frac{\sigma_{failure}}{N}$$

> **Hence proved.** This is the **fundamental design equation** in KTU problems.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Minimum Diameter of a Solid Shaft Subjected to Combined Bending and Torsion

This is a **classic 14-mark KTU question**. The derivation integrates FoS with combined stress theory.

### Problem Setup:
A solid circular shaft of diameter $d$ is subjected to a bending moment $M$ and a torque $T$. Using the **Maximum Shear Stress Theory (Tresca)**, derive the expression for the minimum diameter.

### Step 1: Nominal Stresses from Mechanics of Materials

The bending stress at the outer fibre of the circular cross-section:

$$\sigma_b = \frac{M \cdot c}{I} = \frac{M \cdot (d/2)}{\pi d^4 / 64} = \frac{32 M}{\pi d^3}$$

The torsional shear stress at the outer surface:

$$\tau_t = \frac{T \cdot r}{J} = \frac{T \cdot (d/2)}{\pi d^4 / 32} = \frac{16 T}{\pi d^3}$$

### Step 2: Maximum Shear Stress in the Element

The element at the surface experiences $\sigma_b$ and $\tau_t$. The principal stresses are:

$$\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_b}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left( \frac{\sigma_b}{2} \right)^2 + \tau_t^2}$$

The maximum shear stress in the element is:

$$\tau_{max} = \sqrt{\left( \frac{\sigma_b}{2} \right)^2 + \tau_t^2}$$

### Step 3: Apply Tresca Failure Criterion

Per **Maximum Shear Stress Theory (Tresca)**, failure occurs when:

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{\sigma_y}{2}$$

Therefore, the design condition is:

$$\sqrt{\left( \frac{\sigma_b}{2} \right)^2 + \tau_t^2} = \frac{\sigma_y}{2N}$$

(Here, $N$ is the Factor of Safety applied to the yield strength.)

### Step 4: Substitute the Stress Expressions

$$\sqrt{\left( \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{32 M}{\pi d^3} \right)^2 + \left( \frac{16 T}{\pi d^3} \right)^2} = \frac{\sigma_y}{2N}$$

Simplify the term inside the square root:

$$\sqrt{\left( \frac{16 M}{\pi d^3} \right)^2 + \left( \frac{16 T}{\pi d^3} \right)^2} = \frac{\sigma_y}{2N}$$

Factor out $\frac{16}{\pi d^3}$:

$$\sqrt{\left( \frac{16}{\pi d^3} \right)^2 \left( M^2 + T^2 \right)} = \frac{\sigma_y}{2N}$$

$$\frac{16}{\pi d^3} \sqrt{M^2 + T^2} = \frac{\sigma_y}{2N}$$

### Step 5: Solve for $d^3$

Multiply both sides by $\pi d^3$:

$$16 \sqrt{M^2 + T^2} = \frac{\pi d^3 \sigma_y}{2N}$$

Multiply both sides by $2N$:

$$32 N \sqrt{M^2 + T^2} = \pi d^3 \sigma_y$$

Divide by $\pi \sigma_y$:

$$d^3 = \frac{32 N \sqrt{M^2 + T^2}}{\pi \sigma_y}$$

### Final Design Equation:

$$\boxed{d_{min} = \left[ \frac{32 N \sqrt{M^2 + T^2}}{\pi \sigma_y} \right]^{1/3}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examination Standard:** This is one of the most frequently asked derivations. The equivalent moment is often defined as $M_e = \frac{1}{2} \left( M + \sqrt{M^2 + T^2} \right)$, leading to a slightly different (but equivalent) form: $d = \left( 32 N M_e / \pi \sigma_y \right)^{1/3}$.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example: Direct Application of FoS

### **Problem (KTU Model):**
A mild steel link of circular cross-section transmits a tensile load of 60 kN. The ultimate tensile strength and yield strength of the material are 420 MPa and 250 MPa, respectively. Calculate the diameter of the link using a Factor of Safety of **3** based on the yield strength.

### **Solution:**

**Step 1: Identify the design stress.**

Using yield strength as the failure criterion:

$$\sigma_w = \frac{\sigma_y}{N} = \frac{250}{3} = 83.33 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2: Write the axial stress equation.**

For a circular cross-section of diameter $d$:

$$\sigma = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{P}{\frac{\pi d^2}{4}} = \frac{4P}{\pi d^2}$$

**Step 3: Set the working stress equal to the design stress.**

$$\frac{4P}{\pi d^2} = \sigma_w$$

**Step 4: Solve for $d^2$.**

$$d^2 = \frac{4P}{\pi \sigma_w} = \frac{4 \times 60 \times 10^3}{\pi \times 83.33}$$

$$d^2 = \frac{240{,}000}{261.8} = 916.7 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 5: Solve for $d$.**

$$d = \sqrt{916.7} = 30.28 \text{ mm}$$

### **Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{d_{min} \approx 30.3 \text{ mm}}$$

**Step 6: Practical recommendation.**
Adopt the next standard size: $d = 32$ mm (to satisfy $\sigma_w \leq 83.33$ MPa with additional margin).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right FoS Basis

| Material Type | Loading Condition | Failure Stress | FoS Formula | Typical $N$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :---: |
| Ductile (mild steel) | Static, monotonic | Yield $\sigma_y$ | $N = \sigma_y / \sigma_w$ | $1.5 - 2.5$ |
| Brittle (cast iron) | Static, monotonic | Ultimate $\sigma_u$ | $N = \sigma_u / \sigma_w$ | $3 - 6$ |
| Ductile | Fluctuating (fatigue) | Endurance $\sigma_e$ | $N = \sigma_e / \sigma_a$ | $2 - 4$ |
| Ductile | Impact | Dynamic yield | $N = \sigma_{yd} / \sigma_{imp}$ | $2 - 5$ |
| Any | High temperature (creep) | Creep rupture | $N = \sigma_{cr} / \sigma_w$ | $2 - 4$ |
| Any | Combined (bending + torsion) | Equivalent stress | $N = \sigma_y / \sigma_{eq}$ | $1.5 - 3$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🏗️ STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Factor of Safety — Engineering Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Design Problem] --> B[Determine Loading Type]
    B --> C{Loading Condition}
    C -->|Static, Ductile| D[Use Yield Strength: sigma_y]
    C -->|Static, Brittle| E[Use Ultimate Strength: sigma_u]
    C -->|Cyclic, Fatigue| F[Use Endurance Limit: sigma_e]
    C -->|High Temperature| G[Use Creep Rupture Strength]
    D --> H[Compute Nominal Stress: sigma_nom]
    E --> H
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Account for Stress Concentration: K_t]
    I --> J[Apply Failure Theory: Tresca / Von Mises / Rankine]
    J --> K[Select Factor of Safety: N]
    K --> L[Compute Allowable Stress: sigma_allow = sigma_fail / N]
    L --> M[Size Cross-Section: A_req = P / sigma_allow]
    M --> N{Deflection / Buckling Check Satisfied?}
    N -->|Yes| O[Final Design Validated]
    N -->|No| P[Increase N or Re-size]
    P --> K
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Working Stress vs. Ultimate Stress Design Philosophies

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph WSM["Working Stress Method WSM"]
        W1[Known Load P] --> W2[Assumed Uniform sigma_w = sigma_fail / N]
        W2 --> W3[Design Section: A = P / sigma_w]
    end
    subgraph USM["Ultimate Stress Method USM"]
        U1[Known Load P] --> U2[Compute Elastic Modulus E and Buckling Coeff]
        U2 --> U3[Apply Load Factors: P_u = n_L x P]
        U3 --> U4[Design Section: A = P_u / sigma_ultimate]
    end
    WSM --> R[Compare: WSM Simpler, USM More Realistic]
    USM --> R
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: FoS Application Across Failure Modes

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    FM[Failure Mode Identification] --> FM1[Static Yielding]
    FM --> FM2[Fatigue Cracking]
    FM --> FM3[Buckling]
    FM --> FM4[Creep]
    FM1 --> SF[Stress Formula: sigma = P / A or M c / I]
    FM2 --> FF[Goodman / Soderberg Diagram]
    FM3 --> BF[Euler / Rankine Buckling]
    FM4 --> CF[Larson-Miller Parameter]
    SF --> FOS[Apply FoS: N = sigma_fail / sigma_work]
    FF --> FOS
    BF --> FOS
    CF --> FOS
    FOS --> SAFE[Component is Safe if N >= N_specified]
```

## 4.4 Schematic: Stress-Strain Curve with FoS Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Origin: 0,0] --> A[Proportional Limit]
    A --> B[Elastic Limit]
    B --> C[Yield Point: sigma_y]
    C --> D[Ultimate Point: sigma_u]
    D --> E[Fracture]
    C --> F[Working Stress: sigma_w = sigma_y / N]
    F --> G[Safety Margin: sigma_y - sigma_w]
    G --> H[Reserve Capacity]
    style C fill:#ffcc00,stroke:#333
    style F fill:#90ee90,stroke:#333
    style G fill:#ff6666,stroke:#333
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The working stress $\sigma_w$ lies well below the yield point. The vertical distance between $\sigma_w$ and $\sigma_y$ is the *safety margin* provided by the Factor of Safety.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 📝 KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## 5.1 PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question Paper]`
**Define Factor of Safety. Why is it necessary in mechanical design?**

#### **Model Answer:**

The **Factor of Safety ($N$)** is defined as the ratio of the failure stress (yield or ultimate) of a material to the working (or allowable) stress to which it is subjected in service:

$$N = \frac{\sigma_{failure}}{\sigma_{working}}$$

**It is necessary in mechanical design for the following reasons:**
1. To account for **uncertainties in loading** (impact, fatigue, accidental overloads).
2. To compensate for **material imperfections** (voids, inclusions, scatter in properties).
3. To accommodate **fabrication errors** (machining tolerances, residual stresses).
4. To provide a **safety margin** against unforeseen service conditions (corrosion, wear, temperature).
5. To ensure **public safety** in case of human-life-dependent applications (bridges, pressure vessels, aircraft).

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between Factor of Safety with respect to yield strength and ultimate strength. When is each used?**

#### **Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Yield Strength Basis | Ultimate Strength Basis |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Failure stress** | Yield strength $\sigma_y$ | Ultimate tensile strength $\sigma_u$ |
| **Formula** | $N = \sigma_y / \sigma_w$ | $N = \sigma_u / \sigma_w$ |
| **Used for** | **Ductile materials** (mild steel, aluminium) | **Brittle materials** (cast iron, concrete) |
| **Philosophy** | Prevents permanent (plastic) deformation | Prevents fracture (sudden failure) |
| **Typical value** | $N = 1.5 - 2.5$ | $N = 3 - 6$ |

Ductile materials yield before they fracture, so designing against yielding is more conservative. Brittle materials show little or no yielding, so the design is based on the ultimate (fracture) strength.

---

## 5.2 PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]`

**(a) Derive the expression for the minimum diameter of a solid circular shaft subjected to a bending moment $M$ and a torque $T$, using the Maximum Shear Stress Theory (Tresca criterion) and a Factor of Safety $N$.** (7 marks)

**(b) A shaft is required to transmit 25 kW at 200 rpm. It is subjected to a bending moment of 1.2 kN·m in addition to torsion. The material has a yield strength of 350 MPa. Using a Factor of Safety of 3 and the Maximum Shear Stress Theory, determine the shaft diameter.** (7 marks)

---

#### **Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

This is the derivation already shown in **Section 3.2** of these notes. Summarized for exam answer:

1. **Bending stress:** $\sigma_b = 32 M / (\pi d^3)$
2. **Torsional shear stress:** $\tau_t = 16 T / (\pi d^3)$
3. **Maximum shear stress in the element:** $\tau_{max} = \sqrt{(\sigma_b/2)^2 + \tau_t^2}$
4. **Tresca failure condition (with FoS $N$):** $\tau_{max} = \sigma_y / (2N)$
5. **Combining and solving for $d$:**

$$d_{min} = \left[ \frac{32 N \sqrt{M^2 + T^2}}{\pi \sigma_y} \right]^{1/3}$$

> **Answer to quote:** *The minimum shaft diameter is $d = \left[ 32 N \sqrt{M^2 + T^2} / (\pi \sigma_y) \right]^{1/3}$.*

---

#### **Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1: Compute the torque transmitted by the shaft.**

Power $P = 25$ kW, speed $N_{rpm} = 200$ rpm.

$$T = \frac{60 \times P}{2 \pi N_{rpm}} = \frac{60 \times 25{,}000}{2 \pi \times 200}$$

$$T = \frac{1{,}500{,}000}{1256.64} = 1193.66 \text{ N·m} \approx 1193.7 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 2: Identify the bending moment.**

$$M = 1.2 \text{ kN·m} = 1200 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 3: Substitute into the design formula.**

$$d^3 = \frac{32 N \sqrt{M^2 + T^2}}{\pi \sigma_y} = \frac{32 \times 3 \times \sqrt{(1200)^2 + (1193.7)^2}}{\pi \times 350 \times 10^6}$$

**Step 4: Compute $\sqrt{M^2 + T^2}$.**

$$M^2 = (1200)^2 = 1{,}440{,}000$$

$$T^2 = (1193.7)^2 = 1{,}424{,}919$$

$$M^2 + T^2 = 2{,}864{,}919$$

$$\sqrt{M^2 + T^2} = 1692.6 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 5: Compute $d^3$.**

$$d^3 = \frac{32 \times 3 \times 1692.6}{\pi \times 350 \times 10^6} = \frac{162{,}489.6}{1.0996 \times 10^9}$$

$$d^3 = 1.478 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3 = 147.8 \text{ mm}^3$$

**Step 6: Solve for $d$.**

$$d = (147.8)^{1/3} = 5.28 \text{ mm}$$

> **Wait, recalculating in N/mm² for consistency:**
> 
> $M = 1.2 \times 10^6$ N·mm, $T = 1.194 \times 10^6$ N·mm, $\sigma_y = 350$ N/mm².
> 
> $$d^3 = \frac{32 \times 3 \times \sqrt{(1.2 \times 10^6)^2 + (1.194 \times 10^6)^2}}{\pi \times 350}$$
> 
> $$\sqrt{1.44 \times 10^{12} + 1.426 \times 10^{12}} = \sqrt{2.866 \times 10^{12}} = 1.693 \times 10^6$$
> 
> $$d^3 = \frac{96 \times 1.693 \times 10^6}{1099.6} = \frac{1.625 \times 10^8}{1099.6} = 147{,}780 \text{ mm}^3$$
> 
> $$d = (147{,}780)^{1/3} \approx 52.8 \text{ mm}$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{d_{min} \approx 53 \text{ mm}}$$

**Practical Recommendation:** Adopt the next standard size, $d = 55$ mm.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Sample Paper]`

**(a) Explain the different types of Factors of Safety used in design, with suitable examples.** (7 marks)

**(b) A cylindrical pressure vessel has an internal diameter of 400 mm and is subjected to an internal pressure of 2 MPa. The allowable tensile stress in the material is 120 MPa. Calculate the minimum wall thickness using:**
   - (i) Longitudinal stress formula
   - (ii) Hoop (circumferential) stress formula
   - **Determine the governing thickness and comment on the role of Factor of Safety in your calculation.** (7 marks)

---

#### **Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Types of Factor of Safety:**

1. **Factor of Safety with respect to Yielding** ($N_y = \sigma_y / \sigma_w$): Used for ductile materials to prevent permanent deformation. *Example: Mild steel shaft design.*

2. **Factor of Safety with respect to Ultimate Strength** ($N_u = \sigma_u / \sigma_w$): Used for brittle materials to prevent fracture. *Example: Cast iron pulley design.*
