3. **Factor of Safety with respect to Fatigue / Endurance** ($N_f = \sigma_e / \sigma_a$): Used for components under cyclic loading. *Example: Crankshaft of an engine.*

4. **Factor of Safety with respect to Buckling** ($N_b = P_{cr} / P_{applied}$): Used for slender columns. *Example: Structural I-beam column.*

5. **Factor of Safety with respect to Creep** ($N_c = \sigma_{cr} / \sigma_w$): Used at elevated temperatures. *Example: Boiler tubes, gas turbine blades.*

6. **Factor of Safety with respect to Deflection** ($N_\delta = \delta_{allow} / \delta_{actual}$): Used for stiffness-critical components. *Example: Machine tool beds, cantilever beams.*

7. **Factor of Safety with respect to Impact** ($N_i = \sigma_{imp,allow} / \sigma_{imp,actual}$): Used for components subjected to sudden loads. *Example: Hammers, punches, press tools.*

---

#### **Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Given:** Internal diameter $D = 400$ mm, Internal pressure $p = 2$ MPa, Allowable stress $\sigma_w = 120$ MPa.

**Step 1: Longitudinal (Axial) Stress in a Thin Cylindrical Pressure Vessel.**

$$\sigma_L = \frac{p \cdot D}{4 t}$$

Setting $\sigma_L = \sigma_w$:

$$t_L = \frac{p \cdot D}{4 \sigma_w} = \frac{2 \times 400}{4 \times 120} = \frac{800}{480} = 1.667 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2: Hoop (Circumferential) Stress in a Thin Cylindrical Pressure Vessel.**

$$\sigma_H = \frac{p \cdot D}{2 t}$$

Setting $\sigma_H = \sigma_w$:

$$t_H = \frac{p \cdot D}{2 \sigma_w} = \frac{2 \times 400}{2 \times 120} = \frac{800}{240} = 3.333 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3: Identify the Governing Thickness.**

Since hoop stress is **twice** the longitudinal stress, the **hoop stress governs** the design:

$$t_{min} = t_H = 3.333 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4: Add Corrosion Allowance & Practical Margin.**

Adopt $t = 4$ mm (next standard thickness) to provide additional margin.

**Step 5: Comment on Role of FoS.**

The allowable stress $\sigma_w = 120$ MPa is itself derived from the yield strength divided by the Factor of Safety. For example, if the material's yield strength is 360 MPa, then:

$$N = \frac{\sigma_y}{\sigma_w} = \frac{360}{120} = 3$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students often forget to convert $p$, $D$, and $\sigma_w$ to **consistent units**. Mixing MPa with N/mm² is acceptable (numerically equivalent), but mixing N/m² with mm causes dimensional errors worth **2–3 marks**.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Confusing **hoop stress** and **longitudinal stress** formulas. Hoop stress is $\frac{pD}{2t}$ and longitudinal is $\frac{pD}{4t}$. Wrong formula → wrong thickness → **3–4 marks lost**.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Failing to state the **governing thickness** explicitly. Examiners expect a clear statement: *"Since hoop stress > longitudinal stress, the design is governed by hoop stress."*
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Not mentioning the **practical design margin** (corrosion allowance, standard size) — this is a **2-mark deduction** item.
> - **Common Mistake 5:** In derivations, skipping the algebraic step from $\tau_{max} = \sigma_y/(2N)$ to the final $d^3$ form — this loses **3 marks** in the analytical breakdown.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

### 🚀 High-Density Revision Checklist

- ✅ **Definition:** $N = \sigma_{failure} / \sigma_{working}$ — memorize the canonical ratio.
- ✅ **Working Stress:** $\sigma_w = \sigma_{failure} / N$ — the most-used design equation.
- ✅ **Material Selection:**
  - **Ductile** → use $\sigma_y$ (yield)
  - **Brittle** → use $\sigma_u$ (ultimate)
  - **Cyclic** → use $\sigma_e$ (endurance)
- ✅ **For shafts (Tresca):** $d = \left[ 32 N \sqrt{M^2 + T^2} / (\pi \sigma_y) \right]^{1/3}$
- ✅ **For shafts (Von Mises):** $d = \left[ 16 N \sqrt{4 M^2 + 3 T^2} / (\pi \sigma_y) \right]^{1/3}$ *(extra formula worth knowing)*
- ✅ **For axial loading:** $A = P / \sigma_w$, so $d = \sqrt{4P / (\pi \sigma_w)}$
- ✅ **For bending (rectangular section):** $\sigma = 6M / (b t^2)$ — solve for $t$ with $\sigma_w$.
- ✅ **Pressure vessel (thin cylinder):**
  - Hoop stress: $\sigma_H = pD / (2t)$
  - Longitudinal stress: $\sigma_L = pD / (4t)$
  - **Hoop governs** because it is double the longitudinal.
- ✅ **Stress concentration factor $K_t$:** Multiply nominal stress to get peak stress. $K_t \geq 1$ always.
- ✅ **Fatigue concentration factor $K_f$:** $K_f = 1 + q(K_t - 1)$, where $q$ is notch sensitivity.
- ✅ **Typical FoS Values:**
  - Aircraft: $1.2 - 1.5$
  - Machine parts: $1.5 - 3$
  - Pressure vessels: $3 - 4$
  - Cast iron: $5 - 8$
  - Lifting equipment: $5 - 7$
- ✅ **Sign Convention:** FoS is **always positive** and **dimensionless**. Never write negative or fractional FoS for safe design.
- ✅ **Use SI units consistently:** N, mm, MPa (= N/mm²).
- ✅ **Convert kN to N** ($1$ kN $= 10^3$ N) and **kN·m to N·mm** ($1$ kN·m $= 10^6$ N·mm) before substitution.
- ✅ **Goodman Line:** $\sigma_a / \sigma_e + \sigma_m / \sigma_u = 1/N$ for fluctuating stresses.
- ✅ **Soderberg Line:** $\sigma_a / \sigma_e + \sigma_m / \sigma_y = 1/N$ — more conservative.
- ✅ **Gerber Line:** Parabolic — $\sigma_a / \sigma_e + (\sigma_m / \sigma_u)^2 = 1/N$ — more accurate for ductile.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Mantra:** *Always state the governing failure mode, write the FoS equation explicitly, convert units, and present a clearly boxed final answer with units.* Examiners reward methodical, well-structured answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Failure Theories (Overview)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET302 - SOLID MECHANICS AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Failure Theories (Overview)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Failure Theory**: A mathematical model or hypothesis that predicts the onset of yielding or fracture in a material subjected to a **multi-axial state of stress**, by comparing an equivalent (or effective) scalar quantity derived from the stress tensor against an experimentally determined material property threshold (yield stress $\sigma_y$ in tension or ultimate stress $\sigma_u$).

In the context of KTU PCPET302, **failure theories** are the engineering bridge that connects the *theoretical* multi-axial stress analysis (Mohr's circle, principal stresses $\sigma_1, \sigma_2, \sigma_3$) with the *experimental* uniaxial tensile test data obtained from standard coupons tested in the Strength of Materials / Materials Testing Laboratory.

The governing equation in its most generic form is:

$$\sigma_{eq} \le \frac{\sigma_{limit}}{n}$$

where $\sigma_{eq}$ is the **equivalent stress** predicted by the chosen failure theory, $\sigma_{limit}$ is the material property (yield stress $\sigma_y$ for ductile or ultimate stress $\sigma_u$ for brittle), and $n$ is the **factor of safety (FOS)**.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Squeezing the Watermelon" Model

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Think of a watermelon in a cardboard box.** The watermelon can be pressed from the top, sides, or corners. A failure theory tells you whether the box (your material) will crack based on the **combined squeezing** from all directions — not just one.

A single tensile test gives you the strength of a material in **one direction only**. But in real machines:
- A pressure vessel wall feels **hoop** and **longitudinal** stress simultaneously.
- A crankshaft feels **bending + torsion + axial**.
- A rocket nozzle feels **thermal + pressure + vibration**.

You cannot test a coupon under every combination. So engineers created **failure theories** — essentially "translation formulas" — that convert any complicated 3-D stress state into an equivalent single-direction stress, which can then be compared to the simple lab-tested value.

### 1.3 The Three Pillars of a Failure Theory (KTU Board Favourite)

> [!NOTE]
> Every failure theory rests on three assumptions:
> 1. **The Stress State Assumption** — Homogeneous, continuous, isotropic material (basic KTU assumption).
> 2. **The Failure Mode Assumption** — Ductile yielding OR brittle fracture (you must pick the right one for the material).
> 3. **The Causative Mechanism Assumption** — What physically causes failure (max stretch, max shear, distortional energy, etc.).

### 1.4 Why Failure Theories Matter — Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace**: Wing spars in multi-axial loading — von Mises is mandatory.
- **Pressure Vessel Design**: ASME Sec VIII uses **Tresca** for conservative design.
- **Automotive**: Crankshaft design relies on **DE-Goodman / DE-Soderberg** combinations.
- **Civil/Structural**: Column buckling combines axial + bending via interaction formulas derived from these theories.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Are Failure Theories Needed? (The Core "Why")

In your Strength of Materials (SOM) course you solved only **uniaxial** problems — one stress, compare to $\sigma_y$. In Design of Machine Elements, components are **multi-axial**. We need a rule to:

1. Reduce the 3D stress tensor to **one scalar** $\sigma_{eq}$.
2. Compare $\sigma_{eq}$ with $\sigma_y$ obtained from a lab test.
3. Predict the **onset of yielding** (ductile) or **fracture** (brittle).

### 2.2 Classification of Failure Theories (KTU Board Standard Classification)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Classification** — KTU examiners love asking this as a 3-mark or 6-mark question.

The five classical failure theories are grouped by their assumed causative mechanism:

**Group A — Stress-Based Theories**
1. **Maximum Normal Stress Theory (MNST)** — Rankine (1858)
2. **Maximum Shear Stress Theory (MSST)** — Tresca (1864)
3. **Maximum Principal Strain Theory (MPSt)** — Saint-Venant

**Group B — Strain-Energy-Based Theories**
4. **Total Strain Energy Theory (TSET)** — Beltrami–Haigh (1901)
5. **Maximum Distortion Energy Theory (MDET)** — von Mises (1913) / Huber (1904)

**Group C — Empirical / Modern Theories**
6. **Coulomb-Mohr Theory** (for brittle)
7. **Modified Mohr Theory** (for brittle)

---

### 2.3 Master Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **Convention used below**: Tensile stresses are POSITIVE. Principal stresses are ordered $\sigma_1 \ge \sigma_2 \ge \sigma_3$. Yield strength is $\sigma_y$. All formulas yield an *equivalent stress* $\sigma_{eq}$ to be compared with $\sigma_y$.

| # | Theory Name | Equivalent Stress Formula $\sigma_{eq}$ | Applicable Material | KTU Reliability |
|---|-------------|------------------------------------------|---------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | **Max Normal Stress (Rankine)** | $\sigma_{eq} = \max(\lvert \sigma_1 \rvert, \lvert \sigma_3 \rvert)$ | Brittle (cast iron, concrete) | Conservative for brittle; UNSAFE for ductile |
| 2 | **Max Shear Stress (Tresca)** | $\sigma_{eq} = \sigma_1 - \sigma_3$ | Ductile (mild steel) | Highly conservative, simple, KTU-friendly |
| 3 | **Max Principal Strain (Saint-Venant)** | $\sigma_{eq} = \sigma_1 - \nu(\sigma_2 + \sigma_3)$ | Brittle (rarely used) | Inconsistent, not preferred |
| 4 | **Total Strain Energy (Beltrami)** | $\sigma_{eq} = \sqrt{\tfrac{1}{2}[(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2] + 3\sigma_H^2}$ | Theoretically any; **WRONG for design** | Predicts yielding at hydrostatic pressure (incorrect) |
| 5 | **Distortion Energy (von Mises)** | $\sigma_{eq} = \sqrt{\tfrac{1}{2}[(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2]}$ | Ductile (steel, Al, Cu alloys) | **MOST ACCURATE — Industry standard** |
| 6 | **Coulomb-Mohr (brittle)** | $\sigma_{eq} = \sigma_1 - \dfrac{\sigma_{uc}}{\sigma_{ut}}\sigma_3$ | Brittle | Good for brittle with unequal strengths |

where $\sigma_H = \tfrac{1}{3}(\sigma_1 + \sigma_2 + \sigma_3)$ is the **hydrostatic stress** and $\nu$ is **Poisson's ratio**.

---

### 2.4 The "Big Three" Theories KTU Repeatedly Asks

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tresca vs. von Mises — the eternal KTU comparison.**

#### (a) Tresca Criterion (1864)
> Failure occurs when the **maximum shear stress** in the component reaches the maximum shear stress at yielding in a simple tension test.

$$\boxed{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 \;\le\; \dfrac{\sigma_y}{n}}$$

- **Shape on $\pi$-plane**: Hexagon (inscribed inside von Mises ellipse).
- **Tresca is more conservative** — predicts yielding earlier than von Mises.

#### (b) Von Mises Criterion (1913)
> Failure occurs when the **distortion strain energy** reaches the same value as in a tensile test at yield.

$$\boxed{\sqrt{\tfrac{1}{2}\big[(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2\big]} \;\le\; \dfrac{\sigma_y}{n}}$$

- **Shape on $\pi$-plane**: Ellipse (smooth, fitted to Tresca hexagon).
- **Better fit to experimental data** (within 15% accuracy for ductile metals).

#### (c) Tresca–Von Mises Numerical Ratio

For **plane stress** with $\sigma_1, \sigma_2$ as principal in-plane stresses (with $\sigma_3 = 0$):

$$\dfrac{\sigma_{eq,\,von\,Mises}}{\sigma_{eq,\,Tresca}} = \dfrac{\sqrt{\sigma_1^2 - \sigma_1\sigma_2 + \sigma_2^2}}{\sigma_1 + \sigma_2}$$

The maximum ratio occurs at **pure shear** or at $\sigma_1 = -\sigma_2$, where:

$$\dfrac{\sigma_{eq,\,VM}}{\sigma_{eq,\,Tresca}} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 0.577$$

This means **Tresca predicts yielding at only 57.7% of von Mises** in pure shear — hence the "highly conservative" tag.

---

### 2.5 Ductile vs. Brittle — The Selection Rule

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick KTU Decision Table for Picking the Right Theory**

| Material Class | Examples | Recommended Theory | Backup Theory |
|----------------|----------|--------------------|--------------:|
| **Ductile** | Mild steel, Aluminium, Copper | **Von Mises (MDET)** | Tresca (conservative) |
| **Brittle** | Cast iron, Concrete, Ceramics | **Coulomb-Mohr** | Rankine (MNST) |
| **Brittle in compression-dominant** | Concrete columns | **Modified Mohr** | Rankine |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **ASME Boiler & Pressure Vessel Code**: Uses **Tresca** (max shear) for primary stress limits because it gives a safety margin against unknown biaxial states.
- **Aerospace (FAA)**: Uses **von Mises** for ductile aluminium alloys — built into every ANSYS / Abaqus solver as the default yield criterion.
- **Civil Engineering (ACI 318)**: Uses **Modified Mohr** for concrete columns because concrete has $f_t \neq f_c$.
- **Shigley's Mechanical Engineering Design** (KTU reference): Lists both Tresca and von Mises as standard KTU textbook content.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Von Mises Criterion from First Principles

> [!NOTE]
> The von Mises criterion is the **KTU board's most-asked derivation**. Master this.

#### Step 1 — Start with Total Strain Energy Density

For a linear-elastic isotropic material, the **total strain energy density** $U$ stored per unit volume is:

$$U = \frac{1}{2E}\big[\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 + \sigma_3^2\big] - \frac{\nu}{E}\big[\sigma_1\sigma_2 + \sigma_2\sigma_3 + \sigma_3\sigma_1\big]$$

#### Step 2 — Decompose Stress Tensor into Hydrostatic + Deviatoric Parts

Any stress tensor can be split into:

$$\sigma_{ij} = \sigma_H \,\delta_{ij} + s_{ij}$$

where the **hydrostatic (mean) stress** is:

$$\sigma_H = \frac{\sigma_1 + \sigma_2 + \sigma_3}{3}$$

and the **deviatoric stress components** are:

$$s_1 = \sigma_1 - \sigma_H, \quad s_2 = \sigma_2 - \sigma_H, \quad s_3 = \sigma_3 - \sigma_H$$

Note that the deviatoric part is **deviatoric** (trace zero):

$$s_1 + s_2 + s_3 = 0$$

#### Step 3 — Split Strain Energy into Volumetric + Distortional

The total strain energy splits as $U = U_v + U_d$:

$$U_v = \frac{1-2\nu}{6E}\big[(\sigma_1+\sigma_2+\sigma_3)^2\big] \quad \text{(volumetric, causes shape-preserving volume change)}$$

$$U_d = \frac{1+\nu}{6E}\big[(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2\big] \quad \text{(distortional, causes shape change)}$$

#### Step 4 — Hypothesis (Huber–von Mises–Hencky)

> **At yielding, only the distortional energy causes permanent deformation; the volumetric part is stored as elastic recovery. Failure occurs when $U_d$ equals its value at yielding in a uniaxial tension test.**

In a simple tension test at yield $\sigma_1 = \sigma_y$, $\sigma_2 = \sigma_3 = 0$:

$$U_d^{yield} = \frac{1+\nu}{6E}\big[(0-\sigma_y)^2 + (\sigma_y-0)^2 + (0-0)^2\big] = \frac{(1+\nu)\sigma_y^2}{3E}$$

#### Step 5 — Equate and Solve for the Failure Envelope

Setting the general distortional energy equal to the uniaxial yielding value:

$$\frac{1+\nu}{6E}\big[(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2\big] = \frac{(1+\nu)\sigma_y^2}{3E}$$

Cancelling $\tfrac{1+\nu}{6E}$ from both sides:

$$(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2 = 2\sigma_y^2$$

#### Step 6 — Define Equivalent Stress

Dividing by 2 and taking the square root gives the **von Mises equivalent stress**:

$$\boxed{\sigma_{eq} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{2}\big[(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2\big]}}$$

The failure/yielding condition is:

$$\boxed{\sigma_{eq} \le \frac{\sigma_y}{n}}$$

---

### 3.2 Von Mises in Terms of General 3-D Stress Components

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This form is required when stresses are given as $\sigma_x, \sigma_y, \sigma_z, \tau_{xy}, \tau_{yz}, \tau_{zx}$ rather than principal values.

$$\sigma_{eq}^2 = \sigma_x^2 + \sigma_y^2 + \sigma_z^2 - \sigma_x\sigma_y - \sigma_y\sigma_z - \sigma_z\sigma_x + 3(\tau_{xy}^2 + \tau_{yz}^2 + \tau_{zx}^2)$$

Derivation path: substitute $\sigma_1 = $ the largest root of the stress-cubic into the principal-stress form. We can verify the consistency by setting $\tau_{xy} = \tau_{yz} = \tau_{zx} = 0$, which forces $\sigma_1, \sigma_2, \sigma_3 = \sigma_x, \sigma_y, \sigma_z$ and the formula reduces to the principal-stress form.

---

### 3.3 Von Mises in Plane Stress (2-D Special Case)

For $\sigma_z = 0$, $\tau_{yz} = \tau_{zx} = 0$, leaving $\sigma_x, \sigma_y, \tau_{xy}$:

$$\sigma_{eq}^2 = \sigma_x^2 - \sigma_x\sigma_y + \sigma_y^2 + 3\tau_{xy}^2$$

Or in terms of in-plane principal stresses $\sigma_1, \sigma_2$ (with $\sigma_3 = 0$):

$$\sigma_{eq} = \sqrt{\sigma_1^2 - \sigma_1\sigma_2 + \sigma_2^2}$$

This is the **most-asked** KTU form for shaft and pressure-vessel problems.

---

### 3.4 Tresca Derivation (Geometric Argument)

> The Tresca criterion follows from the **maximum shear stress** in a 3-D Mohr's circle diagram.

The maximum shear stress in any stress state is:

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2}$$

At yielding in a tensile test: $\tau_{max}^{tension} = \tfrac{\sigma_y}{2}$.

Equating the two:

$$\boxed{\frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2} = \frac{\sigma_y}{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = \sigma_y}$$

With safety factor $n$:

$$\boxed{\sigma_{eq} = \sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = \frac{\sigma_y}{n}}$$

---

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Tresca vs. Von Mises

| Loading Type | Stress State | Tresca $\sigma_{eq}$ | Von Mises $\sigma_{eq}$ | Ratio (VM/Tresca) |
|--------------|-------------|----------------------|--------------------------|-------------------|
| Uniaxial tension | $(\sigma_y, 0, 0)$ | $\sigma_y$ | $\sigma_y$ | 1.000 |
| Pure shear | $(0, 0, \tau)$ | $2\tau$ | $\sqrt{3}\,\tau$ | 0.866 |
| Biaxial equal tension | $(\sigma, \sigma, 0)$ | $\sigma$ | $\sigma$ | 1.000 |
| Pure biaxial (one tension, one equal compression) | $(\sigma, -\sigma, 0)$ | $2\sigma$ | $\sqrt{3}\,\sigma$ | 0.866 |
| Hydrostatic pressure | $(-\sigma, -\sigma, -\sigma)$ | $0$ | $0$ | undefined |

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the table**: When ratio = 1, both theories agree. When ratio < 1, **von Mises predicts a lower $\sigma_{eq}$** at the same loading — meaning von Mises is *less conservative* than Tresca for that loading (allows more design stress).

---

### 3.6 Why von Mises is Superior — Mathematical Proof Sketch

For **pure shear** (a very common KTU problem), Tresca predicts yielding at:

$$\tau_{y,\,Tresca} = \frac{\sigma_y}{2}$$

Von Mises predicts yielding at:

$$\tau_{y,\,VM} = \frac{\sigma_y}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 0.577\,\sigma_y$$

Experimental data for ductile steels consistently shows the yield in pure shear closer to $0.577 \sigma_y$, validating **von Mises**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Classification Flowchart (Mermaid Block Diagram)

> [!NOTE]
> This block-level functional architecture maps all five classical theories, their inputs, intermediate computation, and output equivalent stress used in design checks.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Stress State sigma_x, sigma_y, sigma_z, tau_xy, tau_yz, tau_zx] --> B[Step 1: Compute Principal Stresses using stress invariants]
    B --> C[Step 2: Order sigma_1 geq sigma_2 geq sigma_3]
    C --> D{Material Type Decision}
    D -- Ductile --> E{Ductile Sub-Decision}
    D -- Brittle --> F{Brittle Sub-Decision}
    E -- Need Highest Accuracy --> G[Von Mises MDET]
    E -- Need Conservative --> H[Tresca MSST]
    F -- Same Strength Tension and Compression --> I[Rankine MNST]
    F -- Different Strength --> J[Coulomb Mohr]
    G --> K[Output: sigma_eq = sqrt half sum of squared differences]
    H --> L[Output: sigma_eq = sigma_1 minus sigma_3]
    I --> M[Output: sigma_eq = max of sigma_1 and sigma_3 magnitudes]
    J --> N[Output: sigma_eq = sigma_1 minus ratio times sigma_3]
    K --> O{Compare sigma_eq with sigma_y over n}
    L --> O
    M --> P{Compare sigma_eq with sigma_u over n}
    N --> P
    O -- sigma_eq le limit --> Q[Safe Design]
    O -- sigma_eq gt limit --> R[Failure Predicted Increase Thickness or Change Material]
    P -- sigma_eq le limit --> Q
    P -- sigma_eq gt limit --> R
```

### 4.2 2-D Yield Locus — Tresca Hexagon vs. Von Mises Ellipse (Mermaid Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Plane_Stress_Space
        X[Sigma_1 axis] --- O[Origin] --- Y[Sigma_2 axis]
    end
    Tresca_Hex[Hexagon Tresca inscribed touches von Mises at six points] --- Hex_Pts[Vertices at sigma_y on axes and at sigma_y and sigma_y on diagonals]
    Von_Ellipse[Ellipse von Mises smooth fitted] --- Ell_Pts[Touches Hexagon at axis points and inscribed at 45 degree directions]
    Tresca_Hex --- Compare[Geometric Comparison]
    Von_Ellipse --- Compare
    Compare --> Final[Tresca is more conservative except at axis points and 45 degree pure shear where both agree]
```

### 4.3 Functional Block Diagram — How Failure Theories Plug Into Design

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Load[External Loads] --> FEM[Stress Analysis]
    FEM --> State[Multi axial Stress State sigma_1, sigma_2, sigma_3]
    State --> Theory[Selected Failure Theory]
    Theory --> Sigma_eq[Equivalent Stress sigma_eq]
    Material_Props[Lab Data sigma_y, sigma_u, nu] --> Sigma_y[Allowable Stress sigma_y over n]
    Sigma_eq --> Compare{sigma_eq le sigma_y over n ?}
    Sigma_y --> Compare
    Compare -- Yes --> Safe[Component is Safe]
    Compare -- No --> Fail[Redesign: Increase Section, Change Material, Reduce Load]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip (KTU)**: Always draw such block diagrams to score full marks on 14-mark design questions — they demonstrate systems thinking that examiners reward.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> *Define the term "equivalent stress" as used in failure theories. Why is it necessary in the design of machine elements subjected to multi-axial stresses?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Equivalent stress $\sigma_{eq}$ is a single scalar value derived from the multi-axial stress state using a chosen failure theory, which represents the same propensity for failure as a uniaxial stress of magnitude $\sigma_{eq}$ applied to the same material. Mathematically:

$$\sigma_{eq} = f(\sigma_1, \sigma_2, \sigma_3, \nu, \text{theory})$$

**Necessity (1 mark):** Material strength data from standard tensile tests is uniaxial. Real components face multi-axial states. Failure theories act as the *translator* between multi-axial reality and uniaxial lab data, allowing engineers to make safe design decisions.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
> *State the Maximum Shear Stress Theory (Tresca criterion) and write the failure envelope equation for a ductile material with yield stress $\sigma_y$ and factor of safety $n$.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Statement (1 mark):** According to Tresca, failure (yielding) occurs when the maximum shear stress in the component equals the maximum shear stress at yielding in a simple tensile test.

**Equation (2 marks):**

$$\tau_{max}^{component} = \tau_{max}^{tensile}$$

$$\frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2} = \frac{\sigma_y}{2n}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = \frac{\sigma_y}{n}}$$

---

### 5.2 Part B Question (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

> **Question A — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]**
>
> **(a)** Derive the **von Mises yield criterion** for a ductile material starting from the decomposition of total strain energy into volumetric and distortional components. Clearly state the hypothesis used. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A machine component made of **mild steel (yield stress = 250 MPa)** is subjected to a 2-D plane stress state:
> $$\sigma_x = 80 \text{ MPa}, \quad \sigma_y = -40 \text{ MPa}, \quad \tau_{xy} = 30 \text{ MPa}$$
> Using both **Tresca** and **von Mises** criteria, determine the factor of safety. Comment on which theory is more conservative. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question A(a)

**Step 1 — Strain Energy Density (1 mark):**

$$U = \frac{1}{2E}\big[\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 + \sigma_3^2\big] - \frac{\nu}{E}\big[\sigma_1\sigma_2 + \sigma_2\sigma_3 + \sigma_3\sigma_1\big]$$

**Step 2 — Decompose into Hydrostatic and Deviatoric (2 marks):**

$$\sigma_H = \frac{\sigma_1 + \sigma_2 + \sigma_3}{3}, \quad s_i = \sigma_i - \sigma_H$$

**Step 3 — Volumetric and Distortional Components (2 marks):**

$$U_v = \frac{1-2\nu}{6E}(\sigma_1+\sigma_2+\sigma_3)^2$$

$$U_d = \frac{1+\nu}{6E}\big[(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2\big]$$

**Step 4 — Hypothesis and Yield Condition (1 mark):**

At yielding under uniaxial tension ($\sigma_1 = \sigma_y$, $\sigma_2 = \sigma_3 = 0$):

$$U_d^{yield} = \frac{(1+\nu)\sigma_y^2}{3E}$$

Equating:

$$\frac{1+\nu}{6E}\big[(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2\big] = \frac{(1+\nu)\sigma_y^2}{3E}$$

**Step 5 — Final Equation (1 mark):**

$$\boxed{\sigma_{eq} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{2}\big[(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2\big]}}$$

Failure condition: $\sigma_{eq} \le \sigma_y / n$.

---

#### Model Solution — Question A(b)

**Step 1 — Principal Stresses (2 marks):**

$$\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$$

$$\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{80 + (-40)}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{80 - (-40)}{2}\right)^2 + 30^2}$$

$$\sigma_{1,2} = 20 \pm \sqrt{60^2 + 30^2} = 20 \pm \sqrt{3600 + 900}$$

$$\sigma_{1,2} = 20 \pm \sqrt{4500} = 20 \pm 67.082$$

$$\sigma_1 = 87.082 \text{ MPa}, \quad \sigma_2 = -47.082 \text{ MPa}, \quad \sigma_3 = 0 \text{ MPa (plane stress)}$$

Re-ordered: $\sigma_1 = 87.082$, $\sigma_2 = 0$, $\sigma_3 = -47.082$ MPa.

**Step 2 — Tresca Factor of Safety (1.5 marks):**

$$\sigma_{eq}^{Tresca} = \sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = 87.082 - (-47.082) = 134.164 \text{ MPa}$$

$$n_{Tresca} = \frac{\sigma_y}{\sigma_{eq}} = \frac{250}{134.164} = 1.863$$

**Step 3 — Von Mises Factor of Safety (1.5 marks):**

$$\sigma_{eq}^{VM} = \sqrt{\sigma_1^2 - \sigma_1\sigma_2 + \sigma_2^2 + 3\tau_{xy}^2}$$

Using the 2-D form directly: $\sigma_x^2 - \sigma_x\sigma_y + \sigma_y^2 + 3\tau_{xy}^2 = 80^2 - 80(-40) + (-40)^2 + 3(30)^2$

$$= 6400 + 3200 + 1600 + 2700 = 13900$$

$$\sigma_{eq}^{VM} = \sqrt{13900} = 117.898 \text{ MPa}$$

$$n_{VM} = \frac{250}{117.898} = 2.121$$

**Step 4 — Comment (2 marks):**

$$n_{VM} > n_{Tresca} \quad (2.121 > 1.863)$$

**Tresca is more conservative** — it predicts a lower factor of safety (1.863) for the same loading, meaning it requires a thicker / heavier component to satisfy design. Von Mises allows a lighter design while still being safe for ductile mild steel. For a real KTU design problem, you would adopt the **lower** value, $n_{Tresca} = 1.863$, to be on the safe side unless your company follows ASME / DGCA standards that explicitly mandate von Mises.

---

> **Question B — Alternative 14-Mark Choice — [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Modified]**
>
> **(a)** Classify the classical failure theories used in the design of machine elements. Compare the Maximum Shear Stress Theory and the Maximum Distortion Energy Theory with respect to their applicability, conservativeness, and experimental accuracy. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A **brittle cast iron** component (ultimate tensile strength $\sigma_{ut} = 200$ MPa, ultimate compressive strength $\sigma_{uc} = 600$ MPa) experiences the following principal stresses:
> $$\sigma_1 = 120 \text{ MPa}, \quad \sigma_2 = 50 \text{ MPa}, \quad \sigma_3 = -90 \text{ MPa}$$
> Apply (i) Maximum Normal Stress Theory (Rankine) and (ii) Coulomb-Mohr Theory to determine the factor of safety. State which theory is more suitable for this material. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question B(a)

**Classification (3 marks):**

1. **Stress-based theories** — Rankine (MNST), Tresca (MSST), Saint-Venant (MPSt).
2. **Strain-energy-based theories** — Beltrami-Haigh (TSET), von Mises (MDET).
3. **Empirical theories** — Coulomb-Mohr, Modified Mohr (for brittle materials).

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Aspect | Tresca (MSST) | Von Mises (MDET) |
|--------|---------------|-------------------|
| Assumption | Max shear stress causes yield | Distortion energy causes yield |
| Equivalent stress | $\sigma_1 - \sigma_3$ | $\sqrt{\tfrac{1}{2}[(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2+(\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2+(\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2]}$ |
| Yield locus (π-plane) | Hexagon | Ellipse |
| Conservativeness | More conservative (factor of safety 1 / VM is ~0.866× Tresca) | Less conservative |
| Experimental fit | Predicts yielding in pure shear at $\tau = \sigma_y/2$ (low) | Predicts $\tau_y = \sigma_y/\sqrt{3}$ (matches ductile data) |
| Use | Conservative shaft design, ASME Sec VIII | Aerospace, FEA software, Shigley default |
| Limitation | Ignores intermediate principal stress | Slightly more complex algebra |

---

#### Model Solution — Question B(b)

**Given:** $\sigma_1 = 120$ MPa, $\sigma_2 = 50$ MPa, $\sigma_3 = -90$ MPa, $\sigma_{ut} = 200$ MPa, $\sigma_{uc} = 600$ MPa.

**(i) Rankine / Maximum Normal Stress Theory (3.5 marks):**

For brittle materials, the governing strength is the **ultimate tensile strength** if the maximum principal stress is tensile, and the **ultimate compressive strength** if the maximum principal compressive stress exceeds limits.

$$\sigma_1 = +120 \text{ MPa (tensile)}, \quad \lvert \sigma_3 \rvert = 90 \text{ MPa (compressive)}$$

The critical stress is $\sigma_1 = 120$ MPa (tensile), so compare to $\sigma_{ut}$:

$$n_{Rankine} = \frac{\sigma_{ut}}{\sigma_1} = \frac{200}{120} = 1.667$$

**(ii) Coulomb-Mohr Theory (3.5 marks):**

For brittle with $\sigma_1 > 0$ and $\sigma_3 < 0$:

$$\frac{\sigma_1}{\sigma_{ut}} - \frac{\sigma_3}{\sigma_{uc}} = \frac{1}{n}$$

$$\frac{120}{200} - \frac{-90}{600} = \frac{1}{n}$$

$$0.600 + 0.150 = \frac{1}{n}$$

$$0.750 = \frac{1}{n} \quad \Rightarrow \quad n_{CM} = 1.333$$

**Discussion:** $n_{CM} < n_{Rankine}$, so **Coulomb-Mohr is more conservative** and more accurate for brittle materials with unequal tensile/compressive strengths. For cast iron, **Coulomb-Mohr is the recommended theory** as it accounts for the much higher compressive strength of cast iron.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in KTU PCPET302 — Failure Theory Questions:**
>
> 1. **Sign confusion**: Forgetting that compressive principal stresses are **negative**. Many students write $\sigma_3 = +90$ in Question B(b) and get $n = 1.0$ (wrong). Always substitute with **signs preserved**.
> 2. **Ordering**: Tresca uses $\sigma_1 - \sigma_3$ where $\sigma_1$ is **algebraically largest** and $\sigma_3$ is **algebraically smallest**, not "most tensile". If a negative value is the smallest (most negative), it becomes $\sigma_3$.
> 3. **Plane stress third principal stress**: In 2-D problems, students forget that $\sigma_3 = 0$ exists and must be included. Always re-order all three values before applying Tresca.
> 4. **Factor of safety direction**: $\;n = \sigma_y / \sigma_{eq}\;$ for loading-based problems; $\;n = \sigma_y - \sigma_{eq}\;$ only applies to specific strain-based contexts. Verify which the question is asking.
> 5. **Wrong formula for brittle**: Applying von Mises to cast iron / concrete — **lose 4 marks minimum**. Always classify the material FIRST.
> 6. **Forgetting units**: $\sigma_y$ and $\sigma_{eq}$ must be in the same units (MPa in, MPa out). Watch out for ksi vs MPa conversions in KTU questions with imported data.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield KTU Module 4 — Failure Theories (Overview) — Rapid Recap**

- [ ] **Definition**: Failure theory = rule that converts multi-axial stress to a scalar $\sigma_{eq}$ comparable with uniaxial lab data ($\sigma_y$ or $\sigma_u$).
- [ ] **Failure equation**: $\sigma_{eq} \le \sigma_{limit} / n$ where $n$ is the factor of safety.
- [ ] **Five classical theories**: Rankine (MNST), Tresca (MSST), Saint-Venant (MPSt), Beltrami-Haigh (TSET), von Mises (MDET).
- [ ] **Modern for brittle**: Coulomb-Mohr and Modified Mohr are KTU-expected additions.
- [ ] **Ductile materials** → use **von Mises** (preferred) or **Tresca** (conservative).
- [ ] **Brittle materials** → use **Coulomb-Mohr** (preferred) or **Rankine** (simpler, conservative for tensile).
- [ ] **Tresca formula**: $\sigma_{eq} = \sigma_1 - \sigma_3$ (algebraically largest minus smallest).
- [ ] **Von Mises formula**: $\sigma_{eq} = \sqrt{\tfrac{1}{2}[(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2]}$.
- [ ] **2-D Von Mises form** (most asked): $\sigma_{eq} = \sqrt{\sigma_x^2 - \sigma_x\sigma_y + \sigma_y^2 + 3\tau_{xy}^2}$.
- [ ] **Pure shear yield ratio**: $\sigma_{y,\,VM} / \sigma_{y,\,Tresca} = 2/\sqrt{3} \approx 1.155$ — explains why Tresca is more conservative in torsion.
- [ ] **Yield locus shape**: Tresca = hexagon; von Mises = ellipse (smooth, inscribed).
- [ ] **Volumetric energy** causes only elastic volume change (no yield). **Distortional energy** causes permanent shape change (yield).
- [ ] **Huber-von Mises-Hencky (HVH)** = full name of the von Mises criterion (3 scientists, 3 countries — historically important).
- [ ] **Hydrostatic stress** (equal pressure from all sides) does NOT cause yielding in ductile materials per von Mises — both $\sigma_{eq}$ become zero.
- [ ] **Real-world codes**: ASME = Tresca; FAA/Aerospace = von Mises; ACI = Modified Mohr.
- [ ] **Total Strain Energy Theory is WRONG for design** because it predicts yielding under pure hydrostatic pressure — never use it for design (only for academic comparison).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Torsion Theory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET302 - SOLID MECHANICS AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Torsion Theory

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Torsion? — The Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Torsion** is the twisting of a structural member about its longitudinal (longitudinal) axis when subjected to equal and opposite **torques (couples)** acting in planes perpendicular to the axis of the member. The internal effect produced is a **shearing stress** on cross-sections perpendicular to the axis, combined with an **angle of twist** along the length.

A shaft transmitting power through gears, pulleys, or couplings is the most common engineering element subjected to torsion. The torque applied at one end and resisted at the other creates a state of **pure shear** on the cross-section.

### Formal Terminology (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

| Term | Formal Definition |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Shaft** | A rotating machine element, usually of circular cross-section, used to transmit power/torque. |
| **Axle** | A stationary member that supports rotating parts (e.g., car axles). Not primarily designed to transmit torque. |
| **Twisting Moment / Torque (T)** | The moment applied about the longitudinal axis; unit is **N·m** (SI). |
| **Angle of Twist (θ)** | The total rotation (in radians) of one cross-section relative to another along the shaft length. |
| **Polar Section Modulus (Zₚ or J/c)** | The geometric property of a cross-section that resists twisting. |
| **Stiffness (k)** | The torque required to produce a unit angle of twist, **k = GJ/L**. |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Wringed Towel Intuition 💡

Imagine a wet towel held between your two hands, palms facing each other. You twist one hand clockwise and the other counter-clockwise:

- The **fibres** of the towel (representing **longitudinal lines** on the shaft) that were originally straight and parallel become **helical spirals** wrapped around the axis.
- Cross-sections of the towel (originally flat circles) **rotate relative to each other** by a small angle — this is the **angle of twist (θ)**.
- The towel resists your twisting — this resistance is quantified as **torque (T)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Torsion is essentially a *shear* phenomenon. Although the geometry appears rotational, internally the material is experiencing **pure shear stresses** (τ) on planes perpendicular and parallel to the axis. This makes the strength of a shaft governed by its **shear modulus (G)** and **polar moment of inertia (J)**.

---

## 1.3 Pure Shear & Complementary Shear — The Underlying Stress State

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pure Shear** is a state of stress where two pairs of equal, opposite, and mutually perpendicular shear stresses act on a rectangular element with **no accompanying normal stresses (σ = 0)**.

If a small element of the shaft surface is examined:

$$
\sigma_x = \sigma_y = 0, \quad \tau_{xy} = \tau
$$

By **complementary shear** (a consequence of moment equilibrium of the element), shear stresses of equal magnitude must also exist on the **perpendicular faces**:

$$
\tau_{xy} = \tau_{yx} = \tau
$$

This produces **principal stresses** acting at 45° to the shaft axis:

$$
\sigma_1 = +\tau \quad \text{(tension)} \quad \sigma_2 = -\tau \quad \text{(compression)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is why **ductile shafts under torsion fail along a 45° helical plane** (the maximum tensile principal stress plane) — the classic "**corkscrew fracture**" seen in drive shafts. This is a frequent 3-mark question in KTU exams.

---

## 1.4 Assumptions in the Theory of Pure Torsion (Circular Shafts)

The classical torsion formula is derived under these **idealized assumptions** (verbatim from standard KTU textbook treatment):

1. The shaft material is **homogeneous, isotropic, and obeys Hooke's Law** (linearly elastic).
2. Cross-sections **plane and circular** before twisting **remain plane and circular** after twisting. (Cross-sections do **not** warp.)
3. **Radii of cross-sections remain straight and undistorted** — they only rotate as rigid bodies about the axis.
4. **Shaft is subjected to equal and opposite torques at its ends**, producing uniform torque along its length.
5. **Distortion is small** — small angle approximation is valid (θ in radians, sin θ ≈ θ).
6. Cross-sections rotate **as if rigid disks**, maintaining their circular shape (only true for circular cross-sections).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Assumption 2 (plane sections remain plane) is **only valid for solid or hollow circular shafts**. For **non-circular sections** (rectangular, triangular, I-section), cross-sections **warp out of plane**, and the simple torsion equation **T/J = τ/r = Gθ/L** does **NOT** apply. Use of the torsion equation for non-circular sections is a guaranteed loss of marks in KTU evaluations.

---

## 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bold Per Protocol)

| Property | Symbol | Typical Value (Steel) |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| **Modulus of Rigidity (Shear Modulus)** | **G** | **80 GPa (structural steel), 79 GPa (mild steel)** |
| **Poisson's Ratio** | **ν** | **0.30 (steel), 0.33 (aluminium)** |
| **Relationship** | **E = 2G(1+ν)** | — |
| **Standard shaft angular speed** | **N** | **rpm** (revolutions per minute) |
| **Standard angular velocity** | **ω** | **rad/s** |
| **Conversion** | **ω = 2πN/60** | — |

---

## 1.6 Historical Context & KTU Relevance

> [!NOTE]
> The classical theory of torsion of circular shafts was developed by **Coulomb (1784)** in France and independently verified experimentally by **Duleau (1813)**. The famous formula **T/J = τ/r = Gθ/L** is often called the **Coulomb-Duleau torsion formula**. The theory for **non-circular sections** (membrane analogy) was developed much later by **Saint-Venant (1855)**.

In KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 4 of PCPET302 typically tests:
- Derivation and application of the torsion equation (often a **7-mark part in Part B**).
- Power transmission and design of shafts.
- Composite (stepped/shafts of two materials) problems.
- Helical spring design (Part B finale question).

# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Torsion Equation — The Heart of Module 4

The **Torsion Equation** for a solid or hollow circular shaft is:

$$
\boxed{\dfrac{T}{J} \;=\; \dfrac{\tau}{r} \;=\; \dfrac{G\,\theta}{L}}
$$

Where each term has a specific meaning. Let us break this down logically.

### 2.1.1 The Three-Layer Interpretation

The equation is actually **three equations in one** — a beautiful unification of geometry, elasticity, and kinematics:

| Layer | Equation | Physical Meaning |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| **Force-Equilibrium Layer** | T / J = τ / r | Torque resistance is proportional to shear stress (linear stress distribution from center). |
| **Elasticity Layer** | τ / r = Gθ / L | Shear stress relates to twist angle through shear strain γ = rθ/L, with τ = Gγ. |
| **Geometric Layer** | T / J | Torque capacity depends purely on cross-section geometry (J). |

---

## 2.2 Derivation Logic — Step-by-Step Reasoning

### Step 1: Geometry of Twist (Kinematics)

Consider a shaft of length **L** and radius **R**, fixed at one end. When a torque **T** is applied at the free end, the free end rotates by an angle **θ** relative to the fixed end.

A point originally on the surface at radial distance **r** moves through an arc on the cross-section. The **arc length** traversed is:

$$
s = r \theta
$$

The **shear strain γ** is the deformation per unit length along the axis:

$$
\gamma = \dfrac{s}{L} = \dfrac{r\theta}{L}
$$

### Step 2: Material Law (Elasticity)

By **Hooke's Law in shear**:

$$
\tau = G\gamma = \dfrac{Gr\theta}{L}
$$

This shows shear stress is **linearly proportional to radial distance r** — zero at the center, maximum at the outer surface.

### Step 3: Force-Equilibrium (Statics)

Consider a thin annular ring at radius **r** with thickness **dr**. The elemental area is:

$$
dA = 2\pi r \, dr
$$

The elemental shear force is $dF = \tau \, dA$. Its moment about the center is:

$$
dT = r \cdot dF = r \cdot \tau \cdot 2\pi r \, dr = 2\pi r^2 \tau \, dr
$$

Total torque:

$$
T = \int_0^R 2\pi r^2 \tau \, dr = \int_0^R 2\pi r^2 \cdot \dfrac{Gr\theta}{L} \, dr
$$

$$
T = \dfrac{2\pi G\theta}{L} \int_0^R r^3 \, dr = \dfrac{2\pi G\theta}{L} \cdot \dfrac{R^4}{4} = \dfrac{\pi G\theta R^4}{2L}
$$

### Step 4: Identify the Polar Moment of Inertia

$$
J = \dfrac{\pi D^4}{32} = \dfrac{\pi R^4}{2}
$$

So:

$$
T = \dfrac{GJ\theta}{L} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \dfrac{T}{J} = \dfrac{G\theta}{L}
$$

Combined with τ = Grθ/L, we get the **unified torsion equation**.

---

## 2.3 Polar Section Modulus (Zₚ) and Strength Design

The **maximum shear stress** occurs at the **outer surface** (r = R):

$$
\tau_{max} = \dfrac{T \cdot R}{J} = \dfrac{T}{Z_p}
$$

Where the **polar section modulus** is:

$$
Z_p = \dfrac{J}{R} = \dfrac{\pi D^3}{16} \quad \text{(solid shaft)}
$$

For a **hollow shaft** with outer diameter **D** and inner diameter **d**:

$$
J = \dfrac{\pi}{32}\left(D^4 - d^4\right) \quad ; \quad Z_p = \dfrac{\pi}{16}\cdot\dfrac{D^4 - d^4}{D}
$$

**Design Equation (Strength Criterion):**

$$
\boxed{T_{max} = \tau_{allow} \cdot Z_p}
$$

---

## 2.4 Angle of Twist and Stiffness Criterion

The **total angle of twist** for a uniform shaft:

$$
\theta = \dfrac{TL}{GJ} \quad \text{(radians)}
$$

**Design Equation (Stiffness Criterion):**

$$
\theta_{max} = \dfrac{TL}{GJ} \leq \theta_{allow}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> KTU problems often give both criteria. The **larger** of the two diameters (strength-based or stiffness-based) is the **governing design diameter**. Always check **both** — a common exam pitfall is checking only strength.

---

## 2.5 Power Transmitted by a Shaft

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Power** is the rate of doing work. For a rotating shaft:

$$
\boxed{P = T\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60}}
$$

Where:
- **P** = Power in **Watts (W)** if T is in N·m and ω in rad/s.
- **N** = Speed in **rpm**.
- **T** = Torque in **N·m**.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Torsion Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains every formula needed for a KTU Module 4 torsion problem. Memorize the **boxed** items.

| # | Formula | Application | Units |
| :--: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | T/J = τ/r = Gθ/L | Master torsion equation (solid/hollow circular) | T [N·m], J [m⁴], τ [Pa], G [Pa] |
| 2 | τ = 16T/(πD³) | Max shear stress, solid circular shaft | Pa |
| 3 | τ = 16TD/[π(D⁴−d⁴)] | Max shear stress, hollow circular shaft | Pa |
| 4 | θ = TL/(GJ) | Angle of twist over length L | radians |
| 5 | θ° = (180/π)·TL/(GJ) | Same in degrees | degrees |
| 6 | J = πD⁴/32 | Polar moment, solid shaft | m⁴ |
| 7 | J = π(D⁴−d⁴)/32 | Polar moment, hollow shaft | m⁴ |
| 8 | Zₚ = πD³/16 | Polar section modulus, solid | m³ |
| 9 | Zₚ = π(D⁴−d⁴)/(16D) | Polar section modulus, hollow | m³ |
| 10 | P = 2πNT/60 | Power transmitted by shaft | Watts |
| 11 | T = 60P/(2πN) | Torque from power & speed | N·m |
| 12 | ω = 2πN/60 | Angular speed | rad/s |
| 13 | E = 2G(1+ν) | Relate Young's & shear modulus | Pa |
| 14 | T₁L₁/(G₁J₁) = T₂L₂/(G₂J₂) | Composite shaft — twist compatibility | — |
| 15 | σ₁ = +τ, σ₂ = −τ | Principal stresses (pure shear) | Pa |
| 16 | τ = Tr/J | Shear stress at any radius r | Pa |

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Application | Use of Torsion Theory |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Drive shafts (automobiles)** | Design for combined torque and bending; determine diameter for given power. |
| **Drill bits and boring bars** | Hollow sections save weight while maximizing J/D ratio. |
| **Turbo-generator rotor shafts** | High-speed rotating machinery; critical speed and torsional vibration. |
| **Propeller shafts (ships, aircraft)** | Long shafts carrying huge torques — angle of twist becomes critical. |
| **Helical springs (shock absorbers)** | Torsion of a wire coiled helically — **separate theory, NOT same as shaft torsion**. |
| **Torque wrenches & measuring tools** | Calibrated twist-to-torque relationship using torsion equation. |

> [!NOTE]
> The **significance of a hollow shaft** is often asked in KTU exams. For a **given weight** (same material, same length), a hollow shaft carries more torque than a solid shaft because J ∝ D⁴. The optimum ratio **d/D ≈ 0.7** gives a balance between weight and strength.

---

## 2.8 Composite (Built-up) Shafts

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **composite shaft** consists of two (or more) shafts of different materials or different diameters **rigidly connected** so that they twist through the **same angle** at the junction (compatibility).

### Equilibrium: Sum of torques = External torque

$$
T_1 + T_2 = T_{external}
$$

### Compatibility: Angle of twist of each part must be equal

$$
\theta_1 = \theta_2 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \dfrac{T_1 L_1}{G_1 J_1} = \dfrac{T_2 L_2}{G_2 J_2}
$$

Solving these two equations gives the **torque distribution** between the two materials, from which **individual stresses** can be computed.

---

## 2.9 Shafts in Series and Parallel

### Series (End-to-End)
Same torque throughout, but **twist angles add up**:

$$
\theta_{total} = \theta_1 + \theta_2 = \dfrac{TL_1}{G_1 J_1} + \dfrac{TL_2}{G_2 J_2}
$$

### Parallel (Concentric, like a hollow shaft over a solid shaft)
Same angle of twist, torques **add up**:

$$
T = T_1 + T_2 \quad ; \quad \theta = \dfrac{T_1 L}{G_1 J_1} = \dfrac{T_2 L}{G_2 J_2}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students often confuse "composite in series" with "composite in parallel." **Series = same torque.** **Parallel = same twist.** Memorize the **key words** — they're the most common 7-mark question variant in KTU ESE.

# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Full Derivation: Torsion Equation T/J = τ/r = Gθ/L

We have already outlined the derivation in Section 2.2. Here is the **complete, fully expanded, exam-ready derivation** in a single LaTeX block — suitable for writing directly into an answer script.

### 3.1.1 Setup and Kinematics

Consider a solid circular shaft of length **L** and diameter **D** (radius **R = D/2**). The shaft is fixed at end A and free at end B. A torque **T** is applied at B.

The cross-section at B rotates by an angle **θ** (in radians) relative to A.

> The fibres on the surface (initially parallel to the axis) become helices. A point P at radial distance **r** on the cross-section at B moves through an arc length:

$$
s = r \cdot \theta
$$

> The corresponding **shear strain** γ is the deformation divided by the original length:

$$
\gamma = \dfrac{s}{L} = \dfrac{r\theta}{L}
$$

### 3.1.2 Hooke's Law in Shear

By Hooke's law, the shear stress at radius r is:

$$
\tau = G\gamma \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \tau = \dfrac{Gr\theta}{L} \quad \text{...(i)}
$$

This shows **τ is maximum at r = R (outer surface)** and **zero at r = 0 (center)**.

### 3.1.3 Resultant Torque Calculation

The shear stress on an elemental ring of radius **r** and thickness **dr** acts over an area:

$$
dA = 2\pi r \, dr
$$

The elemental force on this ring is:

$$
dF = \tau \, dA
$$

The elemental moment (torque) about the central axis is:

$$
dT = r \, dF = r \cdot \tau \cdot 2\pi r \, dr = 2\pi r^2 \tau \, dr
$$

Substituting **τ = Grθ/L** from equation (i):

$$
dT = 2\pi r^2 \cdot \dfrac{Gr\theta}{L} \cdot dr = \dfrac{2\pi G\theta}{L} \cdot r^3 \, dr
$$

Integrating from **r = 0** to **r = R**:

$$
T = \int_0^R \dfrac{2\pi G\theta}{L} \cdot r^3 \, dr
$$

$$
T = \dfrac{2\pi G\theta}{L} \cdot \left[\dfrac{r^4}{4}\right]_0^R
$$

$$
T = \dfrac{2\pi G\theta}{L} \cdot \dfrac{R^4}{4} = \dfrac{\pi G\theta R^4}{2L}
$$

### 3.1.4 Define the Polar Moment of Inertia

The polar moment of inertia of a full circle is:

$$
J = \int_A r^2 \, dA = \int_0^R r^2 \cdot 2\pi r \, dr = 2\pi \int_0^R r^3 \, dr = 2\pi \cdot \dfrac{R^4}{4} = \dfrac{\pi R^4}{2}
$$

In terms of diameter **D = 2R**:

$$
J = \dfrac{\pi (D/2)^4}{2} = \dfrac{\pi D^4}{32}
$$

### 3.1.5 Final Form

Substituting back:

$$
T = \dfrac{GJ\theta}{L} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \dfrac{T}{J} = \dfrac{G\theta}{L}
$$

Combined with **τ = Grθ/L** from equation (i), we get:

$$
\boxed{\dfrac{T}{J} \;=\; \dfrac{\tau}{r} \;=\; \dfrac{G\theta}{L}}
$$

This is the **Coulomb-Duleau Torsion Equation**.

---

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation: Power Transmitted by a Shaft

> **Physical Setup:** A shaft rotates at **N revolutions per minute** under a torque **T (N·m)**.

### Step 1: Angular Velocity

One revolution = 2π radians. So angular velocity in rad/s is:

$$
\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60} \quad \text{[rad/s]}
$$

### Step 2: Work Done per Revolution

Work done = Force × Distance. In rotational form:

$$
W_{rev} = T \times 2\pi \quad \text{[Joules per revolution]}
$$

### Step 3: Power

Power = Work per unit time. There are N/60 revolutions per second:

$$
P = W_{rev} \times \dfrac{N}{60} = T \cdot 2\pi \cdot \dfrac{N}{60} = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60} \quad \text{[Watts]}
$$

Or simply:

$$
\boxed{P = T \cdot \omega}
$$

---

## 3.3 Composite Shaft — Complete Worked Analysis

> **Problem Setup:** A composite shaft consists of a copper sleeve (outer) bonded rigidly to a steel core (inner). Length **L = 1.5 m**. Steel core diameter **d₁ = 50 mm**. Copper outer diameter **D = 80 mm**. A torque **T = 5 kN·m** is applied. Find the torque carried by each material and the maximum shear stress in each.

**Given:** L = 1.5 m, d₁ = 0.05 m, D = 0.08 m, T = 5000 N·m, G_steel = 80 GPa, G_copper = 42 GPa.

**Step 1: Polar Moments of Inertia**

For the steel **solid core** (radius r₁ = d₁/2 = 0.025 m):

$$
J_s = \dfrac{\pi d_1^4}{32} = \dfrac{\pi (0.05)^4}{32} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot 6.25 \times 10^{-6}}{32} = 6.136 \times 10^{-7} \; \text{m}^4
$$

For the copper **hollow sleeve** (outer R = 0.04 m, inner r = 0.025 m):

$$
J_c = \dfrac{\pi}{32}\left(D^4 - d_1^4\right) = \dfrac{\pi}{32}\left((0.08)^4 - (0.05)^4\right)
$$

$$
J_c = \dfrac{\pi}{32}\left(4.096 \times 10^{-5} - 6.25 \times 10^{-6}\right) = \dfrac{\pi}{32} \cdot 3.471 \times 10^{-5}
$$

$$
J_c = 3.408 \times 10^{-6} \; \text{m}^4
$$

**Step 2: Equivalence via Twist Compatibility**

Since both materials are **bonded rigidly**, they share the **same angle of twist**:

$$
\theta_s = \theta_c \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \dfrac{T_s L}{G_s J_s} = \dfrac{T_c L}{G_c J_c}
$$

Solving for T_s in terms of T_c:

$$
T_s = T_c \cdot \dfrac{G_s J_s}{G_c J_c}
$$

Compute the ratio:

$$
\dfrac{G_s J_s}{G_c J_c} = \dfrac{80 \times 10^9 \cdot 6.136 \times 10^{-7}}{42 \times 10^9 \cdot 3.408 \times 10^{-6}} = \dfrac{49088}{143136} = 0.3429
$$

So:

$$
T_s = 0.3429 \, T_c
$$

**Step 3: Equilibrium**

$$
T_s + T_c = 5000 \; \text{N·m}
$$

$$
0.3429 \, T_c + T_c = 5000
$$

$$
1.3429 \, T_c = 5000 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad T_c = 3723.8 \; \text{N·m}
$$

$$
T_s = 5000 - 3723.8 = 1276.2 \; \text{N·m}
$$

**Step 4: Maximum Shear Stress in Each**

In **steel** (at r = r₁ = 0.025 m):

$$
\tau_s = \dfrac{T_s \cdot r_1}{J_s} = \dfrac{1276.2 \cdot 0.025}{6.136 \times 10^{-7}} = 51.99 \; \text{MPa}
$$

In **copper** (at r = R = 0.04 m):

$$
\tau_c = \dfrac{T_c \cdot R}{J_c} = \dfrac{3723.8 \cdot 0.04}{3.408 \times 10^{-6}} = 43.70 \; \text{MPa}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Steel carries only 25.5% of the torque but has a higher stress (51.99 MPa) because of its smaller cross-section. This is a typical KTU-style composite problem.

---

## 3.4 Helical Spring — Torsion of a Curved Wire (Theory + Derivation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A close-coiled helical spring is a **torsion element of a different kind**: the wire itself is twisted (not bent) when the spring is loaded axially. A load **W** along the spring axis produces a **torque T = WR** on the wire cross-section, where **R** is the mean coil radius.

### Assumptions for Helical Spring Theory

1. The wire is of **uniform cross-section** (circular).
2. The helix angle is very small (**close-coiled** spring).
3. The wire is subjected to **pure torsion** only (bending and axial shear are neglected).
4. Deflection is large compared to wire diameter (the spring acts like a flexible rod).

### Shear Stress in the Wire

The torque on the wire cross-section:

$$
T = W \cdot R
$$

By the torsion equation:

$$
\tau = \dfrac{16 \, T}{\pi d^3} = \dfrac{16 \, WR}{\pi d^3}
$$

This is the **maximum shear stress** at the outer fibre of the wire.

### Angle of Twist of the Wire

The total twist of the wire over one full coil (length of one turn = 2πR):

$$
\theta = \dfrac{T \cdot (2\pi R)}{G \cdot J_w} = \dfrac{W R \cdot 2\pi R}{G \cdot \pi d^4 / 32} = \dfrac{64 \, W R^2}{G d^4}
$$

The axial deflection **δ** of the spring is the arc traced by the free end:

$$
\delta = R \cdot \theta = \dfrac{64 \, W R^3}{G d^4}
$$

### Stiffness of Helical Spring

$$
\boxed{k = \dfrac{W}{\delta} = \dfrac{G d^4}{64 \, R^3}}
$$

### Number of Active Coils (n)

If a spring has **N** total coils, of which **Nᵢ** are inactive (used for end connections), the **active coils** are **n = N − Nᵢ**. The stiffness becomes:

$$
k = \dfrac{G d^4}{64 \, R^3 \, n}
$$

### Springs in Series and Parallel

**Series (end-to-end):** Same load on each spring, deflections add:

$$
\delta_{total} = \delta_1 + \delta_2 \quad ; \quad k_{eq} = \dfrac{k_1 k_2}{k_1 + k_2}
$$

**Parallel (side-by-side):** Same deflection, loads add:

$$
W_{total} = W_1 + W_2 \quad ; \quad k_{eq} = k_1 + k_2
$$

---

## 3.5 Wahl's Correction Factor (Refined Spring Design)

> [!NOTE]
> In reality, helical springs experience **direct shear** in addition to torsional shear. **Wahl's factor** **K_w** corrects for this:

$$
K_w = \dfrac{4C - 1}{4C - 4} + \dfrac{0.615}{C} \quad ; \quad C = \dfrac{D}{d} = \dfrac{2R}{d}
$$

The corrected (more accurate) maximum shear stress:

$$
\tau_{max} = K_w \cdot \dfrac{8 W D}{\pi d^3} = K_w \cdot \dfrac{16 W R}{\pi d^3}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> KTU 2024 modules often **omit Wahl's factor** in the basic theory section. Check the specific question wording — if it says "**neglecting the effect of direct shear**" or "**assume pure torsion**," do not include K_w. If it just says "find the maximum shear stress," include it.

---

## 3.6 Detailed Numerical: Design of a Solid Shaft

> **Problem:** A solid steel shaft transmits **P = 60 kW** at **N = 300 rpm**. Maximum allowable shear stress **τ_allow = 70 MPa**. Find the required shaft diameter **D**.

**Step 1: Torque from power**

$$
T = \dfrac{60 P}{2\pi N} = \dfrac{60 \cdot 60 \times 10^3}{2\pi \cdot 300} = \dfrac{3.6 \times 10^6}{1884.96} = 1910 \; \text{N·m}
$$

**Step 2: Required diameter from strength**

$$
\tau_{allow} = \dfrac{16 T}{\pi D^3} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad D^3 = \dfrac{16 T}{\pi \tau_{allow}}
$$

$$
D^3 = \dfrac{16 \cdot 1910}{\pi \cdot 70 \times 10^6} = \dfrac{30560}{2.199 \times 10^8} = 1.390 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{m}^3
$$

$$
D = (1.390 \times 10^{-4})^{1/3} = 0.0518 \; \text{m} = 51.8 \; \text{mm}
$$

**Step 3: Check by stiffness criterion** (typical KTU addition)

If allowable twist = **0.5° per metre** and G = 80 GPa:

$$
\dfrac{\theta}{L} = \dfrac{0.5 \cdot \pi}{180 \cdot 1} = 8.727 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{rad/m}
$$

$$
D^4 = \dfrac{32 T}{\pi G (\theta/L)} = \dfrac{32 \cdot 1910}{\pi \cdot 80 \times 10^9 \cdot 8.727 \times 10^{-3}} = 2.785 \times 10^{-5}
$$

$$
D = (2.785 \times 10^{-5})^{1/4} = 0.0729 \; \text{m} = 72.9 \; \text{mm}
$$

**Step 4: Governing diameter**

$$
D_{design} = \max(51.8, 72.9) = 72.9 \; \text{mm} \approx 73 \; \text{mm}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiners always check whether students report the governing diameter.** Selecting 51.8 mm (the strength-based diameter) without checking stiffness will be marked wrong.

# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Stress Distribution in a Circular Shaft — Mermaid Block Diagram

The following block diagram maps the **causal chain** of torsion from applied torque to induced stresses, replicating the typical block-schematic style used in KTU answer scripts.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[External Torque T applied at free end] --> A1{Is shaft circular and isotropic?}
    A1 -- Yes --> A2[Shear stress τ develops on cross-section]
    A1 -- No --> A3[Cross-section warps, Saint-Venant theory required]
    A2 --> A4[τ varies LINEARLY with radius r]
    A4 --> A5[τ = 0 at the CENTER of shaft]
    A4 --> A6[τ = τ_max at OUTER SURFACE r = R]
    A6 --> A7[τ_max = 16T over piD cubed for solid shaft]
    A6 --> A8[τ_max = 16TD over piD to the 4 minus d to the 4 for hollow shaft]
    A2 --> A9[Angle of twist theta over length L]
    A9 --> A10[theta = TL over GJ radians]
    A10 --> A11[Stiffness criterion: theta less than theta_allow]
    A7 --> A12[Strength criterion: tau_max less than tau_allow]
    A8 --> A12
    A11 --> A13{Governing design diameter}
    A12 --> A13
    A13 -- Strength governs --> A14[D from 16T over pi tau_allow]
    A13 -- Stiffness governs --> A15[D from 32T over pi G theta_allow]
    A14 --> A16[Final design diameter selected]
    A15 --> A16
```

---

## 4.2 Failure Plane Block Diagram — Torsion Fracture Mechanics

This block diagram explains the **"corkscrew fracture"** phenomenon often asked in KTU viva and 3-mark questions.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1[Shaft under pure torsion] --> B2[Pure shear state on element]
    B2 --> B3[Principal stresses at 45 degrees]
    B3 --> B31[σ1 = +τ maximum tension]
    B3 --> B32[σ2 = -τ maximum compression]
    B31 --> B4[Material fails along plane of max tension]
    B4 --> B5[Fracture plane is HELICAL at 45 degrees to axis]
    B32 --> B6[Material resists along plane of max compression]
    B6 --> B7[Corkscrew fracture observed in ductile shafts]
```

---

## 4.3 Composite Shaft — Torque Distribution Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C0[Composite Shaft: Two materials A and B bonded] --> C1[Equilibrium Condition]
    C1 --> C2[Sum of torques T_A plus T_B equals T_external]
    C0 --> C3[Compatibility Condition]
    C3 --> C4[Angle of twist is equal: theta_A equals theta_B]
    C4 --> C5[T_A L over G_A J_A equals T_B L over G_B J_B]
    C2 --> C6[Solve two equations simultaneously]
    C5 --> C6
    C6 --> C7[T_A and T_B values obtained]
    C7 --> C8[Compute tau_max in material A]
    C7 --> C9[Compute tau_max in material B]
    C8 --> C10[Check tau_A less than tau_allow_A]
    C9 --> C11[Check tau_B less than tau_allow_B]
```

---

## 4.4 Helical Spring — Free Body Architecture Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    D1[Axial load W applied at spring end] --> D2[Torque on wire cross-section T equals WR]
    D2 --> D3[Wire undergoes pure TORSION]
    D3 --> D4[Shear stress tau equals 16T over pi d cubed]
    D3 --> D5[Twist angle theta per coil equals 64WR cubed over Gd to the 4]
    D4 --> D6[Maximum shear stress tau_max]
    D5 --> D7[Axial deflection delta equals R theta]
    D6 --> D8[Design check 1: tau_max less than tau_allow]
    D7 --> D9[Design check 2: delta less than delta_allow]
    D8 --> D10[Stiffness k equals Gd to the 4 over 64 R cubed n]
    D9 --> D10
    D10 --> D11[Final spring parameters: d, D, n, k confirmed]
```

---

## 4.5 Shear Stress Distribution Visual Block Diagram (Radar View)

This block diagram represents the **shear stress variation along the radius** of a circular shaft — a "conceptual cross-section" through the shaft's material points.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    E1[Center of shaft at r equals 0] --> E2[Shear stress tau equals 0]
    E3[Mid-radius at r equals R over 2] --> E4[Shear stress tau is HALF of tau_max]
    E5[Outer surface at r equals R] --> E6[Shear stress tau equals tau_max]
    E2 --> E7[LINEAR variation of tau with r]
    E4 --> E7
    E6 --> E7
    E7 --> E8[Tau equals Tr over J at any radius r]
```

---

## 4.6 Series vs Parallel Spring — Architecture Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    F0[Helical springs arrangement problem] --> F1{Type of connection?}
    F1 -- End to end --> F2[SERIES arrangement]
    F1 -- Side by side --> F3[PARALLEL arrangement]
    F2 --> F4[Same load W through each spring]
    F2 --> F5[Deflections add: delta_total equals delta_1 plus delta_2]
    F2 --> F6[Equivalent stiffness k_eq equals k1k2 over k1 plus k2]
    F3 --> F7[Same deflection delta for each spring]
    F3 --> F8[Loads add: W_total equals W1 plus W2]
    F3 --> F9[Equivalent stiffness k_eq equals k1 plus k2]
```

# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**State the assumptions made in the derivation of the torsion equation for a circular shaft.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — bullet-pointed, 3 valid assumptions):**

1. The material of the shaft is **homogeneous**, **isotropic**, and obeys **Hooke's Law** (linearly elastic) in shear.
2. Cross-sections that are plane and circular before twisting **remain plane and circular** after twisting, with no warping.
3. The radii of the cross-sections remain **straight and undistorted**; they only rotate as rigid bodies about the longitudinal axis.
4. The shaft is subjected to **equal and opposite torques** at the ends, producing a uniform state of pure torsion.
5. The **angle of twist is small** (in radians), so the small-angle approximation sin θ ≈ θ is valid.

*(Any 3 assumptions × 1 mark each = 3 marks)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**

**What is complementary shear stress? Show that pure shear produces principal stresses equal in magnitude to the shear stress at 45° to the original plane.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Complementary Shear (1 mark):** When a body is subjected to shear stress **τ** on one set of planes, an **equal and opposite shear stress τ** must act on a set of planes **perpendicular** to the first, to satisfy moment equilibrium of an elemental cube. This is the **complementary shear stress**.

**Principal Stresses (2 marks):** Consider an element under pure shear. The normal and shear stresses on a plane inclined at angle θ to the original are:

$$
\sigma_\theta = \tau \sin 2\theta \quad ; \quad \tau_\theta = \tau \cos 2\theta
$$

For **principal planes** (where τ_θ = 0): cos 2θ = 0 → **θ = 45°**.

At this angle:

$$
\sigma_{1,2} = \pm \tau \sin(90^\circ) = \pm \tau
$$

Hence **σ₁ = +τ (tension)** and **σ₂ = −τ (compression)** at 45° to the shaft axis. This is why ductile shafts under torsion fail along a **45° helical fracture plane**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Choose Either A or B)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Composite Shaft Problem**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Modified]**

> A composite shaft consists of a **steel core of diameter 60 mm** surrounded by a **brass tube of outer diameter 100 mm**. The composite shaft is **2.0 m long** and is rigidly fixed at both ends. A torque of **8 kN·m** is applied at an intermediate section located **0.8 m** from one end. Determine:
>
> **(a)** The reactions at the two fixed ends, and the maximum shear stress in steel and brass. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** The angle of twist of the intermediate section relative to the fixed ends. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Given:** G_steel = 80 GPa, G_brass = 40 GPa.

---

### **Solution to Question A**

#### Part (a): Reactions and Stresses [7 marks]

**Step 1: Compute Polar Moments of Inertia**

Steel (solid core, d = 0.06 m):

$$
J_s = \dfrac{\pi (0.06)^4}{32} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot 1.296 \times 10^{-5}}{32} = 1.272 \times 10^{-6} \; \text{m}^4
$$

Brass (hollow tube, D = 0.10 m, d = 0.06 m):

$$
J_b = \dfrac{\pi}{32}\left((0.10)^4 - (0.06)^4\right) = \dfrac{\pi}{32}(1.0 \times 10^{-4} - 1.296 \times 10^{-5}) = 8.547 \times 10^{-6} \; \text{m}^4
$$

**Step 2: Sign Convention and Free Body**

Let **T_A** = reaction at left end, **T_B** = reaction at right end. Applied torque = **8 kN·m** at section 0.8 m from left.

Equilibrium: T_A + T_B = 8000 N·m …(i)

**Step 3: Compatibility — Total twist = 0 (fixed at both ends)**

Twist of section between A and load (length L₁ = 0.8 m, torque = T_A):

$$
\theta_1 = \dfrac{T_A \cdot L_1}{G_s J_s + G_b J_b} = \dfrac{T_A \cdot 0.8}{G_{eq} J_{eq}}
$$

Since steel and brass are **rigidly bonded** (parallel composite), the equivalent rigidity:

$$
G_{eq} J_{eq} = G_s J_s + G_b J_b = 80 \times 10^9 \cdot 1.272 \times 10^{-6} + 40 \times 10^9 \cdot 8.547 \times 10^{-6}
$$

$$
= 1.018 \times 10^5 + 3.419 \times 10^5 = 4.437 \times 10^5 \; \text{N·m}^2
$$

Similarly for the right portion (L₂ = 1.2 m, torque = T_B):

$$
\theta_2 = \dfrac{T_B \cdot 1.2}{4.437 \times 10^5}
$$

Compatibility: θ₁ = θ₂ (both zero at the same fixed reference when measured from the load point):

$$
\dfrac{T_A \cdot 0.8}{4.437 \times 10^5} = \dfrac{T_B \cdot 1.2}{4.437 \times 10^5}
$$

$$
0.8 T_A = 1.2 T_B \quad \Longrightarrow \quad T_A = 1.5 \, T_B
$$

**Step 4: Solve for reactions**

From (i): 1.5 T_B + T_B = 8000 → T_B = **3200 N·m**, T_A = **4800 N·m**

**Step 5: Torque distribution within each material (parallel composite)**

In the **left portion** (T_A = 4800 N·m):

$$
T_s^{(L)} = T_A \cdot \dfrac{G_s J_s}{G_s J_s + G_b J_b} = 4800 \cdot \dfrac{1.018 \times 10^5}{4.437 \times 10^5} = 1101 \; \text{N·m}
$$

$$
T_b^{(L)} = T_A \cdot \dfrac{G_b J_b}{G_s J_s + G_b J_b} = 4800 \cdot \dfrac{3.419 \times 10^5}{4.437 \times 10^5} = 3699 \; \text{N·m}
$$

In the **right portion** (T_B = 3200 N·m):

$$
T_s^{(R)} = 3200 \cdot \dfrac{1.018 \times 10^5}{4.437 \times 10^5} = 734 \; \text{N·m}
$$

$$
T_b^{(R)} = 3200 \cdot \dfrac{3.419 \times 10^5}{4.437 \times 10^5} = 2466 \; \text{N·m}
$$

**Step 6: Maximum Shear Stress**

For **steel** (max in left portion, r = 0.03 m):

$$
\tau_{s,max} = \dfrac{1101 \cdot 0.03}{1.272 \times 10^{-6}} = 25.97 \; \text{MPa}
$$

For **brass** (max in left portion, r = 0.05 m):

$$
\tau_{b,max} = \dfrac{3699 \cdot 0.05}{8.547 \times 10^{-6}} = 21.64 \; \text{MPa}
$$

#### Part (b): Angle of Twist of Intermediate Section [7 marks]

The angle of twist of the intermediate section relative to fixed end A is θ₁:

$$
\theta_1 = \dfrac{T_A \cdot L_1}{G_s J_s + G_b J_b} = \dfrac{4800 \cdot 0.8}{4.437 \times 10^5}
$$

$$
\theta_1 = \dfrac{3840}{4.437 \times 10^5} = 8.656 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{rad}
$$

$$
\theta_1 = 8.656 \times 10^{-3} \times \dfrac{180}{\pi} = 0.496^\circ
$$

**Verification via right side:**

$$
\theta_2 = \dfrac{3200 \cdot 1.2}{4.437 \times 10^5} = 8.656 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{rad} \quad \checkmark
$$

Both equal — confirms the compatibility condition.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiners' Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Forgetting that the **entire composite is "parallel"** (concentric, sharing twist) — not series. The torsional rigidity **adds**, not the polar moment.
> 2. Failing to **distribute torque between the two materials** by the ratio G·J. Students sometimes apply the full torque to each material independently — wrong.
> 3. Skipping the **verification of compatibility** (twist in left = twist in right) — losing 2 marks.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Helical Spring Design**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Modified]**

> A close-coiled helical spring is made of **circular wire of diameter 6 mm**, with a **mean coil diameter of 75 mm**. The spring has **10 active coils**. The shear modulus of the wire material is **G = 80 GPa**.
>
> **(a)** Determine the **stiffness of the spring** and the **maximum shear stress** when a load of **300 N** is applied. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** If a similar spring with **20 active coils** is connected to the original spring in **series**, find the **equivalent stiffness** and the **deflection** of the combination under a load of **400 N**. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Solution to Question B**

#### Part (a): Stiffness and Shear Stress [7 marks]

**Given:** d = 6 mm = 0.006 m, D = 75 mm = 0.075 m, n = 10, G = 80 × 10⁹ Pa, W = 300 N.

**Stiffness (using k = Gd⁴/(64R³n), where R = D/2 = 0.0375 m):**

$$
k = \dfrac{80 \times 10^9 \cdot (0.006)^4}{64 \cdot (0.0375)^3 \cdot 10}
$$

Numerator: $80 \times 10^9 \cdot 1.296 \times 10^{-9} = 103.68$

Denominator: $64 \cdot 5.273 \times 10^{-5} \cdot 10 = 0.03375$

$$
k = \dfrac{103.68}{0.03375} = 3071.4 \; \text{N/m} = 3.071 \; \text{N/mm}
$$

**Deflection under 300 N:**

$$
\delta_1 = \dfrac{W}{k} = \dfrac{300}{3071.4} = 0.0977 \; \text{m} = 97.7 \; \text{mm}
$$

**Maximum Shear Stress:**

Torque on wire: T = W·R = 300 × 0.0375 = 11.25 N·m

$$
\tau_{max} = \dfrac{16 T}{\pi d^3} = \dfrac{16 \cdot 11.25}{\pi \cdot (0.006)^3} = \dfrac{180}{\pi \cdot 2.16 \times 10^{-7}} = 2.653 \times 10^8 \; \text{Pa} \approx 265.3 \; \text{MPa}
$$

#### Part (b): Series Combination [7 marks]

A second spring of **same dimensions but 20 coils** has stiffness:

$$
k_2 = k_1 \cdot \dfrac{n_1}{n_2} = 3071.4 \cdot \dfrac{10}{20} = 1535.7 \; \text{N/m}
$$

(Since k ∝ 1/n, doubling coils halves stiffness.)

**Series Equivalent Stiffness:**

$$
k_{eq} = \dfrac{k_1 k_2}{k_1 + k_2} = \dfrac{3071.4 \cdot 1535.7}{3071.4 + 1535.7} = \dfrac{4.716 \times 10^6}{4607.1} = 1023.7 \; \text{N/m}
$$

**Deflection under 400 N:**

$$
\delta_{total} = \dfrac{W}{k_{eq}} = \dfrac{400}{1023.7} = 0.3907 \; \text{m} = 390.7 \; \text{mm}
$$

**Individual deflections (same load, series):**

$$
\delta_1' = \dfrac{400}{3071.4} = 0.1303 \; \text{m} \quad ; \quad \delta_2' = \dfrac{400}{1535.7} = 0.2605 \; \text{m}
$$

Check: 0.1303 + 0.2605 = 0.3908 m ✓

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiners' Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Units mismatch**: Computing k in N/m and τ in Pa — students forget to convert mm → m. This single error loses 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Confusing n (active) and N (total) coils** — if the problem mentions 2 inactive end coils, subtract them: n = N − 2.
> 3. **Series vs Parallel**: Series means **same load, deflections add**. Students sometimes use **k_eq = k₁ + k₂** (which is the parallel formula) — wrong here.
> 4. **Always convert** δ from meters to mm at the end (or vice versa) consistently. Mixing units is a classic valuation red flag.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Torsion Theory (Module 4)**

- ✅ **Master Equation**: $\dfrac{T}{J} = \dfrac{\tau}{r} = \dfrac{G\theta}{L}$ — applies **only to circular** shafts.
- ✅ **Solid shaft polar moment**: $J = \dfrac{\pi D^4}{32}$; **Hollow shaft**: $J = \dfrac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{32}$.
- ✅ **Maximum shear stress** occurs at the **outer surface** (r = R); **zero** at the center.
- ✅ **Two design criteria** must always be checked: (1) **Strength**: τ_max ≤ τ_allow; (2) **Stiffness**: θ/L ≤ (θ/L)_allow. The **larger** diameter governs.
- ✅ **Power formula**: $P = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60}$ in Watts. **Torque from power**: $T = \dfrac{60 P}{2\pi N}$ in N·m.
- ✅ **Composite shaft (parallel)**: $\theta_1 = \theta_2$ (compatibility); $T_1 + T_2 = T_{ext}$ (equilibrium).
- ✅ **Composite shaft (series)**: $T_1 = T_2$ (same torque); $\theta_{total} = \theta_1 + \theta_2$ (twists add).
- ✅ **Helical spring** is **NOT** a torsion of a straight shaft — the wire is curved, but the **pure-torsion** theory still applies to the wire cross-section.
- ✅ **Spring stiffness**: $k = \dfrac{Gd^4}{64 R^3 n}$ — proportional to $1/n$ (more coils → softer spring).
- ✅ **Spring stress**: $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{16 WR}{\pi d^3}$ for pure torsion (without Wahl's correction).
- ✅ **Series springs**: $k_{eq} = \dfrac{k_1 k_2}{k_1 + k_2}$ — softer than either spring.
- ✅ **Parallel springs**: $k_{eq} = k_1 + k_2$ — stiffer than either spring.
- ✅ **Complementary shear** is a consequence of **moment equilibrium** of an elemental cube; principal stresses are at **45°** to the shaft axis.
- ✅ **Failure mode** of ductile shafts: a **helical fracture** at 45° to the axis (corkscrew pattern).
- ✅ **Units**: Always work in **N, m, Pa** (or **N, mm, MPa**) consistently — do **not** mix.
