<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Torsion of Circular Shafts — Formal Definition**
> Torsion is the twisting of a structural member (shaft) about its longitudinal axis when subjected to equal and opposite torques (twisting moments) applied at its ends. When the cross-section of the shaft is a **solid circle** or a **hollow circle (annulus)**, and the material is homogeneous, isotropic, and behaves in a linear-elastic manner, the analysis is governed by the classical **theory of pure torsion** developed from the symmetry of the circular cross-section.

The fundamental relationship that governs the elastic torsion of a circular shaft is the **Torsion Equation (sometimes called the Elastic Torsion Formula)**:

$$\frac{T}{J} = \frac{\tau}{r} = \frac{G\theta}{L}$$

where,
- **T** = Applied torque (N·m)
- **J** = Polar moment of inertia of the cross-section about the shaft axis (m⁴)
- **τ** = Shear stress at radius *r* (N/m² or Pa)
- **r** = Radial distance from the shaft axis (m)
- **G** = Modulus of rigidity (Shear modulus) of the material (N/m²)
- **θ** = Angle of twist (radians)
- **L** = Length of the shaft (m)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Wet Towel Twist**
> Imagine wringing water out of a wet towel by twisting both ends in opposite directions. The fibers running along the length of the towel remain the same length, but every circular cross-section **rotates relative to the next one** by a small angle. The outermost fibers (furthest from the centerline) experience the *maximum stretch/squeeze*, while the central fibers barely deform. This is *exactly* what happens inside a circular shaft under torque — radial lines stay radial, cross-sections remain plane and undistorted, and the shear stress grows linearly from **zero at the center** to a **maximum at the outer surface**.

## 1.3 Underlying Assumptions of the Torsion Theory

The classical torsion formula is valid **only** when the following assumptions are satisfied:

- The shaft material is **homogeneous**, **isotropic**, and obeys **Hooke's Law** in shear.
- The cross-section of the shaft is **circular** (solid or hollow) and **remains circular** during twisting (circular sections do **not warp** under pure torque).
- Cross-sections that are initially plane and perpendicular to the axis **remain plane and perpendicular to the axis** after twisting (no warping, no out-of-plane distortion).
- The shaft is loaded by **pure torque only** (no bending, no axial load).
- The twist per unit length is **uniform** along the length of the shaft.
- Radial lines in the cross-section **remain radial** and straight after twisting (only their angular position changes).

> [!WARNING]
> **Why these assumptions matter:** If any assumption is violated (e.g., for non-circular sections like rectangular, I-section, or thin-walled open sections), the standard torsion equation **T/J = τ/r = Gθ/L** is **no longer valid**, and a more advanced analysis (Saint-Venant's theory, warping functions, or thin-walled approximation) must be used.

## 1.4 Solid vs. Hollow Circular Shafts — Geometric Distinction

| Feature | Solid Shaft | Hollow Shaft |
|---|---|---|
| Cross-section | Full circular disc | Annulus (ring-shaped) |
| Outer diameter | D | D |
| Inner diameter | 0 | d |
| Material utilization | Sub-optimal (core carries very low stress) | Excellent (material close to high-stress outer fibres is fully used) |
| Weight | Heavier for same strength | Lighter (up to ~40% saving) |
| Cost | Higher material cost | Lower material cost |
| Common applications | Low-cost machinery, low-speed shafts | Automotive drive shafts, aircraft propeller shafts, marine propeller shafts |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Geometric Properties of Circular Cross-Sections

The **Polar Moment of Inertia (J)** measures the cross-section's resistance to twisting. For a circle, it is computed as the second moment of area about the centroidal axis perpendicular to the cross-section.

### 2.1.1 Solid Circular Shaft (Diameter D)

$$J_s = \int_A r^2 \, dA = \int_0^{2\pi}\int_0^{D/2} r^2 \cdot r \, dr \, d\phi = \frac{\pi D^4}{32}$$

### 2.1.2 Hollow Circular Shaft (Outer D D, Inner d)

$$J_h = \int_A r^2 \, dA = \frac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{32}$$

Define the **diameter ratio**:

$$\alpha = \frac{d}{D}$$

So the hollow shaft polar moment can be written compactly as:

$$J_h = \frac{\pi D^4}{32}\,(1 - \alpha^4)$$

## 2.2 Shear Stress Distribution

From the torsion equation, the shear stress at any radius *r* is:

$$\tau = \frac{T \cdot r}{J}$$

This shows that **shear stress varies linearly** with radius. Hence:

- **Maximum shear stress** occurs at the **outer surface** ($r = D/2$).
- **Minimum shear stress** is **zero at the centroid** ($r = 0$).

For a solid shaft:

$$\tau_{\max} = \frac{T \cdot (D/2)}{\pi D^4 / 32} = \frac{16T}{\pi D^3}$$

For a hollow shaft:

$$\tau_{\max} = \frac{T \cdot (D/2)}{\pi (D^4 - d^4)/32} = \frac{16T}{\pi D^3\,(1 - \alpha^4)}$$

The **minimum shear stress** in a hollow shaft (at $r = d/2$) is:

$$\tau_{\min} = \frac{16T}{\pi D^3\,(1/\alpha^3 - 1/D^3)} = \frac{16T \cdot \alpha^3}{\pi D^3\,(1 - \alpha^4)}$$

## 2.3 Angle of Twist

The relative rotation between the two ends of a uniform shaft of length *L* is:

$$\theta = \frac{T \cdot L}{G \cdot J} \quad \text{(radians)}$$

This is also called the **total angle of twist** of the shaft. The **rate of twist** (twist per unit length) is:

$$\frac{\theta}{L} = \frac{T}{G \cdot J} \quad \text{(radians per meter)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The angle of twist depends only on **T, L, G, and J** — it is **independent of the radius** *r*. In other words, every fibre at the same cross-section rotates by the *same angle*, but the *strain* (and hence the stress) depends on its radial position.

## 2.4 Power Transmission by a Shaft

A shaft rotating at **N revolutions per minute (rpm)** and transmitting torque **T** (N·m) delivers a power of:

$$P = 2\pi N T \quad \text{(watts, with N in rev/sec)}$$

Or, in the more common engineering form (N in rpm):

$$\boxed{P = \frac{2\pi N T}{60} = \frac{\pi N T}{30} \quad \text{(watts)}}$$

Solving for T:

$$T = \frac{60 P}{2\pi N} = \frac{30 P}{\pi N} \quad \text{(N·m, P in W, N in rpm)}$$

If power is given in **kilowatts (kW)**:

$$T = \frac{60 \times 1000 \, P_{\text{kW}}}{2\pi N} = \frac{9549 \, P_{\text{kW}}}{N} \quad \text{(N·m)}$$

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **every equation** you must memorize for KTU 2024 ESE on this topic. Cover the right column and recite the formulas left-to-right.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Torsion Equation (Master) | $T / J \;=\; \tau / r \;=\; G\theta / L$ | T in N·m, J in m⁴, τ in Pa, r in m, G in Pa, θ in rad, L in m |
| 2 | Polar moment — Solid shaft | $J_s \;=\; \pi D^4 / 32$ | D = outer diameter (m) |
| 3 | Polar moment — Hollow shaft | $J_h \;=\; \pi (D^4 - d^4) / 32$ | D = outer, d = inner (m) |
| 4 | Polar moment — Hollow (ratio form) | $J_h \;=\; (\pi D^4 / 32)(1 - \alpha^4)$ | $\alpha = d / D$ |
| 5 | Max shear stress — Solid | $\tau_{\max,s} \;=\; 16T / (\pi D^3)$ | Occurs at r = D/2 |
| 6 | Max shear stress — Hollow | $\tau_{\max,h} \;=\; 16T / [\pi D^3 (1 - \alpha^4)]$ | Occurs at r = D/2 |
| 7 | Min shear stress — Hollow | $\tau_{\min,h} \;=\; 16T \alpha^3 / [\pi D^3 (1 - \alpha^4)]$ | Occurs at r = d/2 |
| 8 | Angle of twist | $\theta \;=\; T L / (G J)$ | radians |
| 9 | Rate of twist | $\theta / L \;=\; T / (G J)$ | rad/m |
| 10 | Power transmitted (N in rpm) | $P \;=\; 2\pi N T / 60$ | P in W, T in N·m |
| 11 | Torque from power (P in kW) | $T \;=\; 9549 \, P / N$ | N in rpm, T in N·m |
| 12 | Torque from power (P in W) | $T \;=\; 60P / (2\pi N)$ | N in rpm, T in N·m |
| 13 | Design — Solid (strength) | $D^3 \;\geq\; 16T / (\pi \tau_{\text{allow}})$ | Solve for D |
| 14 | Design — Hollow (strength) | $D^3(1-\alpha^4) \;\geq\; 16T / (\pi \tau_{\text{allow}})$ | Choose α, solve for D |
| 15 | Design — Stiffness criterion | $\theta / L \;\leq\; (\theta/L)_{\text{allow}} \Rightarrow J \;\geq\; T / [G(\theta/L)_{\text{allow}}]$ | Often θ/L allow ≈ 0.25°/m for line shafts |

## 2.6 Engineering Utility & Real-World Applications

- **Automotive drive shafts:** Hollow circular cross-sections save weight (critical for fuel efficiency) while delivering high torque.
- **Aircraft propeller shafts:** Hollow shafts carry huge torques at high rpm with minimum weight.
- **Machine tool spindles:** Solid shafts (where the bore is not needed) provide stiffness and low cost.
- **Turbo-generator rotors:** Solid shafts integrated with the rotor forging.
- **Marine propeller shafts:** Hollow steel shafts carry the propeller torque to the hull gearbox.
- **Hand-cranked tools (chuck keys, screwdrivers):** Solid square or hex shafts — but for cylindrical tools like drill bits, the circular torsion theory applies directly.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why engineers prefer hollow shafts:** In a solid shaft, the material near the center experiences **near-zero stress** but adds mass, cost, and inertia. A hollow shaft with the **same outer diameter** but with the **core removed** has *almost the same* torsional rigidity because the polar moment depends on the **fourth power of diameter** — so the inner fibres contribute little to J anyway. A typical design choice of $\alpha = 0.5$ removes ~94% of the area but only ~6% of the stiffness.

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Polar Moment of Inertia for a Solid Circular Shaft

**Starting point:** A solid circular disc of diameter **D** in the *x-y* plane. We need the second moment of area about the *z*-axis (the centroidal axis perpendicular to the disc).

**Step 1:** Choose a differential area element. In polar coordinates:
$$dA = r \, dr \, d\phi$$

where $r$ is the radial distance from the center and $\phi$ is the angular coordinate.

**Step 2:** Write the integral for the polar moment of inertia:
$$J_s = \int_A r^2 \, dA$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $dA$ and the limits. The radius ranges from $0$ to $D/2$, and the angle from $0$ to $2\pi$:
$$J_s = \int_0^{2\pi}\int_0^{D/2} r^2 \, (r \, dr \, d\phi)$$

**Step 4:** Separate the integrals:
$$J_s = \left(\int_0^{2\pi} d\phi\right) \cdot \left(\int_0^{D/2} r^3 \, dr\right)$$

**Step 5:** Evaluate the angular integral:
$$\int_0^{2\pi} d\phi = 2\pi$$

**Step 6:** Evaluate the radial integral:
$$\int_0^{D/2} r^3 \, dr = \left[\frac{r^4}{4}\right]_0^{D/2} = \frac{1}{4}\left(\frac{D}{2}\right)^4 = \frac{1}{4} \cdot \frac{D^4}{16} = \frac{D^4}{64}$$

**Step 7:** Multiply the two results:
$$J_s = 2\pi \cdot \frac{D^4}{64} = \frac{\pi D^4}{32}$$

> **Result:** $\boxed{J_s = \dfrac{\pi D^4}{32}}$ ✓

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Polar Moment of Inertia for a Hollow Circular Shaft

**Step 1:** The hollow shaft is an annulus with outer radius $R = D/2$ and inner radius $r_i = d/2$.

**Step 2:** Set up the integral with limits from $r_i$ to $R$:
$$J_h = \int_0^{2\pi}\int_{d/2}^{D/2} r^2 \cdot r \, dr \, d\phi$$

**Step 3:** Separate:
$$J_h = \left(\int_0^{2\pi} d\phi\right) \cdot \left(\int_{d/2}^{D/2} r^3 \, dr\right) = 2\pi \cdot \left[\frac{r^4}{4}\right]_{d/2}^{D/2}$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate:
$$J_h = 2\pi \cdot \frac{1}{4}\left[\left(\frac{D}{2}\right)^4 - \left(\frac{d}{2}\right)^4\right] = \frac{\pi}{2}\left[\frac{D^4 - d^4}{16}\right] = \frac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{32}$$

> **Result:** $\boxed{J_h = \dfrac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{32} = \dfrac{\pi D^4}{32}\,(1 - \alpha^4)}$ where $\alpha = d/D$ ✓

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — The Torsion Equation T/J = τ/r = Gθ/L

**Step 1 — Geometry of deformation:** Consider a shaft of length *L* twisted by an angle *θ* at the free end. A fibre on the outer surface, originally at distance *r* from the axis, moves tangentially through an arc length:

$$\Delta = r \cdot \theta$$

**Step 2 — Shear strain:** The shear strain $\gamma$ is the ratio of the tangential displacement to the axial length:

$$\gamma = \frac{\Delta}{L} = \frac{r \cdot \theta}{L}$$

**Step 3 — Hooke's Law in shear:** For a linear-elastic material:

$$\tau = G \cdot \gamma = G \cdot \frac{r \cdot \theta}{L}$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange to obtain the middle ratio:**

$$\frac{\tau}{r} = \frac{G\theta}{L}$$

**Step 5 — Equivalence with the torque ratio:** Now consider the torque equilibrium of the cross-section. The shear stress $\tau$ at radius *r* acts on an area $dA$, producing a differential moment $dT = \tau \cdot r \cdot dA$ about the axis.

$$T = \int_A \tau \cdot r \, dA$$

Substitute $\tau = (G\theta/L) \cdot r$ from Step 3:

$$T = \int_A \left(\frac{G\theta}{L}\right) r \cdot r \, dA = \frac{G\theta}{L} \int_A r^2 \, dA$$

But $\int_A r^2 \, dA = J$ by definition, so:

$$T = \frac{G\theta}{L} \cdot J$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{T}{J} = \frac{G\theta}{L}$$

**Step 6 — Combine Step 4 and Step 5:**

> **Result:** $\boxed{\dfrac{T}{J} \;=\; \dfrac{\tau}{r} \;=\; \dfrac{G\theta}{L}}$ ✓

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Maximum Shear Stress in a Solid Shaft

From $\tau = (T/J)\cdot r$, the maximum occurs at $r_{\max} = D/2$:

$$\tau_{\max} = \frac{T}{J_s} \cdot \frac{D}{2} = \frac{T}{\pi D^4 / 32} \cdot \frac{D}{2} = \frac{32 T}{\pi D^4} \cdot \frac{D}{2} = \frac{16 T}{\pi D^3}$$

> **Result:** $\boxed{\tau_{\max,\,s} = \dfrac{16T}{\pi D^3}}$ ✓

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Maximum Shear Stress in a Hollow Shaft

Substitute $J_h$ and $r_{\max} = D/2$:

$$\tau_{\max,h} = \frac{T}{J_h}\cdot\frac{D}{2} = \frac{T}{\pi(D^4-d^4)/32}\cdot\frac{D}{2} = \frac{16T}{\pi D^3(1-\alpha^4)}$$

> **Result:** $\boxed{\tau_{\max,h} = \dfrac{16T}{\pi D^3 (1 - \alpha^4)}}$ where $\alpha = d/D$ ✓

## 3.6 Derivation 6 — Power-Transmitted Relation

A torque *T* applied to a shaft rotating at angular velocity $\omega$ does work at a rate (power):

$$P = T \cdot \omega$$

Convert rpm to rad/s:
$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60}$$

Substitute:
$$P = T \cdot \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi N T}{60} = \frac{\pi N T}{30}$$

> **Result:** $\boxed{P = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60}\text{ W}}$ (N in rpm, T in N·m)

Inverting for T with P in **kW** and N in **rpm**:

$$T = \frac{60\,P}{2\pi N} = \frac{30\,P}{\pi N}$$

Converting P to kW: $P_{\text{kW}} = P / 1000$, so

$$T = \frac{30 \times 1000 \, P_{\text{kW}}}{\pi N} = \frac{9549 \, P_{\text{kW}}}{N}$$

> **Result:** $\boxed{T = \dfrac{9549\,P_{\text{kW}}}{N}}\text{ N·m}$ ✓

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Formula (Decision Flow)

| Given in the problem | Step 1 (Convert) | Step 2 (Pick formula) | Step 3 (Compute) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Power & Speed | $T = 9549 P/N$ (kW, rpm → N·m) | Then $\tau_{\max} = 16T/(\pi D^3)$ or $\tau_{\max} = 16T/[\pi D^3(1-\alpha^4)]$ | Solve for unknown D or τ |
| Direct Torque given | None | $D^3 = 16T/(\pi \tau_{\text{allow}})$ for solid | Solve for D |
| Angle of twist constraint | Use $J \geq T/[G(\theta/L)_{\text{allow}}]$ | Then $D^4 = 32J/\pi$ for solid | Solve for D |
| Hollow with α fixed | Substitute α into $(1-\alpha^4)$ | $\tau_{\max} = 16T/[\pi D^3(1-\alpha^4)]$ | Solve for D |
| Hollow with d/D ratio | Treat α = d/D as the unknown | One equation, two unknowns — need an additional constraint (typically α) | Iterate to find compatible D and d |

# Section 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of Torsion Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Inputs: Torque T, Speed N, Length L, Material G] --> B[Power to Torque Conversion if needed]
    B --> C{Shaft type?}
    C -- Solid shaft --> D1[Compute J_s = pi D^4 / 32]
    C -- Hollow shaft --> D2[Compute J_h = pi D^4 1 minus alpha^4 / 32]
    D1 --> E[Shear Stress Distribution tau equals T r / J]
    D2 --> E
    E --> F[Max shear at r = D/2]
    F --> G[Compare with allowable tau_allow]
    G --> H{Strength OK?}
    H -- No --> I[Increase D or reduce T]
    H -- Yes --> J[Compute Angle of Twist theta = TL / GJ]
    J --> K[Compare with allowable theta_allow]
    K --> L{Stiffness OK?}
    L -- No --> I
    L -- Yes --> M[Final Design Accepted]
    M --> N[Report D, d, theta, tau, mass]
```

**How to read this flowchart:**

- The **inputs** (torque, speed, length, material) are processed in the first row.
- The **branching node** (`Shaft type?`) decides whether the polar moment of inertia is computed for a solid or hollow section.
- The **central block** evaluates the shear stress at every radius and finds the maximum at the outer fibre.
- The design is then **validated twice** — first against the strength criterion (allowable stress), then against the stiffness criterion (allowable twist).
- If either fails, the loop returns to increase the diameter or revise the design.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Shear Stress Variation Across Cross-Section

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Center r = 0] -->|tau = 0| B[Inner fibre r = d/2]
    B -->|tau = tau_min| C[Mid radius]
    C -->|tau varies linearly| D[Outer fibre r = D/2]
    D -->|tau = tau_max| E[Surface of shaft]
    E -.->|This is the critical fibre| F[Design based on tau_max]
```

**Reading guide:**

- The shear stress grows **linearly** with radius.
- The **center** of the shaft carries **zero** shear stress.
- The **outer surface** carries the **maximum** shear stress — this is the **design-critical** location.
- For a hollow shaft, the inner surface (at $r = d/2$) carries the **minimum** stress that is *not* zero.

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Strength vs Stiffness Design Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Design Problem: Find D] --> B{Which criterion governs?}
    B -- Strength --> C1[tau_max less or equal to tau_allow]
    B -- Stiffness --> C2[theta per L less or equal to theta_allow per L]
    C1 --> D1[Solve D^3 = 16T / pi tau_allow for solid]
    C1 --> D2[Solve D^3 1 minus alpha^4 = 16T / pi tau_allow for hollow]
    C2 --> E1[Solve J greater or equal to T / G theta_per_L_allow]
    E1 --> F1[Then D^4 = 32J / pi]
    D1 --> G[Choose larger of the two D values]
    D2 --> G
    F1 --> G
    G --> H[Final Design D]
```

**Reading guide:** When **both** strength and stiffness criteria are specified, the **larger diameter** from either equation governs the design. The KTU examiner often asks for a design that satisfies **both** criteria, so always compute both and pick the larger *D*.

# Section 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]

**State the torsion equation for a circular shaft and explain the meaning of each term involved.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The torsion equation for a circular shaft is:

$$\frac{T}{J} = \frac{\tau}{r} = \frac{G\theta}{L}$$

where:
- **T** = applied torque (N·m) — the twisting moment acting on the shaft.
- **J** = polar moment of inertia of the cross-section (m⁴) — a geometric property measuring the section's resistance to twisting.
- **τ** = shear stress at radius *r* (N/m²) — the tangential stress developed in the material.
- **r** = radial distance from the shaft axis to the fibre under consideration (m).
- **G** = modulus of rigidity of the material (N/m²) — a material property.
- **θ** = angle of twist (radians) — relative rotation between the two ends.
- **L** = length of the shaft (m).

The equation establishes three equivalent ratios: torque-to-stiffness, stress-to-radius, and a stiffness-to-twist relationship. **(1 mark for equation, 1 mark for any 4 terms, 1 mark for the engineering meaning.)**

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]

**Compare solid and hollow circular shafts with respect to weight and torsional rigidity. State one typical application of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Property | Solid Shaft | Hollow Shaft |
|---|---|---|
| Weight for same outer diameter | Heavier (100% material used) | Lighter (core material removed) |
| Torsional rigidity (J) for same D | $\pi D^4/32$ | $\pi D^4(1-\alpha^4)/32$ (slightly less) |
| Material utilization at high stress | Poor (core material underutilized) | Excellent (most material near outer fibres) |
| Typical application | Machine tool spindles, low-cost low-speed shafts | Automotive drive shafts, aircraft and marine propeller shafts |

**(1 mark for weight, 1 mark for rigidity, 1 mark for applications.)**

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style — Module 4 Torsion]**

**(a)** A solid circular shaft of diameter **80 mm** transmits a torque of **6 kN·m**. Determine:
   (i) The maximum shear stress developed in the shaft.
   (ii) The angle of twist per metre length, if **G = 80 GPa**.

**(b)** A hollow circular shaft has an outer diameter of **100 mm** and inner diameter of **60 mm**. It is required to transmit **120 kW at 200 rpm**. Find:
   (i) The maximum shear stress.
   (ii) The angle of twist in a length of **3 m**, if **G = 80 GPa**.

Compare the results with the solid shaft and comment.

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) (i):** Maximum shear stress in the solid shaft

Given: $D = 80\text{ mm} = 0.08\text{ m}$, $T = 6 \text{ kN·m} = 6000 \text{ N·m}$.

$$\tau_{\max} = \frac{16 T}{\pi D^3} = \frac{16 \times 6000}{\pi \times (0.08)^3}$$

Compute $(0.08)^3 = 0.000512 = 5.12 \times 10^{-4}$ m³.

$$\tau_{\max} = \frac{96000}{\pi \times 5.12 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{96000}{1.6085 \times 10^{-3}} = 5.968 \times 10^{7} \text{ Pa}$$

$$\boxed{\tau_{\max} \approx 59.68 \text{ MPa}}$$

**Part (a) (ii):** Angle of twist per metre

$$J_s = \frac{\pi D^4}{32} = \frac{\pi (0.08)^4}{32}$$

$(0.08)^4 = 4.096 \times 10^{-5}$ m⁴.

$$J_s = \frac{\pi \times 4.096 \times 10^{-5}}{32} = \frac{1.2868 \times 10^{-4}}{32} = 4.021 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^4$$

$$\frac{\theta}{L} = \frac{T}{G J_s} = \frac{6000}{80 \times 10^9 \times 4.021 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{6000}{3.217 \times 10^5}$$

$$\frac{\theta}{L} = 0.01865 \text{ rad/m} = 1.0684 \text{°/m}$$

$$\boxed{\theta/L \approx 0.0186 \text{ rad/m} = 1.07 \text{°/m}}$$

**Part (b) (i):** Torque transmitted

$$T = \frac{9549 \times P}{N} = \frac{9549 \times 120}{200} = \frac{1145880}{200} = 5729.4 \text{ N·m}$$

So $T \approx 5.73$ kN·m.

**Polar moment of inertia (hollow):**

$$\alpha = \frac{d}{D} = \frac{60}{100} = 0.6$$

$$J_h = \frac{\pi D^4}{32}(1 - \alpha^4) = \frac{\pi (0.1)^4}{32}(1 - 0.6^4)$$

$(0.1)^4 = 1 \times 10^{-4}$ m⁴. $\alpha^4 = 0.6^4 = 0.1296$.

$$J_h = \frac{\pi \times 10^{-4}}{32}(1 - 0.1296) = \frac{\pi \times 10^{-4}}{32} \times 0.8704 = 8.541 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^4$$

**Maximum shear stress:**

$$\tau_{\max} = \frac{16 T}{\pi D^3 (1 - \alpha^4)} = \frac{16 \times 5729.4}{\pi \times (0.1)^3 \times 0.8704}$$

$(0.1)^3 = 1 \times 10^{-3}$ m³.

$$\tau_{\max} = \frac{91670.4}{\pi \times 10^{-3} \times 0.8704} = \frac{91670.4}{2.734 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$\boxed{\tau_{\max} \approx 33.53 \text{ MPa}}$$

**Part (b) (ii):** Angle of twist in 3 m

$$\theta = \frac{T L}{G J_h} = \frac{5729.4 \times 3}{80 \times 10^9 \times 8.541 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{17188.2}{6.833 \times 10^5} = 0.02515 \text{ rad}$$

Convert to degrees: $\theta = 0.02515 \times (180/\pi) = 1.441°$.

$$\boxed{\theta \approx 0.0252 \text{ rad} = 1.44°}$$

**Comment:** The hollow shaft (D = 100 mm) carries a slightly *lower* torque (5.73 kN·m vs 6 kN·m) but has a **smaller** maximum shear stress (33.5 MPa vs 59.7 MPa) and is significantly **lighter** in weight. The cross-sectional area of the solid 80-mm shaft is $\pi (0.04)^2 = 5.027 \times 10^{-3}$ m², while the hollow shaft area is $\pi/4 \times (0.1^2 - 0.06^2) = \pi/4 \times 0.0064 = 5.027 \times 10^{-3}$ m² — interestingly the same in this case! But the stress distribution is far better in the hollow shaft.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style — Module 4 Torsion]**

**(a)** Derive the torsion equation $T/J = \tau/r = G\theta/L$ for a circular shaft, stating clearly the assumptions involved.

**(b)** Design a solid steel shaft to transmit **75 kW at 240 rpm**. The allowable shear stress is **60 MPa** and the allowable angle of twist is **1° per metre length**. Take **G = 80 GPa**.

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):** Derivation (Refer to Section 3.3 above for the full derivation with all 6 steps.)

The assumptions are:
1. Material is homogeneous, isotropic, and linearly elastic.
2. Cross-section is circular and remains circular after twisting.
3. Cross-sections remain plane and perpendicular to the axis (no warping).
4. Radial lines remain radial and straight.
5. The twist per unit length is constant.
6. Pure torque only (no bending, no axial load).

**Step 1:** Geometry of deformation — a fibre at radius *r* moves through an arc $r\theta$.

**Step 2:** Shear strain: $\gamma = r\theta / L$.

**Step 3:** Hooke's Law: $\tau = G \gamma = G r\theta / L$.

**Step 4:** Rearrange: $\tau / r = G\theta / L$.

**Step 5:** Torque equilibrium — integrate $\tau r \, dA$ over the section:

$$T = \int_A \tau r \, dA = \frac{G\theta}{L} \int_A r^2 dA = \frac{G\theta J}{L}$$

So $T / J = G\theta / L$.

**Step 6:** Combine: $\boxed{T/J = \tau/r = G\theta/L}$ ✓

**(Award 1 mark for assumptions, 2 marks for derivation, 1 mark for stating the result — but KTU often allows up to 7 marks for derivation alone, so the student must show every step.)**

**Part (b):** Design of a solid steel shaft.

**Step 1:** Compute the torque to be transmitted.

$$T = \frac{9549 \times P}{N} = \frac{9549 \times 75}{240} = \frac{716175}{240} = 2984.1 \text{ N·m}$$

So $T \approx 2.984$ kN·m.

**Step 2 — Design by strength criterion:**

$$\tau_{\max} = \frac{16 T}{\pi D^3} \leq \tau_{\text{allow}}$$

Solving for D:

$$D^3 \geq \frac{16 T}{\pi \tau_{\text{allow}}} = \frac{16 \times 2984.1}{\pi \times 60 \times 10^6}$$

$$D^3 \geq \frac{47745.6}{1.885 \times 10^8} = 2.533 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3$$

$$D \geq (2.533 \times 10^{-4})^{1/3}$$

Compute the cube root: $(2.533)^{1/3} \approx 1.362$, so $D \geq 1.362 \times 10^{-4/3}$ m.

Let us compute carefully:
- $2.533 \times 10^{-4} = 253.3 \times 10^{-6}$.
- $(253.3)^{1/3} \approx 6.32$.
- $(10^{-6})^{1/3} = 10^{-2}$.
- So $D \geq 6.32 \times 10^{-2}$ m $\approx 63.2$ mm.

**Step 3 — Design by stiffness criterion:**

Allowable angle of twist: $1°/\text{m} = \pi/180 \text{ rad/m} = 0.01745 \text{ rad/m}$.

$$J \geq \frac{T}{G \cdot (\theta/L)_{\text{allow}}} = \frac{2984.1}{80 \times 10^9 \times 0.01745} = \frac{2984.1}{1.396 \times 10^9} = 2.137 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^4$$

For a solid shaft, $J = \pi D^4 / 32$, so:

$$D^4 \geq \frac{32 J}{\pi} = \frac{32 \times 2.137 \times 10^{-6}}{\pi} = \frac{6.838 \times 10^{-5}}{\pi} = 2.177 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^4$$

$$D \geq (2.177 \times 10^{-5})^{1/4}$$

Compute: $2.177 \times 10^{-5} = 21.77 \times 10^{-6}$. $(21.77)^{1/4} \approx 2.158$. $(10^{-6})^{1/4} = 10^{-1.5} = 0.03162$.

$$D \geq 2.158 \times 0.03162 = 0.0683 \text{ m} \approx 68.3 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Final design:**

The **stiffness criterion** governs (gives a larger diameter: 68.3 mm > 63.2 mm). Therefore:

$$\boxed{D \geq 68.3 \text{ mm} \rightarrow \text{Adopt standard size } D = 70 \text{ mm}}$$

**Step 5 — Verification of adopted design (D = 70 mm):**

$J = \pi (0.07)^4 / 32 = \pi \times 2.401 \times 10^{-5} / 32 = 2.357 \times 10^{-6}$ m⁴.

$$\tau_{\max} = \frac{16 \times 2984.1}{\pi (0.07)^3} = \frac{47745.6}{\pi \times 3.43 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{47745.6}{1.0776 \times 10^{-3}} = 4.43 \times 10^{7} \text{ Pa} = 44.3 \text{ MPa} < 60 \text{ MPa} \checkmark$$

$$\theta/L = \frac{2984.1}{80 \times 10^9 \times 2.357 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{2984.1}{1.886 \times 10^5} = 0.01583 \text{ rad/m} = 0.907°/\text{m} < 1°/\text{m} \checkmark$$

Both criteria are satisfied. Design is safe.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes KTU examiners actively deduct marks for:**
>
> 1. **Unit conversion blunders (–2 marks):** Failing to convert mm → m before substitution. The entire computation collapses if you use mixed units. Always convert *first*, substitute *later*.
> 2. **Using the wrong formula for hollow shaft (–1 to –2 marks):** Mixing up $\tau_{\max} = 16T/(\pi D^3)$ (solid) with the hollow form. Always include the $(1 - \alpha^4)$ factor.
> 3. **Forgetting to compare with the stiffness criterion (–2 marks):** If the problem states *both* a stress limit *and* an angle-of-twist limit, **compute both diameters and pick the larger**. Many students solve only one and lose 2 marks.
> 4. **Angle-of-twist unit error (–1 mark):** KTU examiners expect the final answer in *radians* or *degrees*, but the formula uses *radians*. If you give the answer in degrees without showing the conversion, expect a deduction.
> 5. **Power-torque formula confusion (–1 mark):** Memorize **T = 9549·P(kW)/N(rpm)** — this single number carries you through a dozen problems.
> 6. **Assuming "D" is the diameter when "R" is the radius (–1 mark):** Always read carefully — many KTU problems switch between D and R. The formula with R uses $J = \pi R^4/2$.
> 7. **Skipping the derivation steps (–2 to –3 marks):** A 14-mark derivation question requires showing *each* assumption and *each* algebraic step. Do not write only the final equation.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this checklist as your last-minute revision sheet the night before the KTU exam.**

- [x] **Master Equation:** $T/J = \tau/r = G\theta/L$ — must be memorized verbatim.
- [x] **Polar Moment — Solid:** $J = \pi D^4/32$ (in terms of diameter) or $J = \pi R^4/2$ (in terms of radius).
- [x] **Polar Moment — Hollow:** $J = \pi (D^4 - d^4)/32$ — do **not** forget the subtraction!
- [x] **α-ratio:** $\alpha = d/D$ — appears in hollow-shaft compact form.
- [x] **Maximum stress location:** Always at the **outer surface** ($r = D/2$).
- [x] **Minimum stress:** Zero at the **center** (solid) or at the **inner surface** (hollow).
- [x] **Linear stress variation:** Shear stress grows **linearly** with *r* — not parabolically, not exponentially.
- [x] **Angle of twist is uniform** across the cross-section; only the *strain* depends on *r*.
- [x] **Power–Torque formula:** $T = 9549 P_{\text{kW}} / N$ — with N in rpm, T in N·m.
- [x] **Two design criteria:** Strength (τ ≤ τ_allow) **and** Stiffness (θ/L ≤ (θ/L)_allow) — pick the **larger** diameter.
- [x] **Hollow shafts are superior** for weight-critical applications (aerospace, automotive) due to the $D^4$ dominance in J.
- [x] **Material property:** Carbon steel G ≈ 77–80 GPa; stainless steel G ≈ 75–78 GPa; aluminium alloy G ≈ 25–28 GPa. Use the value **given in the problem**.
- [x] **Stiffness criterion often governs** for long, slender shafts — always check both.
- [x] **KTU favourite question pattern:** "Design a shaft (solid/hollow) transmitting X kW at Y rpm with given τ_allow and θ_allow" — practice this template thoroughly.
- [x] **Common Pitfall:** If a problem says "find the diameter of a *hollow* shaft with α = 0.6", the *D* you compute is the **outer** diameter; the inner diameter is then $d = 0.6 D$.

> **Final Tip:** Work out at least **three full 14-mark design problems** before your KTU exam — they constitute the bulk of marks in Module 4. Memorize the formula table above as your single most powerful study aid.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Distribution of Shear Stress in Shafts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET302 - SOLID MECHANICS AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Distribution of Shear Stress in Shafts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Torsion** is the twisting of a structural member (shaft) by the application of a couple (torque) acting about its longitudinal axis. When a circular shaft is subjected to equal and opposite torques at its ends, every cross-section experiences a state of **pure shear**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pure Torsion (KTU Definition):** A shaft is said to be under pure torsion when it is subjected to torque only, with no bending moment, axial force, or transverse shear acting on it. The shaft transmits power through this rotational loading.

The **Distribution of Shear Stress in Shafts** describes how the internal shear stress ($\tau$) induced by an applied torque ($T$) varies radially across any cross-section of a circular shaft. According to the **Theory of Pure Torsion**, the shear stress at any radial distance $r$ from the center of the shaft is directly proportional to that radial distance.

$$ \tau \propto r $$

This gives the maximum shear stress at the outer fiber ($r = R$) and zero shear stress at the central axis ($r = 0$).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This topic forms the cornerstone of Module 4 and is heavily tested in Part B (14-mark) questions involving power transmission, angle of twist, and the design of solid versus hollow shafts.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are wringing out a wet towel — both hands apply a twisting motion in opposite directions. The fibers of the towel closest to your hands (the outer surface) slide past each other the most, while the fibers near the very center of the towel barely move at all.

Now extend this analogy to a solid metal shaft, like the **driveshaft of a car** or the **rotor shaft of a turbine**:

- The **outer fibers** of the shaft are stretched and sheared the most — they carry the **highest shear stress**.
- The **inner core** (along the central axis) experiences **zero shear stress** — it contributes almost nothing to resisting the applied torque.
- This is precisely why **hollow shafts** are used in heavy machinery (ship propeller shafts, helicopter rotors, wind turbine masts): removing the low-stress central material saves **weight and material cost** without significantly compromising torque-carrying capacity.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** If the inner fibers are doing nothing useful in a solid shaft, then for the same outer diameter and torque, a hollow shaft can carry **more torque per kilogram** of material than a solid shaft. This is why bicycles, aircraft, and racing cars use tubular components.

## 1.3 Standard Constants and Units

- **Modulus of Rigidity ($G$)** — also called the Shear Modulus. Units: **N/m²** or **Pa** (often GPa in practice).
- **Polar Moment of Inertia ($J$)** — geometric property of the cross-section. Units: **m⁴** or **mm⁴**.
- **Torque ($T$)** — twisting moment. Units: **N·m**.
- **Power ($P$)** — Units: **Watts (W)** or **kW**.
- **Angular Speed ($N$)** — Revolutions Per Minute (**rpm**).

---

## 1.4 Key Assumptions of the Theory of Pure Torsion

For the linear shear stress distribution to hold, the following Saint-Venant assumptions must be satisfied:

1. The shaft material is **homogeneous, isotropic, and linearly elastic** (obeys Hooke's Law in shear).
2. The shaft has a **uniform circular cross-section** throughout its length.
3. Cross-sections **plane before twisting remain plane and perpendicular to the axis after twisting** (no warping).
4. **Radial fibers remain radial and straight** after deformation.
5. The shaft is loaded by **pure torques only** — no axial or bending loads.
6. Distances between cross-sections (shaft length) remain unchanged — only angular rotation occurs.
7. The analysis applies **only to circular cross-sections** (solid or hollow).

> [!WARNING]
> These assumptions break down for **non-circular sections** (rectangular, elliptical, I-sections), short shafts near load application points (Saint-Venant's Principle), and **plastic deformation regimes**. The KTU examiner often tests this awareness.

---

## 1.5 Visualizing the Shear Stress Distribution

Consider a solid shaft of radius $R$ subjected to torque $T$:

- At the **center** ($r = 0$): Shear stress $\tau = 0$
- At any radius $r$: Shear stress $\tau = \dfrac{Tr}{J}$
- At the **outer surface** ($r = R$): Maximum shear stress $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{TR}{J}$

The distribution is **linear**, starting from zero at the axis and rising to a maximum at the circumference.

For a **hollow shaft** with outer radius $R$ and inner radius $r_i$:
- At the **inner surface** ($r = r_i$): Shear stress $\tau_{min} = \dfrac{Tr_i}{J}$
- At the **outer surface** ($r = R$): Shear stress $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{TR}{J}$
- The material **between $r_i$ and $R$** carries the entire torsional load.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept — How the Torsion Equation Emerges

The torsion equation is derived by combining three fundamental relationships:

### Step 1: Geometric Relationship (Strain)

Consider a small element on the surface of a shaft at radius $R$, of length $L$. When torque $T$ is applied, one end rotates relative to the other by an angle $\theta$. The arc length sheared is $R\theta$ over a length $L$, giving the **shear strain** at the surface:

$$ \gamma = \frac{R\theta}{L} $$

For a fiber at radius $r$ (smaller than $R$):

$$ \gamma_r = \frac{r\theta}{L} $$

### Step 2: Constitutive Relationship (Hooke's Law in Shear)

By Hooke's Law in shear:

$$ \tau_r = G \cdot \gamma_r = \frac{G \cdot r \cdot \theta}{L} $$

This shows that **shear stress is directly proportional to the radial distance** $r$.

### Step 3: Static Equilibrium Relationship (Torque)

The torque resisted by an elemental ring of area $dA$ at radius $r$ is:

$$ dT = \tau_r \cdot dA \cdot r = \frac{G \cdot r^2 \cdot \theta}{L} \cdot dA $$

Integrating over the entire cross-section:

$$ T = \frac{G\theta}{L} \int_A r^2 \, dA = \frac{G\theta}{L} \cdot J $$

where $J = \int_A r^2 \, dA$ is the **polar moment of inertia** of the cross-section.

## 2.2 The Torsion Equation (Golden Equation)

Combining the three relationships above yields the **Generalized Torsion Equation**, the most important formula in this module:

$$ \frac{T}{J} = \frac{\tau}{r} = \frac{G\theta}{L} $$

This single equation encapsulates the entire theory of pure torsion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this equation.** Every Part B question in KTU's Module 4 begins with this equation. The examiner expects you to write it down as your first step in any problem.

## 2.3 Polar Moment of Inertia ($J$)

The polar moment of inertia depends solely on geometry:

**Solid Circular Shaft** of diameter $d$ (radius $R = d/2$):

$$ J_{solid} = \frac{\pi d^4}{32} = \frac{\pi R^4}{2} $$

**Hollow Circular Shaft** with outer diameter $D$ and inner diameter $d$:

$$ J_{hollow} = \frac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{32} $$

## 2.4 Maximum Shear Stress

Substituting $r = R$ (or $r = D/2$) into the torsion equation:

$$ \tau_{max} = \frac{T \cdot R}{J} = \frac{16T}{\pi d^3} \quad \text{(for solid shaft)} $$

$$ \tau_{max} = \frac{16T}{\pi D^3 \left(1 - k^4\right)} \quad \text{where } k = \frac{d}{D} \text{ (for hollow shaft)} $$

## 2.5 Angle of Twist

The total angle of twist over a length $L$ for a uniform torque $T$:

$$ \theta = \frac{TL}{GJ} \quad \text{(radians)} $$

For shafts with **multiple segments** carrying different torques:

$$ \theta_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{T_i L_i}{G_i J_i} $$

For shafts with **linearly varying torque** (e.g., stepped loading):

$$ \theta = \int_0^L \frac{T(x)}{GJ(x)} \, dx $$

## 2.6 Power Transmission Equation

The power transmitted by a rotating shaft running at $N$ rpm under torque $T$:

$$ P = \frac{2\pi N T}{60} \quad \text{(Watts, when } N \text{ in rpm and } T \text{ in N·m)} $$

Or in convenient engineering form:

$$ P \, (\text{kW}) = \frac{2\pi N T}{60{,}000} $$

This relation allows conversion between power, speed, and torque — essential when designing drive systems.

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Units | Applicable To |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Torsion Equation | $\dfrac{T}{J} = \dfrac{\tau}{r} = \dfrac{G\theta}{L}$ | Mixed | All circular shafts |
| 2 | Max Shear Stress (Solid) | $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{16T}{\pi d^3}$ | Pa or N/mm² | Solid circular shaft |
| 3 | Max Shear Stress (Hollow) | $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{16T}{\pi D^3 (1 - k^4)}$ | Pa or N/mm² | Hollow circular shaft |
| 4 | Polar Moment (Solid) | $J = \dfrac{\pi d^4}{32}$ | m⁴ or mm⁴ | Solid shaft |
| 5 | Polar Moment (Hollow) | $J = \dfrac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{32}$ | m⁴ or mm⁴ | Hollow shaft |
| 6 | Angle of Twist | $\theta = \dfrac{TL}{GJ}$ | radians | Uniform torque |
| 7 | Angle of Twist (Multi-Segment) | $\theta = \sum \dfrac{T_i L_i}{G_i J_i}$ | radians | Stepped shafts |
| 8 | Power Transmission | $P = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60}$ | Watts | Rotating shafts |
| 9 | Power in kW | $P = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60{,}000}$ | kW | Rotating shafts |
| 10 | Stiffness Factor | $\dfrac{GJ}{L}$ | N·m/rad | Torsional rigidity |

> [!NOTE]
> In the formulas above, when stress is required in **N/mm²** (MPa), use $T$ in **N·mm** and $d, D$ in **mm**. When in **N/m²** (Pa), use SI base units consistently.

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

The shear stress distribution theory is foundational in:

- **Automotive Engineering:** Designing drive shafts, axles, and propeller shafts.
- **Aerospace:** Helicopter rotor masts, aircraft engine shafts.
- **Power Generation:** Turbine rotor shafts in thermal, hydro, and wind plants.
- **Manufacturing:** Spindle shafts of lathes, milling machines, and drill presses.
- **Marine Engineering:** Ship propeller shafts (almost always hollow to save weight).
- **Civil Engineering:** Torsion in beams subjected to eccentric loading.

The principle that **inner fibers carry negligible stress** drives engineers to use **hollow shafts**, **tubular members**, and **thin-walled tubes** wherever weight is critical — exactly the design philosophy behind modern high-performance machinery.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation of the Torsion Equation

### Setup

Consider a solid circular shaft of radius $R$ and length $L$, fixed at one end and subjected to torque $T$ at the other end.

**Step 1 — Visualize the Deformation**

When the shaft twists, a line originally on the surface (parallel to the axis) rotates by angle $\theta$ at the free end. The arc length traced by the outer fiber is $s = R\theta$.

**Step 2 — Compute Shear Strain at Radius $r$**

For a generic fiber at radius $r$, the arc length deformed is $r\theta$. The shear strain (angle of deformation) is:

$$ \gamma_r = \frac{\text{deformation}}{\text{original length}} = \frac{r\theta}{L} $$

**Step 3 — Apply Hooke's Law in Shear**

The shear stress at radius $r$ is:

$$ \tau_r = G \cdot \gamma_r = \frac{G \cdot r \cdot \theta}{L} $$

**Step 4 — Equilibrium of Torque on a Cross-Section**

Consider a thin annular ring of radius $r$, thickness $dr$, and area $dA = 2\pi r \, dr$. The elemental torque resisted by this ring is:

$$ dT = \tau_r \cdot dA \cdot r = \frac{G r \theta}{L} \cdot (2\pi r \, dr) \cdot r = \frac{2\pi G \theta}{L} \cdot r^3 \, dr $$

**Step 5 — Integrate Over the Entire Cross-Section**

$$ T = \int_0^R \frac{2\pi G \theta}{L} \cdot r^3 \, dr = \frac{2\pi G \theta}{L} \cdot \left[ \frac{r^4}{4} \right]_0^R = \frac{2\pi G \theta}{L} \cdot \frac{R^4}{4} $$

$$ T = \frac{G \theta \pi R^4}{2L} $$

**Step 6 — Introduce Polar Moment of Inertia**

By definition:

$$ J = \int_A r^2 \, dA = \int_0^R r^2 \cdot (2\pi r) \, dr = 2\pi \cdot \frac{R^4}{4} = \frac{\pi R^4}{2} $$

Substituting $J$ into the torque equation:

$$ T = \frac{G \theta J}{L} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{T}{J} = \frac{G\theta}{L} $$

**Step 7 — Combine with Step 3**

From Step 3: $\tau_r = G r \theta / L$. So:

$$ \frac{\tau_r}{r} = \frac{G \theta}{L} = \frac{T}{J} $$

Therefore:

$$ \boxed{\frac{T}{J} = \frac{\tau}{r} = \frac{G\theta}{L}} $$

This is the **Generalized Torsion Equation**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Maximum Shear Stress in a Solid Shaft

Starting from:

$$ \tau_{max} = \frac{T \cdot R}{J} $$

Substitute $J = \dfrac{\pi d^4}{32}$ and $R = \dfrac{d}{2}$:

$$ \tau_{max} = \frac{T \cdot (d/2)}{\pi d^4 / 32} = \frac{T \cdot d \cdot 32}{2 \cdot \pi d^4} = \frac{16T}{\pi d^3} $$

$$ \boxed{\tau_{max} = \frac{16T}{\pi d^3}} $$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Maximum Shear Stress in a Hollow Shaft

For a hollow shaft with outer diameter $D$ and inner diameter $d$:

$$ J = \frac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{32} $$

$$ \tau_{max} = \frac{T \cdot (D/2)}{J} = \frac{T \cdot D/2}{\pi (D^4 - d^4)/32} = \frac{16TD}{\pi (D^4 - d^4)} $$

Introducing the ratio $k = d/D$ (so $D^4 - d^4 = D^4(1 - k^4)$):

$$ \boxed{\tau_{max} = \frac{16T}{\pi D^3 (1 - k^4)}} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Writing $\pi d^3$ instead of $\pi D^3$. Always use the **outer diameter $D$**, never the inner diameter, in the denominator.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Comparison of Solid vs Hollow Shaft (Same Material, Same Weight)

This is a classic KTU problem. A solid shaft of diameter $D_s$ is replaced by a hollow shaft of outer diameter $D_h$ and inner diameter $d_h$, with **equal weight per unit length**.

**Equal Area Condition** (since both have the same length and material density):

$$ \frac{\pi D_s^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (D_h^2 - d_h^2)}{4} \Rightarrow D_s^2 = D_h^2 - d_h^2 $$

**Ratio of Maximum Stresses:**

$$ \frac{\tau_{hollow}}{\tau_{solid}} = \frac{16T / [\pi D_h^3 (1 - k^4)]}{16T / [\pi D_s^3]} = \frac{D_s^3}{D_h^3 (1 - k^4)} $$

Using $D_s^2 = D_h^2 (1 - k^2)$, so $D_s^3 = D_h^3 (1 - k^2)^{3/2}$:

$$ \frac{\tau_{hollow}}{\tau_{solid}} = \frac{(1 - k^2)^{3/2}}{(1 - k^4)} = \frac{(1 - k^2)^{3/2}}{(1 - k^2)(1 + k^2)} = \frac{(1 - k^2)^{1/2}}{1 + k^2} $$

For any $k > 0$, this ratio is **less than 1**, meaning the hollow shaft has **lower maximum stress** for the same weight — hence it is more efficient.

---

## 3.5 Numerical Example: Power Transmission Problem

**Problem:** A solid circular shaft of diameter $80$ mm transmits $150$ kW at $250$ rpm. The maximum shear stress must not exceed $60$ MPa. The modulus of rigidity is $80$ GPa. Find:
1. The torque transmitted.
2. The maximum shear stress developed.
3. The angle of twist in degrees per meter length.
4. Whether the shaft is safe.

### Solution:

**(a) Torque Transmitted**

Using $P = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60{,}000}$:

$$ T = \frac{P \times 60{,}000}{2\pi N} = \frac{150 \times 60{,}000}{2\pi \times 250} $$

$$ T = \frac{9{,}000{,}000}{500\pi} = \frac{18{,}000}{\pi} \approx 5729.58 \text{ N·m} $$

**(b) Maximum Shear Stress**

Using $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{16T}{\pi d^3}$ with $T = 5729.58 \times 10^3$ N·mm and $d = 80$ mm:

$$ \tau_{max} = \frac{16 \times 5729.58 \times 10^3}{\pi \times 80^3} = \frac{91{,}673{,}280}{\pi \times 512{,}000} $$

$$ \tau_{max} = \frac{91{,}673{,}280}{1{,}608{,}495.4} \approx 56.99 \text{ N/mm}^2 \approx 57 \text{ MPa} $$

**(c) Angle of Twist per Meter Length**

Polar moment of inertia:

$$ J = \frac{\pi d^4}{32} = \frac{\pi \times 80^4}{32} = \frac{\pi \times 40{,}960{,}000}{32} \approx 4{,}021{,}238 \text{ mm}^4 $$

$$ \theta = \frac{TL}{GJ} = \frac{5729.58 \times 10^3 \times 1000}{80{,}000 \times 4{,}021{,}238} $$

$$ \theta = \frac{5.73 \times 10^9}{3.217 \times 10^{11}} \approx 0.01781 \text{ rad/m} $$

Converting to degrees:

$$ \theta = 0.01781 \times \frac{180}{\pi} \approx 1.02^\circ / \text{m} $$

**(d) Safety Check**

Since $\tau_{max} \approx 57$ MPa $< 60$ MPa, **the shaft is SAFE**.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Each Formula

| Scenario | Key Given | Formula to Apply | Final Unknown |
|---|---|---|---|
| Power, speed → Torque | $P$, $N$ | $T = 60{,}000P / (2\pi N)$ | $T$ |
| Torque, diameter → Stress | $T$, $d$ | $\tau_{max} = 16T / (\pi d^3)$ | $\tau_{max}$ |
| Allowable stress → Required $d$ | $T$, $\tau_{allow}$ | $d = [16T / (\pi \tau_{allow})]^{1/3}$ | $d$ |
| Torque, length, $G$ → Twist | $T$, $L$, $G$, $J$ | $\theta = TL / (GJ)$ | $\theta$ in rad |
| Hollow shaft stress | $T$, $D$, $d$ | $\tau_{max} = 16T / [\pi D^3 (1 - k^4)]$ | $\tau_{max}$ |
| Multiple torques on one shaft | $T_i$, $L_i$ | $\theta_{total} = \sum T_i L_i / (G_i J_i)$ | Total twist |

---

## 3.7 Condition for a Safe Shaft (Design Criterion)

The shaft is safe when:

$$ \tau_{max, \text{developed}} \leq \tau_{allowable} = \frac{\tau_{yield}}{FOS} $$

Where:
- $\tau_{yield}$ is the shear yield strength of the material.
- $FOS$ is the Factor of Safety (typically $2$ to $4$ for shaft design).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Shear Stress Distribution Across a Solid Shaft (Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the conceptual flow of analyzing shear stress distribution in a solid circular shaft, from applied torque to the resulting linear stress profile.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Applied Torque T at Free End] --> B[Shaft Twists by Angle Theta]
    B --> C[Cross Section Remains Plane and Perpendicular]
    C --> D[Outer Fiber Max Shear Strain = R Theta over L]
    D --> E[Hookes Law: Tau r = G r Theta over L]
    E --> F[Tau Proportional to r]
    F --> G[Linear Stress Distribution from Center to Surface]
    G --> H[Tau equals 0 at Center r = 0]
    G --> I[Tau max at Outer Surface r = R]
    I --> J[Tau max equals 16 T over pi d cubed]

    style A fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style J fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style H fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

**Interpretation:** The flowchart traces the logical chain from applied torque to maximum shear stress, making the derivation visually traceable.

---

## 4.2 Comparison Block: Solid vs Hollow Shaft Stress Profile

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SOLID[SOLID SHAFT]
        S1[Center r = 0] --> S2[Tau equals 0]
        S2 --> S3[Mid Radius r = R/2]
        S3 --> S4[Tau equals Tau max by 2]
        S4 --> S5[Outer Surface r = R]
        S5 --> S6[Tau equals Tau max]
    end

    subgraph HOLLOW[HOLLOW SHAFT]
        H1[Inner Surface r equals d over 2] --> H2[Tau equals Tau min]
        H2 --> H3[Mid Wall r equals D plus d over 4]
        H3 --> H4[Linear Variation]
        H4 --> H5[Outer Surface r equals D over 2]
        H5 --> H6[Tau equals Tau max]
    end

    style SOLID fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style HOLLOW fill:#d5f5e3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

**Interpretation:** The diagram contrasts the radial stress profiles. In the solid shaft, stress rises continuously from 0 to $\tau_{max}$. In the hollow shaft, stress starts at $\tau_{min}$ (non-zero at the inner wall) and rises to $\tau_{max}$ — material from $0$ to $r_i$ is wasted.

---

## 4.3 Power Transmission Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Prime Mover: Motor or Engine] -->|Torque T| P2[Shaft Rotating at N rpm]
    P2 -->|Angular Velocity omega = 2 pi N over 60| P3[Power equals T times omega]
    P3 --> P4[P equals 2 pi N T over 60 in Watts]
    P4 --> P5[Driven Machine: Pump, Gear, Propeller]

    style P1 fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style P2 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style P4 fill:#d4edda,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style P5 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

**Interpretation:** The transmission flow shows how the torsion equation links to power engineering. The angular velocity in rad/s is $\omega = 2\pi N/60$, and power is the product $P = T\omega$.

---

## 4.4 Multi-Segment Shaft (Stepped Shaft) Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M1[End A Fixed Support] --> M2[Segment 1: Length L1, Diameter d1, Torque T1]
    M2 --> M3[Pulley 1: External Torque Applied]
    M3 --> M4[Segment 2: Length L2, Diameter d2, Torque T2]
    M4 --> M5[Pulley 2: External Torque Applied]
    M5 --> M6[Segment 3: Length L3, Diameter d3, Torque T3]
    M6 --> M7[End B Free or Loaded]

    style M1 fill:#e2e3e5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style M3 fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style M5 fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style M7 fill:#e2e3e5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

**Interpretation:** In a stepped shaft, each segment carries its own torque (computed from equilibrium using applied external torques). The total angle of twist is the sum:

$$ \theta_{AB} = \sum_{i} \frac{T_i L_i}{G_i J_i} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Most 14-mark questions involve a stepped shaft with 2 or 3 segments and multiple pulleys. The examiner expects a torque diagram first, then per-segment $\theta$ calculations, then summation.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Conceptual Short-Answer

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**Q1.** State the **Generalized Torsion Equation**. Explain why shear stress in a solid circular shaft is maximum at the outer surface and zero at the center.

**Model Answer:**

The Generalized Torsion Equation is:

$$ \frac{T}{J} = \frac{\tau}{r} = \frac{G\theta}{L} $$

where:
- $T$ = Torque applied (N·m)
- $J$ = Polar moment of inertia of the cross-section (m⁴)
- $\tau$ = Shear stress at radius $r$ (Pa)
- $r$ = Radial distance from the center (m)
- $G$ = Modulus of rigidity (Pa)
- $\theta$ = Angle of twist (radians)
- $L$ = Length of the shaft (m)

**Reason for stress variation:** The shear stress $\tau$ is directly proportional to the radial distance $r$. Therefore, at the center ($r = 0$), $\tau = 0$, and at the outer surface ($r = R$), $\tau$ reaches its maximum value $\tau_{max} = TR/J$. Physically, the outer fibers undergo the greatest deformation and hence carry the maximum shear stress.

---

### Question 2: Definition / Comparison

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**Q2.** Compare **solid and hollow circular shafts** of the same weight per unit length in terms of their torque-carrying capacity. Justify why hollow shafts are preferred in high-power transmission systems.

**Model Answer:**

For equal weight per unit length, the cross-sectional areas must be equal:

$$ A_{solid} = A_{hollow} \Rightarrow \frac{\pi D_s^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (D_h^2 - d_h^2)}{4} $$

The maximum shear stress ratio is:

$$ \frac{\tau_{hollow}}{\tau_{solid}} = \frac{\sqrt{1 - k^2}}{1 + k^2} $$

where $k = d_h / D_h$. For any $k > 0$, this ratio is **less than 1**, meaning the hollow shaft develops lower maximum stress for the same torque and weight.

**Conclusion:** Hollow shafts are preferred in high-power systems (e.g., aircraft, marine, and racing applications) because they:
1. Carry more torque per unit weight.
2. Save material and reduce overall mass.
3. Distribute stress more uniformly across the wall thickness.
4. Provide higher torsional stiffness-to-weight ratio.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Pattern

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Format:** Each Part B question offers an internal choice — either Question A or Question B. Both are framed as full 14-mark problems, typically split into sub-parts (a) [7 marks] and (b) [7 marks]. Full step-by-step model solutions are given below.

---

### Question A: Solid Shaft Under Combined Loading and Power Transmission

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**Q.A (a) [7 marks]:** A solid circular shaft of diameter $90$ mm transmits $200$ kW at $300$ rpm. Calculate:
1. The torque transmitted.
2. The maximum shear stress developed in the shaft.
3. The angle of twist per meter length. Take $G = 80$ GPa.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute the Torque**

$$ T = \frac{P \times 60{,}000}{2\pi N} = \frac{200 \times 60{,}000}{2\pi \times 300} = \frac{12{,}000{,}000}{600\pi} = \frac{20{,}000}{\pi} $$

$$ T \approx 6366.20 \text{ N·m} = 6.3662 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm} $$

**Step 2: Compute Maximum Shear Stress**

Using $\tau_{max} = 16T / (\pi d^3)$ with $d = 90$ mm:

$$ \tau_{max} = \frac{16 \times 6.3662 \times 10^6}{\pi \times 90^3} = \frac{101{,}859{,}200}{\pi \times 729{,}000} $$

$$ \tau_{max} = \frac{101{,}859{,}200}{2{,}290{,}221} \approx 44.47 \text{ N/mm}^2 \approx 44.47 \text{ MPa} $$

**Step 3: Compute Angle of Twist per Meter**

Polar moment of inertia:

$$ J = \frac{\pi d^4}{32} = \frac{\pi \times 90^4}{32} = \frac{\pi \times 65{,}610{,}000}{32} \approx 6{,}442{,}018 \text{ mm}^4 $$

$$ \theta = \frac{TL}{GJ} = \frac{6.3662 \times 10^6 \times 1000}{80{,}000 \times 6{,}442{,}018} $$

$$ \theta = \frac{6.3662 \times 10^9}{5.154 \times 10^{11}} \approx 0.01235 \text{ rad/m} $$

Converting to degrees:

$$ \theta = 0.01235 \times \frac{180}{\pi} \approx 0.708^\circ / \text{m} $$

**Final Answers:**
1. $T \approx 6366.20$ N·m
2. $\tau_{max} \approx 44.47$ MPa
3. $\theta \approx 0.0124$ rad/m $= 0.708°$/m

---

**Q.A (b) [7 marks]:** A shaft is required to transmit $250$ kW at $400$ rpm. The maximum permissible shear stress is $70$ MPa. Design the diameter of a solid circular shaft.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute Torque**

$$ T = \frac{250 \times 60{,}000}{2\pi \times 400} = \frac{15{,}000{,}000}{800\pi} = \frac{18{,}750}{\pi} \approx 5968.31 \text{ N·m} $$

Convert to N·mm: $T = 5.96831 \times 10^6$ N·mm.

**Step 2: Apply Maximum Shear Stress Formula**

$$ \tau_{max} = \frac{16T}{\pi d^3} \leq \tau_{allow} = 70 \text{ MPa} $$

Rearranging for $d$:

$$ d^3 \geq \frac{16T}{\pi \tau_{allow}} $$

$$ d^3 \geq \frac{16 \times 5.96831 \times 10^6}{\pi \times 70} = \frac{95{,}492{,}960}{219.911} \approx 434{,}213 \text{ mm}^3 $$

**Step 3: Solve for $d$**

$$ d \geq (434{,}213)^{1/3} \approx 75.78 \text{ mm} $$

**Step 4: Apply Standard Practice**

Round up to the nearest standard size: $d = 80$ mm.

**Final Answer:** Required solid shaft diameter = **80 mm**.

---

### Question B: Hollow Shaft and Stepped Shaft (Internal Choice Alternative)

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**Q.B (a) [7 marks]:** A hollow circular shaft has an outer diameter of $100$ mm and inner diameter of $60$ mm. It transmits $300$ kW at $200$ rpm. Calculate:
1. The torque transmitted.
2. The maximum and minimum shear stress.
3. The angle of twist per meter length. Take $G = 80$ GPa.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute Torque**

$$ T = \frac{300 \times 60{,}000}{2\pi \times 200} = \frac{18{,}000{,}000}{400\pi} = \frac{45{,}000}{\pi} \approx 14{,}323.95 \text{ N·m} $$

Convert: $T = 1.4324 \times 10^7$ N·mm.

**Step 2: Compute Polar Moment of Inertia**

$$ J = \frac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{32} = \frac{\pi (100^4 - 60^4)}{32} $$

$$ J = \frac{\pi (10^8 - 1.296 \times 10^7)}{32} = \frac{\pi \times 8.704 \times 10^7}{32} $$

$$ J \approx 8{,}545{,}855 \text{ mm}^4 $$

**Step 3: Maximum Shear Stress (at outer surface, $r = D/2 = 50$ mm)**

$$ \tau_{max} = \frac{T \cdot R}{J} = \frac{1.4324 \times 10^7 \times 50}{8{,}545{,}855} \approx \frac{7.162 \times 10^8}{8.546 \times 10^6} $$

$$ \tau_{max} \approx 83.81 \text{ N/mm}^2 \approx 83.81 \text{ MPa} $$

**Step 4: Minimum Shear Stress (at inner surface, $r = d/2 = 30$ mm)**

$$ \tau_{min} = \frac{T \cdot r_i}{J} = \frac{1.4324 \times 10^7 \times 30}{8{,}545{,}855} \approx 50.29 \text{ MPa} $$

**Step 5: Angle of Twist per Meter**

$$ \theta = \frac{TL}{GJ} = \frac{1.4324 \times 10^7 \times 1000}{80{,}000 \times 8{,}545{,}855} $$

$$ \theta = \frac{1.4324 \times 10^{10}}{6.837 \times 10^{11}} \approx 0.02095 \text{ rad/m} $$

In degrees: $\theta \approx 1.20°$/m.

**Final Answers:**
1. $T \approx 14{,}323.95$ N·m
2. $\tau_{max} \approx 83.81$ MPa, $\tau_{min} \approx 50.29$ MPa
3. $\theta \approx 0.0210$ rad/m $= 1.20°$/m

---

**Q.B (b) [7 marks]:** A stepped shaft consists of two segments:
- Segment 1: Diameter $60$ mm, Length $1.0$ m, carries torque $2$ kN·m.
- Segment 2: Diameter $80$ mm, Length $1.5$ m, carries torque $5$ kN·m.

Find the **total angle of twist** between the free ends. Take $G = 80$ GPa.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute $J$ for Each Segment**

$$ J_1 = \frac{\pi \times 60^4}{32} = \frac{\pi \times 1.296 \times 10^7}{32} \approx 1.2723 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^4 $$

$$ J_2 = \frac{\pi \times 80^4}{32} = \frac{\pi \times 4.096 \times 10^7}{32} \approx 4.0212 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^4 $$

**Step 2: Compute $\theta$ for Each Segment**

Convert torques to N·mm: $T_1 = 2 \times 10^6$ N·mm, $T_2 = 5 \times 10^6$ N·mm.

$$ \theta_1 = \frac{T_1 L_1}{G J_1} = \frac{2 \times 10^6 \times 1000}{80{,}000 \times 1.2723 \times 10^6} = \frac{2 \times 10^9}{1.018 \times 10^{11}} $$

$$ \theta_1 \approx 0.01965 \text{ rad} $$

$$ \theta_2 = \frac{T_2 L_2}{G J_2} = \frac{5 \times 10^6 \times 1500}{80{,}000 \times 4.0212 \times 10^6} = \frac{7.5 \times 10^9}{3.217 \times 10^{11}} $$

$$ \theta_2 \approx 0.02331 \text{ rad} $$

**Step 3: Total Angle of Twist**

$$ \theta_{total} = \theta_1 + \theta_2 = 0.01965 + 0.02331 = 0.04296 \text{ rad} $$

In degrees:

$$ \theta_{total} = 0.04296 \times \frac{180}{\pi} \approx 2.46^\circ $$

**Final Answer:** Total angle of twist $\approx 0.0430$ rad $= 2.46°$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (KTU Valuation Key):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting Units:** Writing $T = 6366$ without specifying N·m costs 0.5 marks. Always state units clearly.
>
> 2. **Using Wrong Diameter:** In hollow shaft problems, students frequently use $d$ (inner) instead of $D$ (outer) in $\tau_{max} = 16T / (\pi D^3 (1 - k^4))$. The outer diameter **must** appear in the denominator.
>
> 3. **Sign Convention for Torques:** In stepped shaft problems with multiple pulleys, the segment torque is **not** simply the applied torque at one pulley. You must draw a free-body diagram and use equilibrium. Many students skip this step — losing 2–3 marks.
>
> 4. **Mixing Radians and Degrees:** Angle of twist from $\theta = TL/(GJ)$ is in **radians**. Convert to degrees only if explicitly asked.
>
> 5. **Not Stating the Assumption:** Forgetting to mention "plane sections remain plane" or "material is homogeneous and isotropic" costs the first 1 mark in derivation questions.
>
> 6. **Skipping the General Equation:** Always begin a torsion problem by writing $\frac{T}{J} = \frac{\tau}{r} = \frac{G\theta}{L}$ as your starting line. Examiners look for it as a clue that you understand the theory.
>
> 7. **Forgetting to Add Twist Components:** In multi-segment shafts, $\theta_{total} = \sum \theta_i$, **not** the average. Each segment's twist must be computed separately.
>
> 8. **Using $\pi d^3$ Instead of $\pi d^3/16$:** A typo in the formula constants can cascade through an entire problem. Double-check your formula sheet before writing.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Module 4:**

### Core Concepts
- **Torsion Equation:** $\dfrac{T}{J} = \dfrac{\tau}{r} = \dfrac{G\theta}{L}$ — memorize verbatim.
- **Linear Shear Stress Distribution:** $\tau$ increases linearly from $0$ at center to $\tau_{max}$ at the outer surface.
- **Polar Moment of Inertia:** $J_{solid} = \pi d^4 / 32$; $J_{hollow} = \pi(D^4 - d^4) / 32$.

### Key Formulas (Memorize These)
- $\tau_{max}^{solid} = 16T / (\pi d^3)$
- $\tau_{max}^{hollow} = 16T / [\pi D^3 (1 - k^4)]$, where $k = d/D$
- $\theta = TL / (GJ)$ (radians, uniform shaft)
- $\theta_{total} = \sum T_i L_i / (G_i J_i)$ (stepped shaft)
- $P = 2\pi N T / 60$ (Watts); $P = 2\pi N T / 60{,}000$ (kW)

### Assumptions Checklist (For Full Marks in Derivations)
1. Homogeneous, isotropic, linearly elastic material.
2. Uniform circular cross-section.
3. Plane sections remain plane and perpendicular (no warping).
4. Radial fibers remain radial and straight.
5. Pure torsion — no axial or bending loads.
6. Circular cross-section (theory fails for non-circular sections).

### Engineering Judgement Points
- **Hollow shafts** are lighter and stiffer per unit weight — preferred in aerospace, marine, racing.
- **Inner fibers of a solid shaft carry negligible stress** — removing them does not significantly weaken the shaft.
- **Solid shafts** are simpler to manufacture and used for low-to-medium power applications.
- For the **same torque and weight**, the hollow shaft has **lower $\tau_{max}$**.

### Numerical Problem-Solving Workflow
1. **Identify** what's given and what's required.
2. **Convert units** to a consistent system (e.g., N, mm, MPa).
3. **Compute torque** from power if needed: $T = 60{,}000P / (2\pi N)$.
4. **Compute $J$** for the cross-section.
5. **Apply the appropriate formula** ($\tau_{max}$, $\theta$, or design).
6. **Check safety** against allowable stress.
7. **Present final answer** with units.

### Common Units Conversions to Remember
- $1$ GPa $= 10^3$ MPa $= 10^9$ Pa
- $1$ N·m $= 10^3$ N·mm
- $1$ rad $= 180/\pi \approx 57.296°$
- $1$ kW $= 1000$ W
- $1$ rpm $= 2\pi / 60$ rad/s $\approx 0.1047$ rad/s

### Power Equation Variants (All Useful)
- $P = T\omega$, where $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$ rad/s
- $T (N \cdot m) = \dfrac{60{,}000 \cdot P (kW)}{2\pi N (rpm)}$
- $T (N \cdot m) = \dfrac{9549 \cdot P (kW)}{N (rpm)}$ (a quick mental shortcut)

### Final Word
Master the **Torsion Equation** first. Every problem in this module is built on it. Then practice converting between **power, torque, speed**, and computing **shear stress and angle of twist**. A solid grasp of these will guarantee full marks on any Part B question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Power Transmitted by Solid, Stepped and Hollow Shafts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCPET302 - SOLID MECHANICS AND DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Power Transmitted by Solid, Stepped and Hollow Shafts # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **shaft** is a rotating machine element, usually of circular cross-section, used to transmit power from one location to another. In the context of **Power Transmission**, the topic of Solid, Stepped, and Hollow shafts deals with the design and sizing of these components such that they can safely transmit a specified **Power (P)** at a given rotational **Speed (N)** without exceeding the allowable **Shear Stress (τ)** and **Angular Twist (θ)** limits. The general power transmission relationship used in KTU 2024 scheme derivations is: $$P = \frac{2\pi N T}{60}$$ where **P** is in **Watts (W)**, **N** is in **revolutions per minute (rpm)**, and **T** is the **Torque in N·m**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A solid shaft has a uniform cross-section throughout its length. A **stepped shaft** has two or more different diameters along its length (used to accommodate bearings, gears, and pulleys). A **hollow shaft** is a tube with an outer diameter $D$ and inner diameter $d$. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are holding a long iron rod (the shaft) with a motor (power source) spinning it from one end to turn a fan or a wheel at the other end. The rod is twisting along its length. - A **Solid Shaft** is like a solid wooden stick — easy to make, strong, but heavy and uses a lot of material. - A **Hollow Shaft** is like a metal pipe — it can carry the *same torque* with much less weight because the material at the very center contributes almost nothing to resisting twist (the stress is zero at the centerline and maximum at the outer surface). - A **Stepped Shaft** is like a metal rod with bumps — different sections of different diameters to mount gears, pulleys, or bearings, where the loading or mounting requirements differ along the length. > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Insight:** Bicycle axles, drive shafts in cars, propeller shafts of ships, and turbine shafts in power plants are all examples. The propeller shaft of a car is often a *hollow* tube to save weight and improve fuel efficiency, while still transmitting high torque. ## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants The KTU 2024 scheme expects familiarity with the following standard values: - **Modulus of Rigidity (G) for Steel:** **80 GPa = 80 × 10⁹ N/m²** = **80 kN/mm²** - **Modulus of Rigidity (G) for Cast Iron:** **40 to 45 GPa** - **Permissible Shear Stress (τ) for Mild Steel Shafts:** **40 to 60 MPa** - **Permissible Angle of Twist:** **0.25° per metre length** in standard designs. - **Power (P):** measured in **Watts (W)**; **1 kW = 1000 W**, **1 HP = 745.7 W** ≈ **746 W**. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Core Logic of Shaft Design The design of a shaft transmitting power is governed by **two main criteria**: 1. **Strength Criterion (Based on Shear Stress):** The maximum torsional shear stress induced in the shaft must not exceed the permissible shear stress of the material. 2. **Stiffness / Rigidity Criterion (Based on Angle of Twist):** The angle of twist per unit length of the shaft must be within the allowable limit (typically **1° over the entire shaft length** or **0.25° per metre**). ### Step-by-Step Logic Flow: - **Step 1:** Calculate the **Torque (T)** required from the given Power and Speed using the fundamental relation. $$T = \frac{60 \times P}{2\pi N}$$ - **Step 2:** Apply the **Torsion Equation** $\frac{T}{J} = \frac{\tau}{r} = \frac{G\theta}{L}$ to relate the torque to the shaft diameter. - **Step 3:** Substitute the appropriate **Polar Moment of Inertia (J)** for solid or hollow cross-section. - **Step 4:** Equate the maximum shear stress to the permissible value (Strength Criterion) to find the required diameter. - **Step 5:** Equate the angle of twist to the permissible value (Rigidity Criterion) to find the required diameter. - **Step 6:** Take the **larger** of the two diameters obtained as the safe design diameter. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Solid Shaft (diameter d) | Hollow Shaft (outer D, inner d, ratio k = d/D) | Units | |---|----------|-------------------------|--------------------------------------------------|-------| | 1 | Power – Torque relation | $P = \frac{2\pi N T}{60}$ | Same as solid | W, rpm, N·m | | 2 | Torque in terms of Power | $T = \frac{60\,P}{2\pi N} = 9550 \cdot \frac{P_{kW}}{N}$ | Same as solid | N·m | | 3 | Polar Moment of Inertia | $J = \frac{\pi d^{4}}{32}$ | $J = \frac{\pi (D^{4} - d^{4})}{32} = \frac{\pi D^{4}}{32}(1 - k^{4})$ | m⁴ or mm⁴ | | 4 | Maximum Shear Stress | $\tau_{max} = \frac{16\,T}{\pi d^{3}}$ | $\tau_{max} = \frac{16\,T}{\pi D^{3}(1 - k^{4})}$ | Pa or N/m² | | 5 | Angle of Twist | $\theta = \frac{32\,T\,L}{\pi G d^{4}}$ (radians) | $\theta = \frac{32\,T\,L}{\pi G D^{4}(1 - k^{4})}$ (radians) | rad | | 6 | Design by Strength | $d^{3} = \frac{16\,T}{\pi \tau_{per}}$ | $D^{3} = \frac{16\,T}{\pi \tau_{per}(1 - k^{4})}$ | m or mm | | 7 | Design by Rigidity | $d^{4} = \frac{32\,T\,L}{\pi G \theta_{per}}$ | $D^{4} = \frac{32\,T\,L}{\pi G \theta_{per}(1 - k^{4})}$ | m or mm | | 8 | Weight Ratio (Hollow/Solid) | — | $\frac{W_{hollow}}{W_{solid}} = (1 - k^{2})$ | — | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Note for KTU Exams:** The angle of twist θ in the formula is **per unit length** if the formula is written as $\theta/L$. When using the design equation, $\theta_{per}$ is the *total* twist over the *entire length L*. Always double-check whether the question gives $\theta$ per metre or total. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Solid shafts** are used in low-cost machinery, short drive lines, and where simplicity is key (e.g., small electric motors, hand tools). - **Hollow shafts** are used where **weight reduction** and **high strength-to-weight ratio** are critical — aircraft propeller shafts, marine drive shafts, helicopter rotor shafts, automotive drive shafts. - **Stepped shafts** are universal in real machines because different sections need to mount pulleys, gears, couplings, and bearings of varying sizes. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight for KTU:** For the *same material and same torque*, a hollow shaft with a typical ratio k = 0.5 weighs only **75%** as much as a solid shaft, yet has nearly the same strength. This is because the material near the neutral axis (center) carries very little stress. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Power – Torque Relationship **Statement:** A shaft rotating at N rpm under torque T transmits a power P given by $P = \frac{2\pi N T}{60}$. **Derivation:** The work done by a torque T in one complete revolution is: $$W = T \times (2\pi) \quad \text{(work = torque × angle in radians)}$$ The number of revolutions per second is $\frac{N}{60}$ (where N is in rpm). Therefore, the work done per second (which is power) is: $$P = T \times 2\pi \times \frac{N}{60}$$ $$\boxed{P = \frac{2\pi N T}{60} \text{ Watts}}$$ Inverting to find torque: $$T = \frac{60\,P}{2\pi N} = \frac{P \times 60}{2\pi N}$$ If P is in kW: $$T = \frac{1000 \times 60 \times P_{kW}}{2\pi N} = 9549.3 \cdot \frac{P_{kW}}{N} \approx 9550 \cdot \frac{P_{kW}}{N} \text{ N·m}$$ ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Shear Stress Equation for a Solid Shaft **Starting Point:** Torsion equation for a circular shaft: $$\frac{T}{J} = \frac{\tau}{r}$$ For a **solid circular shaft** of diameter d: - Polar moment of inertia: $J = \frac{\pi d^{4}}{32}$ - Outer radius: $r = \frac{d}{2}$ Substituting: $$\frac{T}{\frac{\pi d^{4}}{32}} = \frac{\tau}{\frac{d}{2}}$$ $$\frac{32 T}{\pi d^{4}} = \frac{2\tau}{d}$$ Cross-multiplying: $$32 T \cdot d = 2\tau \cdot \pi d^{4}$$ $$32 T = 2\tau \pi d^{3}$$ $$\tau = \frac{16 T}{\pi d^{3}}$$ **Design Equation (Rearranging for d):** $$d^{3} = \frac{16 T}{\pi \tau}$$ $$\boxed{d = \sqrt[3]{\frac{16 T}{\pi \tau_{per}}}}$$ ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Shear Stress Equation for a Hollow Shaft For a **hollow shaft** with outer diameter D and inner diameter d, define the ratio $k = \frac{d}{D}$ (also called $\alpha$ or C in some textbooks). - Polar moment of inertia: $J = \frac{\pi (D^{4} - d^{4})}{32} = \frac{\pi D^{4}}{32}(1 - k^{4})$ - Outer radius: $r = \frac{D}{2}$ Using the torsion equation: $$\frac{T}{J} = \frac{\tau_{max}}{D/2}$$ $$\frac{T}{\frac{\pi D^{4}}{32}(1 - k^{4})} = \frac{\tau_{max}}{D/2}$$ $$\frac{32 T}{\pi D^{4}(1 - k^{4})} = \frac{2\tau_{max}}{D}$$ $$\tau_{max} = \frac{16 T}{\pi D^{3}(1 - k^{4})}$$ **Design Equation:** $$D^{3} = \frac{16 T}{\pi \tau_{per}(1 - k^{4})}$$ $$\boxed{D = \sqrt[3]{\frac{16 T}{\pi \tau_{per}(1 - k^{4})}}}$$ ## 3.4 Derivation 4: Angle of Twist for Solid and Hollow Shafts Starting again from the torsion equation: $$\frac{T}{J} = \frac{G\theta}{L}$$ Therefore: $$\theta = \frac{T L}{G J}$$ For a **solid shaft** of diameter d: $$\theta = \frac{T L}{G \cdot \frac{\pi d^{4}}{32}} = \frac{32 T L}{\pi G d^{4}} \text{ (radians)}$$ For a **hollow shaft** of outer diameter D and ratio k: $$\theta = \frac{32 T L}{\pi G D^{4}(1 - k^{4})} \text{ (radians)}$$ **Design Equation Based on Rigidity Criterion:** For solid shaft: $$d^{4} = \frac{32 T L}{\pi G \theta_{per}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad d = \sqrt[4]{\frac{32 T L}{\pi G \theta_{per}}}$$ For hollow shaft: $$D^{4} = \frac{32 T L}{\pi G \theta_{per}(1 - k^{4})} \quad \Rightarrow \quad D = \sqrt[4]{\frac{32 T L}{\pi G \theta_{per}(1 - k^{4})}}$$ ## 3.5 Derivation 5: Weight Comparison Between Solid and Hollow Shafts A solid shaft and a hollow shaft of the **same material** and **same length L** transmitting the **same torque T** with the **same maximum shear stress τ**: Setting shear stresses equal: $$\frac{16 T}{\pi d_{solid}^{3}} = \frac{16 T}{\pi D_{hollow}^{3}(1 - k^{4})}$$ $$d_{solid}^{3} = D_{hollow}^{3}(1 - k^{4})$$ $$D_{hollow} = \frac{d_{solid}}{(1 - k^{4})^{1/3}}$$ The **weight** is proportional to the cross-sectional area. Ratio of areas: $$\frac{A_{hollow}}{A_{solid}} = \frac{\frac{\pi}{4}(D^{2} - d^{2})}{\frac{\pi}{4}d_{solid}^{2}} = \frac{D^{2}(1 - k^{2})}{d_{solid}^{2}}$$ Using $D = \frac{d_{solid}}{(1 - k^{4})^{1/3}}$: $$D^{2} = \frac{d_{solid}^{2}}{(1 - k^{4})^{2/3}}$$ Therefore: $$\frac{A_{hollow}}{A_{solid}} = \frac{1 - k^{2}}{(1 - k^{4})^{2/3}}$$ Since weight is proportional to area (for same length and density): $$\boxed{\frac{W_{hollow}}{W_{solid}} = \frac{1 - k^{2}}{(1 - k^{4})^{2/3}}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Verification Example:** If k = 0.5, then numerator = $1 - 0.25 = 0.75$, denominator = $(1 - 0.0625)^{2/3} = (0.9375)^{2/3} \approx 0.957$. Ratio = $0.75 / 0.957 \approx 0.784$. So the hollow shaft weighs only **78.4%** of the solid shaft, while having the **same strength**. ## 3.6 Stepped Shaft Design Concept A **stepped shaft** has multiple sections of different diameters. For a stepped shaft with two sections: - Section 1: length $L_1$, diameter $d_1$, torque $T_1$ - Section 2: length $L_2$, diameter $d_2$, torque $T_2$ **Strength criterion is checked at each section** independently: $$\tau_1 = \frac{16 T_1}{\pi d_1^{3}} \le \tau_{per}$$ $$\tau_2 = \frac{16 T_2}{\pi d_2^{3}} \le \tau_{per}$$ **Rigidity criterion** uses the **total angle of twist** (sum of individual twists): $$\theta_{total} = \frac{32 T_1 L_1}{\pi G d_1^{4}} + \frac{32 T_2 L_2}{\pi G d_2^{4}} \le \theta_{per}$$ For **constant torque throughout** the length but stepped diameters, the diameter at the **most critical section** (maximum torque × longest lever) governs. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Power Transmission Flow Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Power Source
P in kW] --> B[Calculate Torque
T = 9550 P/N] B --> C{Shaft Type?} C --> C1[Solid Shaft
Diameter d] C --> C2[Stepped Shaft
D1, D2, D3...] C --> C3[Hollow Shaft
Outer D, Inner d, Ratio k] C1 --> D1[Shear Stress Check
tau = 16T/pi d^3] C2 --> D2[Check Each Section
tau_i = 16T_i/pi d_i^3] C3 --> D3[Shear Stress Check
tau = 16T/pi D^3 1-k^4] D1 --> E1[Angle of Twist Check
theta = 32TL/pi G d^4] D2 --> E2[Total Twist
sum of theta_i] D3 --> E3[Angle of Twist Check
theta = 32TL/pi G D^4 1-k^4] E1 --> F[Take MAX of d strength and d rigidity] E2 --> F E3 --> F F --> G[Final Design Diameter d] G --> H[Check Weight Optimization
if Hollow vs Solid] ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Shaft Design ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph INPUT[Input Parameters] I1[Power P] I2[Speed N] I3[Length L] I4[Material G, tau] I5[Ratio k for hollow] end subgraph COMPUTE[Computation Engine] P1[Calculate Torque T] P2[Calculate Polar J] P3[Apply Torsion Equation] P4[Strength Diameter d1] P5[Rigidity Diameter d2] end subgraph OUTPUT[Design Output] O1[Required Diameter d] O2[Maximum Shear Stress tau] O3[Total Twist theta] O4[Weight Saving percentage] end I1 --> P1 I2 --> P1 I3 --> P5 I4 --> P4 I4 --> P5 I5 --> P2 P1 --> P3 P2 --> P3 P3 --> P4 P3 --> P5 P4 --> O1 P5 --> O1 P1 --> O2 P1 --> O3 I5 --> O4 ``` ## 4.3 Cross-Sectional Comparison Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph SOLID[Solid Shaft Cross Section] S1[Outer diameter d] S2[Stress = 0 at center] S3[Stress = max at outer surface r = d/2] S1 --> S2 S1 --> S3 end subgraph HOLLOW[Hollow Shaft Cross Section] H1[Outer diameter D] H2[Inner diameter d = kD] H3[Stress = 0 at inner surface] H4[Stress = max at outer surface r = D/2] H1 --> H2 H1 --> H3 H1 --> H4 end subgraph STEPPED[Stepped Shaft Side View] ST1[Section 1 diameter d1] ST2[Section 2 diameter d2 bigger] ST3[Section 3 diameter d3 smaller] ST4[Fillet transitions] ST1 --> ST2 ST2 --> ST3 ST2 --> ST4 end ``` ## 4.4 Solid vs Hollow Shaft — Key Design Comparison ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Choose Shaft Type] --> B{Optimization Goal?} B --> B1[Minimum Weight
Use Hollow] B --> B2[Minimum Cost
Use Solid] B --> B3[Mixed Loading
Use Stepped] B1 --> C1[k = 0.5 typical
Weight saved ~22 percent] B2 --> C2[Single diameter
Easy manufacturing] B3 --> C3[Multiple diameters
Mount gears and pulleys] C1 --> D[Final Check Strength and Rigidity] C2 --> D C3 --> D D --> E[Approved Design] ``` # 5.
