# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4]`

**Q: Define a coupling. List any four types of rigid couplings used in power transmission.**

**Model Answer:**

A **coupling** is a mechanical device that connects two rotating shafts for the purpose of transmitting power (torque) from one shaft to another. It may also accommodate slight misalignments between the shafts.

The four types of rigid couplings are:

1. **Sleeve (Muff) coupling** — A hollow cylinder fitted over the two shaft ends, locked by a key.
2. **Clamp (Split-muff / Compression) coupling** — A split sleeve tightened by bolts; relies on friction.
3. **Flange coupling** — Two flanges keyed to the shafts and bolted together.
4. **Solid / Marine coupling** — A heavy rectangular block (used in marine propeller shafts).

> [!NOTE]
> Examiner awards 1 mark for definition, ½ mark each for the four types. Drawing a small diagram gets you a bonus mark.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Module 4]`

**Q: Differentiate between a rigid coupling and a flexible coupling. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Rigid Coupling | Flexible Coupling |
|---|---|---|
| Shaft alignment required | **Perfectly aligned** shafts only | Tolerates slight **misalignment** |
| Compensation | Cannot compensate for misalignment | Compensates angular / parallel / axial misalignment |
| Construction | Simple, solid, no flexible element | Has a flexible element (rubber bush, pins, cross, disc) |
| Cost | Low | Higher |
| Use | Light loads, aligned industrial drives | Heavy drives with vibration/shock, vehicle shafts |
| Examples | Sleeve (Muff), Flange, Clamp, Solid | Bushed-pin, Universal (Hooke's), Oldham, Jaw, Disc |

**Two examples each:**

- **Rigid:** Sleeve coupling, Flange coupling.
- **Flexible:** Universal coupling, Bushed-pin flexible coupling.

> [!WARNING]
> Students often confuse the *types* with the *applications*. Do **not** list "car drive shaft" as a type of coupling — that is an *application*. The correct type is "Universal coupling."

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> At KTU ESE (End Semester Exam), Module 4 carries one 14-mark question with internal choice. You must attempt *either* Question A **or** Question B.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** A muff (sleeve) coupling is required to connect two shafts transmitting **20 kW at 250 rpm**. The permissible shear stress for the sleeve material is **42 MPa** and the permissible crushing stress for the shaft surface is **85 MPa**. Design the coupling and find the dimensions of the sleeve.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Two shafts are connected by a flange coupling with **6 bolts**. The shaft diameter is **60 mm**, the bolt circle diameter is **$3d$**, and the bolts have a diameter of **20 mm**. The flange thickness is **$0.75 d$**. The permissible shear stress for the shaft is **45 MPa** and for the bolt is **30 MPa**. Find the maximum power that can be transmitted at **300 rpm**, considering the bolt in shear.

---

#### Solution to Question A (a) — Sleeve Coupling Design

**Step 1: Design Torque**

$$
T = \frac{60{,}000 \times 20}{2\pi \times 250} = \frac{1{,}200{,}000}{1570.8} = 763.94~\text{N·m} = 763{,}944~\text{N·mm}
$$

**Step 2: Shaft Diameter**

$$
d = \sqrt[3]{\dfrac{16 \times 763{,}944}{\pi \times 42}} = \sqrt[3]{\dfrac{12{,}223{,}104}{131.95}} = \sqrt[3]{92{,}635} \approx 45.32~\text{mm}
$$

Take **$d = 50$ mm** (next standard size).

**Step 3: Sleeve Dimensions**

$$
D = 2d + 13 = 2 \times 50 + 13 = 113~\text{mm}
$$

$$
L = 3.5d = 3.5 \times 50 = 175~\text{mm}
$$

**Step 4: Check Sleeve in Torsion**

$$
T_{\text{sleeve}} = \frac{\pi}{16} \times 42 \times \frac{113^{4}-50^{4}}{113}
$$

$$
113^{4} = 163{,}047{,}361, \quad 50^{4} = 6{,}250{,}000
$$

$$
T_{\text{sleeve}} = \frac{\pi}{16} \times 42 \times \frac{163{,}047{,}361 - 6{,}250{,}000}{113} = 2.625\pi \times 1{,}387{,}126 \approx 11{,}440{,}200~\text{N·mm} \approx 11{,}440~\text{N·m}
$$

Since $11{,}440 \gg 763.94$, the sleeve is **SAFE in torsion** ✓

**Step 5: Check Hub in Crushing**

$$
T_{\text{crush}} = \frac{85 \times 50 \times 175 \times (113+50)}{4} = \frac{85 \times 50 \times 175 \times 163}{4}
$$

$$
= \frac{121{,}256{,}250}{4} = 30{,}314{,}063~\text{N·mm} \approx 30{,}314~\text{N·m}
$$

Since $30{,}314 \gg 763.94$, the hub is **SAFE in crushing** ✓

**Final Design:** $d = 50$ mm, $D = 113$ mm, $L = 175$ mm.

---

#### Solution to Question A (b) — Flange Coupling Power Limit

**Step 1: Geometric Data**

$$
d = 60~\text{mm}, \quad D_1 = 3d = 180~\text{mm}, \quad d_b = 20~\text{mm}, \quad n = 6
$$

**Step 2: Torque from Bolt Shear Criterion**

Each bolt is in single shear, area $A_b = \pi d_b^{2}/4$. The torque resisted is:

$$
T = n \cdot \tau_b \cdot \frac{\pi d_b^{2}}{4} \cdot \frac{D_1}{2} = 6 \times 30 \times \frac{\pi \times 20^{2}}{4} \times \frac{180}{2}
$$

$$
T = 6 \times 30 \times 314.16 \times 90 = 6 \times 30 \times 28{,}274 = 5{,}089{,}320~\text{N·mm} \approx 5089~\text{N·m}
$$

**Step 3: Power Transmitted at N = 300 rpm**

$$
P = \frac{2 \pi N T}{60{,}000} = \frac{2 \pi \times 300 \times 5089}{60{,}000} = \frac{9{,}591{,}480}{60{,}000} = 159.86~\text{kW}
$$

**Final Answer:** $P_{\max} \approx \mathbf{160~\text{kW}}$ based on bolt shear criterion.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** — `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Module 4]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** A flange coupling is to be designed to transmit **30 kW at 500 rpm**. The shaft and key are made of steel with permissible shear stress of **45 MPa**. The bolts are to be of mild steel with permissible shear stress of **30 MPa** and permissible crushing stress of **60 MPa**. Assume **4 bolts** and bolt circle diameter **$3d$**. Find (i) shaft diameter, (ii) bolt diameter, and (iii) flange thickness.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain with a neat sketch the construction and working of (i) a **bushed-pin flexible coupling** and (ii) a **universal (Hooke's) coupling**. State one industrial application of each.

---

#### Solution to Question B (a) — Flange Coupling Design

**Step 1: Design Torque**

$$
T = \frac{60{,}000 \times 30}{2\pi \times 500} = \frac{1{,}800{,}000}{3141.6} = 572.96~\text{N·m} = 572{,}958~\text{N·mm}
$$

**Step 2: Shaft Diameter**

$$
d = \sqrt[3]{\dfrac{16 \times 572{,}958}{\pi \times 45}} = \sqrt[3]{\dfrac{9{,}167{,}328}{141.37}} = \sqrt[3]{64{,}853} \approx 40.21~\text{mm}
$$

Take **$d = 45$ mm** (next standard size).

**Step 3: Bolt Circle Diameter and Flange Thickness**

$$
D_1 = 3d = 3 \times 45 = 135~\text{mm}
$$

Assume **$t = d = 45$ mm** (initial).

**Step 4: Bolt Diameter (from Shear Criterion)**

$$
d_b = \sqrt{\dfrac{8T}{n\,\pi\,D_1\,\tau_b}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{8 \times 572{,}958}{4 \times \pi \times 135 \times 30}}
$$

$$
d_b = \sqrt{\dfrac{4{,}583{,}664}{50{,}894.6}} = \sqrt{90.06} = 9.49~\text{mm}
$$

Take **$d_b = 12$ mm** (next standard size; M12 bolt).

**Step 5: Check Flange Thickness (from Crushing Criterion)**

$$
t_{\text{required}} = \dfrac{2T}{n\,D_1\,d_b\,\sigma_{cb}} = \dfrac{2 \times 572{,}958}{4 \times 135 \times 12 \times 60}
$$

$$
t_{\text{required}} = \dfrac{1{,}145{,}916}{388{,}800} = 2.95~\text{mm}
$$

Since $t_{\text{required}} = 2.95~\text{mm} \ll t_{\text{assumed}} = 45~\text{mm}$, the flange is **very safe in crushing**. We may reduce $t$ to about **10 mm** for economy, but for standard practice, keep $t = d = 45$ mm (or $0.5d = 22.5$ mm).

**Final Design:** $d = 45$ mm, $D_1 = 135$ mm, $d_b = 12$ mm, $t = 45$ mm (assumed), $n = 4$.

---

#### Solution to Question B (b) — Flexible Couplings (Theory)

**(i) Bushed-Pin Flexible Coupling:**

- **Construction:** Two flanges are keyed to the two shafts. The flanges have projections with pins. The pins are surrounded by **rubber bushes** that fit into holes in the mating flange.
- **Working:** Torque is transmitted from one shaft → its flange → pins (via rubber bushes) → mating flange → other shaft. The **rubber bushes** cushion shocks, dampen vibrations, and allow small misalignments.
- **Application:** Connecting electric motors to reciprocating pumps and compressors where vibration and slight misalignment occur.

**(ii) Universal (Hooke's) Coupling:**

- **Construction:** Two yokes (forks) at 90° to each other, connected by a **cross-shaped intermediate block (spider)** with four trunnions (pins) using needle bearings.
- **Working:** The cross-block transmits torque between the two yokes. The angle between the two shafts (called the **half-angle $\alpha$**) can be **up to 15°–20°**, accommodating large angular misalignment.
- **Application:** Rear-axle drive of cars, trucks, and propeller shafts in marine applications.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Where students lose marks on this question:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the unit conversion**: $P$ (kW) → $T$ (N·mm) uses $T = 60{,}000{,}000 \cdot P / (2\pi N)$. Mixing N·m and N·mm costs 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Using the wrong formula for bolt shear**: Many students wrongly use $T = n \cdot \frac{\pi}{16} d_b^{3} \tau_b$. The *correct* form using tangential force at radius $D_1/2$ is $T = n \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} d_b^{2} \tau_b \cdot \frac{D_1}{2}$.
> 3. **Skipping the standard-size rounding**: KTU expects you to round $d$ and $d_b$ up to the *next standard size* (e.g., 12, 16, 20, 25, 30, 35, 40, 45, 50, 55, 60 mm). Not doing this loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Not drawing a labelled diagram** in the flexible-coupling theory part. Even a 30-second freehand sketch of the cross-block and yokes gets you 2 free marks.
> 5. **Confusing $D$ and $D_1$**: $D$ is the **sleeve outer diameter** (in sleeve coupling) or the **flange outer diameter** (in flange coupling). $D_1$ is the **bolt circle diameter**. Do not interchange them.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Rapid-Revision Checklist — Print this for last-minute review.**

### **A. Definitions You Must Memorize**
- **Coupling** — device to transmit torque between two shafts.
- **Rigid coupling** — for perfectly aligned shafts.
- **Flexible coupling** — for misaligned shafts; absorbs shock.
- **Muff/Sleeve coupling** — hollow cylinder, simplest rigid type.
- **Flange coupling** — two bolted flanges, the most designed rigid type.
- **Universal coupling** — Hooke's joint, large angular misalignment.
- **Bushed-pin coupling** — uses rubber bushes for vibration damping.

### **B. The Master Equations to Memorize (8 formulas)**

| # | Equation | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T = 60{,}000\,P/(2\pi N)$ N·m | Torque from power |
| 2 | $d = \sqrt[3]{16T/(\pi\tau)}$ | Shaft diameter |
| 3 | $D = 2d + 13$ mm | Sleeve outer diameter |
| 4 | $L = 3.5d$ mm | Sleeve length |
| 5 | $T_{\text{sleeve}} = \frac{\pi}{16}\tau(D^{4}-d^{4})/D$ | Sleeve torsion check |
| 6 | $T_{\text{crush}} = \sigma_c \cdot d \cdot L \cdot (D+d)/4$ | Hub crushing check |
| 7 | $d_b = \sqrt{8T/(n\pi D_1 \tau_b)}$ | Bolt diameter (flange) |
| 8 | $t = 2T/(n D_1 d_b \sigma_{cb})$ | Flange thickness (crushing) |

### **C. Standard Proportions to Memorize**

- Sleeve: $D = 2d + 13$, $L = 3.5d$.
- Flange: $D_1 = 3d$, $t = d$ (assumed), $n = 4$ or 6, $d_b \approx 0.5d$ to $d$.

### **D. KTU Common Pitfalls (Lose Marks Here)**

1. ❌ Unit errors (N·m vs N·mm vs MPa) — **3 marks lost per error**.
2. ❌ Not rounding $d$ and $d_b$ to standard sizes — **1 mark lost**.
3. ❌ Forgetting the $D_1/2$ moment-arm in the bolt-shear derivation — **2 marks lost**.
4. ❌ Skipping the standard-proportions table at the start of the solution — **2 marks lost**.
5. ❌ No labelled diagram in flexible-coupling theory — **2 marks lost**.
6. ❌ Writing $\tau$ instead of $\tau_s$ / $\tau_b$ without defining — **½ mark lost**.

### **E. Quick Comparison Chart for Exam**

| Feature | Sleeve (Muff) | Clamp | Flange |
|---|---|---|---|
| Alignment tolerance | None | None | None |
| Assembly ease | Hard (must slide) | Easy (split) | Moderate |
| Torque capacity | Low–Medium | Medium | High |
| Maintenance | Difficult | Easy | Moderate |
| KTU exam frequency | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★★★ |

### **F. Mnemonic to Remember the Bolt Equation**

> **"Tangential Force × Radius = Torque" → "F = 2T/(nD₁)"** — and the shear area is $\pi d_b^2/4$. Equate and solve for $d_b$. Done!
